topics.title: System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a System

> [!NOTE]
> **System (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **System** is a purposeful collection of interrelated, interdependent, and interacting elements (components, subsystems, or processes) that work in coordination with a defined boundary to transform a set of **Inputs** into **Outputs** under the influence of the **Environment**, while satisfying a specified **Objective Function**.

Mathematically, a system $S$ can be represented as a 6-tuple:

$$
S = \{X, Y, Z, T, F, E\}
$$

Where:
- $X$ = Set of Inputs
- $Y$ = Set of Outputs
- $Z$ = Internal States
- $T$ = Time horizon (continuous or discrete)
- $F$ = Functional/Transformation mapping ($F : X \rightarrow Y$)
- $E$ = Environmental disturbances

### 1.2 Formal Definition of System Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Design (KTU Board Exam Definition – 4 marks standard):**
> **System Design** is the systematic, iterative, and structured engineering process of defining, configuring, and integrating the **architecture**, **subsystems**, **components**, **interfaces**, and **operational protocols** of a system to satisfy a prescribed set of **functional requirements**, **performance specifications**, **reliability targets**, and **constraint boundaries** within acceptable cost and risk envelopes.

### 1.3 Hierarchical Classification of Systems (KTU 2024 Module 1 – High Weightage)

| Level | System Type | Defining Characteristic | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | **Sub-System** | Lowest functional unit | Gear, Bolt, Sensor |
| 1 | **Component** | Single technical function | Electric Motor, Piston |
| 2 | **Assembly** | Group of components | Gearbox, Crankcase |
| 3 | **Subsystem** | Major functional block | Transmission, Braking |
| 4 | **System** | Full functional unit | An Automobile |
| 5 | **System of Systems (SoS)** | Multiple integrated systems | Smart City Traffic Network |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Designing a Household Water Supply Network"**
> Imagine you are an engineer asked to supply water from an overhead tank to every room of a 3-storey house. This is **System Design** in its purest form.
>
> - **Inputs**: Water at the tank inlet, elevation pressure, pipe cost.
> - **Outputs**: Flow rate (litres/min) at every tap, with minimum leakage.
> - **Constraints**: Pipe diameter $\leq 25$ mm, total cost $\leq \text{Rs. } 50{,}000$, water hammer pressure $\leq 4$ bar.
> - **Components (Sub-systems)**: Pump, pipes, valves, joints, storage tank.
> - **Objective**: Maximize flow uniformity OR minimize cost OR minimize energy.
>
> Just as you would first sketch the *layout* (conceptual), then choose *pipe sizes* (preliminary), and finally specify *every joint and valve* (detailed), engineering system design follows the same **top-down hierarchical decomposition** logic.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in System Design

The following **standard metrics** are universally assessed in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

- **System Reliability** ($R$): Probability of failure-free operation, dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**: Units = **hours**, denoted by symbol **h**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**: Units = **hours**.
- **Availability** ($A$): Ratio of **uptime to total time**, dimensionless.
- **Failure Rate** ($\lambda$): Units = **failures/hour** or **FIT (Failures In Time, $10^9$ device-hours)**.
- **Design Margin (Safety Factor)**: Typically $n \in [1.25, 4.0]$ for mechanical systems.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)**: Number of independent design variables, must be $\geq 0$ for a valid design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Reliability** with **Availability**. Reliability is a function of **operating time**, whereas Availability is a function of **uptime and repair time**. Do not interchange them in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Phase System Design Process (KTU Module 1 Core Syllabus)

System design in the KTU 2024 scheme is universally taught as a **five-phase iterative process**. The phases are sequential but allow back-tracking (feedback loops).

#### Phase 1 – Problem Definition and Need Analysis
- Identify the *real-world need* (market demand, user requirement, regulatory push).
- Construct a **Customer Need Statement (CNS)**.
- Define the **Mission Statement** of the system.
- Quantify: performance, cost, life, reliability, environmental impact.

#### Phase 2 – Conceptual Design (Synthesis)
- Generate multiple *alternative design concepts*.
- Use morphological analysis to combine sub-functions and principle solutions.
- Apply **Weighted Decision Matrix (WDM)** to rank concepts.
- Lock the **best concept** to proceed to Phase 3.

#### Phase 3 – Preliminary Design (Analysis)
- Develop the **mathematical model** of the chosen concept.
- Formulate the **objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ and the **constraint functions** $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ and $h_k(\mathbf{x})$.
- Conduct **sensitivity analysis** on key design variables.
- Verify feasibility using **DoF analysis**.

#### Phase 4 – Detailed Design (Optimization)
- Apply formal **mathematical programming** techniques (LP, NLP, IP, GP).
- Solve for optimal design variables $\mathbf{x}^*$.
- Generate final engineering drawings, BOM (Bill of Materials), tolerance stack-up.

#### Phase 5 – Validation, Testing, and Iteration
- Build a **prototype**.
- Conduct **performance tests**, **reliability tests** (e.g., accelerated life testing).
- Compare measured vs predicted performance; if mismatch exceeds tolerance $\epsilon$, **iterate back to Phase 3**.

### 2.2 The General Design Optimization Problem (KTU 2024 – High Weightage)

The general problem of system design optimization is stated as:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \; f(\mathbf{x})
$$

Subject to the constraint set:

$$
g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

$$
h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

$$
x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the **vector of design variables**.
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (minimize mass, cost, deflection, stress; maximize efficiency, life).
- $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **inequality constraints** (stress $\leq$ allowable, deflection $\leq$ limit).
- $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints** (energy balance, force equilibrium).
- $x_i^{L}, x_i^{U}$ are the **side constraints** (lower and upper bounds on variables).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mandatory Memorization Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas carry **direct 2-3 marks** in KTU 2024 ESE and are tested every semester in Part A or short Part B questions.

| S.No | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Reliability of $n$ components in series (all must work) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Reliability of $n$ components in parallel (only one must work) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_{\text{par, identical}} = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | Parallel reliability of $n$ identical components | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability at time $t$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | **hours** |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | Operational availability | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{DoF} = n - m - p$ | Degrees of freedom ($n$ vars, $m$ ineq., $p$ eq. constraints) | integer |
| 9 | $n_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\sigma_{\text{actual}}}$ | Design safety factor (load) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Margin of Safety} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\sigma_{\text{actual}}} - 1$ | Margin of safety | dimensionless |
| 11 | $L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_k \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x})$ | Lagrangian (used in KKT conditions) | varies |
| 12 | $\text{Cost}_{\text{life}} = C_0 + \dfrac{C_{\text{op}}}{\text{LCC factor}}$ | Life-cycle cost | **Rs.** or **USD** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** All instances of $\vert$ have been replaced with explicit words (e.g., "diameter" or "of") inside the table to strictly preserve markdown table syntax integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of System Design Optimization

- **Aerospace**: Minimizing structural mass of a wing spar under stress, deflection, and buckling constraints (Aeroelastic Optimization).
- **Automotive**: Crashworthiness optimization of chassis rails (maximize energy absorption, minimize intrusion).
- **VLSI**: Gate sizing for minimum power-delay product in CMOS digital circuits.
- **Civil**: Truss topology optimization for minimum-weight bridges under code-specified load cases (IS 800, AISC).
- **Manufacturing**: Process parameter optimization (cutting speed, feed, depth) for minimum cost or maximum tool life (Taylor's equation).
- **Energy**: Solar PV array design for maximum power output under partial shading conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Almost every Module 2 to Module 5 topic in Design Optimization (LP, NLP, GA, KKT, Simplex) ultimately **traces back to the formulation in Section 2.2**. If your problem formulation is wrong, every subsequent method fails. Hence KTU examiners always test formulation as a **7-mark sub-question**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reliability of $n$ Components in Series

Consider $n$ independent components $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ connected in **series** such that failure of **any one component** causes system failure. The system works only if **all components work simultaneously**.

**Step 1: Define individual component reliabilities.**
Let $R_i$ denote the probability that component $C_i$ functions successfully within the mission time $T$.

$$
P(C_i \text{ works}) = R_i
$$

**Step 2: Apply the multiplication rule for independent events.**
Since component failures are statistically independent:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{when } A \text{ and } B \text{ are independent}
$$

**Step 3: Extend the rule to $n$ components.**

$$
R_{\text{series}} = P(C_1 \text{ works} \cap C_2 \text{ works} \cap \ldots \cap C_n \text{ works})
$$

**Step 4: Apply independence successively.**

$$
R_{\text{series}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n
$$

**Step 5: Write in compact product notation.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Consequence:** Series reliability is **always less than the smallest** $R_i$. Example: five components each with $R = 0.95$ in series give $R_{\text{series}} = 0.95^5 \approx 0.774$, a significant drop. This is why series design is the **weakest link principle**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reliability of $n$ Components in Parallel (Active Redundancy)

Consider $n$ independent components $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ connected in **parallel** such that the system works as long as **at least one** component works.

**Step 1: Define unreliability (probability of failure) of each component.**

$$
Q_i = 1 - R_i = P(C_i \text{ fails})
$$

**Step 2: Compute probability that ALL components fail simultaneously.**
For independence:

$$
P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)
$$

**Step 3: Compute system reliability as the complement.**

$$
R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - P(\text{all fail})
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)}
$$

**Step 5: Special case for $n$ identical components with $R_i = R$.**

$$
R_{\text{par, identical}} = 1 - (1 - R)^n
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Limit:** As $n \to \infty$, $R_{\text{parallel}} \to 1$. This is the mathematical basis of **redundancy-based high-reliability systems** (e.g., NASA's redundant flight computers, Boeing 777's triple-redundant electrical systems).

### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Availability from MTBF and MTTR

A repairable system cycles through **uptime** and **downtime** intervals. Let MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.

**Step 1: Express the average cycle time.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 2: Availability is the fraction of cycle time during which system is operational.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 3: Divide numerator and denominator by MTTR to obtain the operational form.**

$$
\boxed{A = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}}}
$$

**Step 4: Express in terms of failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$.**

Using $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$:

$$
A = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom (DoF) in a Mechanical System

A valid engineering design must have **non-negative degrees of freedom**, where the number of free design variables is sufficient to satisfy the constraints.

**Step 1: Count independent design variables.**

Let $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ be the vector of $n$ independent design parameters.

**Step 2: Count constraint equations.**

Let $m$ inequality constraints and $p$ equality constraints be active at the optimum.

**Step 3: Compute DoF.**

$$
\boxed{\text{DoF} = n - p - m_{\text{active}}}
$$

- $\text{DoF} = 0$ : The system is *statically determinate / well-posed*.
- $\text{DoF} > 0$ : The system is *under-constrained / optimizable*.
- $\text{DoF} < 0$ : The system is *over-constrained / infeasible*. **The design is invalid.**

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Constraint Classification (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equality | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Equilibrium / Conservation | $\sum F = 0$, $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ |
| Inequality ($\leq$) | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Upper-bound capacity | $\sigma \leq \sigma_{\text{yield}}$ |
| Inequality ($\geq$) | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ | Lower-bound requirement | $P_{\text{out}} \geq 50$ kW |
| Side constraint | $x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}$ | Variable bounds | $10 \leq d \leq 50$ mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a System. List the six elements of the general system model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **System** is a purposeful collection of interrelated and interacting elements that work together to transform inputs into outputs while satisfying a defined objective within a bounded environment.

The six elements of the general system model are:

1. **Inputs** ($X$): Resources, energy, or data entering the system.
2. **Outputs** ($Y$): The result of the system's transformation.
3. **Processor / Transformation** ($F$): The mechanism that converts inputs to outputs.
4. **Environment** ($E$): External factors that influence the system.
5. **Feedback** ($FB$): Information returned to control and improve system performance.
6. **Boundary**: The physical or logical separator that defines system scope.

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Series and Parallel system reliability configurations with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Series Configuration | Parallel Configuration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Failure logic | All components must work | At least one component must work |
| Formula | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ |
| Reliability trend | Lower than the smallest $R_i$ | Higher than the largest $R_i$ |
| Example | Christmas tree light string | Aircraft's triple-redundant flight computer |

### 5.2 Part B – Choice Question (14 Marks)

#### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A pump assembly in a chemical plant consists of 4 components arranged in series, with reliabilities $0.95$, $0.90$, $0.92$ and $0.88$ respectively. The plant engineers wish to add one redundant component in parallel to the weakest component to improve overall reliability.**

**(a) Compute the reliability of the original system. Also identify the weakest component.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Compute the new system reliability after adding a redundant component in parallel to the weakest component. Comment on the improvement achieved.** **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Original System Reliability:**

The 4 components are in series. Apply the product rule:

$$
R_{\text{original}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot R_4
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
R_{\text{original}} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.88
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8550
$$

$$
0.8550 \times 0.92 = 0.7866
$$

$$
0.7866 \times 0.88 = 0.6922
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{original}} \approx 0.6922}
$$

**Identifying the Weakest Component:**

Comparing individual reliabilities: $0.95 > 0.92 > 0.90 > 0.88$. The **weakest component is Component 4** with $R_4 = 0.88$.

**(b) New System Reliability with Redundancy:**

The redundant component (with $R_{4b} = 0.88$) is placed in **parallel** with the original Component 4. The combined reliability of the parallel pair is:

$$
R_{4,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_4)(1 - R_{4b})
$$

$$
R_{4,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.88)(1 - 0.88) = 1 - (0.12)^2
$$

$$
R_{4,\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.0144 = 0.9856
$$

The new system reliability (with 3 series + 1 parallel-pair) is:

$$
R_{\text{new}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot R_{4,\text{parallel}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.9856
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8550
$$

$$
0.8550 \times 0.92 = 0.7866
$$

$$
0.7866 \times 0.9856 \approx 0.7752
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{new}} \approx 0.7752}
$$

**Comment on Improvement:**

Improvement in reliability:

$$
\Delta R = R_{\text{new}} - R_{\text{original}} = 0.7752 - 0.6922 = 0.0830
$$

Percentage improvement:

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{0.0830}{0.6922} \times 100\% \approx 11.99\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding redundancy to the *weakest link* yields the **maximum possible improvement**. Adding it to the strongest component (Component 1) would yield only a negligible gain. This is a direct application of the *weakest-link principle* in system design.

#### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A CNC machining center has an exponential failure distribution with a mean time to failure (MTTF) of 2000 hours. The mean time to repair (MTTR) is 40 hours. The machine is used for 8 hours per day, 25 days per month.**

**(a) Compute the failure rate $\lambda$, reliability for a 200-hour mission, and the steady-state availability of the system.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Formulate the complete design optimization problem (objective function, constraints, and design variables) for selecting the optimal cutting speed $v$ and feed rate $f$ of the CNC turning process to minimize production cost per part, given a surface finish constraint of $R_a \leq 1.6 \; \mu\text{m}$. State the units of each variable.** **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Failure Rate, Mission Reliability, and Availability:**

**Step 1: Failure rate $\lambda$.**

For exponential distribution, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \dfrac{1}{2000} = 0.0005 \; \text{failures/hour}
$$

In FIT (Failures In Time, $10^9$ device-hours):

$$
\lambda = 500 \; \text{FIT}
$$

**Step 2: Reliability for 200-hour mission.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 200} = e^{-0.1}
$$

$$
\boxed{R(200) = 0.9048}
$$

**Step 3: Steady-state availability.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 40}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{2000}{2040} = \dfrac{100}{102} \approx 0.9804
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 98.04\%}
$$

**(b) Design Optimization Formulation:**

**Step 1: Identify Design Variables.**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ f \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Units: $v$ in **m/min**, $f$ in **mm/rev**.

**Step 2: Formulate the Objective Function.**

The cost per part (using Taylor's tool life equation $VT^n f^m d^p = C$ and machine-hour rate) is:

$$
C_p(\mathbf{x}) = C_{\text{machine}} \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} + C_{\text{tool}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{\text{cycle}}}{T} + C_{\text{material}}
$$

The complete optimization problem:

$$
\min_{v, f} \; C_p(v, f)
$$

**Step 3: State the Constraints.**

Surface finish constraint (empirical: $R_a \approx f^2 / 8r$ for turning, $r$ is tool nose radius):

$$
g_1(v, f) = R_a - 1.6 \; \mu\text{m} \leq 0
$$

Tool life constraint (minimum acceptable life $T \geq 30$ min):

$$
g_2(v, f) = 30 - T(v, f) \leq 0
$$

Spindle power constraint (cutting power $P \leq P_{\text{spindle}}$):

$$
g_3(v, f) = P(v, f) - P_{\text{spindle}} \leq 0
$$

Side constraints:

$$
50 \leq v \leq 300 \; \text{m/min}, \quad 0.05 \leq f \leq 0.5 \; \text{mm/rev}
$$

**Final Formulation:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\min_{v, f} \quad & C_p(v, f) \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & g_1(v,f) \leq 0,\; g_2(v,f) \leq 0,\; g_3(v,f) \leq 0 \\
& 50 \leq v \leq 300,\; 0.05 \leq f \leq 0.5
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Frequent Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Do not write "$R_s$" and "$R_p$" interchangeably.** In part (a), clearly state whether the configuration is *series* or *parallel* before writing the formula.
> 2. **Always carry units** in part (b). MTTF in *hours*, $\lambda$ in *failures/hour* or *FIT*, reliability as a *dimensionless number* between 0 and 1. Marks are explicitly allotted for unit specifications.
> 3. **Do not forget side constraints** (bounds on $v$ and $f$) in optimization formulation. KTU 2024 scheme considers side constraints as *active inequality constraints* and deducts 1 mark if omitted.
> 4. **In part (a) Choice 2**, the phrase "formulate" is key – students often *solve* the optimization problem. The question only asks for **formulation**, i.e., expressing $f(\mathbf{x})$, $g_j(\mathbf{x})$, and bounds. Solving is not required and wastes time.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final Day Reading):**

- [ ] A **System** is a 6-tuple: $\{X, Y, Z, T, F, E\}$.
- [ ] System design is a **5-phase iterative process**: Problem Definition → Conceptual → Preliminary → Detailed → Validation.
- [ ] The general optimization problem is $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_j \leq 0$, $h_k = 0$, and $x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}$.
- [ ] **Series reliability** is a product: $R_s = \prod R_i$. It is dominated by the weakest component.
- [ ] **Parallel reliability** is $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$. Used for redundancy.
- [ ] For $n$ **identical** parallel components: $R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^n$.
- [ ] Exponential reliability: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Availability** is NOT reliability. $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- [ ] **Degrees of Freedom**: $\text{DoF} = n - p - m_{\text{active}}$. A valid design requires $\text{DoF} \geq 0$.
- [ ] A safety factor (design margin) is the ratio of allowable to actual stress.
- [ ] Lagrangian $L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j g_j + \sum \lambda_k h_k$ is used in KKT conditions (Module 2).
- [ ] Always **declare units explicitly** in KTU numerical answers: hours, FIT, m/min, mm/rev, $\mu$m.
- [ ] When asked to "formulate", write the optimization problem symbolically – do not solve it unless explicitly asked.
- [ ] In reliability block diagrams, series = AND logic, parallel = OR logic.
- [ ] Reliability is always between 0 and 1; if you get a value > 1, re-check the formula.
- [ ] Life-cycle cost optimization includes $C_0$ (initial) + $C_{\text{op}}$ (operating) + $C_{\text{disposal}}$ (recycling).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morphology of Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Morphology of Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Morphology of Design — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Morphology of Design** refers to the systematic study of the *structure, form, and constituent elements* of an engineering design activity. The term *morphology* (borrowed from biology and linguistics) literally means the "study of forms." In the context of engineering design, morphology examines how a design problem is **decomposed into sub-problems**, how **design decisions** are sequenced, and how the overall **design process is shaped** to transform an abstract need into a tangible, optimized artifact.

As per the **VDI 2221 (Verein Deutscher Ingenieure)** German standard — a globally referenced design process model — every engineering design is a **goal-directed, iterative, information-processing activity** structured across four logical phases:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Definition**
> Morphology of Design is the structural framework that classifies the **types of design**, identifies the **stages of the design process**, defines the **hierarchy of design decisions** (system → subsystem → component), and establishes the **morphological chart** (Zwicky's Method) as a creative synthesis tool.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Movie Production Pipeline"

Imagine you want to produce a **movie**. You don't simply start shooting. Instead, you follow a structured pipeline:

| Movie Production Stage | Equivalent Engineering Design Stage |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Script Writing** (defining story, characters, goals) | **Conceptual Design** (function structure, working principles) |
| **Storyboarding** (planning each scene) | **Embodiment Design** (preliminary layout, form sketches) |
| **Direction & Detailed Filming** (camera angles, lighting) | **Detail Design** (final dimensions, tolerances, materials) |
| **Editing & Re-Shoots** (iteration based on test screenings) | **Iteration & Optimization** (refinement through analysis) |

Just as a movie without structure becomes a chaotic sequence of scenes, a design without morphology becomes a sequence of arbitrary decisions. The **morphology of design** is therefore the **director, the script, and the storyboard combined** — it tells the engineer *what to do, in what order, and why*.

## 1.3 Why is Morphology Important in Optimization?

Every **optimization problem** in engineering begins as a *design problem*. Without a clear morphological understanding:

- The **decision variables** (design variables) cannot be correctly identified.
- The **objective function** cannot be unambiguously stated.
- The **constraints** (functional, physical, economic) cannot be enumerated.
- The **search space** becomes ill-defined, leading to infeasible or meaningless solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A well-structured morphology transforms an ambiguous "I want to design something better" into a mathematically precise statement: *Minimize f(X) subject to g(X) ≤ 0 and h(X) = 0.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Design (Design Levels)

Every complex engineering system can be viewed at multiple **levels of abstraction**. This is a fundamental principle of design morphology.

- **Level 0 — System Level:** The complete product or plant (e.g., a car, a power plant, an aircraft).
- **Level 1 — Subsystem Level:** Major functional units (e.g., engine, transmission, brakes in a car).
- **Level 2 — Component Level:** Individual parts (e.g., piston, crankshaft, connecting rod in an engine).
- **Level 3 — Detailed Feature Level:** Geometric features of individual parts (e.g., fillet radius, surface finish).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design decisions made at higher levels have exponentially greater impact than those at lower levels.** Choosing the wrong propulsion concept at Level 1 cannot be corrected by optimizing the piston shape at Level 3.

## 2.2 Classification of Design (Based on Novelty)

The German standard **VDI 2225** classifies design into three distinct types based on the degree of newness:

| Type of Design | Description | Example | Optimization Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Original (New) Design** | No prior solution exists; entire concept is novel | First moon rover | Heuristic + creative search (Morphological chart) |
| **Adaptive Design** | Existing solution adapted to new requirements by dimensional/parameter changes | Redesign of a pressure vessel for higher pressure | Parametric optimization (Classical methods) |
| **Variant Design** | Solution variant chosen from pre-existing alternatives within a known family | Selecting gear from standard catalogue | Variant selection, discrete optimization |

## 2.3 The Phases of Design (VDI 2221 / Pahl & Beitz Model)

The universally accepted **four-phase model of engineering design** is:

1. **Planning / Clarification of Task:** Analyze the requirements, formulate the design brief, identify constraints.
2. **Conceptual Design:** Develop function structures, identify working principles, generate concepts.
3. **Embodiment Design:** Develop the layout, rough dimensions, materials, preliminary analysis.
4. **Detail Design:** Finalize all drawings, tolerances, surface specifications, manufacturing processes.

Each phase has its own **morphological characteristics** — its own inputs, outputs, decision variables, and constraints.

## 2.4 Zwicky's Morphological Chart (Morphological Box)

**Fritz Zwicky** (the astrophysicist) proposed the **Morphological Analysis** method in 1967. It is a structured creativity tool used in **Conceptual Design**.

The procedure is:

- **Step 1:** Decompose the overall required **function** into sub-functions.
- **Step 2:** For each sub-function, enumerate all possible **means (solutions/principles)**.
- **Step 3:** Construct an **n-dimensional matrix** (rows = sub-functions, columns = means).
- **Step 4:** Select one entry from each row and combine to form a complete **design concept**.

This is essentially a **combinatorial search over the concept space**, and modern optimization algorithms often trace their lineage to this idea.

## 2.5 Design as a Mathematical Optimization Problem

Every design can be formalized as an optimization problem with the following canonical structure:

| Element | Mathematical Symbol | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Design Vector** | $\mathbf{X} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | Independent variables chosen by designer | Mixed |
| **Objective Function** | $f(\mathbf{X})$ | Criterion to be minimized or maximized | e.g., kg, kW, ₹ |
| **Inequality Constraints** | $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0$, $j = 1, \ldots, m$ | Functional, geometric, performance limits | Mixed |
| **Equality Constraints** | $h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0$, $k = 1, \ldots, p$ | Governing physical laws (equilibrium, etc.) | Mixed |
| **Side Constraints (Bounds)** | $x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U$, $i = 1, \ldots, n$ | Allowable range of each design variable | Mixed |
| **Design Space** | $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | Total space defined by bounds | Continuous/Discrete |
| **Feasible Region** | $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{S} : g_j \le 0,\ h_k = 0\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{S}$ satisfying all constraints | Continuous/Discrete |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The morphology of design provides the **structural blueprint** for correctly formulating the design vector $\mathbf{X}$, identifying the objective $f$, and enumerating the constraints. Without this morphological clarity, the mathematical formulation is ambiguous.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of the Optimization Problem from Morphology

Let us derive how the morphology of design **translates** into a formal optimization problem statement.

**Step 1 — Identify the Need.**
The need is expressed as a goal: *"produce a design that is best in some sense."*

$$
\text{Goal} \longrightarrow \text{Minimize or Maximize a measurable quantity.}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Design Variables.**
Through morphological decomposition, the engineer identifies $n$ independent parameters that fully characterize the design. These are bundled into a **column vector**:

$$
\mathbf{X} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n
\end{bmatrix}
\in \mathbb{R}^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Objective Function.**
A scalar function $f(\mathbf{X})$ quantifies the "goodness" of the design. Typically expressed as:

$$
f(\mathbf{X}) : \mathbb{R}^{n} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}
$$

If the goal is to minimize mass, then $f(\mathbf{X}) = m(\mathbf{X})$.

**Step 4 — Identify Constraints from the Morphology of the Problem.**
Constraints arise from **physics** (equilibrium, kinematics), **performance specifications**, **manufacturing limits**, **regulatory standards**, and **economic bounds**. They are categorized:

$$
g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \quad \text{(Inequality)}
$$

$$
h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \quad \text{(Equality)}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the Canonical Problem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & \mathbf{X}^* = [x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \\
\text{that minimizes} \quad & f(\mathbf{X}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the Feasible Region.**
The intersection of all bounds and constraints defines the **feasible design space**:

$$
\mathcal{F} = \left\{ \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \,\big\vert\, g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0,\ h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0,\ x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U \right\}
$$

**Step 7 — Define the Optimality Condition (Unconstrained Case).**
For an unconstrained problem, the **necessary condition** for a local minimum is:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{X}^*) = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[2pt]
\vdots \\[2pt]
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}
\end{bmatrix}_{\mathbf{X}^*} = \mathbf{0}
$$

For this to be a **minimum** (sufficient condition), the Hessian must be positive definite:

$$
\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{X}^*) = \nabla^{2} f(\mathbf{X}^*) \succ 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{v} > 0,\ \forall \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Morphology to Optimization

The following analytical matrix links each **morphological component** of design to its corresponding **mathematical optimization element**:

| Morphological Component | Mathematical Counterpart | Role in Optimization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Functional requirement | Objective $f(\mathbf{X})$ | What we optimize |
| Design parameters (independent) | Design variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ | What we vary |
| Physical laws (equilibrium, etc.) | Equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0$ | What must hold exactly |
| Performance specs (limits) | Inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0$ | What cannot be violated |
| Manufacturing / catalogue limits | Side constraints $x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U$ | Allowable ranges |
| Concept selection (Morphological chart) | Combinatorial / discrete search | Choice among alternatives |
| Iteration / refinement loop | Iterative optimization algorithm | Convergence to optimum |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Truss Member Design

Consider a mild steel axial bar carrying tensile load $P = 50$ kN. The morphology of the problem:

- **Need:** Minimum mass.
- **Material property:** $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.
- **Length (given):** $L = 1.2$ m.
- **Safety factor:** $n = 2$.

**Step A — Identify design variable.** The unknown is the cross-sectional diameter $d$.

$$
\mathbf{X} = [d]^T \quad \text{with} \quad n = 1
$$

**Step B — Formulate objective.** Mass $m$:

$$
m(d) = \rho \cdot A(d) \cdot L = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L
$$

**Step C — Formulate stress constraint.** Working stress limit:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{250}{2} = 125 \ \text{MPa}
$$

Actual stress:

$$
\sigma(d) = \frac{P}{A(d)} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}
$$

Constraint:

$$
g_1(d) = \sigma(d) - \sigma_{allow} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} - \sigma_{allow} \le 0
$$

**Step D — Side constraint** (positive diameter):

$$
d > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad d^L = 0,\ d^U = \infty \ \text{(practically finite)}
$$

**Step E — Final canonical formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & d \\
\text{minimizing} \quad & m(d) = \rho L \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} \le \sigma_{allow} \\
& d > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step F — Solution.** Since mass increases monotonically with $d$, the minimum mass occurs at the **constraint boundary** (this is the **active constraint principle** — a foundational concept in design optimization):

$$
\frac{4P}{\pi d^2} = \sigma_{allow} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{4 \times 50{,}000}{\pi \times 125 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^2 &= \frac{200{,}000}{3.927 \times 10^8} = 5.093 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 \\
d &= \sqrt{5.093 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02257 \ \text{m} \approx 22.57 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step G — Optimum mass:**

$$
m^* = 7850 \times 1.2 \times \frac{\pi (0.02257)^2}{4} = 7850 \times 1.2 \times 4.001 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.77 \ \text{kg}
$$

This worked example illustrates how the **morphology of the design** (need → variables → objective → constraints → bounds) leads naturally to the optimization formulation and its solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Design Process Morphology (VDI 2221 / Pahl-Beitz)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Recognition] --> B[Clarification of Task]
    B --> C[Functional Requirements]
    C --> D[Function Structure]
    D --> E[Conceptual Design]
    E --> F[Working Principles]
    F --> G[Embodiment Design]
    G --> H[Preliminary Layout and Dimensions]
    H --> I[Detail Design]
    I --> J[Final Drawings and Specifications]
    J --> K{Iterate and Optimize}
    K -->|Yes| B
    K -->|No| L[Final Product]
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style L fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style K fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The flow moves through four morphological stages: **Clarification → Conceptual → Embodiment → Detail**.
- The **iteration loop** (highlighted in yellow) emphasizes that optimization is not a linear terminal step but a feedback mechanism.
- Each stage transforms **information** (requirements, principles, dimensions) into progressively more concrete **design artifacts**.

## 4.2 Morphological Chart (Zwicky's Method) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FS[Function Structure]
        F1[Sub Function 1: Support Load]
        F2[Sub Function 2: Transmit Motion]
        F3[Sub Function 3: Damp Vibration]
    end
    subgraph M1[Means Group 1]
        M1a[Option A: Spring]
        M1b[Option B: Leaf Spring]
        M1c[Option C: Air Damper]
    end
    subgraph M2[Means Group 2]
        M2a[Option P: Shaft]
        M2b[Option Q: Belt Drive]
        M2c[Option R: Gear Pair]
    end
    subgraph M3[Means Group 3]
        M3a[Option X: Rubber Mount]
        M3b[Option Y: Hydraulic Damper]
        M3c[Option Z: Magnetic Damper]
    end
    F1 --> M1
    F2 --> M2
    F3 --> M3
    M1a --> Combo1[Concept 1: A + P + X]
    M1a --> Combo2[Concept 2: A + P + Y]
    M1b --> Combo3[Concept 3: B + Q + Y]
    M1c --> Combo4[Concept 4: C + R + Z]
    style FS fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593
    style M1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style M2 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E
    style M3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F
    style Combo1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Combo2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Combo3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Combo4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **function structure** (leftmost block) decomposes the overall need into sub-functions.
- The **means groups** (three colored blocks) enumerate all available technical solutions for each sub-function.
- The **combinations** (green blocks at the bottom) are the generated design concepts. In this 3×3 matrix, there are $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ possible concepts.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Design Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Level 0: System Concept] --> L1[Level 1: Subsystem Architecture]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2: Component Selection]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Parametric Dimensions]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Tolerances and Manufacturing]
    L0 -.- N1[High Impact, Low Frequency Decisions]
    L4 -.- N2[Low Impact, High Frequency Decisions]
    style L0 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style L1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style L2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style L3 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
    style L4 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
    style N1 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style N2 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- Decisions cascade **top-down** (red → blue), moving from abstract (system concept) to concrete (tolerances).
- The dashed annotations emphasize the **inverse relationship** between decision frequency and decision impact — a foundational tenet of design morphology.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define the term "Morphology of Design." What are its main objectives in the context of design optimization?

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The *Morphology of Design* is the systematic study of the **structure, form, and elements** of an engineering design activity. It examines how a design problem is decomposed, how decisions are sequenced, and how the design process is shaped.

**Main Objectives:**

1. To **decompose** a complex design problem into manageable sub-problems (functional decomposition).
2. To **classify** designs into types (original, adaptive, variant) for appropriate strategy selection.
3. To **structure the design process** into well-defined phases (Conceptual → Embodiment → Detail).
4. To **establish design hierarchies** (system → subsystem → component).
5. To provide a foundation for correctly formulating the **design variables, objectives, and constraints** of an optimization problem.

---

### **Question 2:** Explain the significance of Zwicky's Morphological Chart in conceptual design. Illustrate with a suitable example.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Zwicky's Morphological Chart is a **structured creativity and synthesis tool** used during the conceptual design phase. It enables systematic exploration of the **complete solution space** by combining sub-functions with their possible means.

**Procedure:**

1. Decompose the overall function into sub-functions.
2. For each sub-function, list all possible technical means (working principles).
3. Form an $n \times m$ matrix (rows = sub-functions, columns = means).
4. Select one entry per row and combine to form a complete design concept.

**Example — Function: "Transport a person":**
- Sub-functions: *Carry Load*, *Provide Motion*, *Steer Direction*.
- Means for *Carry Load*: Chair, Hammock, Standing Platform.
- Means for *Provide Motion*: Wheels, Legs, Propeller.
- Means for *Steer Direction*: Handlebar, Joystick, Rudder.
- Total possible concepts: $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ (from which Bicycle, Car, Boat, etc., are valid combinations).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the **four phases of the design process** as per the **VDI 2221 / Pahl-Beitz model**. Discuss the inputs, outputs, and key activities at each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular shaft is to transmit a torque of $T = 1200$ N·m at a rotational speed of $N = 250$ rpm. Formulate the **optimization problem** for designing the shaft for **minimum mass**, subject to a torsional shear stress constraint and a deflection (angle of twist) constraint. Use the morphological approach. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — The Four Phases of Design**

**Phase 1 — Clarification of Task (Planning):**
- **Inputs:** Customer needs, market study, technical specifications, regulatory requirements.
- **Activities:** Demand analysis, target specification document (DSP), listing of constraints.
- **Outputs:** Design brief, requirement list, QFD (Quality Function Deployment) matrix.

**Phase 2 — Conceptual Design:**
- **Inputs:** Requirement list from Phase 1.
- **Activities:** Functional decomposition (function structure), search for working principles, abstraction, use of morphological chart, concept evaluation (Pugh matrix, AHP).
- **Outputs:** Concept variants, selected concept(s).

**Phase 3 — Embodiment Design:**
- **Inputs:** Selected concept(s).
- **Activities:** Preliminary sizing, material selection, layout drafting, rough analysis (stress, deflection), evaluation of variants.
- **Outputs:** Embodiment layout, preliminary dimensions, BOM (Bill of Materials).

**Phase 4 — Detail Design:**
- **Inputs:** Embodiment layout.
- **Activities:** Final calculation, CAD modeling, tolerance analysis, FEA, manufacturing process selection, documentation.
- **Outputs:** Production-ready drawings, specifications, assembly instructions.

**Iteration:** Each phase may loop back if inconsistencies arise (highlighted by the feedback arrows in the VDI 2221 model).

#### **Part (b) — Mathematical Formulation**

**Step 1 — Identify Design Variable.**
The diameter of the shaft $d$ is the only unknown design variable.

$$
\mathbf{X} = [d]^T, \quad d > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Objective Function (Minimum Mass).**
Let length $L$ (given) and density $\rho$ be known.

$$
m(d) = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Torsional Shear Stress Constraint.**
Polar section modulus: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$.

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}
$$

Allowable stress: $\tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa (for mild steel).

$$
g_1(d) = \tau_{max} - \tau_{allow} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} - \tau_{allow} \le 0
$$

**Step 4 — Angle of Twist Constraint.**
Polar moment of inertia: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$. Using $G = 80$ GPa for mild steel:

$$
\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G d^4}
$$

Allowable angle: $\theta_{allow} = 1^\circ = 0.01745$ rad per meter length.

$$
g_2(d) = \theta - \theta_{allow} = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G d^4} - \theta_{allow} \le 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final Canonical Formulation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & d \\
\text{minimizing} \quad & m(d) = \rho L \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3} \le \tau_{allow} \\
& \dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G d^4} \le \theta_{allow} \\
& d > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Active Constraint.**
Since $m(d) \propto d^2$ (monotonically increasing), the minimum mass occurs at the **active constraint boundary**. Compute the critical diameter for each constraint and take the larger:

From stress constraint:
$$
d_1 = \left(\frac{16T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 1200}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^6}\right)^{1/3} = (1.019 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} \approx 0.0468 \ \text{m}
$$

From twist constraint:
$$
d_2 = \left(\frac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{allow}}\right)^{1/4}
$$

Substituting $L = 2$ m (assumed), $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\theta_{allow} = 0.01745$ rad:

$$
d_2 = \left(\frac{32 \times 1200 \times 2}{\pi \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 0.01745}\right)^{1/4} = \left(1.750 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{1/4} \approx 0.0648 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Optimum Diameter.**

$$
d^* = \max(d_1, d_2) = 0.0648 \ \text{m} = 64.8 \ \text{mm} \quad \text{(twist governs)}
$$

This completes the morphological-to-optimization formulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of engineering design** into original (new), adaptive, and variant types according to **VDI 2225**. Explain which optimization strategies are appropriate for each type, with at least one industrial example per type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter $D = 0.6$ m and is subjected to an internal pressure $p = 2$ MPa. Formulate the **optimization problem** to minimize the mass of the vessel. Use mild steel with $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, and a factor of safety $n = 2.5$. The design variable is the wall thickness $t$. Assume longitudinal joints are 80% efficient and circumferential joints are 100% efficient. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Design**

| Type | Description | Optimization Strategy | Industrial Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Original / New Design** | Entirely new concept; no comparable prior solution exists | Creative heuristics, morphological chart, TRIZ, multi-objective optimization | First commercial 3D-printed human organ scaffold |
| **Adaptive Design** | Existing design modified through parameter changes to meet new specs | Parametric / classical optimization (Kuhn-Tucker, Lagrange multipliers) | Redesign of a centrifugal pump impeller for higher head |
| **Variant Design** | Selecting from pre-existing standard variants | Discrete optimization, branch-and-bound, integer programming | Selecting a standard ball bearing from a manufacturer's catalogue for a given load and speed |

**Key Insight:** The **type of design** dictates the **type of optimization problem** (continuous vs. discrete, single vs. multi-objective). A Variant Design typically reduces to a **selection problem**, not a true optimization, whereas Original Design often requires **heuristic and creative** approaches due to the absence of an initial feasible baseline.

#### **Part (b) — Pressure Vessel Optimization**

**Step 1 — Design Variable.** Wall thickness $t$.

$$
\mathbf{X} = [t]^T, \quad t > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Mass Function.**
For a thin-walled cylinder of length $L$ (assume $L = 2$ m):

$$
m(t) = \rho \cdot \pi D \cdot L \cdot t = \rho \pi D L t
$$

**Step 3 — Stress Constraints.**
Allowable stresses:

$$
\sigma_{allow,long} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot \eta_{long}}{n} = \frac{250 \times 0.8}{2.5} = 80 \ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow,circ} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot \eta_{circ}}{n} = \frac{250 \times 1.0}{2.5} = 100 \ \text{MPa}
$$

Stresses (thin-walled theory):

$$
\sigma_{long} = \frac{pD}{4t}, \qquad \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{pD}{2t}
$$

Constraints:

$$
g_1(t) = \frac{pD}{4t} - \sigma_{allow,long} \le 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t \ge \frac{pD}{4 \sigma_{allow,long}}
$$

$$
g_2(t) = \frac{pD}{2t} - \sigma_{allow,circ} \le 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t \ge \frac{pD}{2 \sigma_{allow,circ}}
$$

**Step 4 — Canonical Formulation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & t \\
\text{minimizing} \quad & m(t) = \rho \pi D L t \\
\text{subject to} \quad & t \ge \dfrac{pD}{4 \sigma_{allow,long}} \\
& t \ge \dfrac{pD}{2 \sigma_{allow,circ}} \\
& t > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Critical Thickness Computation.**

$$
t_{long} = \frac{pD}{4 \sigma_{allow,long}} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{4 \times 80} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} = 3.75 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
t_{hoop} = \frac{pD}{2 \sigma_{allow,circ}} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{2 \times 100} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} = 6.0 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Optimum Thickness.**
Since mass $m(t) \propto t$ (monotonically increasing), the minimum mass occurs at:

$$
t^* = \max(t_{long}, t_{hoop}) = \max(3.75, 6.0) = 6.0 \ \text{mm} \quad \text{(hoop stress governs)}
$$

**Step 7 — Optimum Mass.**

$$
m^* = 7850 \times \pi \times 0.6 \times 2 \times 0.006 \approx 177.5 \ \text{kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting joint efficiency:** Many students use $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n$ directly. In pressure vessel design, **joint efficiencies** ($\eta_{long}$ and $\eta_{circ}$) **must be included**, or marks are docked under "realistic engineering modeling."
> 2. **Confusing hoop vs. longitudinal stress dominance:** The hoop stress is always **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylinder. Hence the **hoop constraint typically governs** unless joints are unusually weak longitudinally.
> 3. **Missing the active-constraint principle:** Marks are awarded for explicitly stating that the optimum occurs at the **constraint boundary**, not at some interior point.
> 4. **Not verifying thin-wall assumption:** Students should check that $t/D < 1/10$. Here $6.0/600 = 0.01$, which satisfies the assumption comfortably.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Morphology of Design**

- **Morphology of Design** = study of the **structure and form** of the engineering design activity.
- **Origin of term:** Fritz Zwicky's **Morphological Analysis** (1967), generalized into a process framework by **VDI 2221** and **Pahl & Beitz**.
- **Four design phases (VDI 2221):** Clarification → Conceptual → Embodiment → Detail.
- **Three design types (VDI 2225):** Original (new), Adaptive, Variant.
- **Hierarchical levels:** System (L0) → Subsystem (L1) → Component (L2) → Feature (L3).
- **Morphological chart:** An $n \times m$ matrix mapping **sub-functions** to **technical means**, used for creative concept generation.
- **Total possible concepts:** Product of all entries per row (e.g., 3 functions × 3 means each = $3^3 = 27$ concepts).
- **Optimization canonical form:** Minimize $f(\mathbf{X})$ subject to $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \le 0$, $h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0$, and side constraints.
- **Active constraint principle:** For a monotonically increasing objective (e.g., mass vs. thickness), the optimum lies on the **boundary of the most restrictive active constraint**.
- **Necessary condition for unconstrained optimum:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{X}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Sufficient condition:** $\nabla^{2} f(\mathbf{X}^*) \succ 0$ (Hessian positive definite).
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Decisions at higher morphological levels have **larger impact and lower frequency**; decisions at lower levels have **smaller impact and higher frequency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Workable Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Workable Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal language of **Design Optimization**, a **Workable Design** (also called a **Feasible Design**) is defined as:

> A vector of design variables $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ that **simultaneously satisfies all functional requirements, performance specifications, and constraint boundaries** of an engineering design problem, irrespective of whether it yields the best (optimal) value of the objective function.

Mathematically, a design $\mathbf{x}$ is called *workable* if it lies entirely within the **Feasible Region** $\mathcal{F}$ of the design space:

$$\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F} \iff \begin{cases} g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, & j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\ h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, & k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\ x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U, & i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \end{cases}$$

where:
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ represents **inequality constraints** (stress limits, deflection bounds, geometric limits)
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ represents **equality constraints** (equilibrium equations, geometric compatibility)
- $x_i^L, x_i^U$ are the **lower and upper side bounds** on the design variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The word *workable* does **not** mean *best*. It simply means the design **can be manufactured, will perform its intended function, and respects every engineering restriction imposed on it.** Optimization is the subsequent act of selecting the **best among all workable designs.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu Intuition

Imagine you walk into a restaurant for dinner with three rules:
1. You must spend less than ₹300 (**budget constraint**).
2. The dish must be vegetarian (**food-type constraint**).
3. You must consume at least 15 g of protein (**nutrition constraint**).

A **workable dish** is *any single dish on the menu that satisfies all three rules*. There may be many — Paneer Butter Masala, Chole Bhature, Veg Biryani — all of them are workable choices.

A dish costing ₹290 with 35 g protein is *workable* but possibly not the *best*. The **optimal dish** is the one that maximizes taste-per-rupee (the **objective function**) among all workable dishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away for KTU:** Every optimal design is a workable design, but every workable design is **NOT** optimal. The optimization process is essentially a *filtering and ranking operation* over the universe of workable designs.

## 1.3 Why This Concept Matters in KTU Examinations

In **Module 1 of PEPET743**, this topic establishes the *language of design optimization*. Almost every subsequent module — classical optimization methods, Lagrange multipliers, KKT conditions, and modern heuristic algorithms — operates on the **set of workable designs**. Without a precise understanding of what constitutes a workable design, a student cannot:
- Formulate a feasible region,
- Apply the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) optimality conditions**, or
- Identify why a particular design is **infeasible**.

## 1.4 Classification of Design Variables in a Workable Design

A workable design specification contains **three distinct categories** of variables, which the student must recognize:

| Category | Symbol | Role in Workable Design | Example (Pressure Vessel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Design Variables** | $\mathbf{x}$ | Free parameters chosen by the designer | Inner diameter, wall thickness |
| **State Variables** | $\mathbf{s}$ | Dependent variables determined by $\mathbf{x}$ | Resulting stress, deformation |
| **Preassigned Parameters** | $\mathbf{p}$ | Fixed constants from problem statement | Material density, applied load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only **Design Variables** are manipulated to move from one workable design to another. State variables are *consequences*, and preassigned parameters are *fixed inputs*.

## 1.5 The Three-Tier Hierarchy of Design Quality

KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy. Memorize it:

1. **Infeasible Design** — Violates at least one constraint. Cannot be built/used.
2. **Workable (Feasible) Design** — Satisfies every constraint. Can be built and will function.
3. **Optimal Design** — The single (or set of) workable design(s) that extremizes the objective function.

> [!NOTE]
> An optimal design that exists in theory but violates a single constraint is **infeasible**, hence **NOT workable**, and is therefore **not an engineering solution at all.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Optimization Problem — Canonical Statement

Every workable design problem in KTU is a special case of the **general non-linear programming (NLP)** formulation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T \\
\text{to minimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p \\
& x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}$$

where $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (e.g., minimize weight, minimize cost, maximize efficiency).

A vector $\mathbf{x}$ is a **workable design** if and only if it produces *zero constraint violation* across this entire set.

## 2.2 Components of a Workable Design — Step-by-Step Logic

To systematically check whether a candidate design $\mathbf{x}$ is workable, the KTU board expects the following six-step logical procedure:

**Step 1 — Identify the design variables.**
Determine which parameters the designer can freely choose. These become components of the vector $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2 — Identify preassigned parameters.**
List all quantities that are fixed by the problem statement (loads, material properties, code-mandated factors of safety). These do **not** appear in $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 3 — Express the objective function.**
Write $f(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly in terms of the design variables. This is the *criterion* by which optimization will eventually rank workable designs.

**Step 4 — Formulate all inequality constraints.**
These represent *upper or lower bounds* on performance quantities: maximum permissible stress $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$, maximum deflection $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$, minimum natural frequency $f_n \geq f_{req}$, minimum buckling load, etc.

**Step 5 — Formulate all equality constraints.**
These are typically derived from *physics*: equilibrium, compatibility, geometric relations (e.g., area $A = \pi r^2$).

**Step 6 — Verify the candidate.**
Plug the candidate $\mathbf{x}$ into every $g_j$ and $h_k$. If all are satisfied, the design is **workable**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important memory aid for KTU Module 1.** Memorize the structure, not just the symbols — examiners rephrase the same six relations in every variant.

| # | Mathematical Expression | Name | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Design vector | All design variables combined | — |
| 2 | $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Objective function | Quantity to minimize (weight) or maximize (efficiency) | kg, ₹, s |
| 3 | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Inequality constraint $j$ | Performance or geometric bound | depends on $j$ |
| 4 | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Equality constraint $k$ | Governing physical law | depends on $k$ |
| 5 | $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ | Side bound on $x_i$ | Manufacturability range | m, mm |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min f(\mathbf{x}) \text{ s.t. } \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ | Optimal design | Best workable design | — |
| 7 | $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \, \vert \, g_j \leq 0, \, h_k = 0, \, x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U\}$ | Feasible Region | Universe of all workable designs | — |
| 8 | $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \mathcal{F}$ | Infeasible Region | Universe of unusable designs | — |
| 9 | $v_j(\mathbf{x}) = \max(0, g_j(\mathbf{x}))$ | Constraint violation of $j$ | How badly $g_j$ is broken | same as $g_j$ |
| 10 | $V(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} v_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Total constraint violation | Aggregate infeasibility measure | sum of above |

> **Avoid pipe notation in tables.** Note that the vertical-bar symbol is replaced by `\vert` to keep the markdown table rendering safe.

## 2.4 Geometric Interpretation in $n$-Dimensional Design Space

For a simple 2-variable problem, the design space is the **$x_1$–$x_2$ plane**. The feasible region $\mathcal{F}$ is the *intersection* of all regions defined by the constraints. Every point inside $\mathcal{F}$ is a workable design.

- A point **inside** $\mathcal{F}$ → **Workable design** ✔
- A point **on the boundary** of $\mathcal{F}$ → **Workable design** (active constraint) ✔
- A point **outside** $\mathcal{F}$ → **Infeasible design** ✘
- A point inside $\mathcal{F}$ that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x})$ → **Optimal design** ★

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Field | What constitutes a Workable Design | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical / Pressure Vessel** | Diameter, thickness satisfying ASME stress + buckling + deflection codes | Avoids catastrophic rupture |
| **Civil / Truss Bridge** | Member cross-sections satisfying stress, slenderness, deflection limits | Guarantees structural safety |
| **Aerospace / Wing** | Airfoil geometry satisfying lift, drag, structural, fuel-volume constraints | Ensures flight-worthiness |
| **Electrical / Transformer** | Core dimensions, wire gauge satisfying flux-density, heating, voltage-drop limits | Prevents insulation failure |
| **Manufacturing / Process** | Cutting speed, feed, depth-of-cut satisfying tool-life, surface-roughness limits | Achieves producible parts |

## 2.6 Common KTU-Level Pitfalls in Identifying Workable Designs

1. **Forgetting side bounds.** A design that satisfies every $g_j$ and $h_k$ but has $x_i < x_i^L$ is **NOT workable**.
2. **Treating state variables as design variables.** Stress is a *consequence*, not a variable to be freely chosen.
3. **Ignoring the direction of inequality.** $g_j \leq 0$ is the **standard form**; examiners will rearrange as $g_j \geq 0$ to test your ability to bring it back to standard form before applying algorithms.
4. **Confusing "workable" with "optimal".** This is the single most common mark-losing error in Module 1 answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Classifying Any Given Design

The KTU board often asks: *"Given a candidate design vector $\mathbf{x}_0$, determine whether it is workable, infeasible, or optimal."* The following exhaustive procedure covers every case.

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical framework below is the **canonical algorithm** you must reproduce in the exam for full marks.

### Step-by-Step Decision Procedure

**Step 1.** Compute the objective value $f(\mathbf{x}_0)$. Write it numerically.

**Step 2.** For each inequality constraint $j = 1, \ldots, m$, evaluate $g_j(\mathbf{x}_0)$. Record the numerical value.

**Step 3.** For each equality constraint $k = 1, \ldots, p$, evaluate $h_k(\mathbf{x}_0)$. Record the numerical value.

**Step 4.** For each design variable $i = 1, \ldots, n$, check that $x_i^L \leq x_{0,i} \leq x_i^U$.

**Step 5.** Compute the aggregate constraint violation:
$$V(\mathbf{x}_0) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \max(0, \, g_j(\mathbf{x}_0)) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \vert h_k(\mathbf{x}_0) \vert$$

**Step 6.** Apply the classification rule:

- If $V(\mathbf{x}_0) > 0$ → **Infeasible design**.
- If $V(\mathbf{x}_0) = 0$ → **Workable design**. Now check optimality:
    - If $f(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is the lowest value observed among *all* workable designs surveyed → **Optimal design**.
    - Otherwise → **Sub-optimal but workable design**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Three-Bar Truss Classification

Consider a **symmetric three-bar truss** carrying a vertical load $P = 20$ kN. The design variables are the cross-sectional areas:
$$\mathbf{x} = [A_1, \, A_2]^T$$
with the geometric constraint $A_1 = A_2$ (symmetry). Material: $E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

### Formulation

The objective (minimize volume, hence weight) is:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \rho L (2A_1 + \sqrt{2}A_2)$$

Subject to the stress constraint in the inclined member:
$$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\sqrt{2}A_1 + A_2)}{\sqrt{2}A_1^2 + 2A_1 A_2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0$$

with $\sigma_{allow} = 100$ MPa.

### Step 1 — Test candidate $\mathbf{x}_A = [A_1, A_2] = [500, 500]$ mm²

Compute the stress in the inclined bar:
$$\sigma_{inc} = \frac{P(\sqrt{2}A_1 + A_2)}{\sqrt{2}A_1^2 + 2A_1 A_2}$$

Plug numbers:
$$\sigma_{inc} = \frac{20{,}000 \times (\sqrt{2} \cdot 500 + 500)}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 500^2 + 2 \cdot 500 \cdot 500}$$

$$= \frac{20{,}000 \times (707.11 + 500)}{353{,}553 + 500{,}000} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 1207.11}{853{,}553}$$

$$= \frac{24{,}142{,}200}{853{,}553} \approx 28.28 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $28.28 \leq 100$, constraint $g_1$ is satisfied. With side bounds typically $A_i \geq 10$ mm², this is a **workable design**.

### Step 2 — Test candidate $\mathbf{x}_B = [50, 50]$ mm²

Re-compute stress:
$$\sigma_{inc} = \frac{20{,}000 \times (70.71 + 50)}{3535.5 + 5000} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 120.71}{8535.5} \approx 282.8 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $282.8 > 100$, **constraint violated**. Aggregate violation $V(\mathbf{x}_B) > 0$. **Infeasible design.**

### Step 3 — Compare weights

$$f(\mathbf{x}_A) = \rho L (2 \cdot 500 + \sqrt{2} \cdot 500) = \rho L \cdot 1707.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$f(\mathbf{x}_B) = \rho L (2 \cdot 50 + \sqrt{2} \cdot 50) = \rho L \cdot 170.71 \text{ mm}^2$$

Design $\mathbf{x}_B$ is lighter — but it is **infeasible**, hence not a candidate. Design $\mathbf{x}_A$ is workable. The **optimum** will be the *smallest* workable $A_1 = A_2$ that still keeps $\sigma_{inc} \leq 100$ MPa.

### Step 4 — Find the optimum by setting active constraint

Set $g_1 = 0$ with $A_1 = A_2 = A$:
$$\frac{P(\sqrt{2}A + A)}{\sqrt{2}A^2 + 2A^2} = \sigma_{allow}$$

$$\frac{P(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(2+\sqrt{2})A} = \sigma_{allow}$$

Solve for $A$:
$$A^* = \frac{P(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(2+\sqrt{2})\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 2.4142}{3.4142 \times 100}$$

$$A^* = \frac{48{,}284}{341.42} \approx 141.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

So the **optimal workable design** is $\mathbf{x}^* = [141.4, \, 141.4]^T$ mm² — strictly *smaller* (lighter) than the candidate $\mathbf{x}_A = [500, 500]^T$, yet still **workable**.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation — Why Some Designs Are Workable and Others Aren't

### Case A: A design with **no active constraints**

If $g_j(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for every $j$, and $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ for every $k$, the design lies in the **strict interior** of $\mathcal{F}$. Such a design is **workable** but is generally *not* optimal — there exists room to improve $f(\mathbf{x})$ without yet hitting a wall.

### Case B: A design with **one active inequality constraint**

If exactly one $g_p(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ while all others are strictly negative, the design lies **on the boundary** of $\mathcal{F}$. The optimization algorithm can move *along* this boundary (tangentially) to reduce $f$ further, until a second constraint becomes active. At that point, **Case C** takes over.

### Case C: A design with **multiple active constraints**

If several constraints are simultaneously zero, the design sits at a **corner of the feasible region**. The **KKT conditions** (covered in a later KTU module) state that if $\mathbf{x}^*$ is optimal, there must exist non-negative multipliers $\mu_j$ such that:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

with $\mu_j \geq 0$ and $\mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (**complementary slackness**). Every term of this equation must be written explicitly in the KTU answer — examiners award partial credit for each gradient and each multiplier.

## 3.4 Summary Computation Table

| Design | $A_1$ (mm²) | $A_2$ (mm²) | $\sigma_{inc}$ (MPa) | Workable? | Weight rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_A$ | 500 | 500 | 28.28 | ✔ Yes | Heavy |
| $\mathbf{x}_B$ | 50 | 50 | 282.8 | ✘ No | Lightest (but infeasible) |
| $\mathbf{x}^*$ | 141.4 | 141.4 | 100.0 | ✔ Yes (optimal) | Lightest feasible |

This single table, when reproduced correctly, typically fetches **full marks** in KTU Module 1 questions on workable design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Design Classification

The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps every conceptual interaction between the *problem statement*, the *candidate design vector*, the *constraint evaluator*, and the *classification output*. It is rendered as a Mermaid block to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PROBLEM STATEMENT"] --> nodeB["DESIGN VARIABLES X"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["PREASSIGNED PARAMETERS P"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["CANDIDATE DESIGN X0"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["CONSTRAINT EVALUATOR"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["EVAL GJ OF X0"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["EVAL HK OF X0"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["EVAL SIDE BOUNDS"]
    F["EVAL GJ OF X0"] --> nodeI["VIOLATION FUNCTION V OF X0"]
    G["EVAL HK OF X0"] --> nodeI
    H["EVAL SIDE BOUNDS"] --> nodeI
    I["VIOLATION FUNCTION V OF X0"] --> nodeJ{"V GREATER THAN ZERO"}
    J -->|YES| nodeK["INFEASIBLE DESIGN"]
    J -->|NO| nodeL["WORKABLE DESIGN"]
    L --> nodeM{"F IS MINIMUM OVER ALL WORKABLE"}
    M -->|YES| nodeN["OPTIMAL DESIGN"]
    M -->|NO| nodeO["SUBOPTIMAL WORKABLE DESIGN"]
```

## 4.2 Feasible Region Topology (2D Cross-Section)

For a two-variable design problem $(x_1, x_2)$, the following block diagram shows how each constraint *carves* the design space. The shaded intersection is the universe of **workable designs**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["DESIGN SPACE R2"] --> nodeQ["AFTER G1 LESS THAN ZERO"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["AFTER G2 LESS THAN ZERO"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["AFTER G3 LESS THAN ZERO"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["FEASIBLE REGION F"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["INFINITELY MANY WORKABLE DESIGNS"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["ONE OPTIMAL DESIGN X STAR"]
```

## 4.3 The Optimization Process as a Hierarchy Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAll["ALL CANDIDATE DESIGN VECTORS"] --> nodeFeas{"IS DESIGN WORKABLE"}
    nodeFeas -->|NO| nodeReject["REJECTED INFEASIBLE"]
    nodeFeas -->|YES| nodeWorkable["SET OF WORKABLE DESIGNS"]
    nodeWorkable --> nodeRank["RANK BY OBJECTIVE FUNCTION F"]
    nodeRank --> nodeBest["MINIMUM F ACHIEVED AT X STAR"]
    nodeBest --> nodeOptimal["OPTIMAL DESIGN X STAR"]
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Map — Constituents of a Workable Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DESIGN VARIABLES X"] --> B["OBJECTIVE FUNCTION F OF X"]
    A --> C["INEQUALITY CONSTRAINTS GJ"]
    A --> D["EQUALITY CONSTRAINTS HK"]
    A --> E["SIDE BOUNDS X LOW AND X UP"]
    B --> F["WORKABLE DESIGN CRITERIA"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["OUTPUT FEASIBLE REGION F"]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flowchart for KTU Exam Answers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["RECEIVE CANDIDATE X0"] --> nodeCheckBounds["VERIFY X LOW LESS THAN X0 LESS THAN X UP"]
    nodeCheckBounds --> nodeBoundsOK{"BOUNDS OK"}
    nodeBoundsOK -->|NO| nodeOut1["INFEASIBLE STOP"]
    nodeBoundsOK -->|YES| nodeCheckIneq["EVAL ALL GJ LESS THAN ZERO"]
    nodeCheckIneq --> nodeIneqOK{"ALL SATISFIED"}
    nodeIneqOK -->|NO| nodeOut2["INFEASIBLE STOP"]
    nodeIneqOK -->|YES| nodeCheckEq["EVAL ALL HK EQUAL ZERO"]
    nodeCheckEq --> nodeEqOK{"ALL SATISFIED"}
    nodeEqOK -->|NO| nodeOut3["INFEASIBLE STOP"]
    nodeEqOK -->|YES| nodeWorkable["WORKABLE DESIGN CONFIRMED"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define a *workable design*. How does it differ from an *optimal design*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **workable design** is a vector of design variables $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfies **every** constraint of the problem: all inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, all equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, and all side bounds $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$. It is therefore **manufacturable and functional**, but it need not be the best possible.

An **optimal design** is the *single* workable design (or set) that extremizes the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ — yielding the minimum weight, minimum cost, or maximum efficiency.

**Key distinction (1 mark):** Every optimal design is workable, but a workable design is optimal only if no other workable design gives a better value of $f(\mathbf{x})$.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the four essential components that must be specified before any design can be classified as workable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Design variables** — the parameters the designer is free to choose, written as a vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
2. **Objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — the criterion by which alternative designs will be ranked.
3. **Constraints** — all inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ and equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
4. **Side bounds** — the lower and upper limits $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ on each design variable.

A candidate is workable only when **all four** components are simultaneously satisfied.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question B1 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat schematic in the design space, explain the difference between the **feasible region**, the **infeasible region**, and the location of the **optimal design**. Clearly distinguish a workable design from an optimal design.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **cylindrical pressure vessel** of internal diameter $D = 0.5$ m is to be designed to withstand an internal pressure $p = 2$ MPa. The allowable hoop stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 120$ MPa and the allowable longitudinal stress is $\sigma_{allow,L} = 80$ MPa. Using thin-wall theory, determine whether the candidate designs listed below are *workable*, *infeasible*, or *optimal*. Material density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, vessel length $L = 1.5$ m.

| Candidate | Wall thickness $t$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 6 mm |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 12 mm |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 3 mm |

Take the objective as **minimize vessel mass** and use the minimum thickness that satisfies both stress limits.

---

#### Model Solution to B1(a) — [7 marks]

**Schematic description (3 marks):** Draw a 2-D design space with $x_1$ on the horizontal axis and $x_2$ on the vertical axis. Shade the **feasible region** $\mathcal{F}$ as the intersection of two strip-like regions corresponding to $g_1 \leq 0$ and $g_2 \leq 0$. Show the **infeasible region** $\mathcal{S}$ as everything outside. Mark three points:
- $\mathbf{x}_A$ **inside** $\mathcal{F}$ → *workable but suboptimal*,
- $\mathbf{x}_B$ **on the boundary** of $\mathcal{F}$ → *workable, possibly optimal*,
- $\mathbf{x}_C$ **outside** $\mathcal{F}$ → *infeasible*.

**Explanatory content (4 marks):** The **feasible region** is the set of all $\mathbf{x}$ that simultaneously satisfy every inequality, equality, and side-bound constraint. The **infeasible region** is its complement. Every point in the feasible region is a **workable design**. The **optimal design** is the unique (or set of) points in $\mathcal{F}$ that yields the lowest value of $f(\mathbf{x})$. Geometrically, this corresponds to moving the level curves of $f(\mathbf{x})$ (e.g., circles of constant weight) inward until they just *touch* $\mathcal{F}$ at a single point — the optimum.

---

#### Model Solution to B1(b) — [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Write the thin-wall stress formulas (1 mark).**

Hoop stress:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}$$

Longitudinal stress:
$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the two inequality constraints (1 mark).**

Bring to standard form $g_j \leq 0$:

$$g_1(t) = \frac{pD}{2t} - \sigma_{allow,h} \leq 0$$

$$g_2(t) = \frac{pD}{4t} - \sigma_{allow,L} \leq 0$$

Plug $p = 2$ MPa, $D = 0.5$ m:
