$$\frac{pD}{2} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{2} = 0.5 \text{ MPa·m} = 500 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$\frac{pD}{4} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{4} = 0.25 \text{ MPa·m} = 250 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute minimum required thickness from each constraint (2 marks).**

From $g_1 \leq 0$:
$$t \geq \frac{pD}{2\sigma_{allow,h}} = \frac{500}{120} = 4.17 \text{ mm}$$

From $g_2 \leq 0$:
$$t \geq \frac{pD}{4\sigma_{allow,L}} = \frac{250}{80} = 3.125 \text{ mm}$$

The governing constraint is $g_1$, giving $t_{min} = 4.17$ mm. Use $t_{min} = 5$ mm (round-up for manufacturability).

**Step 4 — Classify each candidate (2 marks).**

| Candidate | $t$ (mm) | $\sigma_h$ (MPa) | $\sigma_L$ (MPa) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 6 | $500/6 = 83.3$ ✔ | $250/6 = 41.7$ ✔ | **Workable** |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 12 | $500/12 = 41.7$ ✔ | $250/12 = 20.8$ ✔ | **Workable but not optimal** |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 3 | $500/3 = 166.7$ ✘ | $250/3 = 83.3$ ✘ | **Infeasible** |

**Step 5 — Identify the optimal design (1 mark).**

The minimum workable thickness is $t^* = 4.17$ mm. Among the three candidates, $\mathbf{x}_1 = 6$ mm is the lightest *feasible* option, but the *true* optimum is $t^* = 4.17$ mm. The candidate $\mathbf{x}_1$ is therefore **workable and near-optimal**; the global optimum $t^*$ is **workable and optimal**; $\mathbf{x}_3$ is **infeasible**.

---

### Question B2 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **classification hierarchy** of design solutions in optimization. Use a labelled diagram to show how the universe of all design vectors is partitioned into *infeasible*, *workable*, and *optimal* sets. Discuss the role of the **aggregate constraint violation function** $V(\mathbf{x})$ in this classification.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **two-bar truss** carries a vertical load $P = 10$ kN. The two bars make angles $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 60°$ with the horizontal. The design variables are the cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, both bounded by $50 \leq A_i \leq 1500$ mm². The yield stress is $\sigma_Y = 250$ MPa. Classify each of the following candidate designs as *workable*, *infeasible*, or *optimal* with respect to the constraint that **stress in each bar must not exceed the yield stress**.

| Candidate | $A_1$ (mm²) | $A_2$ (mm²) |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 200 | 200 |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 80 | 80 |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 500 | 500 |

The minimum-area workable design is to be identified as the optimal one.

---

#### Model Solution to B2(a) — [7 marks]

**Hierarchy description (3 marks):** The universe of all design vectors $\mathbb{R}^n$ is partitioned into two disjoint sets — the **infeasible region** $\mathcal{S}$ and the **feasible region** $\mathcal{F}$. Within $\mathcal{F}$, designs are further classified as:
- **Workable (any point in $\mathcal{F}$)**
- **Optimal (the subset of $\mathcal{F}$ that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x})$)**

The diagram (already provided in Section 4.3 of these notes) shows the partitioning with two nested sets: a larger outer ellipse representing all workable designs and a single highlighted point inside representing the optimal design.

**Aggregate violation function (4 marks):** Define the per-constraint violation:
$$v_j(\mathbf{x}) = \max(0, \, g_j(\mathbf{x}))$$
$$w_k(\mathbf{x}) = \vert h_k(\mathbf{x}) \vert$$

The aggregate violation function is:
$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} v_j(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} w_k(\mathbf{x})$$

Properties:
- $V(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ always (sum of non-negative terms).
- $V(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ if and only if $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ (every constraint satisfied).
- $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ if and only if $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ (at least one violation).
- $V(\mathbf{x})$ is a **continuous, piecewise-differentiable scalar** used by penalty-function methods to *convert* the constrained problem into an unconstrained one.

The classification rule:

$$\text{Design} \;\; \mathbf{x} \;\; \text{is} \;\; \begin{cases} \text{Infeasible} & \text{if } V(\mathbf{x}) > 0 \\ \text{Workable} & \text{if } V(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \\ \text{Optimal} & \text{if } V(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \text{ AND } f(\mathbf{x}) \text{ is the global minimum over } \mathcal{F} \end{cases}$$

---

#### Model Solution to B2(b) — [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Equilibrium to find bar forces (1 mark).**

Vertical equilibrium:
$$P = F_1 \sin\theta_1 + F_2 \sin\theta_2$$

Horizontal equilibrium:
$$F_1 \cos\theta_1 = F_2 \cos\theta_2$$

Solve: $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\theta_1}{\cos\theta_2} = F_1 \cdot \frac{\cos 30°}{\cos 60°} = F_1 \cdot \frac{0.866}{0.5} = 1.732 F_1$

Substitute into vertical equilibrium:
$$P = F_1 \sin 30° + 1.732 F_1 \sin 60° = F_1 (0.5 + 1.732 \times 0.866) = F_1 (0.5 + 1.5) = 2.0 F_1$$

Therefore:
$$F_1 = \frac{P}{2} = 5 \text{ kN}, \qquad F_2 = 1.732 \times 5 = 8.66 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Stress formulas (1 mark).**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}, \qquad \sigma_2 = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum required area from yield constraint (1 mark).**

For bar 1: $A_1 \geq F_1 / \sigma_Y = 5000 / 250 = 20$ mm².
For bar 2: $A_2 \geq F_2 / \sigma_Y = 8660 / 250 = 34.64$ mm².

Both lower bounds ($20, 34.64$ mm²) are below the side-bound $A_i \geq 50$ mm². So the **governing lower bound** is the side bound: $A_i \geq 50$ mm².

**Step 4 — Classify each candidate (3 marks).**

| Candidate | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $\sigma_1$ (MPa) | $\sigma_2$ (MPa) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 200 | 200 | $5000/200 = 25$ ✔ | $8660/200 = 43.3$ ✔ | **Workable** |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 80 | 80 | $5000/80 = 62.5$ ✔ | $8660/80 = 108.25$ ✔ | **Workable** |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 500 | 500 | $5000/500 = 10$ ✔ | $8660/500 = 17.32$ ✔ | **Workable but heavy** |

All three candidates are workable — but $\mathbf{x}_3$ is wasteful, $\mathbf{x}_1$ is heavy relative to the feasible minimum, and $\mathbf{x}_2$ is closest to the minimum.

**Step 5 — Identify the optimal design (1 mark).**

The minimum-area workable design is $(A_1, A_2) = (50, 50)$ mm² — the side-bound corner of $\mathcal{F}$. Among the three candidates, **$\mathbf{x}_2 = (80, 80)$ mm² is the closest to optimal**; the true optimum $(50, 50)$ mm² is the global optimum but is not among the candidates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention on $g_j$:** Many students forget to bring constraints into the standard form $g_j \leq 0$. A constraint like $\sigma \leq \sigma_Y$ is correctly rewritten as $g = \sigma - \sigma_Y \leq 0$, not $g = \sigma_Y - \sigma \geq 0$. The latter will fail every standard algorithm.
> 2. **Distinguishing workable from optimal:** Examiners award **2 marks specifically for stating which candidate is workable and a separate 2 marks for identifying the optimal**. Conflating the two loses both marks.
> 3. **Side bounds are constraints:** A design that satisfies every $g_j$ and $h_k$ but has $A_i = 30$ mm² when $A_i \geq 50$ mm² is **infeasible**, not workable.
> 4. **Objectivity of the optimum:** The optimal design depends on the *chosen* objective function. Minimizing area (volume) and minimizing stress yield different optima — students must specify the objective before declaring a design optimal.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Concept of Workable Design**

- ☐ A **workable design** satisfies **every** constraint (inequality, equality, and side-bound).
- ☐ Mathematically, workable design means $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is the feasible region.
- ☐ **Optimal design** = the workable design that yields the best value of the objective function.
- ☐ Hierarchy: **Infeasible ⊂ Universe**, **Workable ⊂ Universe**, **Optimal ⊂ Workable**.
- ☐ The **aggregate constraint violation** $V(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ is the necessary and sufficient condition for workability.
- ☐ **Design variables** are free parameters chosen by the designer; **state variables** are consequences; **preassigned parameters** are fixed inputs.
- ☐ Always bring constraints into **standard form** $g_j \leq 0$ and $h_k = 0$ before applying any optimization algorithm.
- ☐ Side bounds $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ are constraints and must be checked for workability.
- ☐ The optimum is **objective-dependent** — change the objective, change the optimum (but the feasible region stays the same).
- ☐ Geometric picture: **Feasible region** is the shaded zone; optimal design is the point where the level curve of $f(\mathbf{x})$ just *touches* the boundary.
- ☐ Common KTU trap: confusing *workable* with *optimal* — examiners test this distinction in nearly every paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Example on Workable System and Optimal Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Practical Example on Workable System and Optimal Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal vocabulary of KTU-aligned Design Optimization (PEPET743), every engineering artefact must pass through **two distinct gates of validation** before it can be called a *complete* design solution.

### A. Workable System (Feasible Design)

A **Workable System** is a candidate design configuration that satisfies **all** prescribed functional requirements, performance specifications, manufacturing constraints, safety codes, and regulatory limits — yet it is **not necessarily the best** solution among the available alternatives. In the language of mathematical programming, it is any point lying inside the **feasible region** $S$ of the design space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> A design is called a *Workable (Feasible) System* if it meets every equality constraint $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and every inequality constraint $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ prescribed by the problem, regardless of the value of the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$.

Formally, a design vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is **workable** if:

$$
\mathbf{x} \in S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \;:\; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0,\; g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0,\; j=1,\ldots,m,\; k=1,\ldots,p\}
$$

### B. Optimal Design

An **Optimal Design** is that *unique* (or sometimes a set of) workable design(s) for which the chosen **objective function** attains its most desirable value — minimum for cost/weight, or maximum for efficiency/performance — among all feasible candidates.

$$
\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} f(\mathbf{x})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Every optimal design is necessarily a workable design, but the converse is false.** This single sentence is the most examination-relevant logical truth in Module 1, and KTU examiners frequently test it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spice Box and the Chef"

Imagine a chef in Kerala preparing *fish curry*. The **recipe book** prescribes:
- Salt must lie in $\lvert g \rvert \leq 15\,\text{g}$,
- Coconut oil $\leq 30\,\text{ml}$,
- Cooking time $\geq 12\,\text{min}$,
- The gravy must reduce to exactly $1/3$ of its initial volume (an *equality* constraint).

A cook following *any* combination of salt, oil, and time that meets the recipe produces a **workable fish curry** — edible, safe, and on-spec. But the *master chef* who picks the precise proportions to produce the curry with **minimum oil usage** while still passing the taste panel is producing the **optimal fish curry**.

| Engineering | Cooking Analogy |
| :--- | :--- |
| Design variables $\mathbf{x}$ | Salt, oil, spice quantities |
| Constraints $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Recipe limits (taste, safety) |
| Objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Total oil used (we wish to minimize) |
| Workable System | Any edible, on-spec curry |
| Optimal Design | The least-oily edible curry |

## 1.3 Why Both Concepts Matter

- A **workable system** guarantees the design *functions* and is *safe*.
- An **optimal design** guarantees the design is also the *most economical / efficient / lightest*.
- In real industrial practice (ISRO, HAL, Toyota, Boeing), the gap between "workable" and "optimal" represents **millions of rupees** in materials, fuel, and warranty costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 (Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish *feasibility* (a constraint-satisfaction problem) from *optimality* (a search problem inside the feasible region). The former is binary — *pass/fail*; the latter is comparative — *better/worse*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Design Decision Pipeline

A typical KTU design problem is structured into four mathematically rigorous steps:

1. **Identify Design Variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ — the independent parameters the designer is free to choose.
2. **Formulate the Objective Function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — a scalar metric (weight, cost, deflection, stress-concentration factor) to be minimized or maximized.
3. **Enumerate the Constraints** $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0,\; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ — physical limits, code limits, and geometric limits.
4. **Solve** the resulting constrained optimization problem using analytical or numerical means.

> [!NOTE]
> In Module 1, KTU restricts itself to **analytical solutions** of single-variable or two-variable problems using classical calculus (Lagrange multipliers, KKT conditions, or direct substitution). Numerical methods (Rosenbrock, Simplex, SLP, SQP) appear in later modules.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Feasible / Workable Condition | $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Design is *safe* and *functional* |
| 2 | Optimality Statement | $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} f(\mathbf{x})$ | Best feasible design |
| 3 | Equality Constraint (axial bar) | $\sigma = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | Stress limit |
| 4 | Volume of Circular Shaft | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$ | Used as objective |
| 5 | Spring Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{8FD}{\pi d^3} K_w \leq \tau_{allow}$ | Spring design constraint |
| 6 | Deflection of Cantilever | $\delta = \dfrac{WL^3}{3EI} \leq \delta_{allow}$ | Stiffness constraint |
| 7 | KKT Stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_k \nabla g_k + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ | First-order necessary condition |
| 8 | Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_k \, g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Active vs inactive constraints |
| 9 | Active Constraint Test | $g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Constraint is binding at optimum |
| 10 | Non-Negativity | $x_i \geq 0$ | Geometric feasibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert the standard constraint $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$ into the canonical form $g(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0$ before applying any KKT test. A negative $g$ is feasible; a positive $g$ is *infeasible*.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Workable–Optimal Distinction

- **Aerospace (GE, Pratt & Whitney):** A turbine blade must be workable (survive centrifugal + thermal load) *and* optimal (minimum mass, since every kilogram costs ~$1 million to launch into LEO).
- **Automotive (Tata, Toyota):** Suspension springs must be feasible (no permanent deformation under load) and optimal (minimum wire diameter → minimum unsprung mass → better fuel economy).
- **Civil (KTU Alumni's Kerala PWD):** A bridge truss must be workable (satisfy IRC loading codes) and optimal (minimum steel tonnage → minimum cost).

The practical example in the next section demonstrates **exactly this logic** on a textbook KTU problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The flagship practical example of KTU Module 1 (PEPET743) is the "Stepped Bar Under Axial Tensile Load."** It is short enough to be solved in an exam, yet rich enough to demonstrate every concept of workable and optimal design. We solve it in exhaustive detail, then present a second illustrative problem.

---

## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — Stepped Circular Bar Under Axial Load

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **two-step circular bar** is rigidly fixed at the left end and loaded by an axial tensile force $P$ at the free right end. The bar consists of two cylindrical segments:

- **Segment 1** (left): length $L_1$, diameter $d_1$
- **Segment 2** (right): length $L_2$, diameter $d_2$

Both segments are made of the **same material** with allowable tensile stress $\sigma_{allow}$ and density $\rho$.

**Tasks:**
- (a) Identify the **workable designs** (feasible region).
- (b) Find the **optimal design** that minimizes the total weight (volume) of the bar.

### 3.1.2 Step A — Identify Design Variables

The variables the designer is free to choose are the two diameters:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad d_1, d_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{+}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step B — Formulate the Objective Function

The total volume (and hence the weight, since material is uniform) of the bar is:

$$
V(d_1, d_2) = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 L_1 + \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 L_2
$$

The optimization problem is to **minimize this volume**.

### 3.1.4 Step C — Enumerate the Constraints

The internal axial force in each segment equals $P$ (since the bar carries only one external load at the free end and no other transverse loads).

The axial stress in each segment is:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{P}{\pi d_1^2/4} = \frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2}
$$

The **stress constraints** (failure must not occur in either segment) are:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2} \leq \sigma_{allow} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d_1^2 \geq \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2} \leq \sigma_{allow} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d_2^2 \geq \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}
$$

Combining the two, we obtain a **single minimum diameter** that satisfies both:

$$
d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step D — Define the Feasible (Workable) Region

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Any design with $d_1 \geq d_{\min}$ AND $d_2 \geq d_{\min}$ is a workable system.** Geometrically, the feasible region is the **positive quadrant** of the $(d_1, d_2)$ plane **above and to the right** of the corner point $(d_{\min}, d_{\min})$.

Mathematically:

$$
S = \{(d_1, d_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;:\; d_1 \geq d_{\min},\; d_2 \geq d_{\min}\}
$$

A designer who *picks* $d_1 = 1.5\,d_{\min}$ and $d_2 = 2.0\,d_{\min}$ produces a **workable** bar — it will not fail — but it is **not optimal**, because material is being wasted.

### 3.1.6 Step E — Solve the Optimization

Since the objective $V$ is **monotonically increasing** in both $d_1$ and $d_2$ (larger diameters mean more material), the minimum of $V$ on the feasible region $S$ must lie on the **lower-left corner** of $S$, which is the point where both constraints are *active* (i.e., binding):

$$
d_1^* = d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}, \qquad d_2^* = d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **optimal design is a uniform bar** of diameter $d_{\min}$ throughout — i.e., the stepping is **unnecessary** under a pure axial load.

### 3.1.7 Step F — Compute the Optimal Volume

$$
V^* = \frac{\pi}{4} (d_{\min})^2 (L_1 + L_2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}} \cdot (L_1 + L_2)
$$

$$
V^* = \frac{P(L_1 + L_2)}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** $V^* = P L_{total} / \sigma_{allow}$ — the minimum volume is directly proportional to the load and the total length, and inversely proportional to the allowable stress. A stronger material (higher $\sigma_{allow}$) yields a lighter design — a fundamental engineering trade-off.

### 3.1.8 Verification using Lagrange Multipliers (KTU Required Method)

We now solve it via the **KKT conditions**, as KTU examiners expect this method for two-variable problems.

**Canonical form of constraints** (with $g \leq 0$ convention):

$$
g_1(d_1) = \frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

$$
g_2(d_2) = \frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

**Lagrangian function:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_1^2 L_1 + d_2^2 L_2) + \lambda_1\!\left(\frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2} - \sigma_{allow}\right) + \lambda_2\!\left(\frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2} - \sigma_{allow}\right)
$$

**KKT Stationarity (set partial derivatives to zero):**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_1} = \frac{\pi}{2} d_1 L_1 - \lambda_1 \frac{8P}{\pi d_1^3} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_2} = \frac{\pi}{2} d_2 L_2 - \lambda_2 \frac{8P}{\pi d_2^3} = 0
$$

Solving each for the multiplier:

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{\pi^2 d_1^4 L_1}{16 P}, \qquad \lambda_2 = \frac{\pi^2 d_2^4 L_2}{16 P}
$$

**Complementary Slackness:** Since both constraints are *active* at the optimum,

$$
\lambda_1 \neq 0, \quad \lambda_2 \neq 0, \quad g_1 = 0, \quad g_2 = 0
$$

**From active constraints** $g_1 = 0$ and $g_2 = 0$:

$$
d_1^{*2} = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}, \quad d_2^{*2} = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}
$$

Hence:

$$
d_1^* = d_2^* = d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}} \quad \text{(uniform bar)}
$$

The **multipliers are positive** ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$), confirming a *minimum* (not a maximum or saddle point).

### 3.1.9 Comparison Table — Workable vs Optimal Designs

| Design | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | Workable? | Optimal? | Volume |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| D1 | $1.5 d_{\min}$ | $2.0 d_{\min}$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (wasteful) | $1.625 \cdot V^*$ |
| D2 | $d_{\min}$ | $1.2 d_{\min}$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (wasteful) | $1.22 \cdot V^*$ |
| D3 (Optimal) | $d_{\min}$ | $d_{\min}$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | $V^* = V^*$ |

---

## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — Helical Compression Spring (Brief KTU-Style)

A **helical compression spring** carries a maximum axial load $F_{max}$. The designer is to choose the **wire diameter $d$** and **mean coil diameter $D$**.

**Stress constraint (Wahl-corrected):**

$$
\tau = \frac{8 F_{max} D}{\pi d^3} K_w \leq \tau_{allow}, \qquad K_w = \frac{4D - d}{4D - d} + \frac{0.615\,d}{D}
$$

**Objective — minimize spring weight (volume):**

$$
V(d, D) = \frac{\pi^2 d^2 D n_a}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(with } n_a \text{ active coils)}
$$

**Optimal solution (using KKT):** Both $d$ and $D$ are *coupled* by the active stress constraint, leading to a single optimal *index* $C = D/d$, typically in the range $4 \leq C \leq 12$ for steel springs.

> [!NOTE]
> The spring problem is presented here only as a **conceptual extension** to show that the Stepped Bar result (both constraints active) is the *typical* KKT pattern, not an exception.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Two-Step Bar with Internal Forces

The diagram below shows the physical setup of the practical example, the internal axial force distribution, and the stress variations along the bar length.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wall: Fixed Support] -->|Segment 1: L1, d1| B((Junction Node))
    B -->|Segment 2: L2, d2| C[Free End: Load P]
    C -. Tensile Force P .-> C

    subgraph S1[Segment 1 Internal State]
      S1A[Axial Force N1 = P]
      S1B[Stress sigma1 = 4P over pi d1 squared]
      S1C[Constraint: sigma1 less or equal sigma_allow]
      S1A --> S1B --> S1C
    end

    subgraph S2[Segment 2 Internal State]
      S2A[Axial Force N2 = P]
      S2B[Stress sigma2 = 4P over pi d2 squared]
      S2C[Constraint: sigma2 less or equal sigma_allow]
      S2A --> S2B --> S2C
    end

    B --- S1
    B --- S2
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Workable → Optimal Design Pipeline

This block diagram is the most examination-relevant visual summary of the entire topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Start: Design Problem Statement] --> P2[Identify Design Variables x1 to xn]
    P2 --> P3[Formulate Objective Function f of x]
    P3 --> P4[List Functional and Safety Constraints]
    P4 --> P5[Construct Feasible Region S in Design Space]

    P5 --> Q1{Does design satisfy all constraints?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2[Infeasible Design: REJECT]
    Q2 --> P2
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3[Workable System: FEASIBLE POINT in S]

    Q3 --> R1[Apply KKT or Analytical Optimality Conditions]
    R1 --> R2{Can f be further improved while remaining in S?}
    R2 -->|No| R3[Optimal Design: x star = argmin f over S]
    R2 -->|Yes| R4[Move to a Better Feasible Point]
    R4 --> R1

    R3 --> S1[Report: d1 star and d2 star and V star]
    Q3 --> S2[Report: Set of all feasible d1, d2]

    style Q3 fill:#d4f4dd
    style R3 fill:#fff3a0
    style Q2 fill:#f8c2c2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define the terms (i) Workable System and (ii) Optimal Design in the context of engineering design. How is the former related to the latter?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

(i) **Workable System:** A design configuration that satisfies *all* the prescribed design constraints — equality constraints $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and inequality constraints $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ — is called a *workable (or feasible) system*. It may, however, be far from the best design among feasible alternatives.

(ii) **Optimal Design:** A feasible design that simultaneously *minimises (or maximises)* the chosen objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is called the *optimal design*, mathematically $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} f(\mathbf{x})$.

**Relation:** Every optimal design is necessarily a workable design, but a workable design need not be optimal. The optimal design is a *subset* of the set of all workable designs — it is the "best" point inside the feasible region $S$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any four mathematical conditions that a candidate design $\mathbf{x}$ must satisfy to be considered a workable system in a constrained optimization problem.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A design $\mathbf{x}$ is workable if it satisfies the following conditions:

1. **Equality Constraints:** $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ for all $j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ (e.g., equilibrium equations).
2. **Inequality Constraints:** $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ (e.g., stress ≤ allowable).
3. **Side / Bound Constraints:** $x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}$ (e.g., non-negative geometric dimensions).
4. **Functional / Code Constraints:** Any manufacturing tolerance, safety factor, or regulatory limit imposed externally.

All four must be satisfied **simultaneously**; failure of any one criterion renders the design *infeasible*.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Choice-Based Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A common mark-loss occurs when students compute the *workable* region correctly but forget to **verify that the Lagrange multipliers are non-negative**. Without $\lambda_k \geq 0$, the KKT conditions are violated, and the point is **not a valid minimum** — even if it lies on the constraint boundary. Always confirm $\lambda_k \geq 0$ at the final answer.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Stepped Bar Practical Example*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
>
> A **stepped circular bar** consists of two segments, each of length $L$, made of the same material of allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$. The bar is fixed at one end and carries an axial tensile force $P$ at the free end.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the design optimization problem, clearly identifying the design variables, the objective function, and the constraints. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the **method of Lagrange multipliers (or KKT conditions)**, determine the *optimal diameters* $d_1^*$ and $d_2^*$ and the *minimum volume* of the bar. Show that the optimal bar is **uniform**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): Problem Formulation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Design Variables:**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad d_1, d_2 > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Objective Function (minimise total volume):**

$$
V(d_1, d_2) = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 L + \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 L = \frac{\pi L}{4}(d_1^2 + d_2^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

The axial force in each segment is $P$ (single external load, no transverse load). The stress in each segment must not exceed the allowable:

$$
g_1(d_1) = \frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

$$
g_2(d_2) = \frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Feasible Region:**

$$
S = \{(d_1, d_2) : d_1 \geq d_{\min},\ d_2 \geq d_{\min}\}, \quad \text{where } d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

This is an **unbounded quadrant** with the lower-left corner at $(d_{\min}, d_{\min})$. **(Full 7 marks awarded for the complete formulation.)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): Optimal Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Lagrangian:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{\pi L}{4}(d_1^2 + d_2^2) + \lambda_1\!\left(\frac{4P}{\pi d_1^2} - \sigma_{allow}\right) + \lambda_2\!\left(\frac{4P}{\pi d_2^2} - \sigma_{allow}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — KKT Stationarity:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_1} = \frac{\pi L}{2} d_1 - \frac{8 P \lambda_1}{\pi d_1^3} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_2} = \frac{\pi L}{2} d_2 - \frac{8 P \lambda_2}{\pi d_2^3} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Active Constraints at Optimum:**

At the optimum, $g_1 = 0$ and $g_2 = 0$ (corner point of the feasible region):

$$
d_1^{*2} = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}, \quad d_2^{*2} = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
\therefore d_1^* = d_2^* = d_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Uniformity Conclusion:**

Since $d_1^* = d_2^*$, the **optimal stepped bar is a uniform bar** of constant cross-section.

**Step 5 — Minimum Volume:**

$$
V^* = \frac{\pi L}{4}\left[\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}} + \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}\right] = \frac{2 P L}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiplier Check:**

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{\pi^2 L \, d_1^{*4}}{16 P} = \frac{\pi^2 L}{16P} \cdot \left(\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}\right)^2 = \frac{P L}{\pi \sigma_{allow}^2} > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

By symmetry, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 > 0$, confirming a **valid minimum**. **(Full 7 marks.)**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative: Cantilever Beam Deflection Problem*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> A **cantilever beam** of rectangular cross-section (width $b$, depth $h$) and length $L$ carries a concentrated tip load $W$. The beam material has Young's modulus $E$ and allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allow}$. The tip deflection $\delta$ must not exceed $\delta_{allow}$.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the optimization problem to **minimise the cross-sectional area** $A = bh$ subject to the **stress** and **deflection** constraints. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Solve for the optimal $b$ and $h$, assuming the **stress constraint is active** and the deflection constraint is the **governing design equation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Design variables:** $b$, $h$.

**Objective (minimise area):**

$$
A(b, h) = b \cdot h
$$

**Stress constraint** (bending stress at the fixed end):

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{W L}{b h^2/6} = \frac{6 W L}{b h^2} \leq \sigma_{allow} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad g_1 = \frac{6 W L}{b h^2} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

**Deflection constraint:**

$$
\delta = \frac{W L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{4 W L^3}{E b h^3} \leq \delta_{allow} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad g_2 = \frac{4 W L^3}{E b h^3} - \delta_{allow} \leq 0
$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Working stress design approach:** In most KTU-style problems, the **stiffness (deflection) constraint governs** for slender beams. Setting $g_2 = 0$:

$$
b h^3 = \frac{4 W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}
$$

**Substitute into objective** to obtain the optimum $b/h$ ratio (square cross-section: $b = h$):

$$
A = b h \quad \text{minimised when } b = h
$$

$$
A^* = b^2, \quad b^5 = \frac{4 W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}
$$

$$
\boxed{b^* = h^* = \left(\frac{4 W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}\right)^{1/5}}
$$

The **optimal cross-section is square** ($b^* = h^*$) — a classic and elegant result in KTU Module 1.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ A **workable system** satisfies *all* constraints; an **optimal design** is the *best* workable design with respect to the objective.
- ✅ The set of all workable designs is the **feasible region** $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$.
- ✅ For the **stepped bar under axial load**: $d_1^* = d_2^* = d_{\min}$ (optimal = uniform bar).
- ✅ Minimum volume: $V^* = P L_{total} / \sigma_{allow}$ — independent of the number of steps.
- ✅ For a **cantilever beam** minimising area: $b^* = h^*$ — *square* cross-section is optimal.
- ✅ For a **helical spring** minimising weight: stress constraint is always *active*; optimal index $C = D/d \in [4, 12]$ for steel.
- ✅ **KKT conditions** to remember verbatim: Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility ($\lambda_k \geq 0$), and Complementary Slackness ($\lambda_k g_k = 0$).
- ✅ Always **end the KKT solution with a multiplier check** ($\lambda_k \geq 0$); KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
- ✅ A **workable design** is found by *constraint satisfaction*; an **optimal design** is found by *constrained search inside $S$*.
- ✅ **Conversion rule for canonical form:** rewrite every constraint as $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (move RHS to LHS).
- ✅ The **gradient of the objective** at the optimum points "outward" from the active constraints, balanced by the multipliers.
- ✅ Real-world mantra: *"Make it work, then make it work better."* — Workable, then Optimal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton Raphson Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Newton-Raphson Method** (also known as *Newton's Method* or the *Newton-Fourier Method*) is a **first-derivative, open-bracket, iterative numerical procedure** used in engineering optimization to determine successively refined approximations to the **real root** of a real-valued, differentiable function $f(x) = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 1 Context):**
> The Newton-Raphson Method is a single-variable, gradient-based root-finding algorithm that forms the algorithmic backbone of higher-order optimization techniques (Quasi-Newton, Sequential Quadratic Programming, etc.). In Design Optimization, it is used both directly (to solve $f(x) = 0$) and indirectly (to find stationary points by solving $f'(x) = 0$ for minima/maxima).

The canonical recurrence relation is given by:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

where $f'(x_n) \neq 0$ is the **first derivative** of $f$ evaluated at the current iterate $x_n$.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Sliding Down the Tangent Line" Analogy**

Imagine you are standing on a **smooth curved hillside** on a **foggy night** (representing the function $y = f(x)$). You cannot see the valley below, but you can:
1. **Feel the slope** of the ground beneath your feet (this is $f'(x_n)$, the first derivative).
2. **Imagine a straight line** tangent to the ground at your location, extending downward into the fog.

That tangent line will eventually intersect the horizontal ground level ($y = 0$) at some point $x_{n+1}$ ahead of you. **You take one giant step to that intersection point.** Once there, you are at a new position on the curve. You repeat the process — feel the new slope, project a new tangent, step to its zero-crossing — and you **spiral down to the base of the hill** (the root $x^*$) with astonishing speed.

> **Key Insight:** Because each step uses the *curvature information* (slope), the steps get *dramatically longer* when the curve is flat, and *much smaller* when the curve is steep. This is why Newton-Raphson is one of the fastest-converging algorithms in numerical analysis, achieving **quadratic convergence**.

---

### 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

| Syllabus Sub-Topic | Coverage in This Note |
|---|---|
| Single-variable root finding | Core algorithm (Section 1–3) |
| Iterative methods for design | Algorithm flowchart (Section 4) |
| Rate of convergence analysis | Quadratic convergence proof (Section 2 & 3) |
| Application to unconstrained optimization | $f'(x) = 0$ extension (Section 3.3) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The Newton-Raphson Method is fundamentally a **linearization technique**. Here is the structured logic step-by-step:

1. **Initialization Step:** Choose an initial guess $x_0$ (ideally close to the true root) and a tolerance $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$).
2. **Derivative Verification:** Compute $f'(x_0)$. If $f'(x_0) = 0$, the method **fails** because division by zero is undefined. Choose a new $x_0$.
3. **Tangent Line Projection:** Approximate $f(x)$ near $x_0$ by its first-order Taylor polynomial — a straight tangent line. The $x$-intercept of this line gives the next iterate $x_1$.
4. **Iterative Update:** Apply the recurrence $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ repeatedly.
5. **Convergence Check:** Terminate when either:
   - **Function-value criterion:** $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$
   - **Successive-iterate criterion:** $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$
   - **Relative-error criterion:** $\varepsilon_a = \vert (x_{n+1} - x_n)/x_{n+1} \vert \times 100\% < \varepsilon_a^{max}$
6. **Maximum Iteration Safeguard:** If convergence is not reached within $N$ iterations (e.g., $N = 50$), abort to prevent infinite loops in divergent cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it converge quadratically?**
> The local truncation error at step $n+1$ is proportional to the **square** of the error at step $n$:
> $$E_{n+1} = \mathcal{O}(E_n^2)$$
> This means the number of *correct decimal places* roughly **doubles** with each iteration — a hallmark of quadratic convergence unmatched by linear methods like Bisection or False-Position.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU ESE 2024 — they appear in 90% of Newton-Raphson problems.**

| **#** | **Formula / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Significance** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recurrence Relation | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Core update rule |
| 2 | Taylor Linearization (Origin) | $f(x_{n+1}) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$ | Used to derive (1) |
| 3 | Approximate Relative Error | $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{n+1} - x_n}{x_{n+1}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Convergence test |
| 4 | Quadratic Convergence Error | $E_{n+1} \approx -\dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} \, E_n^2$ | Order of convergence proof |
| 5 | Convergence Sufficient Condition | $\left\vert \dfrac{f(x) \, f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2} \right\vert < 1$ | Local convergence guarantee |
| 6 | Optimization Variant (find $x^*$) | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f'(x_n)}{f''(x_n)}$ | Used in unconstrained minimization |
| 7 | Rate Constant | $\lambda = \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)}$ | Determines convergence speed |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Newton-Raphson is Used in Real Systems

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Structural Optimization** | Finding optimal cross-section where $\partial \text{Cost}/\partial A = 0$ |
| **Aerospace Design** | Solving nonlinear lift-drag equations for minimum drag wing |
| **Mechanical Design** | Root of $f(x) = \sigma_{yield} - \sigma_{actual}$ for safe load factor |
| **Thermal Systems** | Solving transcendental heat-transfer equations (Biot number iterations) |
| **Control Systems** | Pole-placement via root-locus computations |
| **Civil Engineering** | Finding depth of flow in open channels (Manning's equation) |

> **Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Always link Newton-Raphson to *Design Optimization* — the method's true value is not just finding roots, but **finding the stationary points of cost/weight/stress functions** in engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation from the Taylor Series

We start with the **first-order Taylor expansion** of $f(x)$ expanded about the point $x_n$:

$$f(x_{n+1}) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) + \frac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^2 + \frac{f'''(x_n)}{3!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^3 + \cdots$$

**Step 1: Truncate the series.**
We assume $x_{n+1}$ is *close to* $x_n$, so $(x_{n+1} - x_n)$ is small. We neglect all second-order and higher terms (linearization):

$$f(x_{n+1}) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 2: Impose the root condition.**
Our goal is to find $x_{n+1}$ such that $f(x_{n+1}) = 0$. Substitute $f(x_{n+1}) = 0$:

$$0 \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $x_{n+1}$.**
Rearrange algebraically:

$$f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) = -f(x_n)$$

$$x_{n+1} - x_n = -\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Step 4: Final recurrence.**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Quadratic Convergence — Error Recurrence Derivation

Let $x^*$ be the true root, so $f(x^*) = 0$. The error at step $n$ is $E_n = x_n - x^*$. Expanding $f(x_n)$ via Taylor series about $x^*$:

$$f(x_n) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)(x_n - x^*) + \frac{f''(x^*)}{2!}(x_n - x^*)^2 + \cdots$$

Since $f(x^*) = 0$:

$$f(x_n) = f'(x^*) E_n + \frac{f''(x^*)}{2} E_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(E_n^3)$$

Similarly for the derivative:

$$f'(x_n) = f'(x^*) + f''(x^*) E_n + \mathcal{O}(E_n^2)$$

Substitute into the Newton-Raphson recurrence:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f'(x^*) E_n + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*) E_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(E_n^3)}{f'(x^*) + f''(x^*) E_n + \mathcal{O}(E_n^2)}$$

Performing the long division and simplifying (the $E_n$ terms cancel exactly):

$$x_{n+1} - x^* = \frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} E_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(E_n^3)$$

Therefore:

$$E_{n+1} \approx -\frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} E_n^2$$

This confirms **quadratic convergence** — the error squares at every step.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Find the Root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$

**Given:** $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$, initial guess $x_0 = 2$, tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.001$.

**Step 0: Compute the derivative.**

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$$

**Iteration 1** ($n = 0 \to 1$):

$$f(x_0) = f(2) = (2)^3 - 2(2) - 5 = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$$

$$f'(x_0) = f'(2) = 3(2)^2 - 2 = 12 - 2 = 10$$

$$x_1 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)}{f'(x_0)} = 2 - \frac{-1}{10} = 2 + 0.1 = 2.1000$$

$$\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \frac{2.1000 - 2.0000}{2.1000} \right\vert \times 100\% = 4.762\%$$

**Iteration 2** ($n = 1 \to 2$):

$$f(2.1) = (2.1)^3 - 2(2.1) - 5 = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061$$

$$f'(2.1) = 3(2.1)^2 - 2 = 3(4.41) - 2 = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$$

$$x_2 = 2.1 - \frac{0.061}{11.23} = 2.1 - 0.005432 = 2.094568$$

$$\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \frac{2.094568 - 2.100000}{2.094568} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.259\%$$

**Iteration 3** ($n = 2 \to 3$):

$$f(2.094568) \approx (2.094568)^3 - 2(2.094568) - 5 \approx 0.000184$$

$$f'(2.094568) \approx 3(2.094568)^2 - 2 \approx 11.1617$$

$$x_3 = 2.094568 - \frac{0.000184}{11.1617} = 2.094568 - 0.0000165 \approx 2.094551$$

$$\varepsilon_a = \left\vert \frac{2.094551 - 2.094568}{2.094551} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.0008\%$$

Since $\varepsilon_a = 0.0008\% < \varepsilon = 0.001$ in absolute terms, **convergence achieved** in 3 iterations.

#### **Iteration Summary Matrix**

| Iteration $n$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ | $\varepsilon_a$ (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 2.000000 | $-1.000000$ | 10.000000 | 2.100000 | 4.7619 |
| 1 | 2.100000 | 0.061000 | 11.230000 | 2.094568 | 0.2594 |
| 2 | 2.094568 | 0.000184 | 11.161700 | 2.094551 | 0.0008 |
| 3 | 2.094551 | $\approx 0$ | — | — | **Converged** |

**Result:** $x^* \approx 2.0946$ (true root = $2.094551...$)

---

### 3.4 Application to Unconstrained Optimization (Design Optimization Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU Module-1 critical application!**

In design optimization, we often seek the value of $x$ that **minimizes** an objective function $F(x)$. The necessary condition is:

$$\frac{dF}{dx} = 0 \quad \text{(stationary point)}$$

By defining $g(x) = F'(x)$ and $g'(x) = F''(x)$, we apply Newton-Raphson to find where $F'(x) = 0$:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{F'(x_n)}{F''(x_n)}$$

**Example (KTU-type):** Minimize $F(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ starting from $x_0 = 1$.

- $F'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$
- $F''(x) = 12x - 18$
- $F'(1) = 6 - 18 + 12 = 0$ → already stationary at $x = 1$ (verify it's a minimum via $F''(1) = -6 < 0$ — actually a **maximum**)

Applying the optimization Newton step to find the other stationary point:
$x_1 = x_0 - F'(x_0)/F''(x_0) = 1 - 0/(-6) = 1$ (trivial first step; user must choose $x_0 = 2$ to reach $x = 2$).

For $x_0 = 2$: $F'(2) = 24 - 36 + 12 = 0$, $F''(2) = 6 > 0$ → **local minimum** at $x^* = 2$, with $F(2) = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = 7$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize: x0, tolerance eps, max iterations N]
    B --> C{n less than N?}
    C -- No --> D[Report: Method did not converge]
    D --> Z1([END])
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute f xn]
    E --> F[Compute f prime xn]
    F --> G{f prime xn equals zero?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Report: Division by zero - restart with new x0]
    H --> Z2([END])
    G -- No --> I[Calculate xn+1 = xn minus f xn over f prime xn]
    I --> J[Compute approximate error ea]
    J --> K{ea less than eps?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Return root x star equals xn+1]
    L --> Z3([END])
    K -- No --> M[Update: xn equals xn+1, increment n]
    M --> C
```

---

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Function Curve y equals f of x] --> B[Current Point P: xn, f xn on curve]
    B --> C[Draw Tangent Line T at P with slope f prime xn]
    C --> D[Tangent Line T Intersects x axis at xn+1]
    D --> E[New Point Q on Curve at xn+1, f xn+1]
    E --> F[New Tangent Line T2 at Q with slope f prime xn+1]
    F --> G[Tangent T2 Intersects x axis at xn+2 closer to true root]
    G --> H[Converges to True Root x star where f x star equals zero]
```

---

### 4.3 Convergence Behavior Comparison (Linear vs Quadratic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Starting with Initial Guess x0] --> MethodA
    Start --> MethodB
    MethodA[Method A: Bisection or Secant - Linear Convergence] --> A1[Error halves each iteration]
    A1 --> A2[Error: 0.1, 0.05, 0.025, 0.0125, 0.00625]
    MethodB[Method B: Newton Raphson - Quadratic Convergence] --> B1[Error squares each iteration]
    B1 --> B2[Error: 0.1, 0.01, 0.0001, 0.00000001]
    A2 --> Conclusion
    B2 --> Conclusion
    Conclusion[Newton Raphson reaches high accuracy in fewer iterations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowchart (4.1) is your **algorithm template** for KTU programming/numerical problems. The geometric block (4.2) explains *why* the method works. The convergence comparison (4.3) is a common 3-mark question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **State the Newton-Raphson formula for finding the root of a nonlinear equation $f(x) = 0$. Mention its order of convergence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Newton-Raphson iteration formula is:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}, \quad f'(x_n) \neq 0$$

**Derivation basis:** It is derived from the first-order Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ about $x_n$, where the higher-order terms are neglected (linearization).

**Order of convergence:** The Newton-Raphson method has **quadratic convergence**, meaning the error at the $(n+1)$-th iteration is proportional to the square of the error at the $n$-th iteration:

$$E_{n+1} \approx -\frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} E_n^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{n+1} = \mathcal{O}(E_n^2)$$

The number of correct decimal places **approximately doubles** with each successive iteration. ✓

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
> **What are the conditions for convergence of the Newton-Raphson method? List any two limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Conditions for convergence:**
1. The function $f(x)$ must be **continuously differentiable** (i.e., $f$, $f'$, and $f''$ must exist and be continuous) in a neighborhood of the root $x^*$.
2. The initial guess $x_0$ must be **sufficiently close** to the true root $x^*$.
3. The sufficient local convergence condition is:

$$\left\vert \frac{f(x) \, f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2} \right\vert < 1$$

**Limitations:**
1. **Requires derivative computation:** $f'(x)$ must be evaluated at every step. For complex functions, this may be analytically or computationally expensive.
2. **Failure near inflection points / zero slope:** If $f'(x_n) \approx 0$ at any iterate, the method **diverges** (division by near-zero). The method is also sensitive to **poor initial guesses** and can converge to a **different root** than intended (if multiple roots exist). ✓

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice: A or B, 14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the Newton-Raphson iteration formula using the Taylor series expansion. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using Newton-Raphson method, find a real root of the equation $x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ correct to 4 decimal places, taking $x_0 = 2$ as the initial guess. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ about the point $x_n$ is:

$$f(x_{n+1}) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) + \frac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^2 + \frac{f'''(x_n)}{3!}(x_{n+1} - x_n)^3 + \cdots$$

**Step 1 (Truncation):** Assuming $x_{n+1}$ is close to $x_n$, the higher-order terms are negligible:

$$f(x_{n+1}) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 2 (Root condition):** For $x_{n+1}$ to be a root, set $f(x_{n+1}) = 0$:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

**Step 3 (Solve for $x_{n+1}$):**

$$f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n) = -f(x_n)$$

$$x_{n+1} - x_n = -\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

$$\boxed{x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}}$$

provided $f'(x_n) \neq 0$. This is the **Newton-Raphson iteration formula**. ✓

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$, $x_0 = 2$, $\varepsilon = 0.0001$ (4 decimal places).

**Derivative:** $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$

**Iteration 1:**
$f(2) = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$
$f'(2) = 12 - 2 = 10$
$x_1 = 2 - (-1/10) = 2.1$

**Iteration 2:**
$f(2.1) = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061$
$f'(2.1) = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$
$x_2 = 2.1 - (0.061/11.23) = 2.0946$

**Iteration 3:**
$f(2.0946) \approx 0.000184$
$f'(2.0946) \approx 11.1617$
$x_3 = 2.0946 - (0.000184/11.1617) = 2.0946$

**Iteration 4:**
$x_4 \approx 2.0946$ (no change in 4 decimal places)

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 2.0000 | $-1.0000$ | 10.0000 | 2.1000 |
| 1 | 2.1000 | 0.0610 | 11.2300 | 2.0946 |
| 2 | 2.0946 | 0.0002 | 11.1617 | 2.0946 |
| 3 | 2.0946 | $\approx 0$ | — | — |

**Result:** The root, correct to 4 decimal places, is $\boxed{x^* \approx 2.0946}$. ✓

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the Newton-Raphson method with a neat geometric diagram. Discuss the **order of convergence** and **sufficient condition** for convergence. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Apply Newton-Raphson method to find the root of $\cos(x) - x e^x = 0$ with $x_0 = 0$. Perform **three iterations**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory + Geometry (7 Marks):**

The Newton-Raphson method is an **open iterative method** that uses the first derivative to project a tangent line at the current iterate $x_n$ to its $x$-axis intercept, generating the next iterate:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:**
At the point $P(x_n, f(x_n))$ on the curve $y = f(x)$, draw a tangent line with slope $f'(x_n)$. The equation of this tangent is:

$$y - f(x_n) = f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

Setting $y = 0$ to find the $x$-intercept:

$$x = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = x_{n+1}$$

**Order of Convergence:** The method exhibits **quadratic convergence** since:

$$E_{n+1} \approx -\frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} E_n^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert E_{n+1} \vert}{\vert E_n \vert^2} = \left\vert \frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} \right\vert$$

**Sufficient Condition for Convergence (initial-guess region):**
The method converges if:

$$\left\vert \frac{f(x) f''(x)}{[f'(x)]^2} \right\vert < 1 \quad \text{holds near } x_0$$

This guarantees that the iterates contract toward the root $x^*$. ✓

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f(x) = \cos(x) - x e^x$, $x_0 = 0$.

**Derivative (using product rule):**
$f'(x) = -\sin(x) - [e^x + x e^x] = -\sin(x) - e^x(1 + x)$

**Iteration 1:**
$f(0) = \cos(0) - 0 \cdot e^0 = 1 - 0 = 1$
$f'(0) = -\sin(0) - e^0(1 + 0) = 0 - 1 = -1$
$x_1 = 0 - (1 / -1) = 0 - (-1) = 1.0000$

**Iteration 2:**
$f(1) = \cos(1) - 1 \cdot e^1 = 0.5403 - 2.7183 = -2.1780$
$f'(1) = -\sin(1) - e^1(1 + 1) = -0.8415 - 5.4366 = -6.2781$
$x_2 = 1 - (-2.1780 / -6.2781) = 1 - 0.3469 = 0.6531$

**Iteration 3:**
$f(0.6531) = \cos(0.6531) - 0.6531 \cdot e^{0.6531} = 0.7939 - 1.2665 = -0.4726$
$f'(0.6531) = -\sin(0.6531) - e^{0.6531}(1 + 0.6531) = -0.6083 - 1.9396 \cdot 1.6531 = -0.6083 - 3.2063 = -3.8146$
$x_3 = 0.6531 - (-0.4726 / -3.8146) = 0.6531 - 0.1239 = 0.5292$

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | $-1.0000$ | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 1.0000 | $-2.1780$ | $-6.2781$ | 0.6531 |
| 2 | 0.6531 | $-0.4726$ | $-3.8146$ | 0.5292 |

**Result after 3 iterations:** $x_3 \approx 0.5292$ (true root $\approx 0.5178$). ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Forgetting the derivative requirement (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write the formula without stating the condition $f'(x_n) \neq 0$. Always mention this.
> 2. **Skipping the convergence check (–1 mark):** Show the $\varepsilon_a$ calculation in every iteration. Examiners award marks for the *convergence test*, not just the numerical answer.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in $f(x_n)$ (–1 to –2 marks):** Most marks are lost here. Always **show the substitution step**, e.g., "f(2.1) = (2.1)³ − 2(2.1) − 5 = 9.261 − 4.2 − 5 = 0.061". Examiners do not credit skipped arithmetic.
> 4. **Not stating the order of convergence (–1 mark):** A 3-mark question *demands* "quadratic" and the error relation $E_{n+1} = \mathcal{O}(E_n^2)$.
> 5. **Using the wrong starting value or not stating it (–1 mark):** Always explicitly write "Taking $x_0 = ...$" at the start.
> 6. **Confusing signs:** In the recurrence $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$, a **common error is writing a + sign**. Double-check the sign in the formula.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU Exam!**

- ✅ **Core Formula:** $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ — must be memorized **verbatim**.
- ✅ **Convergence:** **Quadratic** ($E_{n+1} \propto E_n^2$); number of correct decimals **doubles** per iteration.
- ✅ **Convergence Condition:** $\left\vert f(x) f''(x) / [f'(x)]^2 \right\vert < 1$ near $x_0$.
- ✅ **Applicability:** $f(x)$ must be **continuously differentiable**; $f'(x_n) \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Sensitive to poor initial guess; fails if $f'(x_n) = 0$; needs derivative evaluation.
- ✅ **Optimization Variant:** $x_{n+1} = x_n - F'(x_n) / F''(x_n)$ — finds minima of $F(x)$.
- ✅ **Convergence Test:** Use $\varepsilon_a = \left\vert (x_{n+1} - x_n) / x_{n+1} \right\vert \times 100\% < \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 0.001$ or $10^{-5}$).
- ✅ **Geometric Meaning:** Tangent line at $x_n$ crosses $x$-axis at $x_{n+1}$.
- ✅ **Advantage over Bisection:** Faster (quadratic vs linear), but requires derivative.
- ✅ **Typical KTU problem type:** Given $f(x)$, find root using $x_0$; show **3 iterations** with $\varepsilon_a$ table.
- ✅ **True root check:** $f(x^*) = 0$; verify your answer by substitution.
- ✅ **KTU Module-1 focus:** Link the method to **design optimization** — finding stationary points of objective functions in engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** One Unknown - Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design Optimization**, a problem classified as **"One Unknown"** refers to a mathematical programming formulation in which the objective function $f(x)$ depends on **exactly one design variable** $x$, and the task is to find the value of $x$ that minimizes (or maximizes) $f(x)$ over a feasible interval $[a, b]$.

The standard mathematical statement is:

$$\text{Find } x^{*} \text{ such that } f(x^{*}) \leq f(x), \quad \forall \, x \in [a, b]$$

Where:
- $x$ is the single **design variable (decision variable)**.
- $f(x)$ is the **objective function** (cost, weight, energy, error, etc.).
- $[a, b]$ is the **search interval** containing the optimum.
- $x^{*}$ is the **optimal design point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** "One-Unknown (One-Dimensional / Univariate) Optimization refers to the systematic reduction of an interval or successive approximation techniques applied to scalar-valued functions of a single design variable to locate stationary points (minima or maxima)."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Finding the Deepest Point of a Valley"**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a **hilly terrain in complete darkness**, equipped only with an altimeter. You are told the valley bottom (minimum) lies somewhere between two flag posts, $A$ and $B$, along a single straight path.
>
> You cannot see the whole path at once. So you take a few elevation readings (function evaluations) at carefully chosen points. By comparing these readings, you eliminate sections of the path that cannot contain the valley bottom. You repeat this narrowing process until the remaining segment is so small that you can confidently mark the deepest spot.
>
> - The two flag posts $A$ and $B$ represent the bounds of the interval $[a, b]$.
> - Each elevation reading represents a function evaluation $f(x)$.
> - The chosen sampling strategy (Fibonacci, Golden Section, Quadratic Fit, Newton-Raphson) represents the **algorithm**.
>
> The fewer readings you need to find the valley, the **more efficient** your algorithm is. KTU examiners reward efficiency!

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants appear repeatedly in one-unknown examples:

| Constant | Symbol | Numerical Value | Used In |
|----------|--------|-----------------|---------|
| Golden Ratio | $\tau$ | $\dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.6180$ | Golden Section |
| Reduction Ratio | $1/\tau$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx 0.6180$ | Golden Section |
| Fibonacci Number | $F_n$ | $F_0=0, F_1=1, F_n = F_{n-1}+F_{n-2}$ | Fibonacci Search |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of One-Unknown Methods

The four principal methods covered under KTU Module 1 for one unknown are categorized as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Taxonomy:**
> 1. **Region Elimination Methods (No Derivatives Required)**
>    - Exhaustive / Uniform Search
>    - Fibonacci Search Method
>    - Golden Section Search Method
> 2. **Approximation / Interpolation Methods (Use Function Values)**
>    - Quadratic Interpolation (3-point parabolic fit)
>    - Cubic Interpolation (4-point cubic fit)
> 3. **Gradient-Based Point Methods (Use Derivatives)**
>    - Newton-Raphson Method
>    - Secant Method (Quasi-Newton)
> 4. **Successive Quadratic / Cubic Estimation Methods**

## 2.2 Logical Steps of Each Method (Structured)

### A. Fibonacci Search Method
1. Choose number of function evaluations $n$.
2. Compute Fibonacci numbers $F_0, F_1, \ldots, F_n$.
3. Place first two interior points at fractions $F_{n-1}/F_n$ and $F_{n-2}/F_n$ from the left.
4. Evaluate $f$ at these points; compare and discard the worse interval.
5. Place the next point using the most recent Fibonacci ratio.
6. Repeat until interval length is smaller than tolerance.

### B. Golden Section Search Method
1. Compute golden ratio $\tau = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$.
2. Place points at distances $(b-a)/\tau$ from each end.
3. Evaluate $f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2)$; discard the segment containing the worse point.
4. **Reuse** the interior point (key advantage — no extra evaluation).
5. Repeat until $(b-a) < \varepsilon$.

### C. Quadratic Interpolation Method
1. Choose (or bracket) three points $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$ with $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2) < f(x_3)$.
2. Fit a parabola through the three points.
3. Find the vertex of the parabola — this becomes the new $x_{\text{new}}$.
4. Replace the worst point with $x_{\text{new}}$ and iterate.

### D. Newton-Raphson Method
1. Start with initial guess $x_0$.
2. Compute first derivative $f'(x_n)$ and second derivative $f''(x_n)$.
3. Update: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f'(x_n)}{f''(x_n)}$.
4. Stop when $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Update / Position Formula | Stopping Criterion |
|---|--------|---------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Uniform Search | $x_i = a + i \cdot \Delta x$ | $i = N$ evaluations |
| 2 | Fibonacci Search | $\rho_n = F_{n-1}/F_n$ | $b_n - a_n \leq L$ |
| 3 | Golden Section | $x_1 = a + (b-a)/\tau$, $x_2 = b - (b-a)/\tau$ | $b_n - a_n \leq \varepsilon$ |
| 4 | Quadratic Fit | $x_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(x_2^2 - x_3^2)f_1 + (x_3^2 - x_1^2)f_2 + (x_1^2 - x_2^2)f_3}{(x_2 - x_3)f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)f_3}$ | $\vert x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}} \vert \leq \varepsilon$ |
| 5 | Cubic Fit | $x_{\min} = x_2 - \dfrac{(x_2 - x_1)(f'(x_2) + \beta - \text{sign}(f'(x_1)-f'(x_2))\sqrt{\text{cubic}})}{2(f'(x_2)-f'(x_1)+2\beta)}$ | $\vert \Delta x \vert \leq \varepsilon$ |
| 6 | Newton-Raphson | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f'(x_n)}{f''(x_n)}$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert \leq \varepsilon$ |
| 7 | Secant | $x_{n+1} = x_n - f'(x_n) \cdot \dfrac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{f'(x_n) - f'(x_{n-1})}$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert \leq \varepsilon$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> - **Golden Section Search** is used in CNC machining parameter tuning, beam-column cross-section optimization, and PID controller gain tuning.
> - **Newton-Raphson** dominates structural FEM solvers (residual force minimization).
> - **Quadratic Interpolation** is used in CAD spline fitting and in surrogate models.
> - **Fibonacci Search** is the theoretical benchmark for "minimum evaluations to achieve a prescribed uncertainty" in numerical analysis courses.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Quadratic Interpolation Formula Derivation

Let three points $(x_1, f_1), (x_2, f_2), (x_3, f_3)$ be fitted to a parabola:

$$f(x) \approx a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$$

Writing the three equations:

$$f_1 = a_0 + a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_1^2$$
$$f_2 = a_0 + a_1 x_2 + a_2 x_2^2$$
$$f_3 = a_0 + a_1 x_3 + a_2 x_3^2$$

Subtracting the first from the second and third:

$$f_2 - f_1 = a_1(x_2 - x_1) + a_2(x_2^2 - x_1^2)$$
$$f_3 - f_1 = a_1(x_3 - x_1) + a_2(x_3^2 - x_1^2)$$

Setting $df/dx = 0$ gives $a_1 + 2 a_2 x_{\min} = 0$, so:

$$x_{\min} = -\dfrac{a_1}{2a_2}$$

Solving the linear system (Cramer's rule / elimination) yields the closed-form expression:

$$x_{\min} = \dfrac{(x_2^2 - x_3^2)f_1 + (x_3^2 - x_1^2)f_2 + (x_1^2 - x_2^2)f_3}{2\left[(x_2 - x_3)f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)f_3\right]}$$

---

## 3.2 SOLVED EXAMPLE 1 — Quadratic Interpolation Method (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 11$ over the interval $[0, 6]$ using **Quadratic Interpolation**. Start with $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 3, x_3 = 6$. Perform two iterations.

### Step 1: Function Evaluations at Initial Points

$$f(x_1) = f(0) = 0 - 0 + 11 = 11$$

$$f(x_2) = f(3) = 9 - 18 + 11 = 2$$

$$f(x_3) = f(6) = 36 - 36 + 11 = 11$$

Since $f(3) < f(0)$ and $f(3) < f(6)$, point $x_2 = 3$ is currently the best. The bracket is valid.

### Step 2: Apply Quadratic Interpolation Formula

Using the KTU formula:

$$x_{\min} = \dfrac{(x_2^2 - x_3^2)f_1 + (x_3^2 - x_1^2)f_2 + (x_1^2 - x_2^2)f_3}{2\left[(x_2 - x_3)f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)f_3\right]}$$

Substituting $x_1=0, x_2=3, x_3=6, f_1=11, f_2=2, f_3=11$:

**Numerator:**

$$N = (9 - 36)(11) + (36 - 0)(2) + (0 - 9)(11)$$
$$N = (-27)(11) + (36)(2) + (-9)(11)$$
$$N = -297 + 72 - 99 = -324$$

**Denominator:**

$$D = 2\left[(3 - 6)(11) + (6 - 0)(2) + (0 - 3)(11)\right]$$
$$D = 2\left[(-3)(11) + (6)(2) + (-3)(11)\right]$$
$$D = 2\left[-33 + 12 - 33\right] = 2(-54) = -108$$

**Result of Iteration 1:**

$$x_4 = \dfrac{-324}{-108} = 3.0000$$

### Step 3: Evaluate f at x₄

$$f(x_4) = f(3) = 2$$

The estimate is already at the minimum. Since $f(x) = (x-3)^2 + 2$, the **true minimum is at $x^* = 3, f^* = 2$** — converged in one step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why one step?** The function itself is a perfect parabola, so the quadratic fit is exact. This is a classic KTU illustrative example.

---

## 3.3 SOLVED EXAMPLE 2 — Golden Section Search Method

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $f(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1$ over the interval $[0, 5]$ using **Golden Section Search** until the interval length is less than $0.5$.

### Step 0: Constants

$$\tau = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.6180, \quad \dfrac{1}{\tau} \approx 0.6180$$

### Iteration 1

**Interior Points:**

$$x_1 = a + \dfrac{b - a}{\tau} = 0 + \dfrac{5}{1.6180} = 3.0902$$

$$x_2 = b - \dfrac{b - a}{\tau} = 5 - 3.0902 = 1.9098$$

**Function Evaluations:**

$$f(x_1) = (3.0902 - 2)^2 + 1 = (1.0902)^2 + 1 = 1.1885 + 1 = 2.1885$$

$$f(x_2) = (1.9098 - 2)^2 + 1 = (-0.0902)^2 + 1 = 0.0081 + 1 = 1.0081$$

Since $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$, the minimum lies in $[a, x_1] = [0, 3.0902]$. **Discard** $[x_1, b]$.

New interval: $a = 0$, $b = 3.0902$, length $L_1 = 3.0902$.

### Iteration 2

Reuse $x_2$ as the new $x_1$ (Golden Section property):

$$x_1^{\text{new}} = x_2^{\text{old}} = 1.9098$$

$$x_2^{\text{new}} = b - \dfrac{b - a}{\tau} = 3.0902 - \dfrac{3.0902}{1.6180} = 3.0902 - 1.9098 = 1.1804$$

**Function Evaluations:**

$$f(x_1^{\text{new}}) = 1.0081 \text{ (already known)}$$

$$f(x_2^{\text{new}}) = (1.1804 - 2)^2 + 1 = (0.8196)^2 + 1 = 0.6717 + 1 = 1.6717$$

Since $f(x_1^{\text{new}}) < f(x_2^{\text{new}})$, new interval is $[x_2^{\text{new}}, b] = [1.1804, 3.0902]$.

Length: $L_2 = 3.0902 - 1.1804 = 1.9098$. Note $L_2 = L_1 / \tau$. ✓

### Iteration 3 (Continue Until L < 0.5)

| Iter | $a$ | $b$ | $L$ |
|------|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | 0.0000 | 5.0000 | 5.0000 |
| 2 | 0.0000 | 3.0902 | 3.0902 |
| 3 | 1.1804 | 3.0902 | 1.9098 |
| 4 | 1.1804 | 2.3493 | 1.1689 |
| 5 | 1.1804 | 1.9024 | 0.7220 |
| 6 | 1.4494 | 1.9024 | 0.4530 ✓ |

**Stopping condition met at Iteration 6.** Approximate minimum lies in $[1.4494, 1.9024]$, midpoint $\approx 1.676$. True minimum is at $x^* = 2$.

---

## 3.4 SOLVED EXAMPLE 3 — Newton-Raphson Method

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ using **Newton-Raphson Method**. Start at $x_0 = 2.5$. Tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.01$.

### Step 1: Derivatives

$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$$

$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

### Step 2: Newton-Raphson Update

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f'(x_n)}{f''(x_n)}$$

### Iteration 0 → 1

At $x_0 = 2.5$:

$$f'(2.5) = 6(6.25) - 18(2.5) + 12 = 37.5 - 45 + 12 = 4.5$$

$$f''(2.5) = 12(2.5) - 18 = 30 - 18 = 12$$

$$x_1 = 2.5 - \dfrac{4.5}{12} = 2.5 - 0.375 = 2.125$$

### Iteration 1 → 2

At $x_1 = 2.125$:

$$f'(2.125) = 6(4.5156) - 18(2.125) + 12 = 27.094 - 38.25 + 12 = 0.844$$

$$f''(2.125) = 12(2.125) - 18 = 25.5 - 18 = 7.5$$

$$x_2 = 2.125 - \dfrac{0.844}{7.5} = 2.125 - 0.1125 = 2.0125$$

### Iteration 2 → 3

At $x_2 = 2.0125$:

$$f'(2.0125) = 6(4.0502) - 18(2.0125) + 12 = 24.301 - 36.225 + 12 = 0.076$$

$$f''(2.0125) = 12(2.0125) - 18 = 24.15 - 18 = 6.15$$

$$x_3 = 2.0125 - \dfrac{0.076}{6.15} = 2.0125 - 0.0124 = 2.0001$$

**Stopping Check:** $\vert x_3 - x_2 \vert = \vert 2.0001 - 2.0125 \vert = 0.0124 > 0.01$. Do one more iteration.

### Iteration 3 → 4 (Convergence)

$$x_4 \approx 2.0000, \quad \text{converged.}$$

**Optimum:** $x^* = 2.0$, $f(x^*) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 3 = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = 7$.

---

## 3.5 SOLVED EXAMPLE 4 — Fibonacci Search Method

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $f(x) = 2x^2 - 8x + 10$ over $[0, 5]$ using **Fibonacci Search** with $N = 4$ function evaluations.

### Fibonacci Numbers (Required)

$$F_0 = 0, \quad F_1 = 1, \quad F_2 = 1, \quad F_3 = 2, \quad F_4 = 3, \quad F_5 = 5$$

### Initial Interval and Point Placement

Initial length $L_0 = b - a = 5$. Required ratio: $F_{N-1}/F_N = F_3/F_4 = 2/3$.

$$x_1 = a + \dfrac{F_{N-1}}{F_N}(b - a) = 0 + \dfrac{2}{3}(5) = 3.3333$$

$$x_2 = a + \dfrac{F_{N-2}}{F_N}(b - a) = 0 + \dfrac{1}{3}(5) = 1.6667$$

### Function Evaluations

$$f(x_1) = 2(11.111) - 8(3.333) + 10 = 22.222 - 26.667 + 10 = 5.556$$

$$f(x_2) = 2(2.778) - 8(1.667) + 10 = 5.556 - 13.333 + 10 = 2.222$$

### Reduction Step

Since $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$, discard $[x_1, b]$. New interval: $[a, x_1] = [0, 3.3333]$.

Place $x_3$ using $F_2/F_3 = 1/2$ of new length:

$$x_3 = a + \dfrac{F_2}{F_3}(x_1 - a) = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(3.3333) = 1.6667$$

$x_3$ coincides with $x_2$, so reuse $f(x_2) = 2.222$. Comparison: $f(x_3) = 2.222$ vs. next point.

Continue shrinking interval until $L \leq 0.5$. **Approximate optimum** lies in the final bracket near $x^* \approx 2$ (true minimum, since $f'(x) = 4x - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$, $f(2) = 2$).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of One-Unknown Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Definition] --> B[Choose Interval a to b]
    B --> C{Method Class?}
    C -->|No Derivatives| D1[Region Elimination]
    C -->|Use Derivatives| D2[Gradient Methods]
    C -->|Use Function Values| D3[Interpolation Methods]

    D1 --> E1A[Fibonacci Search]
    D1 --> E1B[Golden Section Search]
    D1 --> E1C[Uniform Search]

    D2 --> E2A[Newton Raphson Method]
    D2 --> E2B[Secant Method]

    D3 --> E3A[Quadratic Interpolation]
    D3 --> E3B[Cubic Interpolation]

    E1A --> F[Evaluate f at Sample Points]
    E1B --> F
    E1C --> F
    E2A --> G[Compute f prime and f double prime]
    E2B --> G
    E3A --> H[Fit Polynomial to Samples]
    E3B --> H

    F --> I[Discard Worst Sub-interval]
    G --> J[Update x using Newton Step]
    H --> K[Locate Vertex of Polynomial]

    I --> L{Convergence Met?}
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L -->|No| C
    L -->|Yes| M[Report Optimum x star and f x star]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Golden Section Interval Elimination Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Left End a] --> Q1[x1 equals a plus L by tau]
    Q1 --> R1[f x1]
    P2[Right End b] --> Q2[x2 equals b minus L by tau]
    Q2 --> R2[f x2]
    R1 --> S{f x1 LESS THAN f x2?}
    R2 --> S
    S -->|Yes| T1[Discard x2 to b: new b equals x2]
    S -->|No| T2[Discard a to x1: new a equals x1]
    T1 --> U[New shorter interval]
    T2 --> U
    U --> V{Length LESS THAN epsilon?}
    V -->|No| Q1
    V -->|Yes| W[Optimum at midpoint]
```

## 4.3 Newton-Raphson Convergence Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initial Guess x0] --> Init[Set n equals 0]
    Init --> Comp1[Compute f prime at xn]
    Comp1 --> Comp2[Compute f double prime at xn]
    Comp2 --> Check{f double prime equals zero?}
    Check -->|Yes| Fail[Stop: Method Fails]
    Check -->|No| Upd[x n plus 1 equals xn minus f prime by f double prime]
    Upd --> Conv{x n plus 1 minus xn LESS THAN epsilon?}
    Conv -->|Yes| Stop[Report x star equals x n plus 1]
    Conv -->|No| Inc[n equals n plus 1]
    Inc --> Comp1
```
