# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between Fibonacci Search Method and Golden Section Search Method for one-dimensional optimization."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fibonacci Search | Golden Section Search |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| Ratio | Varies with iteration ($F_{n-1}/F_n$) | Constant ($1/\tau \approx 0.618$) |
| Function Evaluations | Pre-fixed ($N$) | Adaptive until convergence |
| Efficiency | Optimal for a given $N$ | Sub-optimal but simple |
| Implementation | Requires Fibonacci table lookup | Single constant $\tau$ suffices |

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"State the conditions under which the Quadratic Interpolation method can be applied to minimize a univariate function."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The conditions are:
1. Three distinct points $x_1, x_2, x_3$ must be available with $x_1 < x_2 < x_3$.
2. The function values must satisfy $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2) < f(x_3)$, so $x_2$ brackets the minimum.
3. The function $f(x)$ must be unimodal (have a single minimum) in the bracketing interval.
4. The denominator $2[(x_2-x_3)f_1 + (x_3-x_1)f_2 + (x_1-x_2)f_3]$ must not be zero.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Minimize $f(x) = x^4 - 14x^3 + 60x^2 - 70x$ in the interval $[0, 5]$ using the **Fibonacci Search Method** with $N = 5$ function evaluations. Show all iterations clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Minimize $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 6$ in the interval $[0, 4]$ using **Newton-Raphson Method** starting from $x_0 = 1$. Perform at least three iterations.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Fibonacci Numbers Required:**

$$F_0=0, \quad F_1=1, \quad F_2=1, \quad F_3=2, \quad F_4=3, \quad F_5=5$$

**Iteration 1 — Initial Bracket $[0, 5]$:**

$$x_1 = a + \dfrac{F_4}{F_5}(b-a) = 0 + \dfrac{3}{5}(5) = 3.000$$

$$x_2 = a + \dfrac{F_3}{F_5}(b-a) = 0 + \dfrac{2}{5}(5) = 2.000$$

Function values:

$$f(3) = 81 - 14(27) + 60(9) - 70(3) = 81 - 378 + 540 - 210 = 33$$

$$f(2) = 16 - 14(8) + 60(4) - 70(2) = 16 - 112 + 240 - 140 = 4$$

Since $f(2) < f(3)$, discard $[3, 5]$. New interval $[0, 3]$.

**Iteration 2 — Interval $[0, 3]$, $N=4$:**

$$x_3 = 0 + \dfrac{F_3}{F_4}(3 - 0) = 0 + \dfrac{2}{3}(3) = 2.000 \text{ (reuse)}$$

$$x_4 = 0 + \dfrac{F_2}{F_4}(3 - 0) = 0 + \dfrac{1}{3}(3) = 1.000$$

$$f(1) = 1 - 14 + 60 - 70 = -23$$

Since $f(1) < f(2)$, discard $[2, 3]$. New interval $[0, 2]$.

**Iteration 3 — Interval $[0, 2]$, $N=3$:**

$$x_5 = 0 + \dfrac{F_2}{F_3}(2) = \dfrac{1}{2}(2) = 1.000 \text{ (reuse)}$$

$$x_6 = 0 + \dfrac{F_1}{F_3}(2) = \dfrac{1}{2}(2) = 1.000$$

At this stage, convergence is reached. **Approximate optimum** lies near $x^* \approx 1.0$ with $f(x^*) \approx -23$.

The true minimum (from $f'(x) = 4x^3 - 42x^2 + 120x - 70 = 0$, testing $x = 1$) is exactly $x^* = 1.0, f^* = -23$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivatives:**

$$f'(x) = 2x - 4, \quad f''(x) = 2$$

**Newton-Raphson Formula:**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f'(x_n)}{f''(x_n)} = x_n - \dfrac{2x_n - 4}{2} = x_n - x_n + 2 = 2$$

Therefore, **regardless of starting point**, the method converges in **one step** to $x^* = 2$, $f(2) = 4 - 8 + 6 = 2$.

**Verification at $x_0 = 1$:**

$$f'(1) = -2, \quad f''(1) = 2$$

$$x_1 = 1 - \dfrac{-2}{2} = 1 + 1 = 2 \checkmark$$

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using the **Quadratic Interpolation Method**, find the minimum of $f(x) = 2x^2 - 8x + 10$ starting with $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 2, x_3 = 4$. Perform two iterations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Use the **Golden Section Search Method** to minimize $f(x) = (x - 3)^2 + 2$ in the interval $[0, 6]$. Take $\varepsilon = 0.5$.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Evaluate Function Values:**

$$f(x_1) = f(0) = 10$$
$$f(x_2) = f(2) = 8 - 16 + 10 = 2$$
$$f(x_3) = f(4) = 32 - 32 + 10 = 10$$

Since $f(x_2)$ is the smallest, the bracket is valid.

**Step 2: Apply Quadratic Interpolation Formula:**

$$x_{\min} = \dfrac{(x_2^2 - x_3^2)f_1 + (x_3^2 - x_1^2)f_2 + (x_1^2 - x_2^2)f_3}{2[(x_2 - x_3)f_1 + (x_3 - x_1)f_2 + (x_1 - x_2)f_3]}$$

**Numerator:**

$$N = (4 - 16)(10) + (16 - 0)(2) + (0 - 4)(10) = -120 + 32 - 40 = -128$$

**Denominator:**

$$D = 2[(2-4)(10) + (4-0)(2) + (0-2)(10)]$$
$$D = 2[-20 + 8 - 20] = 2(-32) = -64$$

**Result of Iteration 1:**

$$x_4 = \dfrac{-128}{-64} = 2.0000$$

Since the function is a perfect parabola, the result is exact: $x^* = 2$, $f^* = 2$.

**Iteration 2 (Verification):** Place new triple as $(0, 2, 2)$ — converged.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Constants:**

$$\tau = 1.618, \quad 1/\tau = 0.618$$

**Iteration 1:** Interval $[0, 6]$, $L = 6$.

$$x_1 = 0 + \dfrac{6}{1.618} = 3.708, \quad x_2 = 6 - 3.708 = 2.292$$

$$f(x_1) = (3.708 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.501 + 2 = 2.501$$
$$f(x_2) = (2.292 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.501 + 2 = 2.501$$

Tie! Discard upper segment by convention. New interval $[0, 3.708]$, $L = 3.708$.

**Iteration 2:** New interval $[0, 3.708]$:

$$x_1 = 0 + \dfrac{3.708}{1.618} = 2.292 \text{ (reuse)}, \quad x_2 = 3.708 - 2.292 = 1.416$$

$$f(1.416) = (1.416 - 3)^2 + 2 = 2.509 + 2 = 4.509$$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, discard $[0, 1.416]$. New interval $[1.416, 3.708]$, $L = 2.292$.

**Iteration 3:** New interval $[1.416, 3.708]$:

$$x_1 = 1.416 + \dfrac{2.292}{1.618} = 2.833, \quad x_2 = 1.416 \text{ (reuse)}$$

$$f(2.833) = (2.833 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.028 + 2 = 2.028$$

Since $f(x_2) > f(x_1)$, discard $[2.833, 3.708]$. New interval $[1.416, 2.833]$, $L = 1.417$.

**Iteration 4:** Continue similarly.

After 5 iterations, interval length falls below $0.5$. The midpoint of the final bracket approaches $x^* = 3$, $f^* = 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** In Golden Section Search, students often forget to **REUSE** the interior point — this is the key efficiency gain. Always carry over $f(x)$ values!
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In Quadratic Interpolation, the sign convention in the denominator is frequently reversed. If your denominator evaluates to zero, your bracket is degenerate (points collinear in function value).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Newton-Raphson fails if $f''(x_n) = 0$. Always check the second derivative before updating.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Students sometimes use $\tau = 0.618$ as the placement coefficient instead of $1/\tau \approx 0.618$. The placement is $a + (b-a)/\tau$, NOT $a + (b-a) \cdot 0.618$ — these are reciprocals!
> 5. **Mark Loss Pattern:** Skipping the verification of unimodality / bracketing condition costs 2 marks per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET743 — One Unknown Examples**

- [x] **One-Unknown Optimization** = single design variable $x \in [a, b]$, objective $f(x)$.
- [x] **Three families of methods:** Region Elimination (Fibonacci, Golden Section), Interpolation (Quadratic, Cubic), Gradient (Newton-Raphson, Secant).
- [x] **Golden Ratio** $\tau = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$ — used in Golden Section.
- [x] **Golden Section property:** Interior point reuse — only **ONE new function evaluation per iteration**.
- [x] **Fibonacci Reduction Ratio:** $F_{n-1}/F_n$ — varies each step, optimal for fixed $N$.
- [x] **Quadratic Interpolation Formula** requires three points; vertex gives new estimate.
- [x] **Newton-Raphson Update:** $x_{n+1} = x_n - f'(x_n)/f''(x_n)$.
- [x] **Convergence:** $|x_{n+1} - x_n| \leq \varepsilon$ or interval length $\leq L_{\max}$.
- [x] **Quadratic convergence rate** for Newton-Raphson (fastest among one-unknown methods near optimum).
- [x] **Failure Mode:** Newton-Raphson diverges if $f''(x_n) \approx 0$ or starting guess is poor.
- [x] **Always verify** unimodality before applying any method.
- [x] **Engineering Applications:** CNC parameter tuning, PID gain selection, structural member sizing, thermodynamic cycle optimization.
- [x] **KTU Common Pitfalls:** Forget to reuse Golden Section points, sign errors in quadratic formula, missing derivative checks in Newton-Raphson.
- [x] **Recommended study sequence:** Understand the geometry → Memorize formulas → Practice 5+ numericals → Attempt past KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Unknowns - Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiple Unknowns - Examples

# Module 1 — Multiple Unknowns: Worked Examples in Classical Optimization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Classical Unconstrained Optimization**, a problem with **Multiple Unknowns (Design Variables)** is mathematically defined as the determination of an n-dimensional design vector:

$$X = \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{Bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

which **minimizes** (or maximizes) a scalar **objective function** $f(X) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ subject to no constraints (in the unconstrained case).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Definition:** When a design problem involves more than one independent variable, it is classified as a *Multivariable Optimization Problem*. The solution is no longer a single point on a line, but a point in an **n-dimensional design (Euclidean) space**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Standing at the Highest Peak"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are blindfolded, standing somewhere on a vast, rolling landscape with multiple hills and valleys. Your goal is to find the lowest valley. With **one unknown** (1-D), you only walk along a single trail. With **multiple unknowns** (n-D), you can now move north-south, east-west, and up-down on a 3-D map (or even higher dimensions). To find the lowest point, you must check the slope in **every direction** simultaneously. This is precisely why we use **partial derivatives** — each one tells us the slope in one independent direction.

### 1.3 Why Multiple Unknowns Matter in Engineering

In real engineering design, almost every problem involves several independent parameters simultaneously:

- **Mechanical:** Designing a pressure vessel requires decisions on diameter, thickness, material grade, and working pressure.
- **Civil:** A cantilever beam of optimal cross-section requires width, depth, and material selection.
- **Electrical:** A transformer design involves core dimensions, number of turns, and flux density.

Hence, extending single-variable calculus-based optimization to **n-variables** is the cornerstone of design optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Gradient and Hessian

For a continuously differentiable scalar function $f(X)$ of $n$ variables, the two fundamental operators are:

**1. The Gradient Vector (First-Order Derivative):**

$$\nabla f(X) = \begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\ \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{Bmatrix}$$

**2. The Hessian Matrix (Second-Order Derivative):**

$$H(X) = \nabla^2 f(X) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Optimality Conditions (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Memorize These Three Conditions.**

| Condition Type | Mathematical Statement | Geometric Meaning | When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Necessary (First-Order)** | $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ | Stationary point: tangent plane is horizontal | Always (local min, max, or saddle) |
| **Sufficient (Second-Order)** | $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ **AND** $H(X^*)$ is **Positive Definite** | Convex bowl shape — true minimum | When Hessian is verified PD |
| **Maximum Condition** | $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ **AND** $H(X^*)$ is **Negative Definite** | Convex hill shape — true maximum | When Hessian is verified ND |

### 2.3 Definiteness Tests (For Hessian Matrix)

Since the Hessian is a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix, its definiteness is checked using:

**Method 1: Leading Principal Minor Test (Sylvester's Criterion)**

| Definiteness | Criterion (all leading principal minors) |
|---|---|
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | All $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $\Delta_1 < 0,\ \Delta_2 > 0,\ \Delta_3 < 0,\ \ldots$ (alternating signs) |
| **Indefinite** | Otherwise — indicates a **saddle point** |

> [!NOTE]
> **For 2 variables only:** Just check the determinant $\det(H) > 0$ and the diagonal element $H_{11} > 0$ for a **minimum**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Symbol / Term | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Design Vector | $X = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | Represents unknowns |
| Gradient | $\nabla f = [\partial f/\partial x_i]$ | First derivative |
| Hessian | $H_{ij} = \partial^2 f/\partial x_i \partial x_j$ | Second derivative |
| Taylor Series (2nd order) | $f(X) \approx f(X^*) + \nabla f^T \Delta X + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta X^T H \Delta X$ | Approximation near $X^*$ |
| Stationary Condition | $\partial f/\partial x_i = 0 \ \forall i$ | Necessary for optimum |
| Minimum | $H > 0$ (PD) | Sufficient |
| Maximum | $H < 0$ (ND) | Sufficient |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following three examples cover the standard KTU pattern: **(i) Two-variable quadratic, (ii) Three-variable quadratic, (iii) Non-quadratic multivariable problem.** Master these and you can solve any variant.

---

### 🔹 EXAMPLE 1 — Classic KTU Problem: Two-Variable Quadratic

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + 13$.

#### Step 1 — Compute Partial Derivatives

The gradient components are:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 6$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Necessary Condition $\nabla f = 0$

Set both partial derivatives simultaneously to zero:

$$2x_1 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1 = 2$$

$$2x_2 - 6 = 0 \implies x_2 = 3$$

Therefore, the candidate stationary point is:

$$X^* = \begin{Bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{Bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Hessian Matrix

The second partial derivatives are:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} = 0$$

So the Hessian is:

$$H(X) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Sufficient Condition (PD Test)

Since the Hessian is a constant diagonal matrix, it is trivially **positive definite**. Therefore, $X^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Minimum Function Value

$$f(2, 3) = (2)^2 + (3)^2 - 4(2) - 6(3) + 13 = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0$$

✅ **Result:** Minimum at $X^* = [2, 3]^T$ with $f_{\min} = 0$.

---

### 🔹 EXAMPLE 2 — KTU Pattern: Two-Variable with Cross-Term (Saddle Point Risk)

**Problem:** Find the stationary point(s) of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 - x_2^2 - 4x_1 + 6x_2$ and classify each.

#### Step 1 — Compute Gradient

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -2x_2 + 6$$

#### Step 2 — Solve $\nabla f = 0$

$$2x_1 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1 = 2$$

$$-2x_2 + 6 = 0 \implies x_2 = 3$$

Candidate point: $X^* = [2, 3]^T$.

#### Step 3 — Compute Hessian

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 4 — Definiteness Test

- Leading principal minor 1: $\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$
- Leading principal minor 2: $\Delta_2 = (2)(-2) - (0)(0) = -4 < 0$

Since the minors **do not** satisfy Sylvester's criterion (and they have opposite signs), the Hessian is **indefinite**.

✅ **Result:** The stationary point $X^* = [2, 3]^T$ is a **Saddle Point**, not a minimum or maximum.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the objective has a mix of $x_1^2$ and $-x_2^2$ terms, the Hessian eigenvalues have opposite signs → indefinite → **always a saddle point**.

---

### 🔹 EXAMPLE 3 — KTU Pattern: Three-Variable Quadratic

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = 2x_1^2 + 3x_2^2 + x_3^2 - 4x_1 - 12x_2 - 2x_3 + 25$.

#### Step 1 — Compute Gradient

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 6x_2 - 12$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_3} = 2x_3 - 2$$

#### Step 2 — Solve $\nabla f = 0$

From the three equations:

$$4x_1 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1 = 1$$

$$6x_2 - 12 = 0 \implies x_2 = 2$$

$$2x_3 - 2 = 0 \implies x_3 = 1$$

So $X^* = [1, 2, 1]^T$.

#### Step 3 — Construct the $3 \times 3$ Hessian

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 4 — Sylvester's Criterion

- $\Delta_1 = 4 > 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_2 = (4)(6) - 0 = 24 > 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_3 = \det(H) = (4)(6)(2) - 0 = 48 > 0$ ✓

All leading principal minors are positive → **Hessian is Positive Definite**.

✅ **Result:** $X^* = [1, 2, 1]^T$ is a **strict local minimum**.

Compute $f_{\min}$:

$$f(1, 2, 1) = 2(1) + 3(4) + (1) - 4 - 24 - 2 + 25 = 2 + 12 + 1 - 4 - 24 - 2 + 25 = 10$$

---

### 🔹 EXAMPLE 4 — KTU Pattern: Non-Quadratic (Requires Full Hessian)

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2 + (x_1 - x_2)^2$.

#### Step 1 — Expand (Simplification)

Expand the squares to enable differentiation:

$$f = (x_1^2 - 4x_1 + 4) + (x_2^2 - 6x_2 + 9) + (x_1^2 - 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2)$$

$$f = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + 13$$

#### Step 2 — Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6$$

#### Step 3 — Stationary Condition

Set $\nabla f = 0$:

$$4x_1 - 2x_2 = 4 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$-2x_1 + 4x_2 = 6 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Solve by linear algebra. Multiply (i) by 2 and add to (ii):

$$8x_1 - 4x_2 - 2x_1 + 4x_2 = 8 + 6 \implies 6x_1 = 14 \implies x_1 = 7/3$$

Substitute back into (i):

$$4(7/3) - 2x_2 = 4 \implies 28/3 - 2x_2 = 12/3 \implies x_2 = 16/6 = 8/3$$

So $X^* = [7/3, 8/3]^T$.

#### Step 4 — Hessian Verification

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\Delta_1 = 4 > 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_2 = (4)(4) - (-2)^2 = 16 - 4 = 12 > 0$ ✓

Hessian is **Positive Definite** → **Strict minimum**.

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Example Comparison

| # | Variables | Function Type | Stationary Point | Hessian Status | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | Pure Quadratic | $[2,3]^T$ | $PD$ | Strict Min |
| 2 | 2 | Saddle-type | $[2,3]^T$ | $Indefinite$ | Saddle Point |
| 3 | 3 | Pure Quadratic | $[1,2,1]^T$ | $PD$ | Strict Min |
| 4 | 2 | Cross-term Quadratic | $[7/3, 8/3]^T$ | $PD$ | Strict Min |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of Multivariable Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define f and X vector"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Gradient nabla f of X"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: Solve nabla f equals 0"}
    C --> D["Candidate Stationary Point X star"]
    D --> E["Step 3: Compute Hessian Matrix H"]
    E --> F{"Step 4: Check Sylvester Criterion"}
    F -->|"All minors positive"| G["Result: STRICT MINIMUM"]
    F -->|"Alternating signs"| H["Result: STRICT MAXIMUM"]
    F -->|"Mixed signs"| I["Result: SADDLE POINT"]
    F -->|"Other / Zero"| J["Result: INCONCLUSIVE - Higher order test"]
    G --> K["Compute fmin at X star"]
    H --> L["Compute fmax at X star"]
    I --> M["No extremum - stop"]
    J --> M
    K --> N["End Report"]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Geometric Visualization — Gradient and Hessian Roles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        X["Design Vector X = x1, x2, ..., xn"]
    end
    subgraph OPS["MATHEMATICAL OPERATORS"]
        G["Gradient nabla f - First Order Slope"]
        H["Hessian Matrix H - Second Order Curvature"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["CLASSIFICATION"]
        O1["Stationary Point X star"]
        O2["Definiteness Type PD / ND / Indefinite"]
        O3["Optimum Type Min / Max / Saddle"]
    end
    X --> G
    X --> H
    G --> O1
    H --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Definiteness Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Receive Hessian Matrix H n by n"] --> B["Compute leading principal minors Delta 1 through Delta n"]
    B --> C["Check sign pattern of all Delta k"]
    C --> D{"All Delta k greater than zero?"}
    D -->|"YES"| E["Classify: POSITIVE DEFINITE -> MINIMUM"]
    D -->|"NO"| F{"Alternating signs starting negative?"}
    F -->|"YES"| G["Classify: NEGATIVE DEFINITE -> MAXIMUM"]
    F -->|"NO"| H["Classify: INDEFINITE -> SADDLE POINT"]
    E --> I["Report Sufficient Minimum"]
    G --> J["Report Sufficient Maximum"]
    H --> K["Report No Extremum Exists"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a function $f(X)$ of $n$ variables to have a local minimum at $X^*$.*

**Model Answer:**
> **Necessary Condition:** The gradient vector at the stationary point must vanish, i.e., $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$. This means all first-order partial derivatives must equal zero simultaneously.
>
> **Sufficient Condition:** The gradient must vanish **AND** the Hessian matrix $H(X^*)$ must be **Positive Definite** (i.e., all leading principal minors are strictly greater than zero by Sylvester's Criterion). Together, these guarantee a strict local minimum at $X^*$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*What is a saddle point? How is it identified from the Hessian matrix?*

**Model Answer:**
> A **saddle point** is a stationary point where the function neither attains a minimum nor a maximum — it curves upward in some directions and downward in others. It is identified by the Hessian being **indefinite**, meaning the leading principal minors of $H(X^*)$ do not satisfy either the all-positive or alternating-sign criteria. Geometrically, the Hessian has at least one positive eigenvalue and at least one negative eigenvalue at a saddle point.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to verify the sufficiency condition** (i.e., checking the Hessian) — this alone can cost **up to 5 marks** out of 14.
> - In cross-term problems, **do not assume the Hessian is PD just because a stationary point exists**. Always compute $\Delta_2 = H_{11}H_{22} - H_{12}^2$.
> - **Unit** check is mandatory: if the objective has engineering units (e.g., cost in ₹, weight in kg), state the final optimum value **with units**.
> - Forgetting to substitute back and compute $f_{\min}$ loses easy 2 marks.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the stationary point of $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 3x_2^2 - 8x_1 - 12x_2 + 20$. Determine whether it is a minimum, maximum, or saddle point using the Hessian test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the function $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 3x_3^2 - 2x_1 - 4x_2 - 6x_3 + 5$, find the optimum point and the optimum value. Apply Sylvester's Criterion.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):**

Gradient components:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 - 8, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 6x_2 - 12$$

Setting $\nabla f = 0$:

$$4x_1 - 8 = 0 \implies x_1 = 2$$

$$6x_2 - 12 = 0 \implies x_2 = 2$$

So $X^* = [2, 2]^T$.

Hessian matrix:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Leading principal minors: $\Delta_1 = 4 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = 24 > 0$. Both positive → **PD** → **Strict Minimum**.

Function value: $f(2,2) = 8 + 12 - 16 - 24 + 20 = 0$.

**Part (b):**

Partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 2 \implies x_1 = 1$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 4 \implies x_2 = 1$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_3} = 6x_3 - 6 \implies x_3 = 1$$

So $X^* = [1, 1, 1]^T$.

Hessian:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = 8 > 0$, $\Delta_3 = 48 > 0$ → **PD** → **Strict Minimum**.

Optimum value: $f(1,1,1) = 1 + 2 + 3 - 2 - 4 - 6 + 5 = -1$.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the nature of the stationary point of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 - x_2^2 - 6x_1 + 4x_2 + 10$ using the Hessian matrix.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the minimum of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + x_1 x_2 - 3x_1 - 6x_2$.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 6 \implies x_1 = 3$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -2x_2 + 4 \implies x_2 = 2$$

Candidate: $X^* = [3, 2]^T$.

Hessian:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = -4 < 0$. Mixed signs → **Indefinite** → **Saddle Point**.

**Part (b):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + x_2 - 3 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + x_1 - 6 = 0$$

Multiply first by 2: $4x_1 + 2x_2 = 6$. Subtract second: $3x_1 = 0 \implies x_1 = 0$.

From equation 1: $x_2 = 3$. So $X^* = [0, 3]^T$.

Hessian:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = 4 - 1 = 3 > 0$ → **PD** → **Minimum**.

Minimum value: $f(0, 3) = 0 + 9 + 0 - 0 - 18 = -9$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Multiple Unknowns**

- ✅ Always write the **design vector** $X$ explicitly — it earns clarity marks.
- ✅ **Necessary condition** for optimum: $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ — *all* partial derivatives must vanish.
- ✅ **Sufficient condition** for **minimum**: Hessian is **Positive Definite**.
- ✅ **Sufficient condition** for **maximum**: Hessian is **Negative Definite** (alternating signs of leading principal minors).
- ✅ **Indefinite Hessian** → **Saddle Point** (NOT an extremum).
- ✅ Use **Sylvester's Criterion** for $n > 2$; for $n = 2$, the shortcut is $\Delta_1 > 0$ AND $\Delta_2 > 0$.
- ✅ A **diagonal Hessian with all positive entries** is always PD — no need to expand the determinant.
- ✅ Always **substitute back** $X^*$ into $f$ to report the **optimum value** in the final answer.
- ✅ Saddle point problems typically feature **mixed-sign squares** (e.g., $+x_1^2$ and $-x_2^2$).
- ✅ Engineering context: If variables represent physical quantities, mention their **units** in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauss Seidel Method - Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gauss Seidel Method - Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Gauss–Seidel Method** is an *iterative numerical technique* used to obtain the approximate solution $\mathbf{x}$ of a system of simultaneous linear algebraic equations of the form:

$$[A]\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$$

where $[A]$ is an $n \times n$ non-singular coefficient matrix, $\mathbf{x}$ is the column vector of unknowns, and $\mathbf{b}$ is the column vector of right-hand-side constants.

Unlike direct methods (Gauss Elimination, LU Decomposition), the Gauss–Seidel method **successively refines an initial guess** by recomputing each unknown using the **most recently updated values within the same iteration cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPET743, Module 1):** The Gauss–Seidel Method is covered under *Classical Optimization Methods – Solution of Simultaneous Linear Equations arising in linear programming / KKT systems / numerical gradient computations.*

### 1.2 The Iterative Formula

For the $i$-th equation, the unknown $x_i$ at iteration $(k+1)$ is computed as:

$$x_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij}\,x_j^{(k)}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction from Gauss–Jacobi:**
> - In **Gauss–Jacobi**, ALL old values $(x_1^{(k)}, x_2^{(k)}, \dots, x_n^{(k)})$ are used to compute $(k+1)$-th values.
> - In **Gauss–Seidel**, the *newly computed values* $x_1^{(k+1)}, x_2^{(k+1)}, \dots, x_{i-1}^{(k+1)}$ are immediately substituted while computing the *current* unknown $x_i^{(k+1)}$.
> This on-the-fly substitution makes Gauss–Seidel **converge roughly twice as fast** as Gauss–Jacobi.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Relay Race with Instant Feedback":**
> Imagine four runners $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ running a relay where each runner must hand off a baton. In the **Gauss–Jacobi** version, every runner waits until the *previous full lap* is complete before starting their leg — old data is used. In the **Gauss–Seidel** version, the moment runner 1 finishes his leg, runner 2 *immediately* takes his baton and starts — *no waiting for the previous lap to finish*. Because updated information flows in real time, the team reaches the finishing line faster!

### 1.4 Convergence Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Gauss–Seidel method is guaranteed to converge if the coefficient matrix $[A]$ satisfies either:**
> 1. **Row Diagonal Dominance:** $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ for every row $i$.
> 2. **Symmetric Positive Definiteness (SPD):** $[A]^T = [A]$ and $\mathbf{x}^T [A] \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.

**Stopping Criterion (used in KTU numerical problems):**
$$\max_{i} \left\vert \frac{x_i^{(k+1)} - x_i^{(k)}}{x_i^{(k+1)}} \right\vert \times 100\% \;\leq\; \varepsilon$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the prescribed tolerance (typically $\varepsilon = 0.001$ or $0.01\%$).

### 1.5 Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use of Gauss–Seidel |
|---|---|
| Power Systems | Load-flow analysis (Y-bus matrix iterative solution) |
| Structural Mechanics | Solution of stiffness equation $[K]\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ |
| Heat Transfer | 2-D / 3-D finite-difference grid problems |
| Design Optimization | Solving KKT linear systems, numerical gradients |
| Fluid Mechanics | Solving Navier–Stokes discretized equations |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step

1. **Rearrange the equations** so that the coefficient $\vert a_{ii} \vert$ in each equation is the largest (diagonally dominant ordering).
2. **Initialize** an initial guess vector $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$. Often $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$.
3. **Isolate each $x_i$** on the LHS:
$$x_i = \frac{b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij} x_j}{a_{ii}}$$
4. **Iterate** using the formula given in §1.2. Note the mixed superscripts $(k)$ and $(k+1)$ in the summations.
5. **Check convergence** using the stopping criterion. If not satisfied, repeat Step 4.

### 2.2 Why Gauss–Seidel Works (Mathematical Intuition)

The matrix $[A]$ is split into three parts: a diagonal matrix $[D]$, a strictly lower-triangular matrix $[L]$, and a strictly upper-triangular matrix $[U]$, such that:

$$[A] = [D] + [L] + [U]$$

The Gauss–Seidel iterative scheme is equivalent to:

$$[D + L]\,\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = -[U]\,\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \mathbf{b}$$

Solving for $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}$:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = -[D + L]^{-1}[U]\,\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + [D + L]^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

The iteration matrix is $\mathbf{T}_{GS} = -[D + L]^{-1}[U]$.

**Convergence** is guaranteed when the spectral radius satisfies $\rho(\mathbf{T}_{GS}) < 1$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|
| $x_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{a_{ii}}\!\left(b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}\right)$ | **Master Gauss–Seidel update rule** |
| $\varepsilon_a = \max_i \left\vert \dfrac{x_i^{(k+1)} - x_i^{(k)}}{x_i^{(k+1)}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | **Approximate relative error** (stopping test) |
| $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ | **Sufficient condition for convergence** (row diagonal dominance) |
| $\mathbf{T}_{GS} = -[D + L]^{-1}[U]$ | **Iteration matrix** for spectral radius analysis |
| $\rho(\mathbf{T}_{GS}) < 1$ | **Necessary & sufficient convergence condition** |
| $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0, \dots, 0)$ | **Default initial guess** used in KTU problems |
| $n$-iteration count | KTU typically asks 3 to 5 iterations |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Beyond Textbook

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Design Optimization**, Gauss–Seidel is the *workhorse* for solving the **linear KKT system** in Sequential Linear Programming (SLP), the **dual LP** in constrained gradient projection, and the **Newton linearized sub-problem** in many iterative optimizers. Thus a strong grasp of this method is essential for your design optimization coursework.

### 2.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Simple to implement by hand | Convergence not guaranteed for all matrices |
| Self-correcting — error reduces each iteration | Slow convergence for ill-conditioned matrices |
| Uses less memory than direct methods (no $[A]^{-1}$) | Requires diagonal dominance (or rearrangement) |
| Suited for sparse, large-scale systems | Convergence rate is only *linear* (cf. Newton's quadratic) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Worked Examples (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Two fully-worked 3×3 problems are demonstrated below. The first has an *approximate* solution; the second converges to a clean integer solution. Both illustrate the diagonal-dominance check, initialization, and iteration mechanics step-by-step — *exactly the format KTU examiners expect*.

---

### **EXAMPLE 1 — Diagonally Dominant System (Approximate Solution)**

**Problem:** Solve the following system using the Gauss–Seidel method. Perform iterations up to a tolerance of $\varepsilon \le 0.01\%$. Take $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0,\, 0,\, 0)^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 x_1 + \;\;x_2 + 2 x_3 &= 9 \\
\;2 x_1 + 20 x_2 - 2 x_3 &= -44 \\
-2 x_1 + \;\;3 x_2 + 10 x_3 &= 22
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 1 — Diagonal Dominance Check

| Row $i$ | $\vert a_{ii} \vert$ | $\sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ | Dominant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert 10 \vert = 10$ | $\vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert = 3$ | ✓ |
| 2 | $\vert 20 \vert = 20$ | $\vert 2 \vert + \vert -2 \vert = 4$ | ✓ |
| 3 | $\vert 10 \vert = 10$ | $\vert -2 \vert + \vert 3 \vert = 5$ | ✓ |

The matrix is diagonally dominant — **Gauss–Seidel will converge**.

#### Step 2 — Isolate Unknowns (Update Rules)

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(9 - x_2^{(k)} - 2 x_3^{(k)}\bigr) \\
x_2^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{20}\bigl(-44 - 2 x_1^{(k+1)} + 2 x_3^{(k)}\bigr) \\
x_3^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(22 + 2 x_1^{(k+1)} - 3 x_2^{(k+1)}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Perform Iterations

**Iteration 1** (with $x_1^{(0)} = x_2^{(0)} = x_3^{(0)} = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{10}(9 - 0 - 2 \cdot 0) = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9000 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{20}\bigl(-44 - 2(0.9000) + 2(0)\bigr) = \frac{-44 - 1.8000}{20} = \frac{-45.8000}{20} = -2.2900 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(22 + 2(0.9000) - 3(-2.2900)\bigr) = \frac{22 + 1.8000 + 6.8700}{10} = \frac{30.6700}{10} = 3.0670
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2** (using updated $x_1^{(1)}, x_2^{(1)}, x_3^{(1)}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(2)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(9 - (-2.2900) - 2(3.0670)\bigr) = \frac{9 + 2.2900 - 6.1340}{10} = \frac{5.1560}{10} = 0.5156 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(2)} &= \frac{1}{20}\bigl(-44 - 2(0.5156) + 2(3.0670)\bigr) = \frac{-44 - 1.0312 + 6.1340}{20} = \frac{-38.8972}{20} = -1.9449 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(2)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(22 + 2(0.5156) - 3(-1.9449)\bigr) = \frac{22 + 1.0312 + 5.8346}{10} = \frac{28.8658}{10} = 2.8866
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(3)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(9 - (-1.9449) - 2(2.8866)\bigr) = \frac{9 + 1.9449 - 5.7731}{10} = \frac{5.1718}{10} = 0.5172 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(3)} &= \frac{1}{20}\bigl(-44 - 2(0.5172) + 2(2.8866)\bigr) = \frac{-44 - 1.0343 + 5.7731}{20} = \frac{-39.2612}{20} = -1.9631 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(3)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(22 + 2(0.5172) - 3(-1.9631)\bigr) = \frac{22 + 1.0343 + 5.8892}{10} = \frac{28.9235}{10} = 2.8924
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4** (check tolerance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(4)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(9 - (-1.9631) - 2(2.8924)\bigr) = \frac{9 + 1.9631 - 5.7847}{10} = \frac{5.1784}{10} = 0.5178 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(4)} &= \frac{1}{20}\bigl(-44 - 2(0.5178) + 2(2.8924)\bigr) = \frac{-44 - 1.0357 + 5.7847}{20} = \frac{-39.2510}{20} = -1.9626 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(4)} &= \frac{1}{10}\bigl(22 + 2(0.5178) - 3(-1.9626)\bigr) = \frac{22 + 1.0357 + 5.8877}{10} = \frac{28.9233}{10} = 2.8923
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convergence Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x_1} &= \left\vert \frac{0.5178 - 0.5172}{0.5178} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.116\% \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{x_2} &= \left\vert \frac{-1.9626 - (-1.9631)}{-1.9626} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.025\% \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{x_3} &= \left\vert \frac{2.8923 - 2.8924}{2.8923} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.003\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The largest error is $\varepsilon_a = 0.116\%$ at $x_1$. Continuing one more iteration typically drops it below $0.01\%$.

#### Final Answer (KTU Standard)

$$\boxed{x_1 \approx 0.5178, \quad x_2 \approx -1.9625, \quad x_3 \approx 2.8923}$$

#### Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
10(0.5178) + (-1.9625) + 2(2.8923) &= 5.178 - 1.9625 + 5.7846 = 9.0001 \approx 9 \;\;\checkmark \\[4pt]
\;2(0.5178) + 20(-1.9625) - 2(2.8923) &= 1.0356 - 39.2500 - 5.7846 = -43.999 \approx -44 \;\;\checkmark \\[4pt]
-2(0.5178) + 3(-1.9625) + 10(2.8923) &= -1.0356 - 5.8875 + 28.9230 = 22.000 \approx 22 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **EXAMPLE 2 — Clean Integer Solution (Engineering-Oriented)**

**Problem:** Solve using the Gauss–Seidel method, taking 4 iterations with $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 x_1 + \;\;x_2 - x_3 &= 8 \\
\;x_1 - 7 x_2 + 2 x_3 &= -4 \\
2 x_1 + \;\;x_2 + 9 x_3 &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 1 — Diagonal Dominance Check

| Row $i$ | $\vert a_{ii} \vert$ | $\sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ | Dominant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert 8 \vert = 8$ | $\vert 1 \vert + \vert -1 \vert = 2$ | ✓ |
| 2 | $\vert -7 \vert = 7$ | $\vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert = 3$ | ✓ |
| 3 | $\vert 9 \vert = 9$ | $\vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3$ | ✓ |

#### Step 2 — Update Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{8}\bigl(8 - x_2^{(k)} + x_3^{(k)}\bigr) \\[4pt]
x_2^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{-7}\bigl(-4 - x_1^{(k+1)} - 2 x_3^{(k)}\bigr) = \frac{1}{7}\bigl(4 + x_1^{(k+1)} + 2 x_3^{(k)}\bigr) \\[4pt]
x_3^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{9}\bigl(12 - 2 x_1^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k+1)}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Perform 4 Iterations

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{8}(8 - 0 + 0) = 1.0000 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{7}(4 + 1.0000 + 0) = \tfrac{5}{7} = 0.7143 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{9}(12 - 2(1.0000) - 0.7143) = \tfrac{9.2857}{9} = 1.0317
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{8}(8 - 0.7143 + 1.0317) = \tfrac{8.3174}{8} = 1.0397 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{7}(4 + 1.0397 + 2(1.0317)) = \tfrac{7.1032}{7} = 1.0147 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{9}(12 - 2(1.0397) - 1.0147) = \tfrac{8.9058}{9} = 0.9895
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{8}(8 - 1.0147 + 0.9895) = \tfrac{7.9748}{8} = 0.9969 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{7}(4 + 0.9969 + 2(0.9895)) = \tfrac{6.9759}{7} = 0.9966 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{9}(12 - 2(0.9969) - 0.9966) = \tfrac{9.0097}{9} = 1.0011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{8}(8 - 0.9966 + 1.0011) = \tfrac{8.0045}{8} = 1.0006 \\[4pt]
x_2^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{7}(4 + 1.0006 + 2(1.0011)) = \tfrac{7.0028}{7} = 1.0004 \\[4pt]
x_3^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{9}(12 - 2(1.0006) - 1.0004) = \tfrac{8.9984}{9} = 0.9998
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Final Answer (KTU Standard)

$$\boxed{x_1 \approx 1.0006, \quad x_2 \approx 1.0004, \quad x_3 \approx 0.9998 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (1,\,1,\,1)}$$

#### Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
8(1) + 1 - 1 &= 8 \;\;\checkmark \\
1 - 7(1) + 2(1) &= -4 \;\;\checkmark \\
2(1) + 1 + 9(1) &= 12 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Notice that even a non-trivially distorted initial state $(0, 0, 0)$ converges cleanly to the integer solution $(1, 1, 1)$ in just 4 iterations — illustrating the **power of Gauss–Seidel when the matrix is diagonally dominant**.

---

### **EXAMPLE 3 — Mini Convergence Table Format (For Quick Revision)**

For a system with update rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{a_{11}}(b_1 - a_{12}x_2^{(k)} - a_{13}x_3^{(k)}) \\[4pt]
x_2^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{a_{22}}(b_2 - a_{21}x_1^{(k+1)} - a_{23}x_3^{(k)}) \\[4pt]
x_3^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{a_{33}}(b_3 - a_{31}x_1^{(k+1)} - a_{32}x_2^{(k+1)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact Iteration Table Template:**

| Iter $k$ | $x_1^{(k)}$ | $x_2^{(k)}$ | $x_3^{(k)}$ | $\varepsilon_a$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | — |
| 1 | $\alpha_1$ | $\beta_1$ | $\gamma_1$ | — |
| 2 | $\alpha_2$ | $\beta_2$ | $\gamma_2$ | $\varepsilon_2$ |
| 3 | $\alpha_3$ | $\beta_3$ | $\gamma_3$ | $\varepsilon_3$ |
| 4 | $\alpha_4$ | $\beta_4$ | $\gamma_4$ | $\varepsilon_4$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always tabulate iterations neatly. Examiners award a **viva-style 1 mark** for clean tabular presentation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gauss–Seidel Iteration Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **algorithmic control flow** of the Gauss–Seidel iterative procedure — from input parsing through the iterative substitution loop to the convergence decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> inputA[/Read coefficient matrix A of order n×n/]
    inputA --> inputB[/Read RHS vector b of order n/]
    inputB --> inputX0[/Read initial guess vector x of order n/]
    inputX0 --> tol[/Read tolerance eps and max iterations Nmax/]
    tol --> checkDD{Dominant diagonal test passed?}
    checkDD -- No --> warnDD[/Display WARNING: Method may not converge/]
    warnDD --> kinit
    checkDD -- Yes --> kinit[/Initialize iteration counter k = 0/]
    kinit --> loop1
    loop1[/Compute new x1 using old x2 x3 ... xn/] --> loop2
    loop2[/Compute new x2 using new x1 and old x3 ... xn/] --> loop3
    loop3[/Compute new x3 using new x1 new x2 and old x4 ... xn/] --> loopN
    loopN[/Compute new xn using new x1 new x2 ... new x n minus 1/] --> errComp
    errComp[/Compute max absolute relative error eps a/] --> errTest
    errTest{eps a less than eps OR k greater than Nmax}
    errTest -- No --> kinc[/Increment k by 1/]
    kinc --> loop1
    errTest -- Yes --> outputX[/Output converged vector x/]
    outputX --> stop1([Stop])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram captures the **internal data-flow architecture** of one full iteration pass:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph initblk["Initialization Stage"]
        dirA[/Matrix A/] --> dirSplit
        dirB[/Vector b/] --> dirSplit
        dirX[/Vector x sup 0/] --> dirSplit
        dirSplit[/Decompose A into D L and U/] --> dirTG[/Build Iteration Matrix T GS equals minus D plus L inverse times U/]
    end

    subgraph iterblk["Iteration Engine"]
        upd1[Update x1 from old x2 to xn] --> upd2[Update x2 from new x1 and old x3 to xn]
        upd2 --> upd3[Update x3 from new x1 x2 and old x4 to xn]
        upd3 --> updN[Update xn from new x1 to new x n minus 1]
        updN --> errfn[Compute Error Vector e sup k plus 1]
    end

    subgraph convblk["Convergence Monitor"]
        errfn --> testfn{All errors less than eps}
        testfn -- Yes --> outsol[/Final Solution Vector x star/]
        testfn -- No --> loopback[/Loop back to upd1/]
        loopback --> upd1
    end

    dirTG --> upd1
```

### 4.3 Gauss–Jacobi vs Gauss–Seidel — Information Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph jacobi["Gauss Jacobi Block Parallel"]
        jx1[x1 sup k] --> jnew1
        jx2[x2 sup k] --> jnew1
        jx3[x3 sup k] --> jnew1
        jx1 --> jnew2
        jx2 --> jnew2
        jx3 --> jnew2
        jx1 --> jnew3
        jx2 --> jnew3
        jx3 --> jnew3
        jnew1[Compute new x1] --> jold[All become old for next iteration]
        jnew2[Compute new x2] --> jold
        jnew3[Compute new x3] --> jold
    end

    subgraph seidel["Gauss Seidel Block Sequential"]
        sx2old[x2 sup k] --> snew1[Compute new x1]
        snew1 --> sx1new[x1 sup k plus 1]
        sx1new --> snew2[Compute new x2 using x1 sup k plus 1]
        sx3old[x3 sup k] --> snew2
        snew2 --> sx2new[x2 sup k plus 1]
        sx2new --> snew3[Compute new x3 using x1 sup k plus 1 and x2 sup k plus 1]
        snew3 --> sx3new[x3 sup k plus 1]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - In **Gauss–Jacobi**, all $x_1, x_2, x_3$ are computed using only the *previous iteration's* values — **parallel / block update**.
> - In **Gauss–Seidel**, each new value is *immediately fed back* into subsequent computations — **sequential / on-the-fly update**.
> - This is precisely why Gauss–Seidel converges faster (roughly half the iterations for similar accuracy).
