## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Newton's divided difference. Write the general Newton's divided difference interpolation formula for $(n+1)$ data points.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Newton's Divided Difference is a method of polynomial interpolation suitable for **unequally spaced** data points. It uses a recursive computation of successive finite quotients of function values, organized in a triangular divided-difference table.

$$P_n(x) = f[x_0] + (x - x_0)f[x_0, x_1] + (x - x_0)(x - x_1)f[x_0, x_1, x_2] + \cdots + (x - x_0)\cdots(x - x_{n-1})f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n]$$

The recursive formula is:

$$f[x_i, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{i+k}] = \frac{f[x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{i+k}] - f[x_i, \ldots, x_{i+k-1}]}{x_{i+k} - x_i}$$

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + General formula (1 mark) + Recursive DD formula (1 mark).

---

**Q2. State any three important properties of divided differences.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Symmetry:** $f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n] = f[x_{\sigma(0)}, x_{\sigma(1)}, \ldots, x_{\sigma(n)}]$ — divided differences are invariant under permutation of their arguments.
2. **Zero property for polynomials:** If $f(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $\le k$, then $f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{k+1}] = 0$ identically.
3. **Order reduction:** The divided difference of $f(x)$ of order $n$ equals the coefficient of $x^n$ in the Newton interpolation polynomial and is the unique value approximating $\dfrac{f^{(n)}(\xi)}{n!}$ for some $\xi$ in the interval.
4. (Bonus) **Additivity:** $f[x_0, \ldots, x_n] = \dfrac{1}{x_n - x_0}\left(f[x_1, \ldots, x_n] - f[x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1}]\right)$.

> **Mark distribution:** Three properties × 1 mark each.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** The following data is given for a function $f(x)$:

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | 0 | 5 | 36 | 91 |

Construct the divided difference table. Using Newton's divided difference formula, find the unique polynomial passing through the given points. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the polynomial obtained in part (a), estimate $f(4)$ and determine whether the polynomial is exact for $f(x) = x^3 + 2x - 3$. Justify your answer using divided-difference theory. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Building the Divided Difference Table:**

First-order DDs:
- $f[x_0,x_1] = \dfrac{5-0}{2-1} = 5$
- $f[x_1,x_2] = \dfrac{36-5}{5-2} = \dfrac{31}{3}$
- $f[x_2,x_3] = \dfrac{91-36}{6-5} = 55$

Second-order DDs:
- $f[x_0,x_1,x_2] = \dfrac{\frac{31}{3} - 5}{5-1} = \dfrac{\frac{16}{3}}{4} = \dfrac{4}{3}$
- $f[x_1,x_2,x_3] = \dfrac{55 - \frac{31}{3}}{6-2} = \dfrac{\frac{134}{3}}{4} = \dfrac{67}{6}$

Third-order DD:
- $f[x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3] = \dfrac{\frac{67}{6} - \frac{4}{3}}{6-1} = \dfrac{\frac{59}{6}}{5} = \dfrac{59}{30}$

Table:

| $x_i$ | $f[x_i]$ | 1st | 2nd | 3rd |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **0** |  |  |  |
|  |  | **5** |  |  |
| **2** | 5 |  | **4/3** |  |
|  |  | 31/3 |  | **59/30** |
| **5** | 36 |  | 67/6 |  |
|  |  | 55 |  |  |
| **6** | 91 |  |  |  |

Polynomial:

$$P_3(x) = 0 + 5(x-1) + \frac{4}{3}(x-1)(x-2) + \frac{59}{30}(x-1)(x-2)(x-5)$$

**(b) Estimate at $x = 4$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_3(4) &= 5(3) + \frac{4}{3}(3)(2) + \frac{59}{30}(3)(2)(-1) \\
&= 15 + 8 + \frac{59}{30}(-6) \\
&= 23 - \frac{354}{30} \\
&= 23 - 11.8 \\
&= 11.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification against $f(x) = x^3 + 2x - 3$:**

- $f(1) = 1 + 2 - 3 = 0$ ✓
- $f(2) = 8 + 4 - 3 = 9$ ✗ (given as 5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since $f(2)$ from the candidate function **does not match** the given data point $(2, 5)$, the polynomial $P_3(x)$ is **NOT** the exact $f(x)$. It is merely the unique cubic interpolating polynomial of degree **at most 3** that passes through the four given data points. The 3rd-order DD is non-zero, confirming $P_3(x)$ is a genuine cubic (not lower order).

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)**

**(a)** Given the tabulated values:

| $x$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 4 | 26 | 58 | 194 | 466 |

Use Newton's divided difference interpolation formula to find $y$ when $x = 6$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Show that the n-th divided difference of $x^n$ is **identically 1**, using Newton's forward divided difference. State and prove the symmetry property of divided differences. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Divided Difference Table Construction:**

First-order DDs:
- $f[x_0,x_1] = \frac{26-4}{2} = 11$
- $f[x_1,x_2] = \frac{58-26}{1} = 32$
- $f[x_2,x_3] = \frac{194-58}{2} = 68$
- $f[x_3,x_4] = \frac{466-194}{2} = 136$

Second-order DDs:
- $f[x_0,x_1,x_2] = \frac{32-11}{3} = 7$
- $f[x_1,x_2,x_3] = \frac{68-32}{3} = 12$
- $f[x_2,x_3,x_4] = \frac{136-68}{4} = 17$

Third-order DDs:
- $f[x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3] = \frac{12-7}{5} = 1$
- $f[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4] = \frac{17-12}{5} = 1$

Fourth-order DD:
- $f[x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4] = \frac{1-1}{7} = 0$

Leading diagonal: $4, 11, 7, 1, 0$

Newton's polynomial:

$$P_4(x) = 4 + 11x + 7x(x-2) + 1 \cdot x(x-2)(x-3) + 0$$

Evaluating at $x = 6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_4(6) &= 4 + 11(6) + 7(6)(4) + (6)(4)(3) \\
&= 4 + 66 + 168 + 72 \\
&= 310
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check the structure:** Since the 4th DD vanishes, the underlying function is **at most cubic**. Indeed, $y = 4 + 11x + 7x^2 + x^3$ gives $y(6) = 4 + 66 + 252 + 216 = 538$... wait — recalculate carefully:
> $P_4(x) = 4 + 11x + 7x(x-2) + x(x-2)(x-3) = 4 + 11x + 7x^2 - 14x + x^3 - 5x^2 + 6x = x^3 + 2x^2 + 3x + 4$
> $P_4(6) = 216 + 72 + 18 + 4 = 310$ ✓

**(b) Theoretical Proofs:**

**Theorem 1:** The $n$-th divided difference of $f(x) = x^n$ is identically **1**.

*Proof by induction:*

**Base case ($n=1$):** $f[x_i, x_{i+1}] = \dfrac{x_{i+1} - x_i}{x_{i+1} - x_i} = 1$ ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume the $(n-1)$-th DD of $x^{n-1}$ is **1**. Now consider the $n$-th DD of $x^n$:

Using the **Leibniz-style** expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n] &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{x_k^n}{\prod_{j \ne k}(x_k - x_j)} \\
&= \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{k=0}^{n} x_k^n \cdot \frac{n!}{\prod_{j \ne k}(x_k - x_j)}
\end{aligned}$$

The quantity $\dfrac{n!}{\prod_{j\ne k}(x_k - x_j)}$ is the $k$-th **Lagrange basis polynomial coefficient**, whose sum over $k$ of $x_k^n$ recovers the **leading coefficient of the unique degree-$n$ polynomial interpolant of $x^n$ at $n+1$ points**. Since $x^n$ is itself degree $n$, the interpolant IS $x^n$, whose leading coefficient is **1**. $\blacksquare$

**Theorem 2 (Symmetry Property):** $f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n]$ is invariant under any permutation of its arguments.

*Proof sketch:* The divided difference equals the coefficient of $x^n$ in the unique degree-$n$ interpolating polynomial of $f$ at $\{x_0, \ldots, x_n\}$. The unique polynomial does not depend on the order in which we label its nodes, hence its leading coefficient (which equals the $n$-th DD) is **symmetric**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU exams:**
> 
> 1. **Order reversal in denominator:** Many students write $\dfrac{f[x_{i+1}] - f[x_i]}{x_i - x_{i+1}}$ instead of $x_{i+1} - x_i$. This gives the *wrong sign* and loses **2-3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the table:** Always draw the full table with all entries — even the obvious ones. Examiners allocate **1 mark** just for the table structure.
> 
> 3. **Reading wrong diagonal:** You must read the **TOP entry** of every column (the leading diagonal), not the bottom. Marks deducted: **2**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to evaluate** the final polynomial at the requested $x$ value. The polynomial alone is incomplete — you must compute $P_n(x_{\text{query}})$.
> 
> 5. **Confusing NDD with Lagrange:** When asked specifically for Newton's method, do NOT give the Lagrange form. They are *equivalent* but earn **zero credit** if the question specifies a method.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ Newton's Divided Difference = polynomial interpolation for **unequally spaced** data.
- ✅ Recursion formula: $f[x_i, \ldots, x_{i+k}] = \dfrac{f[x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{i+k}] - f[x_i, \ldots, x_{i+k-1}]}{x_{i+k} - x_i}$.
- ✅ Always **build the table first** before writing the polynomial.
- ✅ The **leading diagonal** of the table gives the coefficients of the Newton basis $(x-x_0), (x-x_0)(x-x_1), \ldots$
- ✅ **Symmetry** = order of $x$'s doesn't matter; **Zero property** = n-th DD of degree-$\le n$ polynomial is **0** beyond order $n$.
- ✅ NDD → reduces to Newton's forward/backward formula **when points are equally spaced** (the famous KTU trick).
- ✅ Error term: $E(x) = f[x, x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n] \prod_{j=0}^{n}(x - x_j)$.
- ✅ Special identity: $n$-th DD of $x^n$ equals **1**; $n$-th DD of $x^{n-1}$ equals $\sum \dfrac{1}{x - x_j}$.
- ✅ Numerical evaluation: use **Horner's nested form** to reduce round-off: $P_n(x) = f[x_0] + (x - x_0)(f[x_0,x_1] + (x - x_1)(f[x_0,x_1,x_2] + \ldots))$.
- ✅ Engineer's mental check: if 2nd-order DDs are roughly constant → function is **quadratic**; if 3rd-order are constant → **cubic**, etc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Square Regression - Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Least Square Regression - Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: LEAST SQUARE REGRESSION - THEORY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Least Square Regression (LSR)** is a deterministic statistical-mathematical optimization technique used to determine the **best-fitting curve** through a set of experimental or empirical data points by **minimizing the sum of the squares of the vertical deviations** (residuals) between the observed values and the values predicted by the approximating function.

Mathematically, for a given dataset $\{(x_i, y_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, the objective of the Least Square method is to find the coefficients of a chosen model $f(x, \mathbf{a})$ such that the scalar objective function $S(\mathbf{a})$ is minimized:

$$S(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - f(x_i, \mathbf{a}) \right]^2$$

where $\mathbf{a} = [a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_m]^T$ is the vector of unknown model coefficients to be determined.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are an architect plotting the relationship between the *compressive strength* of concrete (y-axis) and the *curing time in days* (x-axis) from 20 different construction sites. The data points will be scattered because of human error, weather changes, and material variation. You now want to draw **one single straight line** that best represents the underlying trend.

> **The "Least Square" Promise:** Rather than letting the line pass *close* to the points, it mathematically guarantees the line such that the **sum of the squared vertical distances** from all the data points to the line is the **absolute minimum** possible. Squaring penalizes large errors heavily, making the fit robust against outliers of average error.

### 1.3 Classification of Least Square Regression Models

| **Regression Type** | **Mathematical Form** | **Application Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear (1st order)** | $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ | Simple two-variable trend analysis |
| **Polynomial (m-th order)** | $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_m x^m$ | Curvilinear engineering data |
| **Multiple Linear** | $y = a_0 + a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 + \cdots + a_k x_k$ | Multivariable design problems |
| **Non-Linear (General)** | $y = f(x, \mathbf{a})$ where $f$ is non-linear in $\mathbf{a}$ | Heat transfer, kinetics, fatigue |

### 1.4 Underlying Assumptions (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET743 syllabus, students are expected to remember the following **four classical assumptions** of the Gauss-Ordinary Least Square (OLS) method:
> 1. The independent variable $x$ is **measured without error** (or with negligible error).
> 2. The dependent variable $y$ is a **random variable** with normally distributed errors.
> 3. The variance of residuals is **constant** (Homoscedasticity).
> 4. The expected mean of the residuals is **zero**: $E[\epsilon_i] = 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Optimization Philosophy

The Least Square method fundamentally solves an **unconstrained optimization problem** of the form:

$$\min_{\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{m+1}} S(\mathbf{a}) = \min_{\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{m+1}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^2(\mathbf{a})$$

where $r_i(\mathbf{a}) = y_i - f(x_i, \mathbf{a})$ is termed the **i-th residual**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

- **Step 1 — Model Selection:** Choose an *a priori* functional form $f(x, \mathbf{a})$ (e.g., linear, quadratic) that is expected to describe the underlying physical behavior.
- **Step 2 — Residual Definition:** Compute the deviation $r_i = y_i - f(x_i, \mathbf{a})$ for every data point.
- **Step 3 — Scalar Objective Formation:** Form the *sum of squared residuals* $S(\mathbf{a}) = \sum r_i^2$. This converts a curve-fitting problem into a formal **mathematical programming problem**.
- **Step 4 — First Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** Because the model is linear in $\mathbf{a}$ and the objective is quadratic, the problem is **convex**. The gradient $\nabla S(\mathbf{a})$ must vanish:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_j} = 0, \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \ldots, m$$

- **Step 5 — Solve Linear System:** This yields a set of $(m+1)$ **normal equations** that are linear in the unknowns. The solution is unique provided the regressor matrix is full rank.
- **Step 6 — Verify Goodness-of-Fit:** Quantify how well the fitted model represents the data using metrics like $R^2$ or the Standard Error of Estimate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete reference** for any numerical problem on Least Square Regression asked in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.

| **Formula** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Objective Function | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Sum of Squared Residuals to be minimized |
| Linear Model | $\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x$ | First-order (straight line) regression model |
| Normal Eq. 1 (Linear) | $\sum y_i = n a_0 + a_1 \sum x_i$ | First necessary condition |
| Normal Eq. 2 (Linear) | $\sum x_i y_i = a_0 \sum x_i + a_1 \sum x_i^2$ | Second necessary condition |
| Slope | $a_1 = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | Slope of best-fit line |
| Intercept | $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$ | y-intercept of best-fit line |
| Mean Values | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i$ | Sample means |
| Slope (Alt. Form) | $a_1 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Convenient computational form |
| Polynomial Model (m) | $\hat{y} = \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x^j$ | General m-th order polynomial |
| Polynomial Normal Eq. | $\sum y_i x_i^k = \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j \sum x_i^{j+k}$ | For $k = 0, 1, \ldots, m$ |
| Multiple Regression | $\hat{y} = a_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} a_j x_j$ | Multivariable model |
| Matrix Form | $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Compact normal equations |
| Solution Vector | $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form least squares solution |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{S_{res}}{S_{tot}} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Goodness of fit $(0 \leq R^2 \leq 1)$ |
| Standard Error of Estimate | $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n - 2}}$ | For linear model (2 parameters) |

> **Note on units:** In the KTU context, $x_i$ and $y_i$ are the *given* physical quantities (e.g., $^\circ$C, MPa, m, s) — the regression coefficients $a_j$ inherit units from the ratio of $y$ to $x^j$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Design:** Fitting S-N (Wöhler) fatigue curves to predict endurance limits of metals.
- **Civil Engineering:** Correlating concrete mix ratios with 28-day compressive strength.
- **Aerospace:** Drag coefficient modeling as a function of Mach number and angle of attack.
- **Thermal Systems:** Experimental determination of heat transfer coefficients in transient cooling.
- **Manufacturing:** Statistical calibration of CNC machine tool wear versus time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Regression: $y = a_0 + a_1 x$

**Given:** $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. The fitted model is $\hat{y}_i = a_0 + a_1 x_i$.

**Step 1 — Form the sum of squared residuals:**
$$S(a_0, a_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - (a_0 + a_1 x_i) \right]^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared binomial term** using $(A - B)^2 = A^2 - 2AB + B^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(a_0, a_1) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i^2 - 2 y_i (a_0 + a_1 x_i) + (a_0 + a_1 x_i)^2 \right] \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 - 2 a_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - 2 a_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_0^2 + 2 a_0 a_1 x_i + a_1^2 x_i^2) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 - 2 a_0 \sum y_i - 2 a_1 \sum x_i y_i + n a_0^2 + 2 a_0 a_1 \sum x_i + a_1^2 \sum x_i^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the First Order Necessary Conditions (FONC):**
$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $a_0$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} &= -2 \sum y_i + 2 n a_0 + 2 a_1 \sum x_i = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \sum y_i &= n a_0 + a_1 \sum x_i \quad \text{...(Normal Equation 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $a_1$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} &= -2 \sum x_i y_i + 2 a_0 \sum x_i + 2 a_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \sum x_i y_i &= a_0 \sum x_i + a_1 \sum x_i^2 \quad \text{...(Normal Equation 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the resulting 2 × 2 linear system using Cramer's rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \begin{vmatrix} n & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{vmatrix} = n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2 \\[6pt]
\Delta_{a_1} &= \begin{vmatrix} n & \sum y_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i y_i \end{vmatrix} = n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-form solutions:**

$$
\boxed{\; a_1 = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; a_0 = \dfrac{\sum y_i - a_1 \sum x_i}{n} = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x} \;}
$$

**Step 8 — Second-Order Sufficiency Test:** The Hessian matrix of $S$ is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a_0^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a_0 \partial a_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a_1 \partial a_0} & \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a_1^2} \end{bmatrix} = 2 \begin{bmatrix} n & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is **positive definite** since $\Delta = n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 \geq 0$ by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (with equality only if all $x_i$ are identical — a case KTU examiners love to flag). Hence, the critical point is the **global minimum**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polynomial Regression: $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_m x^m$

**General form:** $\hat{y}_i = \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x_i^j$ where the number of unknowns is $(m+1)$.

**Step 1 — Form the objective:**
$$S(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x_i^j \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply FONC for each coefficient $a_k$ ($k = 0, 1, \ldots, m$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_k} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x_i^j \right) x_i^k = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i x_i^k &= \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{j+k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the structure:** This is a system of $(m+1)$ linear equations in $(m+1)$ unknowns. In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
n & \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 & \cdots & \sum x_i^m \\
\sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 & \sum x_i^3 & \cdots & \sum x_i^{m+1} \\
\sum x_i^2 & \sum x_i^3 & \sum x_i^4 & \cdots & \sum x_i^{m+2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\sum x_i^m & \sum x_i^{m+1} & \sum x_i^{m+2} & \cdots & \sum x_i^{2m}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_m \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} \sum y_i \\ \sum x_i y_i \\ \sum x_i^2 y_i \\ \vdots \\ \sum x_i^m y_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compact notation** using the regressor matrix $\mathbf{X}$:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 & x_1^2 & \cdots & x_1^m \\ 1 & x_2 & x_2^2 & \cdots & x_2^m \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_n & x_n^2 & \cdots & x_n^m \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}$$

The compact **normal equation** is $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \, \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$, whose solution is $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$, provided $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is non-singular.

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution for KTU 2024 Students:** A polynomial of degree $m$ fitted to $n$ data points will **always pass exactly through all $n$ points** if $m = n-1$ (interpolation). For meaningful regression, KTU examiners expect $m \ll n$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multiple Linear Regression: $y = a_0 + a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 + \cdots + a_k x_k$

**Step 1 — Define the regressor matrix with $k$ independent variables:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{n \times (k+1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1k} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2k} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{nk} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Residuals vector:** $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a}$

**Step 3 — Objective in matrix form:**
$$S(\mathbf{a}) = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{r} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a})$$

**Step 4 — Expand and differentiate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S(\mathbf{a}) &= \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2 \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \, \mathbf{a} \\[4pt]
\nabla_{\mathbf{a}} S &= -2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \, \mathbf{a} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Closed-form result (KTB Board favorite):**

$$
\boxed{\; \mathbf{a} = \left(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \;}
$$

The matrix $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T$ is the celebrated **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse** $\mathbf{X}^{+}$, which gives the optimal solution even when $\mathbf{X}$ is not square (i.e., when $n > k+1$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coefficient of Determination $R^2$

**Step 1 — Define Total Sum of Squares (SST):** $S_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$

**Step 2 — Define Residual Sum of Squares (SSR):** $S_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$

**Step 3 — Define Regression Sum of Squares:** $S_{reg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$

**Step 4 — Fundamental identity:**
$$S_{tot} = S_{reg} + S_{res}$$

**Step 5 — Define $R^2$:**
$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{S_{res}}{S_{tot}} = \frac{S_{reg}}{S_{tot}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $R^2 = 1$ indicates a **perfect fit**; $R^2 = 0$ means the model is **no better than the mean**; negative $R^2$ implies the model is **worse than the trivial mean predictor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Block Diagram of the Least Square Regression Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Collect Empirical Data points x_i, y_i] --> B[Step 1: Select Regression Model f of x, a]
    B --> C[Step 2: Formulate Objective S of a = Sum of y_i minus f of x_i, a squared]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Partial Derivatives dS by da_j = 0 for j = 0 to m]
    D --> E[Step 4: Set Up Normal Equations Matrix Form X transpose X a = X transpose y]
    E --> F{Step 5: Is X transpose X Invertible?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 6: Solve for Coefficient Vector a = X inverse X transpose y]
    F -- No --> H[Step 6 alt: Use SVD or Pseudoinverse for Rank Deficient Case]
    G --> I[Step 7: Compute Predicted Values y hat i = f of x_i, a]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 8: Evaluate Goodness of Fit R squared and Standard Error s_e]
    J --> K[Step 9: Validate Model using Residuals and Physical Reasoning]
    K --> L[STOP: Final Regression Equation and Statistical Report]
```

## 4.2 Architecture Mapping: Linear vs. Polynomial vs. Multiple Regression

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Single Independent Variable x] --> B[Linear Regression: y = a0 + a1 times x]
    A --> C[Polynomial Regression: y = sum a_j times x to power j, j from 0 to m]
    D[Multiple Independent Variables x1, x2, ..., xk] --> E[Multiple Linear Regression: y = a0 + sum a_j times x_j]
    B --> F[Output: 2 unknown coefficients a0, a1]
    C --> G[Output: m + 1 unknown coefficients a0 to a_m]
    E --> H[Output: k + 1 unknown coefficients a0 to a_k]
    F --> I[All feed into unified solution framework: a = X transpose X inverse X transpose y]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Fitted Model for Engineering Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Decomposition of the Sum of Squares

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Variation SST = Sum of y_i minus y_bar squared] --> B[Explained Variation S_reg = Sum of y_hat_i minus y_bar squared]
    A --> C[Unexplained Variation S_res = Sum of y_i minus y_hat_i squared]
    B --> D[Quality Metric: R squared = S_reg divided by SST]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Decision Branch: Is R squared greater than 0.9?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Acceptable Engineering Fit: Proceed to Deployment]
    E -- No --> G[Refit: Increase Polynomial Order or Add Predictors]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instruction:** Each question carries **3 marks**. Answers must be precise and to the point.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"State and derive the normal equations used in fitting a straight line $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ using the method of least squares."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The objective is to minimize the sum of squared residuals $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i)^2$. Differentiating $S$ partially with respect to $a_0$ and $a_1$ and equating to zero gives the two normal equations:

$$\sum y_i = n a_0 + a_1 \sum x_i \quad \text{and} \quad \sum x_i y_i = a_0 \sum x_i + a_1 \sum x_i^2$$

These are solved simultaneously to obtain $a_0$ and $a_1$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between linear and polynomial regression. When is polynomial regression preferred in design optimization problems?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Linear regression** uses a first-order model $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ and assumes a constant rate of change; suitable when the data exhibits an approximately straight-line trend.
- **Polynomial regression** uses $\hat{y} = \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x^j$ of order $m \geq 2$ and is preferred when the underlying physical phenomenon is **curvilinear** (e.g., drag coefficient vs. Reynolds number, creep vs. stress).
- In design optimization, polynomial regression is preferred when a *Response Surface Methodology (RSM)* is being constructed to approximate expensive simulation models.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Question Pattern:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts **(a) — 7 marks** and **(b) — 7 marks**. Both sub-parts are **compulsory**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted from PEPET743 Module 2]**

The following experimental data was collected in a materials testing laboratory relating the **annealing temperature ($x$ in $^\circ$C)** to the **resulting hardness ($y$ in HV)** of an aluminium alloy specimen:

| $x_i$ ($^\circ$C) | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y_i$ (HV)       | 90 | 85  | 74  | 65  | 50  | 38  |

**(a)** Fit a straight line $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ to the data using the method of least squares. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Predict the hardness at $x = 350^\circ$C and compute the **coefficient of determination** $R^2$. Comment on the quality of fit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Fitting the Straight Line**

**Step 1 — Compute the required summations:**

$n = 6$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 100 + 200 + 300 + 400 + 500 + 600 = 2100 \\
\sum y_i &= 90 + 85 + 74 + 65 + 50 + 38 = 402 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 10{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 160{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 360{,}000 = 910{,}000 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (100)(90) + (200)(85) + (300)(74) + (400)(65) + (500)(50) + (600)(38) \\
&= 9000 + 17{,}000 + 22{,}200 + 26{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 22{,}800 = 122{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the slope formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{6(122{,}000) - (2100)(402)}{6(910{,}000) - (2100)^2} \\
&= \frac{732{,}000 - 844{,}200}{5{,}460{,}000 - 4{,}410{,}000} \\
&= \frac{-112{,}200}{1{,}050{,}000} = -0.1069 \text{ HV}/^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the intercept formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= 2100 / 6 = 350, \quad \bar{y} = 402 / 6 = 67 \\
a_0 &= \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x} = 67 - (-0.1069)(350) = 67 + 37.415 = 104.415 \text{ HV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fitted Regression Line:**
$$\boxed{\; \hat{y} = 104.415 - 0.1069 \, x \;}$$

---

**Part (b) — Prediction and Goodness of Fit**

**Step 1 — Prediction at $x = 350^\circ$C:**

$$\hat{y}(350) = 104.415 - 0.1069 \times 350 = 104.415 - 37.415 = 67.00 \text{ HV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute predicted values for each $x_i$ and residuals:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i = 104.415 - 0.1069 x_i$ | $r_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $r_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 90 | 93.725 | -3.725 | 13.876 |
| 200 | 85 | 83.035 | 1.965 | 3.861 |
| 300 | 74 | 72.345 | 1.655 | 2.739 |
| 400 | 65 | 61.655 | 3.345 | 11.189 |
| 500 | 50 | 50.965 | -0.965 | 0.931 |
| 600 | 38 | 40.275 | -2.275 | 5.176 |
| | | | **Total** | **37.772** |

**Step 3 — Compute Total Sum of Squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{tot} &= \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (90-67)^2 + (85-67)^2 + (74-67)^2 + (65-67)^2 + (50-67)^2 + (38-67)^2 \\
&= 529 + 324 + 49 + 4 + 289 + 841 = 2036
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{S_{res}}{S_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{37.772}{2036} = 1 - 0.01856 = 0.9814$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** Since $R^2 = 0.9814 \approx 0.98$, the linear model explains about **98.14%** of the variation in hardness data, indicating an **excellent fit**. The relationship between annealing temperature and hardness is **strongly linear** with a **negative slope**, confirming that hardness decreases as temperature increases.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted from PEPET743 Module 2]**

A heat transfer experiment records the **time of cooling ($x$ in minutes)** and the **temperature drop ($y$ in $^\circ$C)** of a hot metallic sphere:

| $x_i$ (min) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y_i$ ($^\circ$C) | 8.0 | 6.0 | 5.0 | 4.2 | 3.5 | 3.0 | 2.5 | 2.2 |

**(a)** Fit a second-order polynomial $\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ using least squares. Set up the system of normal equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the normal equations using matrix methods and predict the temperature drop at $x = 4.5$ min. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Setting Up the Normal Equations**

**Step 1 — Required summations** for $n = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 1+2+3+4+5+6+7+8 = 36 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 1+4+9+16+25+36+49+64 = 204 \\
\sum x_i^3 &= 1+8+27+64+125+216+343+512 = 1296 \\
\sum x_i^4 &= 1+16+81+256+625+1296+2401+4096 = 8772 \\
\sum y_i &= 8.0+6.0+5.0+4.2+3.5+3.0+2.5+2.2 = 34.4 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 1(8)+2(6)+3(5)+4(4.2)+5(3.5)+6(3)+7(2.5)+8(2.2) \\
&= 8+12+15+16.8+17.5+18+17.5+17.6 = 122.4 \\
\sum x_i^2 y_i &= 1(8)+4(6)+9(5)+16(4.2)+25(3.5)+36(3)+49(2.5)+64(2.2) \\
&= 8+24+45+67.2+87.5+108+122.5+140.8 = 603.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the three normal equations** for $k = 0, 1, 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n a_0 + a_1 \sum x_i + a_2 \sum x_i^2 &= \sum y_i \\
a_0 \sum x_i + a_1 \sum x_i^2 + a_2 \sum x_i^3 &= \sum x_i y_i \\
a_0 \sum x_i^2 + a_1 \sum x_i^3 + a_2 \sum x_i^4 &= \sum x_i^2 y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 36 & 204 \\ 36 & 204 & 1296 \\ 204 & 1296 & 8772 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 34.4 \\ 122.4 \\ 603.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Solving the System and Prediction**

**Step 1 — Apply Cramer's rule** (or Gaussian elimination). For brevity, using matrix inverse:

The determinant of the coefficient matrix:
$$D = 8(204 \cdot 8772 - 1296 \cdot 1296) - 36(36 \cdot 8772 - 1296 \cdot 204) + 204(36 \cdot 1296 - 204 \cdot 204)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 8(1{,}789{,}488 - 1{,}679{,}616) - 36(315{,}792 - 264{,}384) + 204(46{,}656 - 41{,}616) \\
&= 8(109{,}872) - 36(51{,}408) + 204(5{,}040) \\
&= 878{,}976 - 1{,}850{,}688 + 1{,}028{,}160 = 56{,}448
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve each component** (using cofactor expansion):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{a_0} &= 34.4(204 \cdot 8772 - 1296 \cdot 1296) - 36(122.4 \cdot 8772 - 1296 \cdot 603) + 204(122.4 \cdot 1296 - 204 \cdot 603) \\
&= 34.4(109{,}872) - 36(79{,}469.4) + 204(35{,}592) \\
&= 3{,}779{,}596.8 - 2{,}860{,}898.4 + 7{,}260{,}768 = 8{,}179{,}466.4 \\[4pt]
a_0 &= D_{a_0}/D = 8{,}179{,}466.4 / 56{,}448 \approx 8.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{a_1} &= 8(122.4 \cdot 8772 - 603 \cdot 1296) - 34.4(36 \cdot 8772 - 204 \cdot 1296) + 204(36 \cdot 603 - 122.4 \cdot 204) \\
&= 8(291{,}957.6) - 34.4(51{,}408) + 204(-3{,}777.6) \\
&= 2{,}335{,}660.8 - 1{,}768{,}435.2 - 770{,}630.4 = -203{,}404.8 \\[4pt]
a_1 &= D_{a_1}/D = -203{,}404.8 / 56{,}448 \approx -1.886
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{a_2} &= 8(204 \cdot 603 - 1296 \cdot 122.4) - 36(36 \cdot 603 - 204 \cdot 122.4) + 34.4(36 \cdot 1296 - 204 \cdot 204) \\
&= 8(-35{,}640) - 36(-3{,}177.6) + 34.4(5{,}040) \\
&= -285{,}120 + 114{,}393.6 + 173{,}376 = 2{,}649.6 \\[4pt]
a_2 &= D_{a_2}/D = 2{,}649.6 / 56{,}448 \approx 0.1256
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fitted Polynomial:**
$$\boxed{\; \hat{y} = 8.85 - 1.886 \, x + 0.1256 \, x^2 \;}$$

**Step 3 — Prediction at $x = 4.5$ min:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}(4.5) &= 8.85 - 1.886(4.5) + 0.1256(4.5)^2 \\
&= 8.85 - 8.487 + 0.1256(20.25) \\
&= 8.85 - 8.487 + 2.5434 \\
&= 2.9064 \approx 2.91 \; ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

The model predicts a temperature drop of approximately **2.91 $^\circ$C** at $x = 4.5$ minutes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU PEPET743 Examinations:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Slope Formula:** A frequently observed error is the inversion of numerator and denominator in $a_1$. Always cross-verify using the **alternative form** $a_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ which has the same sign convention.
> 2. **Forgetting to Divide by $n$ in Intercept:** Many students write $a_0 = \sum y_i - a_1 \sum x_i$ which is the *unscaled* form. KTU valuators award full marks only when $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$ (or the equivalent divided form) is used.
> 3. **Confusing $R^2$ and $r$ (correlation coefficient):** $R^2$ is the **coefficient of determination** (used in regression), while $r$ is the **correlation coefficient** (used in statistics). They are *not* interchangeable.
> 4. **Missing Units in Final Equation:** Always specify units of the fitted coefficients (e.g., $a_1$ in HV/$^\circ$C for Question A above).
> 5. **Determinant Calculation Errors in Polynomial Regression:** Manual solution of $(m+1) \times (m+1)$ systems is error-prone. KTU students are advised to use Gaussian elimination and double-check each pivot step.
> 6. **Degree vs. Number of Points:** A polynomial of degree $m \geq n$ will give a *singular* matrix $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$. The KTU Board specifically tests whether students recognize this **ill-conditioning**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Least Square Regression Theory:**

- **Definition:** Least Square Regression minimizes $S(\mathbf{a}) = \sum [y_i - f(x_i, \mathbf{a})]^2$ to obtain the best-fit curve.
- **Linear Model Form:** $\hat{y} = a_0 + a_1 x$ — applicable to two-variable linear trends.
- **Slope:** $a_1 = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$
- **Intercept:** $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$
- **Normal Equations Origin:** Derived by setting $\partial S / \partial a_j = 0$ for all coefficients.
- **Polynomial Model:** $\hat{y} = \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j x^j$ with $(m+1)$ normal equations.
- **Matrix Compact Form:** $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$
- **Multiple Regression:** $\hat{y} = a_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} a_j x_j$ — used in multivariable design.
- **Goodness of Fit Metric:** $R^2 = 1 - S_{res}/S_{tot} \in [0, 1]$
- **Standard Error:** $s_e = \sqrt{S_{res}/(n-2)}$ for linear models.
- **Assumptions:** Errors are random, mean-zero, homoscedastic, and uncorrelated.
- **Uniqueness:** Solution exists and is unique if $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is **positive definite** (full column rank).
- **SST Identity:** $S_{tot} = S_{reg} + S_{res}$
- **Why Squared Errors:** Penalizes large residuals quadratically and yields a smooth, convex optimization surface.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Fatigue S-N curves, concrete strength modeling, drag coefficient curves, calibration of sensors, response surface methodology in design optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauss Newton Method for Nonlinear Least Squares Regression - Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gauss Newton Method for Nonlinear Least Squares Regression - Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Gauss–Newton Method** is a specialized iterative descent algorithm used to solve the **Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS) Regression Problem**. It is designed to find the parameter vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ that minimizes the sum of squared residuals of a nonlinear model.

Mathematically, given a nonlinear model function $f(\mathbf{x}, t)$ and a set of $m$ observed data points $(t_i, y_i)$, the objective is to minimize:

$$F(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_i^2(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x})$$

where the residual vector is $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = (r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m)^T$ and each residual is defined as:

$$r_i(\mathbf{x}) = y_i - f(\mathbf{x}, t_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

The Gauss-Newton update rule at iteration $k$ is:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \left[ J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \right]^{-1} J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

where $J(\mathbf{x})$ is the **Jacobian Matrix** of the residual vector with respect to the parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Newton's Method:** The standard Newton's method requires the computation of the full Hessian matrix $H = J^T J + \sum r_i \nabla^2 r_i$. The Gauss-Newton method **drops the second-order term** $\sum r_i \nabla^2 r_i$ and approximates $H \approx J^T J$, which is the foundation of its efficiency for least-squares problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an archer trying to hit the bullseye on a target, but you are standing in a dark room and can only see the arrow's *miss distance* from the center after each shot (the residual). 

- You cannot directly see the slope of the ground (Hessian), but you can estimate the slope by *measuring* how the miss-distance changes when you adjust the bow's angle and elevation (this is the Jacobian).
- The Gauss-Newton method works exactly like this: instead of computing the full curvature (which is expensive and hard), it uses a **linear approximation** of how the residuals change with parameter adjustments, and then solves a simpler linear least-squares sub-problem at each step.
- Each iteration is essentially **"solve a linear least-squares problem that locally approximates the nonlinear one, then move toward that solution."**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **$m$** = number of data observations (typically $m \gg n$).
- **$n$** = number of model parameters.
- **$J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$** = Jacobian matrix.
- **$J^T J \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$** = approximate Hessian (Gauss-Newton approximation).
- **$\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$** = residual vector.
- **Tolerance values:** $\varepsilon_1 = 10^{-8}$ (parameter convergence), $\varepsilon_2 = 10^{-8}$ (function convergence), $\varepsilon_3 = 10^{-8}$ (gradient norm).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the **PEPET743 Design Optimization** course, Module 2 specifically tests the ability of students to (a) formulate the Jacobian, (b) assemble $J^T J$, and (c) execute iterations by hand for 2 to 3-parameter models. Memorize the update rule, the normal-equation form $J^T J \Delta \mathbf{x} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$, and the structure of $J$ for exponential, power, and saturation-type models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Gauss-Newton Algorithm

The Gauss-Newton method is built on the principle of **iterative linearization**. The sequence of logical steps at each iteration $k$ is:

- **Step 1 — Initialize:** Choose an initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$, tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$, and set iteration counter $k = 0$.

- **Step 2 — Compute Residuals:** Evaluate $\mathbf{r}^{(k)} = \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ where each component is $r_i^{(k)} = y_i - f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, t_i)$.

- **Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian:** Construct the $m \times n$ matrix:

$$J_{ij}^{(k)} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_j}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^{(k)}} = -\frac{\partial f(\mathbf{x}, t_i)}{\partial x_j}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^{(k)}}$$

- **Step 4 — Form the Approximate Hessian:** Compute $A^{(k)} = (J^{(k)})^T J^{(k)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and the gradient vector $\mathbf{g}^{(k)} = (J^{(k)})^T \mathbf{r}^{(k)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

- **Step 5 — Solve the Normal Equations:** Solve the linear system $A^{(k)} \mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{g}^{(k)}$ for the search direction $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$.

- **Step 6 — Update Parameters:** Set $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$.

- **Step 7 — Convergence Check:** Terminate if any of the following are satisfied:
  - $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| < \varepsilon_1$ (parameter convergence)
  - $|F(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}) - F(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})| < \varepsilon_2$ (objective convergence)
  - $\|\mathbf{g}^{(k)}\| < \varepsilon_3$ (gradient convergence)
  
  Otherwise, set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why drop the second derivative term?** In least-squares problems, when the residuals $r_i$ are small or the model is mildly nonlinear, the term $\sum r_i \nabla^2 r_i$ is negligible compared to $J^T J$. This makes Gauss-Newton **computationally cheaper** than full Newton's method.
- **How is the search direction derived?** It comes from linearizing the residual: $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}) \approx \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) + J(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{d}$. Minimizing $\|\mathbf{r} + J\mathbf{d}\|^2$ leads directly to the normal equations $J^T J \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$.
- **Why prefer $J^T J$ over Hessian?** It is always symmetric and positive semi-definite, guaranteeing stable behavior in a neighborhood of the optimum (for **zero-residual problems**, it is positive definite).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Curve fitting in biomedical engineering** (e.g., pharmacokinetic drug concentration models: $C(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t}$).
- **Calibration of sensor response curves** in instrumentation.
- **Robot kinematics calibration** where forward kinematics is nonlinear in joint angles.
- **Reaction rate constant estimation** in chemical engineering using nonlinear Arrhenius-type models.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you need for solving KTU exam problems on this topic. No vertical bars are used; absolute value is replaced with `\vert`.

| Quantity | Formula | Dimensions / Notes |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| Objective Function | $F(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_i^2(\mathbf{x})$ | Scalar; $m$ = number of data points |
| Residual | $r_i(\mathbf{x}) = y_i - f(\mathbf{x}, t_i)$ | Signed error at data point $i$ |
| Gradient | $\nabla F(\mathbf{x}) = J(\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x})$ | $n \times 1$ column vector |
| Approximate Hessian | $H_{GN}(\mathbf{x}) = J(\mathbf{x})^T J(\mathbf{x})$ | $n \times n$ symmetric matrix |
| Normal Equations | $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$ | Linear system in $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Gauss-Newton Update | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - (J^T J)^{-1} J^T \mathbf{r}$ | Implicit form; explicit requires solving $J^T J \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$ |
| Jacobian Entry | $J_{ij} = -\dfrac{\partial f(\mathbf{x}, t_i)}{\partial x_j}$ | Row $i$ = data point, Column $j$ = parameter |
| Convergence Criterion 1 | $\Vert \mathbf{d}^{(k)} \Vert \leq \varepsilon_1$ | Parameter vector update norm |
| Convergence Criterion 2 | $\vert F(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}) - F(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \vert \leq \varepsilon_2$ | Objective function drop |
| Convergence Criterion 3 | $\Vert J^T \mathbf{r} \Vert \leq \varepsilon_3$ | Gradient norm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** In Structural Health Monitoring, an exponential decay model $y = A e^{-\lambda t}$ fitted to vibration data using Gauss-Newton can directly identify the **damping ratio** of a structure — a critical parameter for seismic design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

This section contains two exhaustive worked examples that mirror the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Every algebraic step is explicitly written out.

---

## 3.1 Example 1: Exponential Decay Model with Two Parameters

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

We are given the following data of a radioactive decay process:

| $t_i$ | 0.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $y_i$ | 2.05 | 1.50 | 0.95 | 0.65 | 0.40 |

Fit the nonlinear model $f(x_1, x_2, t) = x_1 e^{-x_2 t}$ to this data using the **Gauss-Newton Method**, starting from the initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (2.0, 1.0)^T$. Perform **two iterations** by hand.

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Formulation

The model is $f(x_1, x_2, t) = x_1 e^{-x_2 t}$, with parameters $x_1$ (initial amplitude) and $x_2$ (decay rate).

The residual at the $i$-th data point is:

$$r_i(x_1, x_2) = y_i - x_1 e^{-x_2 t_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, 5$$

The objective is $F(x_1, x_2) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{5} r_i^2$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Jacobian

We require the partial derivatives of $f$ with respect to $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = e^{-x_2 t}$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -x_1 t \, e^{-x_2 t}$$

Since the residual is $r_i = y_i - f$, the Jacobian entries are:

$$J_{i1} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_1} = -e^{-x_2 t_i}$$

$$J_{i2} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_2} = +x_1 t_i \, e^{-x_2 t_i}$$

The Jacobian matrix at any point $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$J(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} -e^{-x_2 t_1} & x_1 t_1 e^{-x_2 t_1} \\ -e^{-x_2 t_2} & x_1 t_2 e^{-x_2 t_2} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ -e^{-x_2 t_5} & x_1 t_5 e^{-x_2 t_5} \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration 0 → 1

At $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (x_1^{(0)}, x_2^{(0)})^T = (2.0, 1.0)^T$, compute the model values $f_i = x_1 e^{-x_2 t_i}$:

$$f_1 = 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.0 \times 0.0} = 2.0 \cdot 1.0000 = 2.0000$$

$$f_2 = 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.0 \times 0.5} = 2.0 \cdot 0.6065 = 1.2131$$

$$f_3 = 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.0 \times 1.0} = 2.0 \cdot 0.3679 = 0.7358$$

$$f_4 = 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.0 \times 1.5} = 2.0 \cdot 0.2231 = 0.4463$$

$$f_5 = 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.0 \times 2.0} = 2.0 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.2707$$

**Compute the residual vector $\mathbf{r}^{(0)}$:**

$$r_1^{(0)} = 2.05 - 2.0000 = 0.0500$$

$$r_2^{(0)} = 1.50 - 1.2131 = 0.2869$$

$$r_3^{(0)} = 0.95 - 0.7358 = 0.2142$$

$$r_4^{(0)} = 0.65 - 0.4463 = 0.2037$$

$$r_5^{(0)} = 0.40 - 0.2707 = 0.1293$$

**Compute the Jacobian entries at $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$:**

For $J_{i1} = -e^{-x_2 t_i}$:

$$J_{11} = -1.0000, \quad J_{21} = -0.6065, \quad J_{31} = -0.3679, \quad J_{41} = -0.2231, \quad J_{51} = -0.1353$$

For $J_{i2} = x_1 t_i e^{-x_2 t_i}$ with $x_1 = 2.0$:

$$J_{12} = 2.0 \cdot 0.0 \cdot 1.0000 = 0.0000$$

$$J_{22} = 2.0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.6065 = 0.6065$$

$$J_{32} = 2.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.3679 = 0.7358$$

$$J_{42} = 2.0 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.2231 = 0.6694$$

$$J_{52} = 2.0 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.5413$$

So:

$$J^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0000 & 0.0000 \\ -0.6065 & 0.6065 \\ -0.3679 & 0.7358 \\ -0.2231 & 0.6694 \\ -0.1353 & 0.5413 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $J^T J$ (a $2 \times 2$ matrix):**

$$(J^T J)_{11} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} J_{i1}^2 = 1.0000 + 0.3678 + 0.1353 + 0.0498 + 0.0183 = 1.5712$$

$$(J^T J)_{22} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} J_{i2}^2 = 0.0000 + 0.3678 + 0.5414 + 0.4481 + 0.2930 = 1.6503$$

$$(J^T J)_{12} = (J^T J)_{21} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} J_{i1} J_{i2} = 0.0000 + (-0.6065)(0.6065) + (-0.3679)(0.7358) + (-0.2231)(0.6694) + (-0.1353)(0.5413)$$

Let us compute term by term:

$$(-0.6065)(0.6065) = -0.3678$$

$$(-0.3679)(0.7358) = -0.2707$$

$$(-0.2231)(0.6694) = -0.1493$$

$$(-0.1353)(0.5413) = -0.0732$$

Summing: $(J^T J)_{12} = 0.0000 - 0.3678 - 0.2707 - 0.1493 - 0.0732 = -0.8610$

Thus:

$$J^T J = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5712 & -0.8610 \\ -0.8610 & 1.6503 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $J^T \mathbf{r}$ (a $2 \times 1$ vector):**

$$(J^T \mathbf{r})_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{5} J_{i1} r_i = (-1.0000)(0.0500) + (-0.6065)(0.2869) + (-0.3679)(0.2142) + (-0.2231)(0.2037) + (-0.1353)(0.1293)$$

Computing term by term:

$$(-1.0000)(0.0500) = -0.0500$$

$$(-0.6065)(0.2869) = -0.1740$$

$$(-0.3679)(0.2142) = -0.0788$$

$$(-0.2231)(0.2037) = -0.0454$$

$$(-0.1353)(0.1293) = -0.0175$$

Sum: $(J^T \mathbf{r})_1 = -0.0500 - 0.1740 - 0.0788 - 0.0454 - 0.0175 = -0.3657$

$$(J^T \mathbf{r})_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{5} J_{i2} r_i = (0.0000)(0.0500) + (0.6065)(0.2869) + (0.7358)(0.2142) + (0.6694)(0.2037) + (0.5413)(0.1293)$$

Computing term by term:

$$(0.6065)(0.2869) = 0.1740$$

$$(0.7358)(0.2142) = 0.1576$$

$$(0.6694)(0.2037) = 0.1363$$

$$(0.5413)(0.1293) = 0.0700$$

Sum: $(J^T \mathbf{r})_2 = 0.0000 + 0.1740 + 0.1576 + 0.1363 + 0.0700 = 0.5379$

Thus:

$$J^T \mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3657 \\ 0.5379 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Solve $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$:**

We need to solve:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1.5712 & -0.8610 \\ -0.8610 & 1.6503 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3657 \\ -0.5379 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant: $\Delta = (1.5712)(1.6503) - (-0.8610)^2 = 2.5930 - 0.7413 = 1.8517$

By Cramer's rule:

$$d_1 = \frac{(0.3657)(1.6503) - (-0.8610)(-0.5379)}{1.8517} = \frac{0.6036 - 0.4631}{1.8517} = \frac{0.1405}{1.8517} = 0.0759$$

$$d_2 = \frac{(1.5712)(-0.5379) - (-0.8610)(0.3657)}{1.8517} = \frac{-0.8452 + 0.3149}{1.8517} = \frac{-0.5303}{1.8517} = -0.2864$$

**Update parameters:**

$$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(0)} + \mathbf{d} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0759 \\ -0.2864 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0759 \\ 0.7136 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.5 Iteration 1 → 2

At $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (2.0759, 0.7136)^T$, compute the model values $f_i = x_1 e^{-x_2 t_i}$:

$$f_1 = 2.0759 \cdot e^{0} = 2.0759$$

$$f_2 = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.7136 \times 0.5} = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.3568} = 2.0759 \cdot 0.7000 = 1.4531$$

$$f_3 = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.7136 \times 1.0} = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.7136} = 2.0759 \cdot 0.4900 = 1.0172$$

$$f_4 = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.7136 \times 1.5} = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-1.0704} = 2.0759 \cdot 0.3430 = 0.7120$$

$$f_5 = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-0.7136 \times 2.0} = 2.0759 \cdot e^{-1.4272} = 2.0759 \cdot 0.2401 = 0.4984$$

**Compute the new residuals:**

$$r_1^{(1)} = 2.05 - 2.0759 = -0.0259$$

$$r_2^{(1)} = 1.50 - 1.4531 = 0.0469$$

$$r_3^{(1)} = 0.95 - 1.0172 = -0.0672$$

$$r_4^{(1)} = 0.65 - 0.7120 = -0.0620$$

$$r_5^{(1)} = 0.40 - 0.4984 = -0.0984$$

**Objective function value:**

$$F(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) = \frac{1}{2}[(-0.0259)^2 + (0.0469)^2 + (-0.0672)^2 + (-0.0620)^2 + (-0.0984)^2]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}[0.000671 + 0.002200 + 0.004516 + 0.003844 + 0.009683] = \frac{1}{2}(0.020914) = 0.01046$$

For comparison, $F(\mathbf{x}^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{2}(0.0500^2 + 0.2869^2 + 0.2142^2 + 0.2037^2 + 0.1293^2) = \frac{1}{2}(0.1792) = 0.0896$.

The objective function has dropped by a factor of more than 8 in one iteration, indicating rapid convergence of the Gauss-Newton method on this well-conditioned problem.

### 3.1.6 Summary Table for Example 1

| Iteration | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $F(\mathbf{x})$ |
|-----------|-------|-------|------------------|
| 0 | 2.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0896 |
| 1 | 2.0759 | 0.7136 | 0.0105 |
| 2 (estimated) | ≈ 2.080 | ≈ 0.70 | ≈ 0.010 |

> [!NOTE]
> The estimated values for iteration 2 can be obtained by repeating steps 3.1.4 with $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ substituted as the new starting point. The student is expected to show at least the structure: compute $f_i$, $r_i$, $J$, $J^T J$, $J^T \mathbf{r}$, solve, and update.

---

## 3.2 Example 2: Power-Law Model with Two Parameters

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Fit the model $f(x_1, x_2, t) = x_1 t^{x_2}$ to the following data:

| $t_i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|
| $y_i$ | 1.8 | 4.7 | 8.5 | 13.2 |

Use the Gauss-Newton method with initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (1.5, 1.2)^T$. Perform **two iterations**.

### 3.2.2 Model and Residual Formulation

The residual is:

$$r_i(x_1, x_2) = y_i - x_1 t_i^{x_2}$$

### 3.2.3 Jacobian Derivation

The partial derivatives of $f = x_1 t^{x_2}$ with respect to the parameters are:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = t^{x_2}$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = x_1 t^{x_2} \ln t$$

Therefore the Jacobian entries are:

$$J_{i1} = -t_i^{x_2}$$

$$J_{i2} = -x_1 t_i^{x_2} \ln t_i$$

### 3.2.4 Iteration 0 → 1

At $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (1.5, 1.2)^T$, compute $f_i = 1.5 \cdot t_i^{1.2}$:

$$f_1 = 1.5 \cdot 1^{1.2} = 1.5 \cdot 1.0 = 1.5000$$

$$f_2 = 1.5 \cdot 2^{1.2} = 1.5 \cdot 2.2974 = 3.4461$$

$$f_3 = 1.5 \cdot 3^{1.2} = 1.5 \cdot 3.7372 = 5.6058$$

$$f_4 = 1.5 \cdot 4^{1.2} = 1.5 \cdot 5.2780 = 7.9170$$

**Residuals:**

$$r_1^{(0)} = 1.8 - 1.5000 = 0.3000$$

$$r_2^{(0)} = 4.7 - 3.4461 = 1.2539$$

$$r_3^{(0)} = 8.5 - 5.6058 = 2.8942$$

$$r_4^{(0)} = 13.2 - 7.9170 = 5.2830$$

**Jacobian entries at $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$:**

For $J_{i1} = -t_i^{1.2}$:

$$J_{11} = -1.0000, \quad J_{21} = -2.2974, \quad J_{31} = -3.7372, \quad J_{41} = -5.2780$$

For $J_{i2} = -x_1 t_i^{1.2} \ln t_i$ with $x_1 = 1.5$:

$$J_{12} = -1.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \ln 1 = -1.5 \cdot 0 = 0.0000$$

$$J_{22} = -1.5 \cdot 2.2974 \cdot \ln 2 = -1.5 \cdot 2.2974 \cdot 0.6931 = -1.5 \cdot 1.5918 = -2.3876$$

$$J_{32} = -1.5 \cdot 3.7372 \cdot \ln 3 = -1.5 \cdot 3.7372 \cdot 1.0986 = -1.5 \cdot 4.1063 = -6.1595$$

$$J_{42} = -1.5 \cdot 5.2780 \cdot \ln 4 = -1.5 \cdot 5.2780 \cdot 1.3863 = -1.5 \cdot 7.3162 = -10.9743$$

So:

$$J^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0000 & 0.0000 \\ -2.2974 & -2.3876 \\ -3.7372 & -6.1595 \\ -5.2780 & -10.9743 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $J^T J$:**

$$(J^T J)_{11} = 1.0000^2 + 2.2974^2 + 3.7372^2 + 5.2780^2 = 1.0000 + 5.2780 + 13.9667 + 27.8573 = 48.1020$$

$$(J^T J)_{22} = 0.0000^2 + 2.3876^2 + 6.1595^2 + 10.9743^2 = 0.0000 + 5.7007 + 37.9395 + 120.4353 = 164.0755$$

$$(J^T J)_{12} = (J^T J)_{21} = (-1.0)(0) + (-2.2974)(-2.3876) + (-3.7372)(-6.1595) + (-5.2780)(-10.9743)$$

$$= 0.0000 + 5.4847 + 23.0184 + 57.9220 = 86.4251$$

Thus:

$$J^T J = \begin{bmatrix} 48.1020 & 86.4251 \\ 86.4251 & 164.0755 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $J^T \mathbf{r}$:**

$$(J^T \mathbf{r})_1 = (-1.0)(0.30) + (-2.2974)(1.2539) + (-3.7372)(2.8942) + (-5.2780)(5.2830)$$

$$= -0.3000 - 2.8806 - 10.8154 - 27.8833 = -41.8793$$

$$(J^T \mathbf{r})_2 = (0)(0.30) + (-2.3876)(1.2539) + (-6.1595)(2.8942) + (-10.9743)(5.2830)$$

$$= 0.0000 - 2.9939 - 17.8274 - 57.9773 = -78.7986$$

**Solve $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$:**

RHS = $-\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 41.8793 \\ 78.7986 \end{bmatrix}$.

Determinant: $\Delta = (48.1020)(164.0755) - (86.4251)^2 = 7893.21 - 7469.20 = 424.01$

By Cramer's rule:

$$d_1 = \frac{(41.8793)(164.0755) - (86.4251)(78.7986)}{424.01} = \frac{6871.71 - 6810.18}{424.01} = \frac{61.53}{424.01} = 0.1451$$

$$d_2 = \frac{(48.1020)(78.7986) - (86.4251)(41.8793)}{424.01} = \frac{3790.45 - 3619.50}{424.01} = \frac{170.95}{424.01} = 0.4032$$

**Update parameters:**

$$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 1.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1451 \\ 0.4032 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.6451 \\ 1.6032 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2.5 Iteration 1 → 2 (Brief)

At $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1.6451, 1.6032)^T$, recompute $f_i = 1.6451 \cdot t_i^{1.6032}$:

$$f_1 = 1.6451 \cdot 1.0 = 1.6451$$

$$f_2 = 1.6451 \cdot 2^{1.6032} = 1.6451 \cdot 3.0409 = 5.0026$$

$$f_3 = 1.6451 \cdot 3^{1.6032} = 1.6451 \cdot 6.0719 = 9.9889$$

$$f_4 = 1.6451 \cdot 4^{1.6032} = 1.6451 \cdot 9.5742 = 15.7505$$

**Residuals:**

$$r_1^{(1)} = 1.8 - 1.6451 = 0.1549$$

$$r_2^{(1)} = 4.7 - 5.0026 = -0.3026$$

$$r_3^{(1)} = 8.5 - 9.9889 = -1.4889$$

$$r_4^{(1)} = 13.2 - 15.7505 = -1.5505$$

The objective value drops significantly from $F(\mathbf{x}^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{2}(0.30^2 + 1.2539^2 + 2.8942^2 + 5.2830^2) = \frac{1}{2}(36.4130) = 18.207$ to:

$$F(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) = \frac{1}{2}(0.1549^2 + 0.3026^2 + 1.4889^2 + 1.5505^2) = \frac{1}{2}(4.8791) = 2.440$$

This is a reduction by a factor of about **7.5x** in one iteration — demonstrating the quadratic-like local convergence of Gauss-Newton.

### 3.2.6 Summary Table for Example 2

| Iteration | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $F(\mathbf{x})$ |
|-----------|-------|-------|------------------|
| 0 | 1.5000 | 1.2000 | 18.207 |
| 1 | 1.6451 | 1.6032 | 2.440 |
| 2 (estimated) | ≈ 1.68 | ≈ 1.60 | ≈ 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Example 2:** The power-law model $y = x_1 t^{x_2}$ is the standard form for the **Allometric Scaling Law** in biology, the **S-N fatigue curve** in mechanical engineering, and the **Weibull cumulative distribution** in reliability engineering. The Gauss-Newton method yields the scaling exponent $x_2$ directly, which is physically meaningful.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gauss-Newton Algorithm Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> initB[Initialize x0 and epsilon]
    initB --> kset[Set k = 0]
    kset --> calcR[Compute residual vector r at xk]
    calcR --> calcJ[Compute Jacobian matrix J at xk]
    calcJ --> formA[Form approximate Hessian A = JT times J]
    formA --> formG[Form gradient vector g = JT times r]
    formG --> solveSys[Solve linear system A times d = minus g]
    solveSys --> updX[Update xkp1 = xk plus d]
    updX --> chk1{Parameter norm less than eps1}
    chk1 -- Yes --> stopH([Converged])
    chk1 -- No --> chk2{Function drop less than eps2}
    chk2 -- Yes --> stopH
    chk2 -- No --> chk3{Gradient norm less than eps3}
    chk3 -- Yes --> stopH
    chk3 -- No --> incK[Increment k = k plus 1]
    incK --> calcR
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Per-Iteration Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stage1[INPUT LAYER]
        i1[Data points ti and yi]
        i2[Current parameters xk]
    end

    subgraph stage2[MODEL EVALUATION]
        m1[Evaluate f at xk ti]
        m2[Compute residuals r = yi minus f]
    end

    subgraph stage3[DERIVATIVE LAYER]
        d1[Compute partial derivatives of f]
        d2[Assemble Jacobian J]
    end

    subgraph stage4[LINEAR ALGEBRA]
        a1[Form JTJ]
        a2[Form JTr]
        a3[Solve JTJ times d = minus JTr]
    end

    subgraph stage5[UPDATE AND CHECK]
        u1[Update xkp1 = xk plus d]
        u2[Convergence test]
    end

    i1 --> m1
    i2 --> m1
    m1 --> m2
    m2 --> d1
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> a1
    d2 --> a2
    m2 --> a2
    a1 --> a3
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> u1
    u1 --> u2
    u2 --> m1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Example 1 Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataNodeA[Observed data: 5 points] --> resNodeB[Residual computation at x0]
    resNodeB --> objNodeC[Objective F equals 0.0896]
    resNodeB --> jacNodeD[Jacobian assembly 5 by 2]
    jacNodeD --> matNodeE[Form JTJ and JTr]
    matNodeE --> solNodeF[Solve 2 by 2 normal equations]
    solNodeF --> updNodeG[Update x1 equals 2.0759 and x2 equals 0.7136]
    updNodeG --> chkNodeH{Convergence check}
    chkNodeH -- Not converged --> resNodeB
    chkNodeH -- Converged --> finNodeI([Final estimate x asterisk])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram style:** Pure block-level functional architecture avoids ambiguity that physical drawings would create. Each box represents a mathematically well-defined operation, and the arrows show strict data dependencies — perfect for KTU's algorithm-tracing questions.
