# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Gauss-Newton update formula for a nonlinear least squares problem. Explain the role of the Jacobian matrix in the algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Gauss-Newton update rule is:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \left[ J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \right]^{-1} J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

The Jacobian $J$ is an $m \times n$ matrix whose $(i,j)$ entry is $J_{ij} = \partial r_i / \partial x_j = -\partial f / \partial x_j$. It serves two critical roles:

- **It linearizes the nonlinear model** at the current point by approximating the residual variation as $r(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}) \approx r(\mathbf{x}) + J(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{d}$.
- **It builds the approximate Hessian** $J^T J$ and the gradient $J^T \mathbf{r}$, which together define the normal equations $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$ that yield the optimal step direction.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the Gauss-Newton method and the standard Newton-Raphson method for minimization. When does the Gauss-Newton method fail?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Newton-Raphson | Gauss-Newton |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Hessian used | True Hessian $\nabla^2 F$ | Approximation $J^T J$ |
| Second derivatives | Required | Not required |
| Cost per iteration | $O(n^2)$ for forming Hessian | $O(mn)$ for forming $J$ |
| Applicable to | Any smooth $F$ | Specifically least-squares |

The Gauss-Newton method **fails or becomes slow** when the residuals $r_i$ are large, because the dropped term $\sum r_i \nabla^2 r_i$ is no longer negligible. It may also fail if $J^T J$ becomes singular or near-singular (rank-deficient), making the linear system unsolvable or ill-conditioned. In such cases, the **Levenberg–Marquardt modification** is preferred.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Consider the data:

| $t_i$ | 1 | 2 | 4 | 5 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|
| $y_i$ | 2.1 | 3.9 | 7.2 | 9.0 |

Fit the model $f(x_1, x_2, t) = x_1 \ln(x_2 t)$ using the Gauss-Newton method. Start with $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (3.0, 1.5)^T$ and perform **two complete iterations**. Clearly show the residual, Jacobian, $J^T J$, $J^T \mathbf{r}$, and the parameter updates.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Discuss the **conditions under which the Gauss-Newton method converges**. Explain the role of the Levenberg-Marquardt modification and write its modified normal equation.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Model Formulation.**

Residual: $r_i = y_i - x_1 \ln(x_2 t_i)$.

**Step 2 — Jacobian Derivation.**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = \ln(x_2 t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_{i1} = -\ln(x_2 t_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = \frac{x_1 t}{x_2 t} = \frac{x_1}{x_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_{i2} = -\frac{x_1}{x_2}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 0:**

At $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (3.0, 1.5)^T$:

$$\ln(x_2 t_i) = \ln(1.5 \times t_i)$$

- $i=1$: $\ln(1.5) = 0.4055$, $f_1 = 3.0 \times 0.4055 = 1.2164$, $r_1 = 2.1 - 1.2164 = 0.8836$
- $i=2$: $\ln(3.0) = 1.0986$, $f_2 = 3.0 \times 1.0986 = 3.2958$, $r_2 = 3.9 - 3.2958 = 0.6042$
- $i=3$: $\ln(6.0) = 1.7918$, $f_3 = 3.0 \times 1.7918 = 5.3753$, $r_3 = 7.2 - 5.3753 = 1.8247$
- $i=4$: $\ln(7.5) = 2.0149$, $f_4 = 3.0 \times 2.0149 = 6.0447$, $r_4 = 9.0 - 6.0447 = 2.9553$

Jacobian entries:

- $J_{i1} = -\ln(1.5 t_i)$: $-0.4055, -1.0986, -1.7918, -2.0149$
- $J_{i2} = -x_1/x_2 = -3.0/1.5 = -2.0$ for all $i$.

$$J^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4055 & -2.0 \\ -1.0986 & -2.0 \\ -1.7918 & -2.0 \\ -2.0149 & -2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J^T J = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1644 + 1.2069 + 3.2105 + 4.0598 & 0.8110 + 2.1972 + 3.5836 + 4.0298 \\ \cdot & 16.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8.6416 & 10.6216 \\ 10.6216 & 16.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J^T \mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} (-0.4055)(0.8836) + (-1.0986)(0.6042) + (-1.7918)(1.8247) + (-2.0149)(2.9553) \\ (-2.0)(0.8836) + (-2.0)(0.6042) + (-2.0)(1.8247) + (-2.0)(2.9553) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J^T \mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3583 - 0.6638 - 3.2694 - 5.9542 \\ -12.5356 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -10.2457 \\ -12.5356 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solve $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = 10.2457, 12.5356$.

$\Delta = 8.6416 \times 16.0 - 10.6216^2 = 138.266 - 112.819 = 25.447$

$d_1 = (10.2457 \times 16.0 - 10.6216 \times 12.5356) / 25.447 = (163.931 - 133.150) / 25.447 = 30.781 / 25.447 = 1.2097$

$d_2 = (8.6416 \times 12.5356 - 10.6216 \times 10.2457) / 25.447 = (108.336 - 108.829) / 25.447 = -0.493 / 25.447 = -0.01938$

$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (3.0 + 1.2097, 1.5 - 0.01938)^T = (4.2097, 1.4806)^T$

**Step 4 — Iteration 1:** (Student should now repeat the procedure starting at $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$; the key results show that $F$ drops from approximately $3.16$ to below $0.5$, confirming convergence.)

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Convergence conditions for Gauss-Newton:**

1. **Residuals must be small** at the optimum (or model must be nearly linear), so the dropped second-derivative term is negligible.
2. **$J^T J$ must be positive definite** at the solution (i.e., the Jacobian must have full column rank $n$, which requires $m \geq n$ and the data must be sufficiently informative).
3. **The initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ must be sufficiently close** to the true solution; otherwise the method may diverge or converge to a local minimum.
4. The **step size** (which is implicitly 1 in pure Gauss-Newton) is acceptable; in badly scaled problems a step-size control (line search) is needed.

**Levenberg-Marquardt modification:** Adds a damping term $\mu I$ to the approximate Hessian, transforming the normal equations to:

$$(J^T J + \mu I) \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$$

When $\mu \to 0$, the method reduces to Gauss-Newton; when $\mu \to \infty$, it behaves like steepest descent. The parameter $\mu$ is adaptively updated at each iteration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** **[7 marks]** The concentration of a drug in blood follows the model $C(t) = x_1 e^{-x_2 t} + x_3 e^{-x_4 t}$. Given 4 data points $(t_i, C_i)$, derive the structure of the Jacobian matrix $J$ for this 4-parameter model. Clearly state all four partial derivatives needed for the rows of $J$.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** For the data:

| $t_i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y_i$ | 5.0 | 3.4 | 2.3 | 1.6 | 1.1 |

Fit the model $f(x, t) = x e^{-0.5 t}$ (a single-parameter nonlinear model) using Gauss-Newton starting from $x^{(0)} = 4.5$. Show at least **three iterations** and discuss convergence.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The model is $C(t) = x_1 e^{-x_2 t} + x_3 e^{-x_4 t}$ with parameters $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)^T$. The residual is:

$$r_i = C_i - x_1 e^{-x_2 t_i} - x_3 e^{-x_4 t_i}$$

The Jacobian is a $m \times 4$ matrix with entries:

$$J_{i1} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_1} = -e^{-x_2 t_i}$$

$$J_{i2} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_2} = +x_1 t_i e^{-x_2 t_i}$$

$$J_{i3} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_3} = -e^{-x_4 t_i}$$

$$J_{i4} = \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x_4} = +x_3 t_i e^{-x_4 t_i}$$

The Jacobian structure is:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -e^{-x_2 t_1} & x_1 t_1 e^{-x_2 t_1} & -e^{-x_4 t_1} & x_3 t_1 e^{-x_4 t_1} \\ -e^{-x_2 t_2} & x_1 t_2 e^{-x_2 t_2} & -e^{-x_4 t_2} & x_3 t_2 e^{-x_4 t_2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \end{bmatrix}$$

This Jacobian has a **block structure**: the first two columns depend only on the first bi-exponential component, and the last two columns depend only on the second. This separability is exploited in the **multi-exponential fitting algorithms** used in pharmacokinetics.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Model:** $f(x, t) = x e^{-0.5 t}$. Here $n = 1$ and $m = 5$.

**Residual:** $r_i = y_i - x e^{-0.5 t_i}$.

**Jacobian:** A $5 \times 1$ column vector with $J_{i1} = -e^{-0.5 t_i}$:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0000 \\ -0.6065 \\ -0.3679 \\ -0.2231 \\ -0.1353 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $J^T J$ (a scalar for $n=1$):**

$$J^T J = \sum_{i=1}^{5} e^{-t_i} = 1.0000 + 0.6065 + 0.3679 + 0.2231 + 0.1353 = 2.3328$$

**Compute $J^T \mathbf{r}$:**

At $x^{(0)} = 4.5$, residuals are $r_i = y_i - 4.5 e^{-0.5 t_i}$:

- $i=1$: $r_1 = 5.0 - 4.5 = 0.5000$
- $i=2$: $r_2 = 3.4 - 4.5 \times 0.6065 = 3.4 - 2.7293 = 0.6707$
- $i=3$: $r_3 = 2.3 - 4.5 \times 0.3679 = 2.3 - 1.6554 = 0.6446$
- $i=4$: $r_4 = 1.6 - 4.5 \times 0.2231 = 1.6 - 1.0041 = 0.5959$
- $i=5$: $r_5 = 1.1 - 4.5 \times 0.1353 = 1.1 - 0.6090 = 0.4910$

$$J^T \mathbf{r} = (-1.0000)(0.5000) + (-0.6065)(0.6707) + (-0.3679)(0.6446) + (-0.2231)(0.5959) + (-0.1353)(0.4910)$$

$$= -0.5000 - 0.4068 - 0.2372 - 0.1330 - 0.0664 = -1.3434$$

**Solve scalar equation $J^T J \, d = -J^T \mathbf{r}$:**

$$d = \frac{-(-1.3434)}{2.3328} = \frac{1.3434}{2.3328} = 0.5760$$

**Update:** $x^{(1)} = 4.5 + 0.5760 = 5.0760$.

**Iteration 1 → 2 (in brief):** Recompute residuals at $x^{(1)} = 5.0760$ and follow the same steps. After two iterations, the estimate stabilizes near $x \approx 5.35$. The single-parameter case illustrates that Gauss-Newton reduces to a **simple scalar ratio** of the form:

$$x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + \frac{\sum_i e^{-0.5 t_i} r_i^{(k)}}{\sum_i e^{-t_i}}$$

This closed-form one-step update makes the single-parameter case ideal for hand computation in KTU exams.

**Convergence discussion:** Since the model is monotonic in $x$ (larger $x$ means larger $f$ everywhere), the Gauss-Newton method converges **monotonically and rapidly** because the linearization is exact for this model form. With 5 data points and 1 parameter, the system is **over-determined** ($m > n$), ensuring $J^T J > 0$ and the normal equation is well-conditioned.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Jacobian:** Students often write $J_{ij} = +\partial f / \partial x_j$ instead of $-\partial f / \partial x_j$. Recall: residual $r = y - f$, so $J = -(\partial f / \partial x)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$:** The objective $F = \frac{1}{2} \sum r_i^2$ has gradient $J^T \mathbf{r}$ (the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels). But if you write the objective as $\sum r_i^2$ (no $\frac{1}{2}$), the gradient becomes $2 J^T \mathbf{r}$ and the normal equations change. **Stick to the $\frac{1}{2}$ convention.**
> 3. **Inverting $J^T J$ by hand for $n > 2$:** This is error-prone. KTU expects you to set up the normal equations and solve them — for $2 \times 2$ systems, Cramer's rule is acceptable. For $3 \times 3$ or larger, show the system setup and indicate the use of Gaussian elimination.
> 4. **Mixing up the update direction:** Some students write $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + (J^T J)^{-1} J^T \mathbf{r}$ instead of $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - (J^T J)^{-1} J^T \mathbf{r}$. The negative sign is **mandatory** because the search direction is $-\nabla F / H_{GN}$.
> 5. **Not checking convergence criteria:** Always explicitly verify which of $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \varepsilon_3$ has been satisfied at the final iteration. Examiners award 1 mark for this concluding step.
> 6. **Decimal precision:** Round only the **final answer** of each iteration to 4 decimal places. Intermediate calculations should retain at least 5-6 digits to avoid accumulation errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PEPET743 Module 2**
>
> - **Objective:** $F(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{r} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - f(\mathbf{x}, t_i))^2$.
> - **Residual:** $r_i = y_i - f(\mathbf{x}, t_i)$ — always observed minus predicted.
> - **Jacobian entry:** $J_{ij} = -\partial f / \partial x_j$ (note the **negative sign**).
> - **Gradient (Gauss-Newton form):** $\nabla F \approx J^T \mathbf{r}$.
> - **Approximate Hessian:** $H_{GN} = J^T J$ — symmetric, positive semi-definite.
> - **Normal equations (must memorize):** $J^T J \, \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$.
> - **Update rule:** $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ where $\mathbf{d}$ is the solution above.
> - **Three convergence tests:** parameter-norm, function-drop, gradient-norm — termination when **any one** is satisfied.
> - **Advantages over Newton-Raphson:** No second derivatives needed; cheaper per iteration; well-suited for least-squares.
> - **Disadvantages:** Fails if residuals are large or $J^T J$ is rank-deficient; only locally convergent.
> - **Remedy:** Levenberg-Marquardt uses $(J^T J + \mu I) \mathbf{d} = -J^T \mathbf{r}$ for robustness.
> - **Common models in KTU problems:** $x_1 e^{-x_2 t}$ (exponential decay), $x_1 t^{x_2}$ (power law), $x_1 \ln(x_2 t)$ (logarithmic), $x_1 t / (x_2 + t)$ (saturation/Michaelis-Menten).
> - **For $n=1$ problems:** The update reduces to a single scalar $x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + (J^T \mathbf{r}) / (J^T J)$.
> - **Engineering applications:** Drug pharmacokinetics, sensor calibration, fatigue S-N curves, radioactive decay fitting, allometric scaling, chemical reaction kinetics.
> - **Mental check at every iteration:** (i) Compute model $f_i$; (ii) Compute residuals $r_i$; (iii) Build $J$; (iv) Form $J^T J$ and $J^T \mathbf{r}$; (v) Solve linear system; (vi) Update $\mathbf{x}$; (vii) Verify reduction in $F$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Steepest Ascent/Steepest Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method of Steepest Ascent/Steepest Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Method of Steepest Ascent / Steepest Descent)**
> The **Method of Steepest Ascent/Descent** is a first-order iterative gradient-based optimization algorithm used to locate the local extremum (maximum or minimum) of an unconstrained scalar objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. At any given iteration point $\mathbf{x}_k$, the method moves along the direction of the gradient vector $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (for **ascent**, i.e., maximization) or opposite to it $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (for **descent**, i.e., minimization), because the gradient is mathematically proven to point along the direction of maximum rate of change of the function.

Mathematically, the iterative update rule is expressed as:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \, \mathbf{d}_k$$

where $\mathbf{d}_k = +\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ for **ascent**, $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ for **descent**, and $\alpha_k \in \mathbb{R}^+$ is the **scalar step size (learning rate)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker

Imagine a **blindfolded hiker** standing on a foggy, mountainous terrain (the function surface $f(\mathbf{x})$) trying to reach the **highest peak** (maximum) or the **deepest valley** (minimum).

- The hiker cannot see the landscape, but can feel the **slope of the ground beneath their feet**.
- They determine the **steepest uphill direction** (the gradient $\nabla f$) by sampling the terrain locally.
- They take a step ($\alpha_k$) along that direction and stop to re-evaluate the slope.
- This process repeats until the ground becomes flat (gradient $\approx 0$), indicating the summit or valley floor has been reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Steepest"?** Among all possible unit directions $\mathbf{d}$ at a point, the gradient $\nabla f$ provides the **maximum directional derivative**, given by $D_{\mathbf{d}} f = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{d}$. The maximum value of this expression is $\vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$, achieved when $\mathbf{d}$ aligns with $\nabla f$. This is a direct consequence of the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Scalar objective function |
| $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Gradient vector at $\mathbf{x}_k$ |
| $\mathbf{d}_k$ | Search direction |
| $\alpha_k$ | Step size (scalar) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance, typically **$10^{-4}$** to **$10^{-6}$** |
| $\mathbf{g}_k$ | Common shorthand for $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Gradient?

For a differentiable scalar field $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, the **directional derivative** along a unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is:

$$D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \hat{\mathbf{u}}$$

To find the direction $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ that **maximizes** this quantity, we apply the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**:

$$D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} f = \nabla f^T \hat{\mathbf{u}} \leq \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \hat{\mathbf{u}} \vert\vert = \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$$

Equality holds when $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is parallel to $\nabla f$. Therefore:
- **Steepest Ascent direction:** $\mathbf{d}_k = +\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$
- **Steepest Descent direction:** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow (Bulleted Algorithm Steps)

1. **Initialization:** Choose starting point $\mathbf{x}_0$, tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$, and iteration counter $k = 0$.
2. **Gradient Evaluation:** Compute $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.
3. **Convergence Check:** If $\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_k \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$, terminate — current point is a stationary point.
4. **Direction Selection:** Set $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ (descent) or $+\mathbf{g}_k$ (ascent).
5. **Line Search:** Solve the one-dimensional sub-problem to find optimal step size $\alpha_k^*$:

$$\alpha_k^* = \arg\min_{\alpha > 0} \; f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$$

6. **Update:** Compute $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k^* \, \mathbf{d}_k$.
7. **Increment:** Set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Step Size for Quadratic Functions:**
> For a positive-definite quadratic form $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$, the optimal step size along $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ has a **closed-form expression**:
>
> $$\alpha_k^* = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{g}_k}$$
>
> This avoids numerical line search and is **the form KTU examiners expect** in numerical problems.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Formulas for Steepest Descent — High-Yield Board Sheet**

| # | Formula / Relation | Purpose / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Generic iteration (minimization) |
| 2 | $\alpha_k^* = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Optimal step for quadratic $f$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{b}$ | Gradient of quadratic form |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}^* = -\mathbf{Q}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | True minimizer (theoretical benchmark) |
| 5 | $\cos \theta_k = \dfrac{-\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_k \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \vert\vert} = 0$ | Consecutive gradients are **orthogonal** for exact line search |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert} \leq \left( \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} \right)$ | Linear convergence rate, $\kappa$ = condition number |
| 7 | Stop Criterion: $\vert\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$ or $\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Termination condition |

### 2.4 Properties & Engineering Utility

- **Zig-Zag Trajectory:** Consecutive search directions are **mutually orthogonal** when exact line search is used, causing the classic zig-zag pattern in poorly scaled problems.
- **Convergence:** Guaranteed to converge for **convex** and **Lipschitz-continuous gradient** functions.
- **Sensitivity to Scaling:** Slow convergence on **ill-conditioned** ($\kappa \gg 1$) problems; fast on well-conditioned ($\kappa \approx 1$) problems.
- **Engineering Applications:**
  * Training simple machine-learning models (least-squares regression).
  * Structural optimization (minimizing weight subject to constraints, solved via penalty methods).
  * Process control parameter tuning.
  * Computer vision: image gradient-based segmentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Step Size for Quadratic Objective

**Given:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$, with $\mathbf{Q}$ symmetric positive-definite.

**Step 1 — Form the one-dimensional sub-problem along $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.**

Define $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$.

**Step 2 — Expand $\phi(\alpha)$ explicitly.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) &= \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{Q} (\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + c
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k + \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k + c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We used the fact that for symmetric $\mathbf{Q}$, $\mathbf{x}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{d}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k$ (scalar equality), so the two middle linear terms combine into $\alpha \, \mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k$.

**Step 3 — Recall that the gradient at $\mathbf{x}_k$ is $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{b}$.**

Since $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k &= \mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q}(-\mathbf{g}_k) + \mathbf{b}^T (-\mathbf{g}_k) = -(\mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q} + \mathbf{b}^T)\mathbf{g}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k
\end{aligned}
$$

(The transpose of a scalar equals itself.)

**Step 4 — Differentiate $\phi(\alpha)$ and set equal to zero for the minimum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\phi}{d\alpha} &= \alpha \, \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k + (\mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha \, \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\alpha$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_k^* = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{d}_k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ and $\mathbf{g}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{d}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k$ (because the denominator is a scalar, invariant to sign), the standard form is obtained:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_k^* = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{g}_k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (Numerical Demonstration)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$ using steepest descent starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

**Step 1 — Identify $\mathbf{Q}$ and $\mathbf{b}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + (-4, -1)\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Compute gradient: $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{b}$.**

At $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Search direction: $\mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = (4, 2)^T$.**

**Step 4 — Compute optimal $\alpha_0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{g}_0 &= (-4)^2 + (-2)^2 = 16 + 4 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{g}_0 &= \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -16 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} = 64 + 8 = 72
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_0^* = \dfrac{20}{72} = \dfrac{5}{18} \approx 0.2778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update point.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0^* \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \dfrac{5}{18} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20/18 \\ 10/18 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/9 \\ 5/9 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Recompute gradient at $\mathbf{x}_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_1 = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \cdot 10/9 - 4 \\ 2 \cdot 5/9 - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 40/9 - 36/9 \\ 10/9 - 18/9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/9 \\ -8/9 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (Orthogonality Property):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{g}_1 = (-4)(4/9) + (-2)(-8/9) = -16/9 + 16/9 = 0 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the orthogonality of consecutive gradients for exact line search.

**Theoretical Minimum (for comparison):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}^* = -\mathbf{Q}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = -\begin{bmatrix} 1/4 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The solution $(10/9, 5/9)^T \approx (1.111, 0.556)^T$ is **already very close** to the true minimum $(1,1)^T$ after just one iteration — illustrating fast convergence on well-conditioned problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Initialize: x0, eps, k = 0]
    A1 --> A2{Convergence met?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Compute gradient g_k]
    A3 --> A4[Set direction d_k = -g_k]
    A4 --> A5[Line search: find alpha_k]
    A5 --> A6[Update x_{k+1} = x_k + alpha_k * d_k]
    A6 --> A7[k = k + 1]
    A7 --> A2
    A2 -- Yes --> A8([Return x_k as optimum])
    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style A8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style A2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style A5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
```

### 4.2 Search Trajectory Diagram (Conceptual Level Sets)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEVELSETS [Contour Map of f]
        B1((x0)) -->|Step 1| B2((x1))
        B2 -->|Step 2| B3((x2))
        B3 -->|Step 3| B4((x3))
        B4 -->|...| B5([x star - Optimum])
    end
    style B1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B4 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Steepest Descent Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Initial Guess x0] --> C2[Gradient Module]
    C2 --> C3[Norm Check Module]
    C3 -- Norm > eps --> C4[Direction Generator: -g]
    C3 -- Norm <= eps --> C5[Output: Converged]
    C4 --> C6[Line Search Engine]
    C6 --> C7[Step Size alpha_k]
    C7 --> C8[Updater: x new = x + alpha d]
    C8 --> C2
    style C1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style C5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C6 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Interpretation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1([Function Surface f]) --> D2[Gradient nabla f]
    D2 --> D3[Direction -nabla f]
    D3 --> D4[Step Size alpha]
    D4 --> D5[New Point]
    D5 --> D1
    style D1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
    style D2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q1. State and justify the direction of steepest descent for minimizing a function $f(\mathbf{x})$.**

**Model Answer:**
The direction of steepest descent at point $\mathbf{x}_k$ is given by $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Justification: By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, the directional derivative $D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f = \nabla f^T \hat{\mathbf{u}} \leq \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$, with equality only when $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is parallel to $\nabla f$. Since minimization requires the most negative rate of change, we choose $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ opposite to $\nabla f$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Write the iterative formula for steepest descent and define each term. Mention one convergence criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_k$ is the current iterate, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is the gradient vector, and $\alpha_k$ is the scalar step size obtained via line search. Convergence criterion: $\vert\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is a small prescribed tolerance (e.g., $10^{-6}$). **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Quadratic Function Iteration*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the optimal step size $\alpha_k$ for minimizing a quadratic function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x} + c$ using the steepest descent method, where $\mathbf{Q}$ is symmetric positive-definite.

**Model Solution:**

Define $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)$. Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) &= \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + c
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\dfrac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = 0$ and using $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\phi}{d\alpha} &= \alpha \, \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_k^* = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{g}_k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, 1)^T$, perform **two iterations** of steepest descent to minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 4x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 8x_1 - 2x_2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Identification:** $\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Iteration 0:** $\mathbf{g}_0 = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{b} = (8 - 8, \, 2 - 2)^T = (0, 0)^T$.

Since $\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_0 \vert\vert = 0$, the algorithm has converged at $\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 1)^T$ in **zero iterations** — the starting point is already optimal because the level sets are axis-aligned.

**(Alternative demonstration with a non-optimal starting point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$):**

$\mathbf{g}_0 = (-8, -2)^T$.

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{64 + 4}{64 \cdot 8 + 4 \cdot 2} = \frac{68}{520} = \frac{17}{130} \approx 0.1308
$$

$\mathbf{x}_1 = (0,0)^T + 0.1308 \cdot (8, 2)^T = (1.046, 0.262)^T$.

$\mathbf{g}_1 = (8 \cdot 1.046 - 8, \; 2 \cdot 0.262 - 2)^T = (0.369, -1.477)^T$.

Orthogonality check: $(-8)(0.369) + (-2)(-1.477) = -2.95 + 2.95 \approx 0 \;\checkmark$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual & Convexity Analysis*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain why the steepest descent method exhibits a **zig-zag pattern** in its iterates. Prove the **orthogonality property** of consecutive gradients.

**Model Solution:**

When exact line search is used, the gradient at the new point $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$ must be **orthogonal to the search direction** $\mathbf{d}_k$, because the function is at a stationary point along the line.

Since $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, we get $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} \perp \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, which gives $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} \perp \mathbf{g}_k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This forces every new direction to be orthogonal to the previous one, causing the iterates to **zig-zag**, especially severe for ill-conditioned problems where the level sets are elongated ellipses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **convergence behavior** of steepest descent for **(i)** well-conditioned and **(ii)** ill-conditioned quadratic functions. Mention how the **condition number** $\kappa(\mathbf{Q})$ affects convergence rate.

**Model Solution:**

The convergence rate for a quadratic is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert} \leq \left( \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Well-conditioned ($\kappa \approx 1$):** Ratio $\approx 0$ → very fast convergence, often within $O(n)$ iterations.

**(ii) Ill-conditioned ($\kappa \gg 1$):** Ratio $\approx 1$ → extremely slow, hundreds of iterations needed for elongated elliptical contours.

Engineering remedy: use **preconditioning** or switch to **Newton's method** or **conjugate gradient method**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Confusing $+\nabla f$ (ascent) with $-\nabla f$ (descent). Always read the question — "minimize" demands the negative gradient.
> 2. **Denominator Mistake:** In $\alpha_k = \tfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{g}_k}$, students frequently compute $\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{g}_k$ incorrectly by **forgetting to multiply by $\mathbf{Q}$ first**. Always compute $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{g}_k$ then take the dot product with $\mathbf{g}_k$.
> 3. **Convergence Criterion Omission:** Failing to specify $\varepsilon$ or the stopping rule when asked about termination.
> 4. **Missing Orthogonality Proof:** In Question A(a) above, merely stating the formula without derivation loses up to **3 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Core Rule:** Move in the direction of $-\nabla f$ for minimization and $+\nabla f$ for maximization.
- **Optimal $\alpha$ for Quadratics:** $\alpha_k^* = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{g}_k}$ — **memorize this verbatim** for KTU numericals.
- **Orthogonality Property:** Consecutive gradients are mutually perpendicular under exact line search — this is the cause of the **zig-zag trajectory**.
- **Convergence:** Guaranteed for strictly convex $f$; rate depends on $\kappa(\mathbf{Q})$.
- **Convergence Rate:** Bounded by $\left( \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} \right)$ per iteration.
- **Stopping Criteria:** $\vert\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$ or $\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert\vert \leq \varepsilon$ or maximum iteration count.
- **True Minimizer:** $\mathbf{x}^* = -\mathbf{Q}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ for quadratic forms (useful for verifying iterations).
- **Limitations:** Slow on ill-conditioned problems; not suitable for highly non-convex landscapes without modifications (momentum, Adam, etc.).
- **Extensions in Modern Use:** Preconditioning, Conjugate Gradient (CG), and quasi-Newton methods (BFGS) all build upon the steepest descent foundation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conjugate Gradient Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conjugate Gradient Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conjugate Gradient Method (CGM):** An iterative numerical algorithm used to solve systems of linear equations of the form $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, where $[\mathbf{A}]$ is a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) matrix, and is also widely applied to unconstrained nonlinear optimization problems of the form $\min f(\mathbf{x})$. The method generates a sequence of **conjugate directions** with respect to $[\mathbf{A}]$, guaranteeing finite convergence in at most $n$ iterations for an $n$-dimensional quadratic problem.

The conjugacy condition between two non-zero vectors $\mathbf{d}_i$ and $\mathbf{d}_j$ with respect to a symmetric positive-definite matrix $[\mathbf{A}]$ is mathematically expressed as:

$$\mathbf{d}_i^{T} [\mathbf{A}] \mathbf{d}_j = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

Such a set of vectors is called **A-conjugate** (or conjugate with respect to $[\mathbf{A}]$), and they form a basis that is mutually orthogonal under the inner product defined by $[\mathbf{A}]$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mountain Hiker Analogy:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded at the peak of a mountain and need to descend to the lowest valley (the optimum) using only a slope-reading device (gradient). The **Steepest Descent Method** would make you zigzag back and forth across the valley, because every step is perpendicular to the previous contour.
>
> The **Conjugate Gradient Method**, however, has a "memory" of the previous directions. It walks down the slope in the first step, then carefully chooses the *next* direction that is **not just steepest**, but also **does not spoil the progress already made**. This is the essence of *conjugacy* — each new direction is intelligently chosen so that the error along previous directions is never reactivated.
>
> Result: Instead of zigzagging, the method takes **at most $n$ clean, decisive steps** to reach the bottom of an $n$-dimensional quadratic bowl.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following metrics govern the performance and classification of CGM variants:

- **Maximum Iterations ($n$):** Equal to the number of design variables for exact convergence on a quadratic.
- **Convergence Tolerance ($\epsilon$):** Typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$, a scalar.
- **Restart Criterion:** Applied when search directions lose conjugacy in non-quadratic problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEPET743 Module 3):** The Conjugate Gradient Method must be studied in two contexts:
> 1. As a **linear solver** for $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ (quadratic minimization linkage).
> 2. As a **nonlinear optimizer** (Fletcher–Reeves, Polak–Ribière, Hestenes–Stiefel updates).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Conjugacy Works

The Conjugate Gradient Method rests on three pillars of theoretical reasoning:

- **Pillar 1 — Quadratic Formulation:** Any unconstrained quadratic optimization problem can be written as:
$$\min f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$$
  where $[\mathbf{A}]$ is symmetric positive-definite (SPD), guaranteeing a unique minimum where the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$.

- **Pillar 2 — Optimal Line Search:** Once a search direction $\mathbf{d}_k$ is fixed, the optimal step length $\alpha_k$ that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ is given analytically by:
$$\alpha_k = -\frac{\mathbf{d}_k^{T} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T} [\mathbf{A}] \mathbf{d}_k}$$

- **Pillar 3 — Conjugacy Guarantees No Rework:** Because conjugate directions are independent under the $[\mathbf{A}]$-inner product, the error component along each previous direction is **never regenerated**. This eliminates the zigzag pathology of steepest descent.

### 2.2 Algorithm Logic Steps (High-Yield)

The standard nonlinear CGM procedure is summarized in five sequential steps:

1. **Initialization:** Choose initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0$, compute initial gradient $\mathbf{g}_0 = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)$, set initial search direction $\mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0$, and set iteration counter $k = 0$.

2. **Line Search:** Compute the optimal step length $\alpha_k$ via exact or inexact line search along $\mathbf{d}_k$.

3. **Update Position:** Compute the new design point $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$.

4. **Compute New Gradient:** Evaluate $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})$.

5. **Conjugacy Update:** Compute the scalar $\beta_{k+1}$ using one of the recognized formulas, then update the search direction as $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} \mathbf{d}_k$.

6. **Convergence Check:** Stop if $\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert \leq \epsilon$ or $k \geq n$; otherwise, increment $k$ and return to Step 2.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation a student needs to solve CGM problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Symbols are defined immediately below the table.

| **Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Purpose / Use** |
|--------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| Quadratic Objective | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$ | Converting QP to linear system |
| Gradient (Quadratic) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$ | Stationarity check |
| Conjugacy Condition | $\mathbf{d}_i^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_j = 0, \; i \neq j$ | Direction independence |
| Optimal Step Size | $\alpha_k = -\dfrac{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}$ | Exact line search |
| Fletcher–Reeves $\beta$ | $\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k} \Vert^{2}}$ | Strong convergence |
| Polak–Ribière $\beta$ | $\beta_{k+1}^{PR} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_{k})}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k} \Vert^{2}}$ | Better practical behavior |
| Hestenes–Stiefel $\beta$ | $\beta_{k+1}^{HS} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_{k})}{\mathbf{d}_{k}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_{k})}$ | Direction-based |
| Direction Update | $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} \mathbf{d}_k$ | Generating next direction |
| Position Update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | Generating next iterate |
| Convergence Test | $\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert \leq \epsilon$ | Termination criterion |

**Notation Glossary:**
- $\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — design variable vector at iteration $k$.
- $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — gradient vector.
- $\mathbf{d}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — search direction vector.
- $[\mathbf{A}] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — symmetric positive-definite Hessian.
- $\alpha_k \in \mathbb{R}$ — scalar step length.
- $\beta_{k+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ — scalar conjugacy coefficient.
- $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ — Euclidean ($\ell_2$) norm.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The CGM is the **workhorse solver** for problems involving:
> - **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Solving $[\mathbf{K}]\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ for displacement fields in structures.
> - **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Pressure-Poisson equations in incompressible flow.
> - **Machine Learning:** Training large-scale Support Vector Machines and Neural Networks (where Hessian-vector products are needed).
> - **Structural Optimization:** Topology and shape optimization of aerospace and civil structures.
> - **Inverse Problems:** Tomography, geophysics, and signal reconstruction.
>
> Its appeal lies in requiring **no matrix storage** of $[\mathbf{A}]$ — only matrix-vector products — making it ideal for very large sparse systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Step Length $\alpha_k$ for a Quadratic Function

Consider minimizing $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$ along a fixed direction $\mathbf{d}_k$ from current point $\mathbf{x}_k$. Define the scalar function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$.

Substitute the new point into $f$:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^{T}[\mathbf{A}](\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) - \mathbf{b}^{T}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + c$$

Expand the quadratic product term using distributive law:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k + \tfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{d}_k + c$$

Group constants, linear, and quadratic terms in $\alpha$:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \alpha \left(\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{d}_k\right) + \text{constant}$$

Recognize that the bracket equals $\mathbf{d}_k^{T}([\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k$:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \alpha \, \mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k + \text{constant}$$

Take the derivative with respect to $\alpha$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality condition for a scalar):

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = \alpha \, \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

Solve for $\alpha_k$:

$$\boxed{\alpha_k = -\frac{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conjugacy Update (Fletcher–Reeves)

Starting from the position update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$, the gradient evolution is derived by differentiating the quadratic:

$$\mathbf{g}_{k+1} = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{b} = [\mathbf{A}](\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) - \mathbf{b}$$

Rearrange to express the gradient increment:

$$\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k = \alpha_k [\mathbf{A}] \mathbf{d}_k$$

The conjugate direction update ansatz is assumed to be of the form:

$$\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} \mathbf{d}_k$$

The conjugacy requirement $\mathbf{d}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$ is imposed. Substitute the expression for $\mathbf{d}_{k+1}$:

$$(-\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} \mathbf{d}_k)^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$$

Expand using linearity of transpose:

$$-\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \beta_{k+1} \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$$

Isolate $\beta_{k+1}$:

$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}$$

Now use the identity $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k = \alpha_k [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$, which gives $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = (\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)/\alpha_k$. Substitute:

$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)}$$

This is the **Hestenes–Stiefel** formula. For a quadratic with exact line search, one can show $\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k+1} = 0$, which after algebraic manipulation (using $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k = \alpha_k[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$ and the conjugate orthogonality of residuals) reduces to the **Fletcher–Reeves** form:

$$\boxed{\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{g}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k}} = \frac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k} \Vert^{2}}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Finite Termination Property

Consider the residual at step $k+1$ defined as $\mathbf{r}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1}$ (negated gradient). Using the position update for a quadratic:

$$\mathbf{r}_{k+1} = -[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_{k+1} + \mathbf{b} = -[\mathbf{A}](\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) + \mathbf{b}$$

Substituting the optimal $\alpha_k = -\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{r}_k / (\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k)$ and expanding:

$$\mathbf{r}_{k+1} = \mathbf{r}_k - \alpha_k [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$$

Apply this residual update inductively. Since the search directions $\{\mathbf{d}_0, \mathbf{d}_1, \dots, \mathbf{d}_{n-1}\}$ form a linearly independent (and indeed $[\mathbf{A}]$-conjugate) set, the residual $\mathbf{r}_{k+1}$ is confined to the span of $\{\mathbf{r}_0, [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{r}_0, \dots, [\mathbf{A}]^{k}\mathbf{r}_0\}$, known as the **Krylov subspace** $\mathcal{K}_k([\mathbf{A}], \mathbf{r}_0)$.

For an $n$-dimensional system, the Krylov subspace spans the entire $\mathbb{R}^n$ at iteration $n$, guaranteeing that $\mathbf{r}_n = \mathbf{0}$, i.e., the algorithm converges in at most $n$ iterations.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$ using CGM, starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^{T}$.

**Solution Setup:** The Hessian and linear coefficient are:
$$[\mathbf{A}] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The exact optimum is $\mathbf{x}^{*} = [\mathbf{A}]^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (1, 1)^{T}$.

**Iteration 0:**
$$\mathbf{g}_0 = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Optimal step:
$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}\mathbf{g}_0}{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_0} = -\frac{-16 - 4}{64 + 8} = \frac{20}{72} = \frac{5}{18}$$

New point:
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 20/18 \\ 10/18 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.111 \\ 0.556 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4(1.111) - 4 \\ 2(0.556) - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.444 \\ -0.889 \end{bmatrix}$$

Fletcher–Reeves update:
$$\beta_1 = \frac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_1 \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_0 \Vert^{2}} = \frac{0.197 + 0.790}{16 + 4} = \frac{0.988}{20} = 0.0494$$

New direction:
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = -\mathbf{g}_1 + \beta_1 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.444 \\ 0.889 \end{bmatrix} + 0.0494 \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.246 \\ 0.988 \end{bmatrix}$$

Optimal step:
$$\alpha_1 = -\frac{\mathbf{d}_1^{T}\mathbf{g}_1}{\mathbf{d}_1^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_1} = -\frac{(-0.246)(0.444) + (0.988)(-0.889)}{(0.246^{2})(4) + (0.988^{2})(2)}$$

Numerator:
$$(-0.246)(0.444) + (0.988)(-0.889) = -0.109 - 0.878 = -0.987$$

Denominator:
$$(0.0605)(4) + (0.976)(2) = 0.242 + 1.953 = 2.195$$

$$\alpha_1 = -\frac{-0.987}{2.195} = 0.450$$

Final point:
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 + \alpha_1 \mathbf{d}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.111 \\ 0.556 \end{bmatrix} + 0.450 \begin{bmatrix} -0.246 \\ 0.988 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 \\ 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Result:** $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1.000, 1.000)^{T} = \mathbf{x}^{*}$. **Convergence achieved in exactly $n = 2$ iterations**, confirming the finite termination property.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithm Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize x0, compute g0]
    B --> C[Set d0 equals -g0]
    C --> D[Set k equals 0]
    D --> E{Converged? norm of gk less than epsilon}
    E -- Yes --> F([Output x star and stop])
    E -- No --> G[Compute optimal step alpha k via line search]
    G --> H[Update x k+1 equals xk plus alpha k times dk]
    H --> I[Compute new gradient g k+1]
    I --> J[Compute beta k+1 using Fletcher Reeves or Polak Ribiere formula]
    J --> K[Update direction d k+1 equals -g k+1 plus beta k+1 times dk]
    K --> L[Increment k equals k plus 1]
    L --> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of CGM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Initial Guess x0] --> A2[Function f of x]
        A2 --> A3[Gradient Function]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Computational Core]
        B1[Gradient Evaluator Module] --> B2[Line Search Engine]
        B2 --> B3[Conjugacy Coefficient Calculator]
        B3 --> B4[Direction Generator]
        B4 --> B5[Position Updater]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Optimum x star] --> C2[Optimal Function Value]
        C2 --> C3[Iteration History Log]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B5
    B5 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B1
    B5 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Conjugacy Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Start with x0 on quadratic surface] --> M2[Compute gradient g0 equals descent direction d0]
    M2 --> M3[Move along d0 to minimize f along line]
    M3 --> M4[At x1, new gradient g1 is computed]
    M4 --> M5{Is new direction conjugate to d0?}
    M5 -- Yes via beta update --> M6[Generate d1 that does not spoil x1 progress]
    M5 -- No, plain steepest --> N1[Risk of zigzag behavior]
    M6 --> M7[Convergence in at most n steps]
    N1 --> N2[Many iterations required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each diagram uses **A-prefix** identifiers for the algorithm flowchart (steps `A` to `L`), **B-prefix** identifiers for the functional architecture (modules `B1` to `B5`), and **M/N-prefix** identifiers for the conceptual map. Purely alphanumeric, no reserved keywords used as node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Conjugate Gradient Method and state its key property for quadratic functions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Conjugate Gradient Method (CGM) is an iterative algorithm that generates a sequence of search directions **conjugate with respect to the Hessian matrix** $[\mathbf{A}]$ to minimize a quadratic function. Its key property is that for an $n$-dimensional quadratic problem, it converges to the exact optimum in **at most $n$ iterations**, since each direction eliminates one degree of freedom of the error. The conjugacy condition $\mathbf{d}_i^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_j = 0$ ensures that progress along one direction is not undone by subsequent moves.

**(Mark Allocation: 1 + 2)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Write the Fletcher–Reeves formula for computing $\beta_{k+1}$ and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Fletcher–Reeves formula is given by:
$$\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \frac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert^{2}}$$
where $\mathbf{g}_k$ and $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}$ are the gradient vectors at iterations $k$ and $k+1$. Its significance lies in guaranteeing **global convergence** for general nonlinear functions (under suitable line search conditions), making it one of the most theoretically robust nonlinear CGM variants. It ensures that the descent direction is generated and that $\beta_{k+1} \geq 0$, preventing direction reversals.

**(Mark Allocation: 1 for formula + 2 for significance)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k$, leading to an *ascent* direction. **Lose 2 marks.**
> - In numerical problems, many students **swap the order of matrix-vector multiplication**, treating $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$ as $\mathbf{d}_k[\mathbf{A}]$. **Lose 1 mark** if dimensions are inconsistent.
> - Computing $\beta$ using the **wrong variant** (e.g., Polak–Ribière when Fletcher–Reeves was specified) is a **common error**.
> - Always check $\alpha_k > 0$ — if not, line search is incorrect and the answer carries through with the error. **No partial credit for step size.**
> - State the **conjugacy condition explicitly** in derivations; examiners award 2 marks just for writing it.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Conjugate Gradient Theory

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the optimal step length $\alpha_k$ for minimizing a quadratic function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$ along a given search direction $\mathbf{d}_k$. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

Let $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$. Substituting:
$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^{T}[\mathbf{A}](\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) - \mathbf{b}^{T}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + c$$

Expanding and using $[\mathbf{A}]^{T} = [\mathbf{A}]$:
$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \alpha (\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{d}_k) + \text{constant}$$

Since $\mathbf{g}_k = [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$, the linear coefficient is $\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k$. Differentiating and setting to zero:
$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = \alpha \, \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

Solving:
$$\boxed{\alpha_k = -\frac{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}}$$

**Assumptions:** $[\mathbf{A}]$ is symmetric positive-definite, $\mathbf{d}_k \neq \mathbf{0}$, and exact line search is performed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Starting from the conjugacy condition $\mathbf{d}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$ and the direction update formula $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k$, derive the Fletcher–Reeves formula for $\beta_{k+1}$.

**Model Solution:**

Substitute the direction update into the conjugacy condition:
$$(-\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k)^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$$

Expanding:
$$-\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = 0$$

Isolating:
$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}$$

Using the gradient recurrence $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} = \mathbf{g}_k + \alpha_k[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$, we get $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k = (\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)/\alpha_k$. Substituting:
$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k)}$$

For exact line search, $\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k+1} = 0$, hence:
$$\mathbf{d}_k^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k) = -\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k$$

Also, $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = 0$ (mutual orthogonality of successive gradients for quadratic CGM). Therefore:
$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \frac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert^{2}}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Numerical Application

**(a) [7 Marks]** Solve the following system using the Conjugate Gradient Method, taking $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^{T}$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Perform at least two iterations and verify the conjugacy condition at the end.

**Model Solution:**

Define $\mathbf{r}_0 = \mathbf{b} - [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, 2)^{T}$. Set $\mathbf{d}_0 = \mathbf{r}_0 = (1, 2)^{T}$.

**Iteration 0:** Compute $\alpha_0$:
$$\alpha_0 = \frac{\mathbf{r}_0^{T}\mathbf{r}_0}{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_0} = \frac{1 + 4}{(1, 2)\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}} = \frac{5}{(1)(6) + (2)(7)} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25$$

Update solution and residual:
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.50 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{r}_1 = \mathbf{r}_0 - \alpha_0 [\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} - 0.25 \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:** Compute $\beta_1$:
$$\beta_1 = \frac{\mathbf{r}_1^{T}\mathbf{r}_1}{\mathbf{r}_0^{T}\mathbf{r}_0} = \frac{0.25 + 0.0625}{1 + 4} = \frac{0.3125}{5} = 0.0625$$

New direction:
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = \mathbf{r}_1 + \beta_1 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix} + 0.0625 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4375 \\ 0.375 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1 step size:**
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{\mathbf{r}_1^{T}\mathbf{r}_1}{\mathbf{d}_1^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_1}$$

Denominator:
$$\mathbf{d}_1^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_1 = (-0.4375, 0.375)\begin{bmatrix} 4(-0.4375) + 1(0.375) \\ 1(-0.4375) + 3(0.375) \end{bmatrix} = (-0.4375)(-1.375) + (0.375)(0.6875)$$

$$= 0.6016 + 0.2578 = 0.8594$$

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{0.3125}{0.8594} = 0.3636$$

Updated solution:
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 + \alpha_1 \mathbf{d}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.50 \end{bmatrix} + 0.3636 \begin{bmatrix} -0.4375 \\ 0.375 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.091 \\ 0.636 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification of Conjugacy:**
$$\mathbf{d}_0^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_1 = (1, 2)\begin{bmatrix} 4(-0.4375) + 0.375 \\ -0.4375 + 3(0.375) \end{bmatrix} = (1)(-1.375) + (2)(0.6875) = -1.375 + 1.375 = 0$$

The conjugacy condition $\mathbf{d}_0^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_1 = 0$ is **verified**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the Conjugate Gradient Method with the Steepest Descent Method in terms of convergence behavior, number of iterations, and computational cost per iteration. State at least four differences.

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Steepest Descent** | **Conjugate Gradient** |
|------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Direction Choice** | Pure negative gradient: $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ | Conjugate: $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k$ |
| **Convergence for Quadratic** | Infinite (often zigzag) | Finite (at most $n$ steps) |
| **Iterations for $n$-var problem** | Often $> n$ | At most $n$ |
| **Memory Required** | Stores only $\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{g}_k$ | Stores $\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{g}_k, \mathbf{d}_k$ (3 vectors) |
| **Cost per Iteration** | Low: 1 gradient + 1 line search | Low–moderate: 1 gradient + 1 matrix-vector product |
| **Sensitivity to Conditioning** | High (slow for ill-conditioned $[\mathbf{A}]$) | Low (handles ill-conditioning via conjugacy) |
| **Zigzag Behavior** | Yes, pathological | No (conjugacy eliminates zigzags) |
| **Restart Required** | No | Yes, periodically for nonlinear problems |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Direction Generation:** Steepest descent uses only the local gradient; CGM incorporates history via the $\beta$ term to prevent undoing previous progress.

2. **Convergence Speed:** For ill-conditioned quadratic problems, steepest descent may require hundreds of iterations; CGM completes in $n$ iterations exactly.

3. **Line Search:** Steepest descent often uses approximate or exact line search; CGM's optimal step is analytically computable for quadratics.

4. **Practical Suitability:** CGM is preferred for large sparse systems (FEA, CFD) because it requires no $[\mathbf{A}]$ inversion and only matrix-vector products.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conjugate Gradient Method**

- **Definition:** Iterative method using $[\mathbf{A}]$-conjugate directions to solve $[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ or minimize quadratics.
- **Core Requirement:** Hessian $[\mathbf{A}]$ must be **symmetric positive-definite (SPD)**.
- **Conjugacy Condition:** $\mathbf{d}_i^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_j = 0, \; i \neq j$ — the only equality to memorize.
- **Optimal Step Size:** $\alpha_k = -\dfrac{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^{T}[\mathbf{A}]\mathbf{d}_k}$ — always positive when $\mathbf{d}_k$ is a descent direction.
- **Fletcher–Reeves:** $\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \Vert^{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert^{2}}$ — strongest convergence guarantee.
- **Polak–Ribière:** $\beta_{k+1}^{PR} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_{k})}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert^{2}}$ — better practical performance, may be truncated to $\beta^{+} = \max(\beta, 0)$.
- **Finite Termination:** Exact optimum reached in at most $n$ iterations for $n$-dimensional quadratic.
- **Restart Criterion:** For nonlinear problems, restart every $n$ iterations by setting $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.
- **Direction Update Sign:** Always $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1}\mathbf{d}_k$ — **negative** gradient sign is mandatory.
- **Convergence Test:** Stop when $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \leq \epsilon$, typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$.
- **Krylov Subspace Connection:** Iterates lie in $\mathcal{K}_k([\mathbf{A}], \mathbf{r}_0)$.
- **Engineering Applications:** FEA, CFD, ML training, structural optimization.
- **No Matrix Storage:** Only matrix-vector products required — ideal for large sparse systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Law Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Law Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Design Optimization, a **Power Law Form** is a mathematical representation in which an objective function, constraint, or system response is expressed as a product of power functions of the decision (design) variables. The canonical power law expression for a single term is:

$$f(x) = c \cdot x_1^{a_1} \cdot x_2^{a_2} \cdot x_3^{a_3} \cdots x_n^{a_n}$$

where $c$ is a constant coefficient (also called the **strength coefficient**), $x_i > 0$ are positive design variables, and $a_i \in \mathbb{R}$ are real-valued **power exponents** (or **elasticities**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power Law Forms are the *fundamental building block* of **Geometric Programming (GP)**, a class of nonlinear optimization problems where the objective and constraints are structured as sums and products of power functions. Mastery of this topic is essential for the Module 3 board questions.

The two principal structures derived from power law forms are:

| Structure | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomial** | $f(x) = c \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_i}$ | Single product power function |
| **Posynomial** | $g(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_{ik}^{a_{ik}}$ | Sum of monomials (generalized polynomial) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **suspension cable bridge**. The strength of the cable depends *not linearly*, but on the **product** of its cross-sectional area (raised to some power) and material yield strength (raised to another power). Doubling one variable does *not* double the capacity — instead, the response scales according to a **power law**. This is exactly the structure of a monomial: the system response **multiplies** contributions, with each contribution **scaled by an exponent** representing its sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples of Power Law Forms:**
> - **Structural mechanics:** Stress concentration factors, fatigue life (Basquin's law: $N = \sigma^{b}$).
> - **Fluid dynamics:** Pressure drop in pipes (Hagen-Poiseuille: $\Delta P \propto Q^{1.75}$).
> - **Cost engineering:** Cost of pressure vessels, gears, and beams scale with size raised to fractional powers (0.6–0.8 due to economies of scale).
> - **Heat transfer:** Convective heat loss $\dot{Q} \propto \Delta T^{1.33}$ (natural convection correlations).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **exponent $a_i$** is dimensionless (it is an elasticity).
- The **coefficient $c$** carries the physical units of the response (e.g., N, W, kg, ₹).
- For a **posynomial**, the sum is dimensionally consistent only if all $c_k$ share the same units.
- Standard classification: $a_i = 0 \Rightarrow$ **constant term**; $a_i = 1 \Rightarrow$ **linear factor**; $a_i = -1 \Rightarrow$ **inverse factor**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Power law forms are structured to exploit a remarkable mathematical property: **logarithmic transformation converts products into sums and powers into multiplicative constants**, making intrinsically nonlinear problems amenable to **convex optimization**.

**Step 1 — Identify the decision variables** $x_i > 0$ (positive by definition in GP).

**Step 2 — Express the objective and constraints** as monomials, posynomials, or signomials.

**Step 3 — Classify the problem type:**
- **Pure Geometric Program (GP):** objective is a posynomial to be minimized, constraints are posynomials bounded above by 1.
- **Signomial Geometric Program (SGP):** at least one constraint contains **negative coefficients** (signomial).
- **Complementary GP (CGP):** involves reverse inequality posynomial constraints.
- **Generalized GP (GGP):** handles non-posynomial forms via additional transformations.

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithmic / exponential substitution** $x_i = e^{y_i}$ to convert the problem into a **convex program**.

**Step 5 — Apply duality** to obtain a **dual geometric program**, which is a *convex optimization* problem that can be solved via standard KKT conditions.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why positivity ($x_i > 0$)?** Because the logarithm $\ln(x_i)$ is undefined for non-positive arguments. The logarithmic transform is the bridge from nonlinear to linear structure.
- **Why monomials are easy?** A monomial becomes an exponential of a linear function after substitution: $c \prod x_i^{a_i} = \exp(\ln c + \sum a_i y_i)$ — a convex function.
- **Why posynomials are harder?** A posynomial is a *sum* of exponentials, which is convex but not linear — hence GP requires dedicated dual theory.
- **Why study signomials?** Engineering problems often yield *negative coefficients* (e.g., costs subtractions, negative constraint slack), so extending GP to signomials broadens applicability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following equations. Direct marks are awarded in Part A and Part B for writing and applying these forms correctly.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(x) = c \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_i}$ | **Canonical monomial** with $c > 0$, $x_i > 0$ |
| 2 | $g(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_{ik}^{a_{ik}}$ | **Posynomial** (sum of $m$ monomials) |
| 3 | $f(x) = e^{a^T y}$ where $y_i = \ln x_i$ | Exponential form after log substitution |
| 4 | $\ln f(x) = \ln c + \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \ln x_i$ | Logarithmic linearization of monomial |
| 5 | $\text{Minimize } g_0(x)$ s.t. $g_j(x) \le 1,\; h_l(x) = 1$ | **Standard GP primal form** |
| 6 | $\text{elasticity of } x_i = \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \ln x_i} = a_i$ | Sensitivity / exponent |
| 7 | $\max v(\lambda) = \prod_k \left[ \frac{c_k}{\delta_k} \right]^{\delta_k} \left[ \sum_i \lambda_{i0} \right]^{\lambda_{i0}}$ | **Dual GP objective** (Beightler-Shetty form) |
| 8 | $\sum_{k \in K(j)} \delta_k = \lambda_{j0}$ and $\sum_{k} \delta_k a_{ik} = 0$ | **Orthogonality & normalization** dual constraints |
| 9 | $\delta_k = \lambda_{j0} \cdot \frac{c_k g_k(x^*)}{g_j(x^*)}$ | Primal-dual relation |
| 10 | $g(x) = c_1 x_1^{a_1} x_2^{a_2} - c_2 x_1^{a_3} x_2^{a_4}$ | **Signomial** (negative coefficient term) |

> **Notation Note:** $g_j(x)$ denotes the $j$-th posynomial constraint; $\delta_k$ are the dual variables associated with each monomial term $k$; $\lambda_{j0}$ is the dual weight for constraint $j$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Power Law Forms |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Minimum weight design of truss, wing, and rocket structures |
| Mechanical | Optimal gear train design, spring optimization, heat exchanger sizing |
| Civil | Optimal column design, concrete mix proportioning, pipe network design |
| Chemical | Optimal reactor sizing, process yield maximization |
| Economics | Cobb-Douglas production functions, cost-benefit modeling |
| Electrical | Optimal resistor networks, transformer core sizing |

The power law structure is so prevalent because **physical laws and engineering correlations are almost universally multiplicative** — Buckingham's $\pi$-theorem and dimensional analysis routinely produce products of variables raised to fractional powers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Logarithmic Transformation of a Monomial

**Statement:** Prove that a monomial $f(x) = c \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_i}$ becomes an exponential of a linear function under the substitution $y_i = \ln x_i$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Write the monomial definition.**

$$f(x) = c \cdot x_1^{a_1} \cdot x_2^{a_2} \cdots x_n^{a_n}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln f(x) = \ln\left[c \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_i}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Use the property $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$ to distribute the logarithm.**

$$\ln f(x) = \ln c + \ln\left[x_1^{a_1}\right] + \ln\left[x_2^{a_2}\right] + \cdots + \ln\left[x_n^{a_n}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power rule $\ln(x^a) = a \ln x$.**

$$\ln f(x) = \ln c + a_1 \ln x_1 + a_2 \ln x_2 + \cdots + a_n \ln x_n$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $y_i = \ln x_i$ to obtain a linear function in $y$.**

$$\ln f(x) = \ln c + a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2 + \cdots + a_n y_n$$

**Step 6 — Recognize this as a linear function in the transformed variables $y$.**

$$\ln f(x) = \ln c + \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i y_i = b_0 + \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate both sides to recover the monomial as an exponential of a linear form.**

$$f(x) = \exp\left[\ln c + \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i y_i\right] = e^{\ln c} \cdot e^{\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify using $e^{\ln c} = c$.**

$$\boxed{f(x) = c \cdot \exp\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i y_i\right] = c \cdot e^{\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}}}$$

**Interpretation:** The exponential of a linear function is **convex** in $\mathbf{y}$. This convexity is the cornerstone that makes Geometric Programming tractable. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Convexity of a Posynomial

**Statement:** Show that a posynomial $g(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_{ik}^{a_{ik}}$ is a **convex function** of $y_i = \ln x_i$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Write the posynomial and apply the substitution $x_i = e^{y_i}$.**

$$g(y) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{y_i \cdot a_{ik}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \, e^{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{ik} y_i}$$

**Step 2 — Define a linear function for each monomial index $k$.**

$$L_k(y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{ik} y_i = \mathbf{a}_k^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the posynomial as a sum of exponentials of linear functions.**

$$g(y) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k \, e^{L_k(y)}$$

**Step 4 — Recall the convexity preservation theorem: a non-negative weighted sum of convex functions is convex.**

> **Theorem:** If $f_k(y)$ is convex for all $k$ and $c_k \ge 0$, then $g(y) = \sum c_k f_k(y)$ is convex.

**Step 5 — Verify that each term $h_k(y) = c_k e^{L_k(y)}$ is convex.**

The exponential of an affine function is convex. We verify by computing the Hessian:

$$\frac{\partial^2 h_k}{\partial y_i \partial y_j} = c_k a_{ik} a_{jk} e^{L_k(y)}$$

This Hessian equals $c_k e^{L_k(y)} \, \mathbf{a}_k \mathbf{a}_k^T$, which is a **rank-one positive semidefinite matrix** (since $c_k > 0$ and $e^{L_k(y)} > 0$).

**Step 6 — Sum the Hessians across all monomial terms.**

$$\nabla^2 g = \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_k e^{L_k(y)} \, \mathbf{a}_k \mathbf{a}_k^T$$

Each summand is positive semidefinite, so the sum is positive semidefinite. Therefore, the Hessian is positive semidefinite everywhere.

**Step 7 — Conclude convexity.**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 g(y) \succeq 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; g(y) \text{ is convex in } \mathbf{y}}$$

**Significance:** A posynomial is **not convex** in the original $x$-space, but it **is convex** in the log-transformed $y$-space. This is the key insight enabling Geometric Programming. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Primal-Dual Relationship for Standard GP

**Primal GP:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & g_0(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{m_0} c_{0k} \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_{0k,i}} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{m_j} c_{jk} \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{a_{jk,i}} \le 1, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& h_\ell(x) = \sum_{k} \ell_{k} \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{b_{\ell k,i}} = 1, \quad \ell = 1, 2, \ldots, q
\end{aligned}$$

**Dual GP formulation (Beightler-Shetty):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\max_{\delta} \quad & v(\delta) = \prod_{k=0}^{m_0} \left(\frac{c_{0k}}{\delta_{0k}}\right)^{\delta_{0k}} \prod_{j=1}^{p} \prod_{k=1}^{m_j} \left(\frac{c_{jk}}{\delta_{jk}}\right)^{\delta_{jk}} \prod_{\ell=1}^{q} \left(\frac{c_\ell}{\delta_\ell}\right)^{\delta_\ell} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{k=0}^{m_0} \delta_{0k} = 1 \quad \text{(normalization)} \\
& \sum_{k=0}^{m_0} a_{0k,i} \delta_{0k} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \sum_{k=1}^{m_j} a_{jk,i} \delta_{jk} + \sum_{\ell=1}^{q} b_{\ell k, i} \delta_\ell = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n \\
& \delta_{0k} \ge 0,\; \delta_{jk} \ge 0,\; \delta_\ell \text{ unrestricted}
\end{aligned}$$

**Key relationships between primal optimum $x^*$ and dual optimum $\delta^*$:**

$$\delta_{0k}^* = \frac{c_{0k} \prod_i (x_i^*)^{a_{0k,i}}}{g_0(x^*)}$$

$$\delta_{jk}^* = \lambda_j^* \cdot \frac{c_{jk} \prod_i (x_i^*)^{a_{jk,i}}}{g_j(x^*)}$$

These follow from the KKT stationarity conditions of the log-transformed problem, expressing each dual variable as the *fractional contribution* of monomial $k$ to its parent posynomial at the optimum.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = 2x_1 x_2^{-1}$ subject to $x_1^2 x_2 \le 1$ and $x_1, x_2 > 0$.

**Solution using dual GP:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients and exponents.**

| Term | $c_k$ | $a_1$ | $a_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Objective monomial | 2 | 1 | $-1$ |
| Constraint monomial | 1 | 2 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Write the dual problem.** One dual variable $\delta$ for the objective, one for the constraint.

$$\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & v(\delta) = \left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)^{\delta} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1-\delta}\right)^{1-\delta} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & a_1\text{ balance: } 1 \cdot \delta + 2(1-\delta) = 0 \\
& 0 \le \delta \le 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the orthogonality condition.**

$$1 \cdot \delta + 2(1 - \delta) = 0$$
$$\delta + 2 - 2\delta = 0$$
$$2 - \delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = 2$$

This violates $0 \le \delta \le 1$, meaning **the dual is infeasible** in this form — the problem as stated has no bounded optimum. We need a second constraint to bound the problem.

> **Conclusion of the example:** This illustrates a critical lesson — not every monomial objective with a single monomial constraint yields a well-posed GP. A well-posed GP requires sufficient constraints to confine the feasible region.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

flowchart TD

    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["Log transformation yields linear function"]
