<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Approximation-Based Optimization for Complex R-Fs

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical workflow (the *Sample → Fit → Search → Refine* cycle) used to optimize complex response functions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Variables x] --> B[Complex Response Function f of x]
    B --> C[Expensive Simulation: CFD, FEA, Crash]
    C --> D[Sample Database: x_k, f_k]
    D --> E[Surrogate Builder]
    E --> F[Quadratic RSM or Kriging or RBF]
    F --> G[Surrogate Model hat f of x]
    G --> H[Optimizer Loop]
    H --> I{Convergence Met?}
    I -- No --> J[Compute Expected Improvement EI]
    J --> K[New Sample x_new]
    K --> C
    I -- Yes --> L[Optimal Design x star]
    L --> M[Validate via Full Simulation]
    M --> N[Final Engineering Solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cycle (C → D → E → F → G → H → I → J → K → C) is the **iterative surrogate-refinement loop**. Each iteration adds exactly **one** new high-fidelity sample — chosen to maximally reduce uncertainty *or* improve the objective. This is the famous **Efficient Global Optimization (EGO)** algorithm by Jones, Schonlau & Welch (1998).

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram of Three Surrogate Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QuadraticRSM[Quadratic RSM Block]
      R1[Input: m samples] --> R2[Build regressor matrix Z]
      R2 --> R3[Solve beta equals Z transpose Z inverse Z transpose f]
      R3 --> R4[Output: polynomial surrogate]
    end

    subgraph KrigingBlock[Kriging Block]
      K1[Input: m samples] --> K2[Estimate correlation matrix R]
      K2 --> K3[Compute generalized mean mu hat]
      K3 --> K4[Compute prediction and variance]
      K4 --> K5[Output: predictor plus uncertainty]
    end

    subgraph RBFBlock[RBF Network Block]
      B1[Input: m samples] --> B2[Choose basis phi, e.g. Gaussian]
      B2 --> B3[Solve linear system Phi w equals f]
      B3 --> B4[Output: interpolant passing through all samples]
    end

    QuadraticRSM --> Compare[Comparison Engine]
    KrigingBlock --> Compare
    RBFBlock --> Compare
    Compare --> Decision[Choose Best Surrogate for the Complex R-F]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map of Complexity → Remedy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify R-F Complexity] --> Q1{Is R-F Smooth?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need Uncertainty Estimate?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is it Discontinuous?}
    Q1 -- Noisy --> Q4{Noise Level?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> K[Use Kriging]
    Q2 -- No --> R[Use Quadratic RSM]
    Q3 -- Yes --> S[Use RBF or SVM]
    Q3 -- No --> M[Use Multi-start Local Search]
    Q4 -- Low --> K
    Q4 -- High --> N[Use Kriging with Nugget]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical engineering drawing?** Complex R-Fs are abstract mathematical objects. The KTU 2024 scheme prioritizes the *workflow* and *decision logic* over physical sketches — hence the block-level architecture above fully captures the required schematic content.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a Complex Response Function. List any four characteristics that make a response function "complex" in engineering design optimization.*

**Model Answer:**
A Complex Response Function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a mathematical mapping from the design space to the performance space that exhibits behaviour defeating classical gradient-based optimization algorithms. The four primary characteristics are:

1. **Multi-modality** — multiple local optima within the design domain.
2. **Non-linearity / Non-convexity** — high-order curvature and saddle points.
3. **Stochasticity (Noise)** — random perturbation $\epsilon(\mathbf{x})$ corrupting the true response.
4. **Implicit / Black-box form** — no closed-form expression; defined only via expensive numerical simulation (CFD, FEA).

*(Auxiliary characteristics accepted: high-dimensionality, discontinuity, expensive gradient evaluation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between a Response Surface Methodology (RSM) surrogate and a Kriging surrogate. In which type of complex R-F is Kriging preferred, and why?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RSM (Quadratic) | Kriging |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Form | Fixed polynomial | Stochastic (Gaussian Process) |
| Exact Interpolation | No (least-squares fit) | Yes (with zero nugget) |
| Uncertainty Estimate | Not provided | Provides variance at every point |
| Computational Cost | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(m^3)$ where $m$ = samples |
| Hyper-parameters | None (once order chosen) | Correlation length, nugget, mean |

**Kriging is preferred for noisy or stochastically complex R-Fs** because it explicitly models the *uncertainty* in the prediction, enabling adaptive sampling via the Expected Improvement criterion. RSM cannot quantify uncertainty, so it is unsuitable when the R-F contains noise or when the optimum is uncertain.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and feature **internal choice**. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**. Full working must be shown for method marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Modeled on KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** for approximating a complex response function. Derive the closed-form expression for the coefficient vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ of a full quadratic model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For an engineering problem with $n = 4$ design variables, determine the minimum number of sample points required for a full quadratic RSM. Design a 4-point augmentation scheme using steepest descent to improve the surrogate iteratively. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Full Derivation of RSM:**

We model the complex R-F $f(\mathbf{x})$ by the surrogate:

$$
\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i}^{n} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j
$$

The unknown coefficients form the vector $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ where $p = (n+1)(n+2)/2$.

For each of the $m$ sample points, define the regressor row vector:

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(k)} = [1,\; x_1^{(k)},\; \ldots,\; x_n^{(k)},\; (x_1^{(k)})^2,\; x_1^{(k)} x_2^{(k)},\; \ldots]
$$

Stacking all $m$ samples gives the design matrix $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}$. The model predictions and observations are:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{f}} = \mathbf{Z}\boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad \mathbf{f} = [f^{(1)}, f^{(2)}, \ldots, f^{(m)}]^T
$$

The least-squares objective is:

$$
S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \left( f^{(k)} - \hat{f}^{(k)} \right)^2 = (\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{Z}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{Z}\boldsymbol{\beta})
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{Z}^T (\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{Z}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

This yields the **Normal Equations**:

$$
\mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z}\, \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{f}
$$

Provided $\mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z}$ is non-singular (i.e., $m \geq p$ and the samples are not co-linear), the closed-form solution is:

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z})^{-1}\, \mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{Z}^+\, \mathbf{f}\;}
$$

where $\mathbf{Z}^+$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse. The matrix $\mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric positive-definite, ensuring uniqueness and stability of the solution.

**Part (b) — Minimum Sample Size & Augmentation:**

For a full quadratic model with $n = 4$ design variables:

$$
p = \frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2} = \frac{(5)(6)}{2} = 15 \text{ coefficients}
$$

Hence the **minimum sample size** is:

$$
m_{\min} = 15
$$

In practice, a Central Composite Design (CCD) requires $m = 2^n + 2n + 1 = 16 + 8 + 1 = 25$ samples for rotational stability.

**Steepest Descent Augmentation Scheme (4 points):**

Starting from the current optimum $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{opt}}^{(0)}$ of the surrogate:

1. Compute the gradient of $\hat{f}$ at the current point: $\nabla \hat{f}(\mathbf{x})$.
2. Determine the steepest descent direction: $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) / \Vert \nabla \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert$.
3. Choose a step size $\alpha$ via line search on the true (expensive) function.
4. Generate four augmentation points:
   - $\mathbf{x}_1 = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{opt}}^{(0)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}$
   - $\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 + \alpha \mathbf{d}$ (continued descent)
   - $\mathbf{x}_3 = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{opt}}^{(0)} + 0.5 \alpha \mathbf{d}$ (intermediate)
   - $\mathbf{x}_4 = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{opt}}^{(0)} + 2\alpha \mathbf{d}$ (extended)
5. Evaluate the true R-F at all four points, refit the surrogate, and repeat.

This iterative refinement progressively *warms up* the surrogate near the optimum while remaining globally valid.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [Modeled on KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **Kriging Best Linear Unbiased Predictor (BLUP)** for a complex R-F. State clearly all assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define the **Expected Improvement (EI)** infill criterion. Explain how it balances *exploitation* and *exploration* during adaptive sampling of a complex R-F. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Kriging BLUP Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
- The complex R-F $Y(\mathbf{x})$ is a realization of a stationary Gaussian process.
- $Y(\mathbf{x}) = \mu + Z(\mathbf{x})$, where $\mu$ is an unknown constant trend and $Z(\mathbf{x})$ is a zero-mean GP with correlation function $R(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
- The covariance structure is known (in practice, estimated via maximum likelihood).

**Step 1 — Linear Predictor:** $\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{Y}$ where $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^m$.

**Step 2 — Unbiasedness:** $E[\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}^*)] = \mu$ implies $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Minimum Variance:** Minimize $\text{Var}[\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}^*) - Y(\mathbf{x}^*)] = \sigma^2 (1 - 2\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{R}\mathbf{c})$.

**Step 4 — Lagrangian Formulation:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{c}, \lambda) = \sigma^2(1 - 2\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{R}\mathbf{c}) - 2\lambda(\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{1} - 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Stationarity:** Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$
\mathbf{R}\mathbf{c} - \mathbf{r} - \lambda \sigma^{-2} \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{0} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{r} + \gamma \mathbf{1}), \quad \gamma = \lambda/\sigma^2
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Constraint** $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{1 - \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}}{\mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{1}}
$$

**Step 7 — Final BLUP:**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \hat{\mu} + \mathbf{r}^T(\mathbf{x}^*)\, \mathbf{R}^{-1}\, (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{1}\hat{\mu})\;}
$$

with $\hat{\mu} = (\mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{1})^{-1} \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{Y}$.

The associated **Mean Squared Error** is:

$$
\text{MSE}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \sigma^2 \left[ 1 - \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r} + \frac{(1 - \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r})^2}{\mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{1}} \right]
$$

**Part (b) — Expected Improvement (EI) Criterion:**

At any unsampled location $\mathbf{x}$, the Kriging predictor $\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x})$ is normally distributed: $\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}), s^2(\mathbf{x}))$.

The *Improvement* over the current best observation $f_{\min}$ is defined as:

$$
I(\mathbf{x}) = \max(f_{\min} - \hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}), 0)
$$

The **Expected Improvement** is:

$$
\boxed{\;EI(\mathbf{x}) = (f_{\min} - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))\, \Phi\!\left(\frac{f_{\min} - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x})}{s(\mathbf{x})}\right) + s(\mathbf{x})\, \varphi\!\left(\frac{f_{\min} - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x})}{s(\mathbf{x})}\right)\;}
$$

where $\Phi$ and $\varphi$ are the standard normal CDF and PDF respectively.

**Exploitation vs. Exploration Balance:**

- **First term** $(f_{\min} - \hat{f}) \Phi(\cdot)$ — large where $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is predicted to be much *better* than $f_{\min}$. This is **exploitation**: searching near the predicted optimum.
- **Second term** $s(\mathbf{x}) \varphi(\cdot)$ — large where $s(\mathbf{x})$ is large (i.e., in regions with few samples and high uncertainty). This is **exploration**: sampling where the surrogate is least certain.

The next sample point is chosen as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}} EI(\mathbf{x})
$$

This automatic balance is what makes EI the most celebrated infill criterion for complex R-F optimization and the core of the **EGO algorithm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\mathbf{Z}^+$ notation** — students often write $\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{Z}^{-1} \mathbf{f}$, which is wrong because $\mathbf{Z}$ is rectangular ($m \times p$ with $m \neq p$). Always state the pseudo-inverse explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint** $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$ in Kriging derivation — losing **2 marks** if skipped.
> 3. **In EI**, writing only the exploitation term $(f_{\min} - \hat{f})\Phi$ without the variance term $s \varphi$ — examiner deducts up to **3 marks** as exploration is the key insight.
> 4. **Units / scaling**: Design variables must be *coded* (scaled to $[-1, +1]$) before fitting RSM. Many students forget this and lose coherence marks.
> 5. **Numerical validation**: KTU 2024 expects a brief validation step (e.g., $R^2$ or RMSE on a hold-out set) for the surrogate — add it whenever feasible.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Complex R-F (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** A complex R-F is *any* response function that defeats classical gradient optimizers due to multi-modality, noise, discontinuity, implicit form, or high dimensionality.
- **Two-pillar remedy:** Approximation (surrogate) + Global Search.
- **Three main surrogates:** Quadratic RSM (simple/smooth), Kriging (with uncertainty), RBF (interpolating/exact).
- **RSM minimum samples:** $m \geq (n+1)(n+2)/2$. For $n = 4$, this gives **15**; CCD gives **25**.
- **Normal Equations:** $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z})^{-1} \mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{f}$. Always use pseudo-inverse for rectangular $\mathbf{Z}$.
- **Kriging BLUP:** $\hat{Y}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \hat{\mu} + \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{1}\hat{\mu})$. Combines trend + deviation.
- **Kriging MSE:** Contains both prediction variance and a correction for the mean uncertainty (the $(1 - \mathbf{1}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{r})^2$ term).
- **Expected Improvement:** Has two terms — $(f_{\min} - \hat{f})\Phi$ (exploitation) and $s\varphi$ (exploration).
- **EGO Algorithm:** Iteratively maximize EI to choose next sample; converges to global optimum of expensive complex R-F.
- **Space-filling designs:** Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) is preferred over random sampling for initial surrogate construction.
- **Coding of variables:** Always scale $\mathbf{x}$ to $[-1, +1]$ before fitting any surrogate to maintain numerical conditioning.
- **Validation:** Always report $R^2$ and RMSE on a hold-out set; never trust a surrogate blindly.
- **Multi-fidelity tip:** When a cheap low-fidelity model exists, use it to *warm-start* the surrogate of the expensive high-fidelity R-F.
- **Avoid these mistakes:** Confusing RSM with RBF; omitting the unbiasedness constraint; using $\mathbf{Z}^{-1}$ instead of $\mathbf{Z}^+$; ignoring noise (use *nugget* in Kriging).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Genetic Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Genetic Algorithms (GA) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a **stochastic, population-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm** inspired by the mechanics of **natural selection** and **Darwinian evolution**. Formally, a GA is defined as an iterative search procedure that maintains a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals* or *chromosomes*) at each generation, evaluates their quality using an objective-derived **fitness function**, and produces the next generation through the probabilistic application of three genetic operators: **selection**, **crossover** (recombination), and **mutation**. The iterative process continues until a predefined convergence criterion or computational budget is exhausted.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme notation for Design Optimization, a GA is mathematically modeled as a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{GA} = \left\{ \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{M}, \Phi, \mathcal{T} \right\}
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ = Population of candidate design solutions
- $\mathcal{E}$ = Encoding scheme (binary, real-valued, permutation, etc.)
- $\mathcal{S}$ = Selection operator
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Crossover (recombination) operator
- $\mathcal{M}$ = Mutation operator
- $\Phi$ = Fitness evaluation function
- $\mathcal{T}$ = Termination criterion

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Survival of the Fittest Engineering Designs"**
>
> Imagine you are a design engineer who must find the **optimal shape of a truss bridge** to minimize weight while maintaining strength. You do not have a closed-form equation — the design space is too complex. So, you generate **100 random bridge designs** (a *population*). You test each one on a simulator, score them, and then keep the **best 20 designs** as "parents" (this is **selection**). You then **combine two parent designs** to produce a child (e.g., the top half of design A and the bottom half of design B) — this is **crossover**. Occasionally, you randomly tweak a small part of a child design (e.g., change one beam thickness by 5%) — this is **mutation**. The new 100 children form the next *generation*. After many generations, the population **converges** toward the best possible bridge design.
>
> This is exactly how a Genetic Algorithm works — it **evolves better solutions** over time, just like nature evolves fitter organisms.

## 1.3 Why Genetic Algorithms are Essential in Design Optimization

Many engineering design problems have:
- **Non-differentiable** objective functions
- **Discrete** or **mixed-integer** design variables
- **Multi-modal** (multiple local optima) landscapes
- **Black-box** simulation environments (FEA, CFD, etc.)

Gradient-based methods (like the Simplex method or steepest descent) **fail** in such cases. GAs require **no gradient information** — only the objective function value — making them universally applicable. This is why GA is a **cornerstone of Module 3** in the KTU 2024 PEPET743 Design Optimization syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Genetic Algorithms belong to the family of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)**, which also include Evolution Strategies (ES), Genetic Programming (GP), and Differential Evolution (DE). In the KTU 2024 PEPET743 syllabus, GAs are introduced as the prototypical population-based stochastic optimizer, forming the conceptual foundation for advanced topics like Multi-Objective GAs (NSGA-II) and Constraint-Handling techniques.

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology Mapping

| GA Term | Engineering Optimization Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Individual / Chromosome | A complete candidate design solution (one design vector $\mathbf{x}$) |
| Gene | A single decision variable (one component $x_i$ of the design vector) |
| Allele | The value assigned to a gene (e.g., 0 or 1 in binary encoding) |
| Population | A set of $N$ candidate solutions evaluated in parallel |
| Generation | One complete iteration of the GA cycle |
| Fitness Function $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ | The objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ — possibly transformed |
| Selection Pressure | The bias toward choosing fitter individuals as parents |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a GA — Six Logical Stages

A canonical Genetic Algorithm executes the following six-step cycle for every generation $g$:

1. **Initialization (Step 1):** Randomly generate a population $\mathcal{P}^{(0)}$ of $N$ individuals. Each individual $\mathbf{x}_i$ is encoded as a chromosome string.

2. **Fitness Evaluation (Step 2):** Compute the **raw fitness** for every individual. For a **minimization** problem $f(\mathbf{x})$, the fitness is usually inverted:
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = \frac{1}{1 + f(\mathbf{x}_i)} \quad \text{or} \quad \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = f_{\max} - f(\mathbf{x}_i)
$$

3. **Selection (Step 3):** Probabilistically select $M$ parents from $\mathcal{P}^{(g)}$ favoring high-fitness individuals. Common methods: **Roulette Wheel**, **Tournament Selection**, **Rank Selection**, **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)**.

4. **Crossover (Step 4):** With probability $p_c$ (typically **0.6 – 0.9**), combine pairs of parents to produce offspring. Common schemes: **Single-Point**, **Two-Point**, **Uniform**, **Arithmetic**.

5. **Mutation (Step 5):** With probability $p_m$ (typically **0.001 – 0.05**), randomly alter genes of offspring to maintain genetic diversity.

6. **Replacement (Step 6):** Form the new population $\mathcal{P}^{(g+1)}$ via generational or elitist replacement, and return to Step 2 unless the **termination criterion** $\mathcal{T}$ is satisfied.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Operator

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection (Exploitation):** Biases the search toward promising regions by giving fitter individuals a higher probability of reproduction. Without selection, GA degrades to a random walk.
>
> **Crossover (Exploration + Exploitation):** Recombines building blocks (schemata) from two parents. It exploits the **schema theorem** — short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially across generations.
>
> **Mutation (Exploration):** Introduces new genetic material not present in either parent. Without mutation, the population can become **prematurely converged** to a local optimum.

## 2.3 Detailed Operator Formulations

### 2.3.1 Roulette Wheel Selection Probability

For a population of $N$ individuals, the probability of selecting individual $i$ is:

$$
p_i = \frac{\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)}
$$

This is essentially the **expected count** of copies individual $i$ receives in the mating pool.

### 2.3.2 Tournament Selection

A group of $k$ individuals (tournament size) is chosen at random; the fittest among them is selected. The probability of selecting the best individual in a tournament of size $k$ is:

$$
P(\text{best wins}) = 1 - \left(1 - p_{\text{best}}\right)^k
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Tournament selection is preferred in KTU exam problems** because it is **scale-invariant** — unlike roulette wheel, it is unaffected by whether fitness values are $0.1$ or $1{,}000{,}000$.

### 2.3.3 Single-Point Crossover

Given parents of length $L$ genes, a cut point $c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$ is selected uniformly at random. Offspring inherit genes $[1, c]$ from one parent and $[c+1, L]$ from the other.

$$
\text{Parent 1: } 1 0 1 \mid 1 0 0 1
$$

$$
\text{Parent 2: } 0 1 1 \mid 0 1 1 0
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 1: } 1 0 1 \mid 0 1 1 0
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 2: } 0 1 1 \mid 1 0 0 1
$$

### 2.3.4 Bit-Flip Mutation

For binary encoding, each gene in a chromosome is flipped (0 → 1 or 1 → 0) with probability $p_m$:

$$
x_i' = \begin{cases} 1 - x_i & \text{with probability } p_m \\ x_i & \text{with probability } 1 - p_m \end{cases}
$$

### 2.3.5 Real-Coded (Continuous) GA Operators

For continuous variables $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the **SBX (Simulated Binary Crossover)** and **Polynomial Mutation** are standard:

$$
\beta_q = (2 u)^{\frac{1}{\eta+1}} \quad \text{(for polynomial mutation / SBX child generation)}
$$

where $u \in [0,1]$ is uniform and $\eta$ is the distribution index (typically $\eta = 20$).

### 2.3.6 Elitism

To prevent loss of the best solution, the top $e$ individuals (typically $e = 1$ to $5\%$) are copied **unchanged** into the next generation. This guarantees **monotonic non-decrease** of the best fitness:

$$
\Phi_{\text{best}}^{(g+1)} \geq \Phi_{\text{best}}^{(g)}
$$

## 2.4 Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975) — The Foundational Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holland's Schema Theorem** quantifies why GAs work. A **schema** $H$ is a similarity template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$ where $*$ is a wildcard.
>
> Examples: $H_1 = 1*0*1$, $H_2 = *1**0$.
>
> A schema is characterized by:
> - **Order** $o(H)$ = number of fixed (non-wildcard) positions
> - **Defining length** $\delta(H)$ = distance between the first and last fixed positions

The **expected number of copies** of schema $H$ in generation $g+1$ is approximately:

$$
E[m(H, g+1)] \geq m(H, g) \cdot \frac{\Phi(H)}{\bar{\Phi}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

where:
- $m(H, g)$ = number of copies of $H$ in generation $g$
- $\Phi(H)$ = average fitness of strings matching $H$
- $\bar{\Phi}$ = average population fitness
- $p_c$ = crossover probability
- $p_m$ = mutation probability
- $L$ = chromosome length

> **Interpretation:** Short, low-order, high-fitness schemata (called **building blocks**) receive exponentially increasing trials across generations. This is the **implicit parallelism** of GAs.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 PEPET743 high-yield — memorize for Part A and Part B.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Roulette Wheel Probability | $p_i = \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \big/ \sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ | Sum = **1** (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Fitness Scaling (Minimization) | $\Phi(\mathbf{x}) = 1 \big/ \left(1 + f(\mathbf{x})\right)$ | Range $\in (0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Tournament Win Probability | $P = 1 - (1 - p_{\text{best}})^k$ | $k$ = tournament size (**2 to 5**) |
| 4 | Schema Theorem (Lower Bound) | $E[m(H, g+1)] \geq m(H,g) \cdot \frac{\Phi(H)}{\bar{\Phi}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m o(H)\right]$ | Schema count (integer) |
| 5 | Convergence Rate (Schema) | $m(H, g+1) \big/ m(H, g) = \frac{\Phi(H)}{\bar{\Phi}} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)$ | $\epsilon$ = disruption probability |
| 6 | Mutation Bit-Flip | $x_i' = 1 - x_i$ with prob. $p_m$ | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ |
| 7 | Crossover Cut Point | $c \in U\{1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$ | Uniform integer |
| 8 | Population Diversity | $\text{div} = \frac{1}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i<j} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Hamming or Euclidean distance |
| 9 | SBX Crossover Spread Factor | $\beta_q = (2u)^{1/(\eta+1)}$ | $\eta \in [2, 100]$ |
| 10 | Convergence Condition (Max Gen) | $g \geq G_{\max}$ | Iterations (integer) |
| 11 | Convergence Condition (Fitness) | $\vert f_{\text{best}}^{(g)} - f_{\text{best}}^{(g-1)} \vert < \epsilon_{\text{tol}}$ | Tolerance threshold |
| 12 | Elitism Guarantee | $f_{\text{best}}^{(g+1)} \leq f_{\text{best}}^{(g)}$ (min) | Monotonic bound |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Genetic Algorithms are deployed across virtually every engineering discipline:

- **Aerospace:** Optimization of airfoil profiles, satellite antenna geometry, rocket nozzle contours.
- **Mechanical:** Topology optimization of structural members, gear train design, heat exchanger network synthesis.
- **Civil:** Truss bridge sizing, scheduling of construction activities, traffic signal timing.
- **Electrical:** VLSI circuit layout, antenna array beamforming, PID controller tuning.
- **Industrial:** Job-shop scheduling, supply chain network design, inventory control.
- **Biomedical:** Drug dosage optimization, prosthetic socket geometry, EEG/ECG signal feature selection.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 PEPET743 Design Optimization** course, the most common exam-level application is the **minimization of a constrained algebraic function** (e.g., $f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$), for which a complete hand-simulated GA iteration is expected.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Hand-Simulated GA on $f(x) = x^2$ over $[0, 31]$

This is a classic KTU-style problem. The objective is to **minimize** $f(x) = x^2$ where $x$ is an integer in $[0, 31]$. We use a 5-bit binary encoding, a population of $N = 4$, single-point crossover, and bit-flip mutation.

### Step 1 — Population Initialization

Randomly generate 4 chromosomes of length $L = 5$ bits:

| Individual | Chromosome (b4 b3 b2 b1 b0) | Decoded $x$ | $f(x) = x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 0 1 1 0 1 | 13 | 169 |
| $x_2$ | 1 1 0 0 0 | 24 | 576 |
| $x_3$ | 0 0 0 1 1 | 3 | 9 |
| $x_4$ | 1 0 1 1 0 | 22 | 484 |

### Step 2 — Fitness Evaluation (Minimization)

Use the **inverted fitness mapping**:

$$
\Phi(x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + f(x_i)}
$$

Compute for each individual:

$$
\Phi(x_1) = \frac{1}{1 + 169} = \frac{1}{170} \approx 0.00588
$$

$$
\Phi(x_2) = \frac{1}{1 + 576} = \frac{1}{577} \approx 0.00173
$$

$$
\Phi(x_3) = \frac{1}{1 + 9} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10000
$$

$$
\Phi(x_4) = \frac{1}{1 + 484} = \frac{1}{485} \approx 0.00206
$$

Sum of fitness:

$$
\sum \Phi = 0.00588 + 0.00173 + 0.10000 + 0.00206 = 0.10967
$$

### Step 3 — Selection Probabilities (Roulette Wheel)

$$
p_1 = \frac{0.00588}{0.10967} \approx 0.0536 \quad (\text{cumulative: } 0.0536)
$$

$$
p_2 = \frac{0.00173}{0.10967} \approx 0.0158 \quad (\text{cumulative: } 0.0694)
$$

$$
p_3 = \frac{0.10000}{0.10967} \approx 0.9118 \quad (\text{cumulative: } 0.9812)
$$

$$
p_4 = \frac{0.00206}{0.10967} \approx 0.0188 \quad (\text{cumulative: } 1.0000)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Individual $x_3$ has overwhelming selection probability ($\approx 91\%$). This demonstrates a well-known weakness of pure roulette wheel — **fitness scaling compression**. In a real exam, you may be asked to apply **linear fitness scaling** to flatten this distribution.

Draw 4 random numbers $r \in [0, 1]$ — assume $r = 0.21, 0.68, 0.45, 0.97$:

- $r_1 = 0.21$ falls in $[0.0694, 0.9812]$ → selects $x_3$
- $r_2 = 0.68$ falls in $[0.0694, 0.9812]$ → selects $x_3$
- $r_3 = 0.45$ falls in $[0.0694, 0.9812]$ → selects $x_3$
- $r_4 = 0.97$ falls in $[0.9812, 1.0000]$ → selects $x_4$

**Mating pool:** $x_3, x_3, x_3, x_4$ — encoded as `00011, 00011, 00011, 10110`.

### Step 4 — Crossover (Single-Point, $p_c = 0.7$)

Pair (P1 = 00011, P2 = 00011) — even if crossover is applied, offspring are identical: `00011, 00011`.
Pair (P3 = 00011, P4 = 10110) — cut point $c = 3$:

$$
\text{P3: } 0\,0\,0 \mid 1\,1 \qquad \text{P4: } 1\,0\,1 \mid 1\,0
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 3: } 0\,0\,0 \mid 1\,0 = 00010 \; (x = 2)
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 4: } 1\,0\,1 \mid 1\,1 = 10111 \; (x = 23)
$$

**New population after crossover:** $\{00011, 00011, 00010, 10111\}$ → $\{x=3, 3, 2, 23\}$.

### Step 5 — Mutation (Bit-Flip, $p_m = 0.05$)

For each of the $4 \times 5 = 20$ bits, draw a uniform random $u \in [0,1]$; flip if $u < 0.05$.

Assume only one bit flips — say the **middle bit (position 2)** of the **first** chromosome `00011`:

$$
00011 \;\longrightarrow\; 00111 \quad (x = 3 \to x = 7)
$$

Final population of Generation 1: $\{00111, 00011, 00010, 10111\}$ → $\{x = 7, 3, 2, 23\}$.

### Step 6 — Evaluate Best Solution of Generation 1

$$
f(7) = 49, \quad f(3) = 9, \quad f(2) = 4, \quad f(23) = 529
$$

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(1)} = f(2) = 4
$$

Compare to Generation 0 best:

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(0)} = f(3) = 9
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The best fitness **improved** from $9$ to $4$ in a single generation. Notice that elitism (carrying $x_3$ forward) would have guaranteed the best never worsens. After 50–100 generations, the GA will converge to $x^* = 0$, $f(x^*) = 0$ — the true global minimum.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operator Selection Guide for KTU Problems

| Engineering Scenario | Recommended Encoding | Recommended Selection | Recommended Crossover | Recommended Mutation | $p_c$ | $p_m$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discrete / combinatorial (TSP, scheduling) | Permutation | Tournament ($k=3$) | OX, PMX | Swap, Inversion | 0.85 | 0.02 |
| Continuous (truss sizing, PID) | Real-valued | Tournament or SUS | SBX, BLX-$\alpha$ | Polynomial, Gaussian | 0.90 | 0.05 |
| Mixed-integer (structural design) | Tag-based (binary + real) | Rank-based | Simulated Binary | Bit-flip + Gaussian | 0.80 | 0.03 |
| Binary feature selection (ML) | Binary | Tournament ($k=2$) | Uniform | Bit-flip | 0.70 | $1/L$ |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Effective Disruption Probability for Schema Theorem

The probability that a single schema $H$ of order $o(H)$ and defining length $\delta(H)$ is **survived unchanged** through crossover and mutation is:

**Crossover survival** (assuming cut point is uniform over $L-1$ possible positions):

$$
P_{\text{xover survive}} = 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}
$$

**Mutation survival** (each fixed position must NOT be flipped; flip prob. is $p_m$ per bit):

$$
P_{\text{mut survive}} = (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \approx 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)
$$

Combined survival:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = \left(1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m \cdot o(H))
$$

$$
P_{\text{survive}} \approx 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)
$$

The final term in the Schema Theorem (Section 2.4) is therefore:

$$
\left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

This rigorously explains why **short, low-order** schemata dominate — they have small $\delta$ and small $o$, hence minimal disruption.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GA Operational Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block below renders the canonical GA control flow. Node IDs are alphanumeric (with letter prefix per the V10 safeguard) and labels contain **no markdown formatting** to ensure clean Mermaid compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([START])
    nodeInit["Initialize Population P of N Individuals with Random Chromosomes"]
    nodeEval["Evaluate Fitness Phi x for Every Individual in P"]
    nodeTerm{"Termination Criterion T Satisfied?"}
    nodeSelect["Selection Operator Pick Parents Favoring High Fitness"]
    nodeXover["Crossover Operator Combine Pairs of Parents to Produce Offspring"]
    nodeMut["Mutation Operator Apply Small Random Gene Perturbations"]
    nodeReplace["Replacement Form Next Generation Population P New"]
    nodeElite["Elitism Copy Top E Individuals Unchanged"]
    nodeStop([STOP Best Solution Returned])

    nodeStart --> nodeInit
    nodeInit --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeTerm
    nodeTerm -- NO --> nodeSelect
    nodeTerm -- YES --> nodeStop
    nodeSelect --> nodeXover
    nodeXover --> nodeMut
    nodeMut --> nodeElite
    nodeElite --> nodeReplace
    nodeReplace --> nodeEval
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GA System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock["INPUT MODULE"]
        nodeA["Problem Definition: Objective f of x, Constraints g of x, Bounds"]
        nodeB["GA Hyperparameters: N, p_c, p_m, L, eta"]
    end

    subgraph engineBlock["EVOLUTIONARY ENGINE"]
        nodeC["ENCODER: Real Integer Binary Permutation"]
        nodeD["POPULATION MEMORY: N Chromosomes"]
        nodeE["FITNESS EVALUATOR: Phi of x"]
        nodeF["SELECTION POOL: Roulette Tournament Rank SUS"]
        nodeG["RECOMBINATION: Single Point Uniform SBX Arithmetic"]
        nodeH["MUTATION: Bit Flip Gaussian Polynomial Swap"]
        nodeI["REPLACEMENT: Generational Elitist Steady State"]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        nodeJ["Convergence Trace: Best Mean Fitness vs Generation"]
        nodeK["Best Solution x star and f x star"]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Convergence Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeG0["Generation 0: Random Population Spread Across Design Space"]
    nodeG1["Generation 1 to 10: Selection Pushes Population Toward High Fitness Regions"]
    nodeG2["Generation 11 to 40: Crossover Combines Building Blocks Mutation Maintains Diversity"]
    nodeG3["Generation 41 onwards: Population Converges to Neighborhood of Global Optimum"]
    nodeGend["Stopping Criterion Met: Return Best Individual as x star"]

    nodeG0 --> nodeG1
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3
    nodeG3 --> nodeGend
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEPET743]**

### **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define Genetic Algorithm. List any four operators used in GA.

**Model Answer:**

A Genetic Algorithm (GA) is a population-based stochastic search technique inspired by the principles of natural selection and genetics. It maintains a set of candidate solutions (population), evaluates their fitness, and iteratively improves them using probabilistic operators.

**Four operators of GA:**

1. **Selection** — chooses fitter individuals as parents (e.g., Roulette Wheel, Tournament).
2. **Crossover (Recombination)** — combines two parents to produce offspring (e.g., Single-Point, Uniform).
3. **Mutation** — randomly perturbs genes to maintain diversity (e.g., Bit-Flip, Gaussian).
4. **Replacement** — forms the next generation from parents and offspring (e.g., Elitist, Generational).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between **Roulette Wheel Selection** and **Tournament Selection** in Genetic Algorithms.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Roulette Wheel Selection | Tournament Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Probabilistic — each individual chosen with probability $\propto \Phi(x_i)$ | Deterministic-stochastic — best of $k$ random individuals wins |
| Fitness scaling sensitivity | **High** — dominated by fittest individual | **Low** — scale-invariant |
| Computational cost | $O(N)$ — needs full fitness sum | $O(k)$ — only $k$ comparisons |
| Selection pressure control | Difficult | **Easy** — by adjusting $k$ |
| Premature convergence risk | High when fitness range is wide | Lower; widely preferred |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Question Paper PEPET743, Module 3, Choice A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **Schema Theorem** of Genetic Algorithms. Define the terms *order* $o(H)$ and *defining length* $\delta(H)$ of a schema. Using the schema $H = *1*0*1$ of length $L = 6$, compute $o(H)$ and $\delta(H)$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0, 31]$ using a Genetic Algorithm with the initial population given below. Use **5-bit binary encoding**, **Roulette Wheel Selection**, **Single-Point Crossover** (cut point = 3), and **Bit-Flip Mutation** with $p_m = 0.05$. Show one complete generation and report the best fitness improvement.

| Individual | Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 0 1 0 1 1 | 11 | 121 |
| $x_2$ | 1 0 0 0 1 | 17 | 289 |
| $x_3$ | 1 1 0 1 0 | 26 | 676 |
| $x_4$ | 0 0 1 1 0 | 6 | 36 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### Part (a) — Schema Theorem Explanation (7 Marks)

**Schema:** A schema $H$ is a similarity template over the ternary alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ denotes a "don't care" position. Every binary chromosome of length $L$ matches $2^{o(H)}$ schemata — this is the source of the **implicit parallelism** in GAs.

**Order** $o(H)$: The number of **fixed** (non-wildcard) positions in the schema.

**Defining length** $\delta(H)$: The distance between the **first** and **last** fixed positions in the schema.

**Computation for $H = *1*0*1$ (length $L = 6$):**

Fixed positions: position 2 (= 1), position 4 (= 0), position 6 (= 1).

$$
o(H) = 3
$$

$$
\delta(H) = (\text{last fixed}) - (\text{first fixed}) = 6 - 2 = 4
$$

**Schema Theorem Statement (Holland, 1975):** Short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata receive exponentially increasing trials in successive generations:

$$
E[m(H, g+1)] \geq m(H, g) \cdot \frac{\Phi(H)}{\bar{\Phi}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

These short low-order high-fitness schemata are called **building blocks** of GA.

---

### Part (b) — One Complete GA Generation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Initial Population (already given):**

| Ind. | Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x)$ | Fitness $\Phi = 1/(1+f)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 01011 | 11 | 121 | $1/122 \approx 0.00820$ |
| $x_2$ | 10001 | 17 | 289 | $1/290 \approx 0.00345$ |
| $x_3$ | 11010 | 26 | 676 | $1/677 \approx 0.00148$ |
| $x_4$ | 00110 | 6 | 36 | $1/37 \approx 0.02703$ |

Total fitness:

$$
\sum \Phi = 0.00820 + 0.00345 + 0.00148 + 0.02703 = 0.04016
$$

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities (Roulette Wheel):**

$$
p_1 = \frac{0.00820}{0.04016} = 0.2042, \quad p_2 = \frac{0.00345}{0.04016} = 0.0859
$$

$$
p_3 = \frac{0.00148}{0.04016} = 0.0368, \quad p_4 = \frac{0.02703}{0.04016} = 0.6731
$$

**Step 3 — Crossover at cut point 3:**

Pair parents — assume pairings $(x_1, x_2)$ and $(x_3, x_4)$:

$$
01011 \mid \text{cut} \mid \quad \text{and} \quad 10001 \mid \text{cut}
$$

With cut $c = 3$:

$$
\text{Offspring 1: } 010 \mid 001 = 01001 \;(x = 9, f = 81)
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 2: } 100 \mid 011 = 10011 \;(x = 19, f = 361)
$$

$$
11010 \mid \text{cut} \quad \text{and} \quad 00110 \mid \text{cut}
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 3: } 110 \mid 110 = 11010 \;(x = 26, f = 676)
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 4: } 001 \mid 010 = 00101 \;(x = 5, f = 25)
$$

**Step 4 — Bit-Flip Mutation** ($p_m = 0.05$): Assume one flip occurs at position 4 of offspring 4:

$$
00101 \;\longrightarrow\; 00001 \quad (x = 5 \to x = 1, f = 25 \to f = 1)
$$

**Step 5 — New Generation:**

| New Individual | Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 01001 | 9 | 81 |
| 2 | 10011 | 19 | 361 |
| 3 | 11010 | 26 | 676 |
| 4 | 00001 | 1 | **1** |

**Best fitness improvement:**

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(0)} = \min(121, 289, 676, 36) = 36
$$

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(1)} = \min(81, 361, 676, 1) = 1
$$

$$
\Delta f = 36 - 1 = 35 \quad \text{(a 97.2% reduction)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best solution after 1 generation: $x = 1$, $f(x) = 1$.** The true global minimum is $x^* = 0$, $f^* = 0$, which the GA would reach within a few more generations.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Question Paper PEPET743, Module 3, Choice B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain with neat flowchart: the various steps involved in a **simple Genetic Algorithm**. Discuss the role of **crossover probability** $p_c$ and **mutation probability** $p_m$ in the search process.

**(b) (7 Marks)** For the following 4 individuals encoded as 4-bit binary strings, perform **one generation** of GA with the **Tournament Selection** (tournament size $k = 2$), **Single-Point Crossover** at position $c = 2$, and **Bit-Flip Mutation** with $p_m = 0.1$. Objective: **minimize** $f(x) = \vert 15 - x \vert$ where $x$ is the decimal value of the chromosome.

| Ind. | Chromosome |
|---|---|
| $A$ | 1011 |
| $B$ | 0101 |
| $C$ | 1110 |
| $D$ | 0010 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### Part (a) — GA Flowchart & Role of $p_c$ and $p_m$ (7 Marks)

**Flowchart (already covered in Section 4.1).** Steps:

1. Start
2. Initialize population $P$ randomly
3. Evaluate fitness $\Phi$ of each individual
4. **Termination check** — if satisfied, return best; else continue
5. Selection — form mating pool
6. Crossover — with probability $p_c$
7. Mutation — with probability $p_m$
8. Replacement — form $P_{\text{new}}$
9. Go to Step 3

**Role of $p_c$ (Crossover Probability):** Typically $0.6$ to $0.9$. High $p_c$ promotes **recombination of building blocks** and faster exploitation of good schemata. Too low → GA behaves like a pure mutation-driven random search. Too high → high-fitness individuals may be disrupted before being preserved.

**Role of $p_m$ (Mutation Probability):** Typically $0.001$ to $0.05$. Provides **genetic diversity** and prevents premature convergence to local optima. Acts as a **background operator** — small changes injected randomly. Too high → search becomes a random walk; too low → population loses diversity and stagnates.

---

### Part (b) — One-Generation Tournament Selection (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Decode and Evaluate Fitness:**

$$
\Phi(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \vert 15 - x \vert}
$$

| Ind. | Chromosome | $x$ (decimal) | $\vert 15 - x \vert$ | Fitness $\Phi$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 1011 | 11 | 4 | $1/5 = 0.2000$ |
| $B$ | 0101 | 5 | 10 | $1/11 \approx 0.0909$ |
| $C$ | 1110 | 14 | 1 | $1/2 = 0.5000$ |
| $D$ | 0010 | 2 | 13 | $1/14 \approx 0.0714$ |

**Step 2 — Tournament Selection ($k = 2$):** Run two tournaments:

- **Tournament 1:** Randomly pick $A$ and $C$ → $C$ wins (0.5000 > 0.2000). **Parent 1 = $C$ = 1110**.
- **Tournament 2:** Randomly pick $B$ and $D$ → $B$ wins (0.0909 > 0.0714). **Parent 2 = $B$ = 0101**.

**Step 3 — Crossover at $c = 2$:**

$$
1110 \mid \text{cut} \quad \text{and} \quad 0101 \mid \text{cut}
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 1: } 11 \mid 01 = 1101 \;(x = 13, \vert 15 - x \vert = 2)
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 2: } 01 \mid 10 = 0110 \;(x = 6, \vert 15 - x \vert = 9)
$$

**Step 4 — Bit-Flip Mutation** ($p_m = 0.1$, i.e., 1 in 10 bits flips). For $4 \times 2 = 8$ total bits, assume one flip occurs at position 0 of offspring 2:

$$
0110 \;\longrightarrow\; 0111 \quad (x = 6 \to x = 7, \vert 15 - x \vert: 9 \to 8)
$$

**Step 5 — New Generation Evaluation:**

| Ind. | Chromosome | $x$ | $\vert 15 - x \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1101 | 13 | 2 |
| 2 | 0111 | 7 | 8 |

**Best fitness change:**

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(0)} = 1 \quad (x = 14, \text{individual } C)
$$

$$
f_{\text{best}}^{(1)} = 2 \quad (x = 13)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The best fitness **worsened** from $1$ to $2$ because the **best individual ($C$) is not guaranteed survival** in pure tournament selection without elitism. Students often forget this. To guarantee monotonic improvement, **always apply elitism** by copying the best individual unchanged to the next generation. The KTU 2024 marking scheme deducts up to **2 marks** if elitism is not discussed when asked about convergence guarantees.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Genetic Algorithms (Module 3, PEPET743)**

- A **GA** is a **population-based, stochastic, gradient-free** optimizer.
- The six stages are: **Initialize → Evaluate Fitness → Select → Crossover → Mutate → Replace**.
- **Encoding** is the mapping from solution space to chromosome space (binary, real, permutation).
- **Fitness function** transforms the objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ into a maximizable quantity.
- **Roulette Wheel** $p_i = \Phi_i / \sum \Phi_j$ — simple but fitness-scale sensitive.
- **Tournament** — pick best of $k$; scale-invariant; **preferred for exams**.
- **Crossover** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ — recombines building blocks.
- **Mutation** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ — preserves diversity, prevents stagnation.
- **Elitism** = carry top $e$ individuals unchanged → guarantees monotonic best-fitness non-decrease.
- **Schema Theorem** — short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially: $E[m(H, g+1)] \geq m(H, g) \cdot (\Phi(H)/\bar{\Phi}) \cdot (1 - p_c \delta/(L-1) - p_m o)$.
- **Termination criteria:** max generations, fitness tolerance, or diversity threshold.
- GA is best suited for **non-differentiable, multi-modal, discrete, or black-box** design problems.
- For continuous variables, use **real-coded GA with SBX crossover and polynomial mutation**.
- Common exam mistakes: forgetting elitism, miscalculating cumulative probabilities, ignoring constraint handling, using wrong $p_m$ (too high = random walk).
- **In exam derivations:** always show fitness sum, cumulative probabilities, mating pool selection, and the resulting offspring chromosomes with their decoded values.
- The KTU 2024 PEPET743 expected GA convergence horizon: **50–200 generations** for typical 2D/3D algebraic test functions.

> - **Module:** Module 3

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO)** is a branch of mathematical optimization concerned with simultaneously optimizing two or more conflicting objective functions subject to a set of constraints. Unlike single-objective problems, MOO does not yield a single optimal solution but rather a set of **Pareto-optimal solutions** that represent the best possible trade-offs among the competing objectives.

The general mathematical formulation of a Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOOP) is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & F(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_k(\mathbf{x})]^T \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_l(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad l = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the **decision variable vector** in the design space $\mathbb{R}^n$
- $F(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective vector** in the objective space $\mathbb{R}^k$ with $k \geq 2$ objectives
- $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ and $h_l(\mathbf{x})$ are the **inequality** and **equality** constraints respectively
- $x_i^{(L)}$ and $x_i^{(U)}$ are the **lower and upper bounds** (side constraints) of the $i^{th}$ design variable

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **Design Space** ($\mathcal{S} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$): The space of decision variables
> - **Objective Space** ($\mathcal{Z} \subset \mathbb{R}^k$): The space of objective function values
> - **Feasible Region** ($\mathcal{F}$): The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying all constraints
> - **Pareto Front** ($\mathcal{P}^*$): The set of non-dominated solutions mapped in objective space

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Buying Dilemma

Imagine you are purchasing a new car with a fixed budget. You want to simultaneously:

| Objective 1 | Objective 2 | Objective 3 |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Minimize Price (₹) | Maximize Fuel Efficiency (km/L) | Maximize Safety Rating (Stars) |

> [!TIP]
> **The Real-World Insight:** You cannot have a car that is the *cheapest*, *most fuel-efficient*, and *safest* all at once. Every design choice forces a **trade-off**. A lighter car is more fuel-efficient but less safe; a heavier car is safer but consumes more fuel. MOO helps you find the **best possible trade-off curve** — the so-called **Pareto Front** — from which you can pick the option that suits your personal priorities.

This is exactly the dilemma MOO addresses. In engineering design (e.g., minimizing weight while maximizing stiffness, or minimizing cost while maximizing performance), the designer must explore the **trade-off surface** to make informed decisions.

## 1.3 The Concept of Pareto Dominance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pareto Dominance (Pareto, 1906):** A feasible solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is said to **dominate** another solution $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ (denoted $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$) if and only if:
> 1. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **no worse** than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in all objectives, i.e., $f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$
> 2. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **strictly better** than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in at least one objective, i.e., $f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ for at least one $j$

A solution is **Pareto Optimal** (or **non-dominated**) if no other feasible solution dominates it. The set of all Pareto-optimal solutions in the objective space is the **Pareto Front**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in MOO

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Purpose |
|--------|--------|----------------------|---------|
| Number of objectives | $k$ | typically 2 to 4 | Defines dimensionality of objective space |
| Population size | $N$ | 50 – 200 (NSGA-II) | Number of candidate solutions per generation |
| Generations | $G$ | 100 – 500 | Number of evolutionary iterations |
| Crossover probability | $p_c$ | 0.8 – 0.95 | Probability of genetic recombination |
| Mutation probability | $p_m$ | 0.01 – 0.1 | Probability of gene mutation |
| Reference point | $\mathbf{z}^*$ | Component-wise min | Used in hypervolume calculation |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Multi-Objective Optimization Exists — The "Why" Behind It

In real-world engineering design, decisions are rarely based on a single criterion. A structural engineer does not only minimize weight — they must simultaneously consider **deflection**, **stress**, **cost**, and **manufacturability**. Single-objective formulations either ignore these competing demands or fold them into a single weighted sum, which **requires a priori knowledge** of weights and may miss important trade-off regions.

MOO arose to address three fundamental limitations of classical optimization:

1. **Conflicting objectives** cannot be combined linearly without losing trade-off information.
2. The **decision maker (DM)** often does not know preferences in advance and wants to see alternatives.
3. The **Pareto Front** provides a complete map of "what is achievable" — empowering the DM to choose intelligently.

## 2.2 Classification of MOO Solution Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MOO methods are classified into three broad categories. Examiners frequently ask students to "Classify and briefly explain MOO methods" — this is a high-yield topic.

### 2.2.1 A Priori Methods (Preference Articulated Before Search)
The DM specifies preferences **before** optimization, and a single solution is produced.
- **Weighted Sum Method (WSM)**
- **Weighted Metric / Min-Max Method**
- **Goal Programming**
- **Lexicographic Ordering**
- **ε-Constraint Method** (sometimes classified as priori)

### 2.2.2 A Posteriori Methods (Generate First, Choose Later)
The algorithm generates the **entire Pareto Front**; the DM then selects a preferred solution.
- **ε-Constraint Method** (commonly classified as posteriori)
- **Normal Boundary Intersection (NBI)**
- **Normal Constraint (NC) Method**
- **Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs):** NSGA-II, SPEA2, MOEA/D

### 2.2.3 Interactive Methods (Progressive Preference Refinement)
The DM iteratively interacts with the algorithm, refining preferences as the search progresses.
- **Reference Point Method**
- **Light Beam Search**
- **Guided Domination Approach**

## 2.3 Detailed Treatment of Major Scalarization Techniques

### 2.3.1 Weighted Sum Method (WSM)

The $k$-objective problem is converted into a single objective by linear scalarization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f_{WSM}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $w_i \geq 0$ are **weighting coefficients** satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1$ (normalized weights).

**Why and How It Works:**
- The weight vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k]^T$ defines the direction in objective space along which we search.
- Different weight vectors produce different Pareto-optimal points.
- Geometrically, minimizing the weighted sum finds the point where a **hyperplane with normal $\mathbf{w}$** is tangent to the feasible objective region.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation (Common Exam Trap):** The weighted sum method **cannot find non-convex portions** of the Pareto Front. If the front is concave in some region, no positive weight vector can reach that region. This is a frequent short-answer question in KTU exams.

### 2.3.2 ε-Constraint Method

One objective is minimized while the others are converted into constraints bounded by ε values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f_p(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k, \; i \neq p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why and How It Works:**
- Varying $\varepsilon_i$ values systematically traces out the Pareto Front.
- Unlike WSM, it **can handle non-convex Pareto Fronts** because it does not restrict the search direction.
- Trade-off: Requires solving a sequence of constrained single-objective problems.

### 2.3.3 Goal Programming (GP)

The DM specifies aspiration levels $\bar{f}_i$ for each objective. The objective becomes the **minimization of deviations** from these goals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert f_i(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}_i \vert^p \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $p = 1$ gives **linear goal programming** and $p = 2$ gives **quadratic goal programming (minimizing Euclidean distance to goal point)**.

### 2.3.4 Weighted Min-Max (Weighted Tchebycheff) Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \max_{i = 1, \ldots, k} \left[ w_i \left( f_i(\mathbf{x}) - z_i^* \right) \right] \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $z_i^* = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}} f_i(\mathbf{x})$ is the individual minimum (ideal point).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method matters:** The Tchebycheff metric **guarantees finding every Pareto-optimal point** (for some weight vector) regardless of convexity — overcoming the WSM limitation. This is a critical theoretical result for KTU exams.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Scalarized Form | Key Formula | When to Use | Pitfall |
|---|--------|----------------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Weighted Sum** | $\min \sum w_i f_i$ | $f_{WSM} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Convex Pareto Fronts | Fails on non-convex fronts |
| 2 | **ε-Constraint** | $\min f_p$ s.t. $f_i \leq \varepsilon_i$ | $f_p(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq \varepsilon_i$ | Non-convex fronts; need uniform spacing | Requires many runs |
| 3 | **Goal Programming** | $\min \sum \vert f_i - \bar{f}_i \vert^p$ | $L_p = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert f_i(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}_i \vert^p \right]^{1/p}$ | When aspiration levels are known | Suboptimal if goals infeasible |
| 4 | **Tchebycheff** | $\min \max w_i(f_i - z_i^*)$ | $\min_{\mathbf{x}} \max_i w_i \left[f_i(\mathbf{x}) - z_i^*\right]$ | Any Pareto Front (convex or not) | May include weakly dominated points |
| 5 | **Weighted $L_p$** | $\min \left[ \sum w_i^p \vert f_i - \bar{f}_i \vert^p \right]^{1/p}$ | $L_p(\mathbf{w}, \bar{\mathbf{f}}) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i^p \vert f_i(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}_i \vert^p \right]^{1/p}$ | Generalization; $p \to \infty$ gives Tchebycheff | Computational cost for $p$ search |
| 6 | **NSGA-II Crowding** | Non-dominated sorting + crowding distance | $CD_i = \sum_{m=1}^{k} \frac{f_m^{(i+1)} - f_m^{(i-1)}}{f_m^{\max} - f_m^{\min}}$ | Evolutionary Pareto generation | Parameter-sensitive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MOO is foundational in **aerospace design** (minimize drag and weight, maximize lift), **automotive crashworthiness** (minimize mass and intrusion, maximize energy absorption), **structural topology optimization**, **process engineering** (maximize yield and purity, minimize cost), and **machine learning hyperparameter tuning**.

---

## 2.5 Pareto Front Quality Metrics

For evaluating algorithmic performance on benchmark MOO problems:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Generational Distance (GD)}: \quad & GD = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert} d_i^2 \right)^{1/2} \\
\text{Inverted Generational Distance (IGD)}: \quad & IGD = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P}^{ref} \vert} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{\vert \mathcal{P}^{ref} \vert} \bar{d}_j^2 \right)^{1/2} \\
\text{Hypervolume (HV)}: \quad & HV = \text{Vol}_k \left( \bigcup_{\mathbf{a} \in \mathcal{P}^*} \left[ z_1^*, a_1 \right] \times \ldots \times \left[ z_k^*, a_k \right] \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $d_i$ is the Euclidean distance from solution $i$ in the obtained set $\mathcal{P}^*$ to the nearest point in the true Pareto Front.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Weighted Sum Optimum and Pareto Optimality

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Foundation:** Every optimal solution to a positively-weighted WSM is a Pareto-optimal point (assuming feasibility and positive weights). We prove this rigorously below — this derivation is a favorite KTU Part B question.

### 3.1.1 Theorem Statement

*If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is an optimal solution of the weighted sum problem*

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\mathbf{x}), \quad w_i > 0, \; \forall i
$$

*then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a Pareto-optimal solution of the original MOOP.*

### 3.1.2 Proof by Contradiction

**Step 1: Assume the contrary.** Suppose $\mathbf{x}^*$ is optimal for the WSM but is **not Pareto-optimal**. Then there exists another feasible $\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{F}$ such that $\mathbf{y}$ dominates $\mathbf{x}^*$.

**Step 2: Apply dominance definition.** Since $\mathbf{y}$ dominates $\mathbf{x}^*$:
$$
f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k
$$
and strict inequality holds for at least one index $j$:
$$
f_j(\mathbf{y}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

**Step 3: Form the weighted sum at $\mathbf{y}$.** Because all $w_i > 0$, multiplying the dominance inequalities by the corresponding weights preserves direction:
$$
w_i f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq w_i f_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \quad \text{for all } i
$$

Summing over all $i$:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\mathbf{y}) < \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

**Step 4: Reach contradiction.** This strict inequality contradicts the optimality of $\mathbf{x}^*$ for the WSM. Hence, the assumption is false, and $\mathbf{x}^*$ must be Pareto-optimal. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The converse is NOT true in general.** A Pareto-optimal point need not be optimal for any positively-weighted WSM if the Pareto Front is non-convex. This is the precise mathematical reason why WSM fails on non-convex fronts.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tchebycheff Method and the Augmented Tchebycheff

### 3.2.1 Geometric Intuition

For a two-objective minimization problem with ideal point $\mathbf{z}^* = (z_1^*, z_2^*)$, the **Tchebycheff method** finds the point on the Pareto Front that minimizes the maximum weighted deviation:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}} \max_{i=1,\ldots,k} \left[ w_i (f_i(\mathbf{x}) - z_i^*) \right]
$$

### 3.2.2 Why Pure Tchebycheff Can Fail

The pure Tchebycheff method can produce **weakly dominated** solutions (solutions where $f_i(\mathbf{y}) = f_i(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all $i$, but they are different points).

### 3.2.3 Augmented Tchebycheff Formulation

To eliminate weakly dominated solutions, an augmented term is added:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}} \left[ \max_{i=1,\ldots,k} w_i(f_i(\mathbf{x}) - z_i^*) + \rho \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i(f_i(\mathbf{x}) - z_i^*) \right]
$$

Where $\rho > 0$ is a small positive scalar (typically $\rho = 10^{-6}$).

**Step-by-step explanation of the augmentation:**

- **Step 1:** The first term $\max_i w_i(f_i - z_i^*)$ ensures we minimize the worst weighted deviation.
- **Step 2:** The second term $\rho \sum_i w_i(f_i - z_i^*)$ acts as a tie-breaker among points with equal maximum deviation.
- **Step 3:** The $\rho$ must be small enough not to change the solution's approximate location on the Pareto Front, but large enough to discriminate weakly dominated points.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Weighted Sum Method (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Solve the following bi-objective problem using the Weighted Sum Method with $w_1 = 0.4, w_2 = 0.6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 \\
\text{Minimize} \quad & f_2(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1:** Form the weighted scalar objective:
$$
f_{WSM} = 0.4(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + 0.6[(x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2]
$$

**Step 2:** Expand each term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{WSM} &= 0.4 x_1^2 + 0.4 x_2^2 + 0.6(x_1^2 - 2x_1 + 1) + 0.6(x_2^2 - 2x_2 + 1) \\
&= 0.4 x_1^2 + 0.4 x_2^2 + 0.6 x_1^2 - 1.2 x_1 + 0.6 + 0.6 x_2^2 - 1.2 x_2 + 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine like terms:
$$
f_{WSM} = (0.4 + 0.6) x_1^2 + (0.4 + 0.6) x_2^2 - 1.2 x_1 - 1.2 x_2 + 1.2
$$
$$
f_{WSM} = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 1.2 x_1 - 1.2 x_2 + 1.2
$$

**Step 4:** Take partial derivatives and set to zero for stationarity:
$$
\frac{\partial f_{WSM}}{\partial x_1} = 2 x_1 - 1.2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0.6
$$
$$
\frac{\partial f_{WSM}}{\partial x_2} = 2 x_2 - 1.2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 0.6
$$

**Step 5:** Verify second-order conditions (Hessian positive definite):
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{eigenvalues: } 2, 2 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Minimum confirmed}
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate objective values at the optimum:
$$
f_1(0.6, 0.6) = 0.36 + 0.36 = 0.72
$$
$$
f_2(0.6, 0.6) = (0.6 - 1)^2 + (0.6 - 1)^2 = 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.32
$$

**Step 7:** Generate other Pareto points by varying $w_1, w_2$:

| $w_1$ | $w_2$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 2.00 |
| 0.4 | 0.6 | 0.6 | 0.6 | 0.72 | 0.32 |
| 0.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 2.00 | 0.00 |

These three points lie on the Pareto Front for this convex problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Scalarization Methods

| Criterion | Weighted Sum | ε-Constraint | Goal Programming | Tchebycheff | NSGA-II |
|-----------|:------------:|:------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:-------:|
| **Convex fronts** | ✓ Works | ✓ Works | ✓ Works | ✓ Works | ✓ Works |
| **Non-convex fronts** | ✗ Fails | ✓ Works | ✗ May fail | ✓ Works | ✓ Works |
| **Generates full front in one run** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Gradient-based** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Population-based** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Medium | Low | Low | High |
| **Handles many objectives ($k > 3$)** | ✗ | ✗ | Limited | ✗ | Limited |
| **Easy to implement** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **Requires a priori preference** | ✓ | Partial | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When answering comparison questions in KTU exams, always present a structured table like this. It demonstrates depth of understanding and is easy for the examiner to award full marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Multi-Objective Optimization Solution Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the complete decision-making workflow of a multi-objective optimization problem, from problem formulation through method selection to final decision-making on the Pareto Front.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Real Engineering Problem] --> B[Identify Decision Variables x1 to xn]
    B --> C[Formulate k Objective Functions f1 to fk]
    C --> D[Define Constraints g and h]
    D --> E{Is DM Preference Known A Priori?}
    E -->|YES| F[Use A Priori Method]
    E -->|NO| G[Use A Posteriori Method]
    F --> F1[Weighted Sum Method]
    F --> F2[Goal Programming]
    F --> F3[Lexicographic Ordering]
    G --> G1[Epsilon Constraint Method]
    G --> G2[NSGA II MOEA]
    G --> G3[MOEA/D Algorithm]
    G --> G4[SPEA2 Algorithm]
    F1 --> H[Solve Single Objective]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    H --> I[Obtain Single Optimal Solution]
    G1 --> J[Generate Pareto Front]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    G4 --> J
    I --> K[DM Selects Solution]
    J --> L[Visualize Pareto Front in Objective Space]
    L --> M[Compute Quality Metrics GD, IGD, HV]
    M --> N{Pareto Front Acceptable?}
    N -->|NO| O[Tune Algorithm Parameters]
    O --> G
    N -->|YES| P[DM Chooses Preferred Trade off Point]
    I --> K
    P --> Q[Implement Design in Engineering]
    K --> Q
    Q --> R[END]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Pareto Dominance and Non-Dominated Sorting in Objective Space

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual geometry of Pareto dominance for a bi-objective minimization problem, showing how solutions are classified as dominated, non-dominated, and weakly dominated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPACE[OBJECTIVE SPACE in R2 with k=2]
        direction TB
        A1[Solution A: f1=2, f2=8] -->|Dominated by C| DOM[DOMINATED REGION]
        A2[Solution B: f1=6, f2=4] -->|Dominated by C| DOM
        A3[Solution C: f1=3, f2=3] -->|Non Dominated| PARETO[PARETO FRONT]
        A4[Solution D: f1=5, f2=2] -->|Non Dominated| PARETO
        A5[Solution E: f1=8, f2=1] -->|Non Dominated| PARETO
        A6[Solution F: f1=4, f2=4] -->|Weakly Dominated| WEAK[WEAKLY DOMINATED]
    end
    DOM --> X[Remove from consideration]
    WEAK --> Y[Filter out via augmented scalarization]
    PARETO --> Z[Retain as Pareto Optimal Set]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: NSGA-II (Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)

> [!NOTE]
> NSGA-II is the **most widely used** MOEA in engineering design optimization and frequently appears in KTU examinations. The block diagram below shows its functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Random Population P0 of size N] --> B[Evaluate Objective Values]
    B --> C[Non Dominated Sorting into Fronts F1, F2, ...]
    C --> D[Compute Crowding Distance for each Front]
    D --> E[Apply Binary Tournament Selection]
    E --> F[Apply SBX Crossover and Polynomial Mutation]
    F --> G[Create Offspring Population Qt of size N]
    G --> H[Combine Parent and Offspring: Rt equals Pt union Qt]
    H --> I[Apply Non Dominated Sorting on Rt of size 2N]
    I --> J[Select Top N Solutions to form Pt+1]
    J --> K{Check Termination: Max Generations Reached?}
    K -->|NO| B
    K -->|YES| L[Output Final Pareto Front Pstar]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Comparison of Scalarization Methods (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCALARIZATION[MULTI OBJECTIVE TO SINGLE OBJECTIVE CONVERSION]
        direction LR
        S1[WEIGHTED SUM] --> S1F[Combines objectives via linear weights]
        S2[EPSILON CONSTRAINT] --> S2F[Converts k-1 objectives to constraints]
        S3[GOAL PROGRAMMING] --> S3F[Minimizes deviation from target goals]
        S4[TCHEBYCHEFF] --> S4F[Minimizes worst weighted deviation]
        S5[WEIGHTED LP NORM] --> S5F[Generalized distance metric with p]
    end
    SCALARIZATION --> OUT[Single Objective Problem]
    OUT --> SOLVER[Classical Solver: KKT, Simplex, Gradient]
    SOLVER --> RESULTS[Pareto Optimal Solutions]
```
