# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Achievement Scalarizing Function (Wierzbicki)

We want to **reformulate** a multi-objective problem as a single-objective problem using a reference point $\bar{q}$. The achievement function must satisfy two properties:

**Property 1 (Pareto Compatibility):** Any $x^*$ minimizing $s(x \vert \bar{q})$ must be **Pareto-optimal**.

**Property 2 (Reference Compatibility):** If $\bar{q}$ itself is attainable and feasible, then $s(\bar{q} \vert \bar{q}) = 0$ and $\bar{q}$ minimizes $s$.

### Step 1: Define the over-achievement

Let $f_i(x) - \bar{q}_i$ denote the gap between actual and desired performance. We want to **penalize positive gaps** (over-shooting the aspiration) more than negative gaps (beating the aspiration).

$$\sigma_i(x) = \frac{f_i(x) - \bar{q}_i}{n_i}$$

### Step 2: Construct the max-component

$$\sigma_{\max}(x) = \max_{i=1,\ldots,k} \sigma_i(x)$$

This penalizes the **worst over-achievement**.

### Step 3: Augment with weighted sum

Adding a small weighted sum ensures **proper Pareto optimality** (not just weak Pareto optimality):

$$s(x \vert \bar{q}) = \sigma_{\max}(x) + \rho \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i(x)$$

### Step 4: Show Pareto compatibility

Suppose $x^*$ minimizes $s$ but is **not Pareto-optimal**. Then there exists $\hat{x}$ such that:

$$f_i(\hat{x}) \leq f_i(x^*) \; \forall i, \quad \text{with strict inequality for some } i_0$$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max}(\hat{x}) &= \max_i (f_i(\hat{x}) - \bar{q}_i)/n_i \\
&\leq \max_i (f_i(x^*) - \bar{q}_i)/n_i = \sigma_{\max}(x^*)
\end{aligned}$$

with strict inequality in at least one component. Combined with the augmentation term, $s(\hat{x}) < s(x^*)$, **contradiction**.

Therefore $x^*$ **must be Pareto-optimal**. ∎

## 3.2 Worked Example — GDF Method on a 2-Variable Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Solved Example for KTU Practice**

Consider:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^2} \{ f_1(x) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2, \; f_2(x) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2 \}$$

subject to $x_1, x_2 \in [0, 4]$.

### Iteration 0 — Initialization

Let $x^{(0)} = (1, 1)$.

### Compute gradients at $x^{(0)}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla f_1(x^{(0)}) &= \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - 2) \\ 2(x_2 - 1) \end{bmatrix}\bigg\vert_{x^{(0)}} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
\nabla f_2(x^{(0)}) &= \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - 1) \\ 2(x_2 - 3) \end{bmatrix}\bigg\vert_{x^{(0)}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### DM specifies trade-off

The DM says: "I am willing to give up 1 unit of $f_1$ to gain 2 units of $f_2$."

So $\lambda_1 = 0.5, \lambda_2 = 0.5$ (normalized weights, $\sum \lambda_i = 1$).

### Form the scalarized problem

$$\begin{aligned}
\min_{x \in [0,4]^2} \quad & 0.5 \left[ (x_1-2)^2 + (x_2-1)^2 \right] \\
&+ 0.5 \left[ (x_1-1)^2 + (x_2-3)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding:

$$F(x) = (x_1 - 1.5)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 + \text{constant}$$

Setting $\nabla F = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 1.5) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 1.5$$

$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 2$$

So $\bar{x}^{(0)} = (1.5, 2)$.

### Step-size update with $\beta = 0.5$

$$\alpha^{(0)} = \frac{0.5}{1} = 0.5$$

$$x^{(1)} = (1, 1) + 0.5 \cdot \big( (1.5, 2) - (1, 1) \big) = (1.25, 1.5)$$

### Verify convergence

At $x^{(1)} = (1.25, 1.5)$:

$$f_1 = (1.25-2)^2 + (1.5-1)^2 = 0.5625 + 0.25 = 0.8125$$

$$f_2 = (1.25-1)^2 + (1.5-3)^2 = 0.0625 + 2.25 = 2.3125$$

The DM can now inspect the trade-off and adjust $\lambda$ for iteration 2.

## 3.3 Convergence Analysis Matrix

| Method | Convergence Guarantee | Preference Info Needed | Computational Cost per Iteration |
|---|---|---|---|
| GDF | Yes, to Pareto point with $\alpha^{(k)} = \beta/k$ | Local MRS at each iterate | Moderate |
| STEM | Weak convergence to compromise | Ideal point + bounds | Low |
| Wierzbicki RP | Yes (proper Pareto) | Reference point $\bar{q}$ | High (non-smooth max) |
| LBS | Yes to Pareto point | Direction $q$ + cone widths $\delta_i$ | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Interactive Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Define f_i, g_j, S]) --> stepB[Step 1: Compute and display candidate Pareto solution x_k]
    stepB --> stepC{Step 2: Present current trade-offs to DM}
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: DM inspects f_x_k and f_x_k_bar]
    stepD --> stepE{Step 4: Is DM satisfied with current solution?}
    stepE -->|Yes| stopF([Stop: Output final design x_star])
    stepE -->|No| stepG[Step 5: DM provides preference: trade-off ratios lambda OR reference point q OR cone delta]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 6: Update scalarized sub-problem with new preference]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 7: Solve min F_scalar x subject to S]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 8: Update x_k plus 1 using Frank Wolfe step]
    stepJ --> stepB
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Interactive Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Interactive Methods in Design Optimization] --> nodeB[Trade-off Methods]
    rootA --> nodeC[Reference Point Methods]
    rootA --> nodeD[Light Beam Methods]
    rootA --> nodeE[Visualization Methods]

    nodeB --> leafB1[GDF Geoffrion Dyer Feinberg 1972]
    nodeB --> leafB2[STEM Benayoun 1971]

    nodeC --> leafC1[Wierzbicki Achievement Function 1980]
    nodeC --> leafC2[Pareto Race 2000]

    nodeD --> leafD1[Jaszkiewicz Slowinski LBS 1999]

    nodeE --> leafE1[Pareto Navigation Granat Makowski]
    nodeE --> leafE2[VIM Visual Interactive Module]
```

## 4.3 Reference Point Method — Iterative Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[DM specifies aspiration point q_bar in objective space] --> p2[Solver minimizes achievement scalarizing function]
    p2 --> p3[Solution x_star obtained and its f_x_star shown to DM]
    p3 --> p4{DM satisfied?}
    p4 -->|Yes| p5[Output final design]
    p4 -->|No| p6[DM adjusts reference point to new q_bar plus 1]
    p6 --> p2
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Interactive Module in Optimization Software

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    archA[Engineering Design Problem Definition] --> archB[Multi-Objective Model Builder]
    archB --> archC[Feasible Region Sampler]
    archC --> archD[Pareto Frontier Approximator]
    archD --> archE[DM Interface Layer]
    archE --> archF[Preference Capture Subsystem]
    archF --> archG[Scalarization Engine]
    archG --> archH[Inner Solver: Frank Wolfe or Simplex]
    archH --> archI[New Candidate Generator]
    archI --> archD
    archE --> archJ[Convergence Monitor]
    archJ --> archK{Stop Criteria Met?}
    archK -->|Yes| archL[Final Pareto Optimal Design Output]
    archK -->|No| archE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**"Differentiate between a priori, a posteriori, and interactive methods of multi-objective optimization. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **A Priori Methods**: The DM specifies all preferences (e.g., weights) **before** optimization begins. *Example*: Weighted-sum method $F(x) = \sum w_i f_i(x)$.
- **A Posteriori Methods**: The full Pareto front is computed **first**, and the DM selects from it afterwards. *Example*: ε-constraint method.
- **Interactive Methods**: The DM supplies preference information **iteratively** during the search, guiding the algorithm. *Example*: Wierzbicki Reference Point Method.

*(1 mark each for definition + example)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**"State the two fundamental properties that a well-designed achievement scalarizing function must satisfy."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Pareto Compatibility** — any minimizer of the scalarizing function must be (properly) Pareto-optimal for the original multi-objective problem. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Reference Compatibility** — if the reference point $\bar{q}$ is feasible, then the scalarizing function should attain its minimum at $\bar{q}$. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

### Question A — Geoffrion-Dyer-Feinberg (GDF) Method [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | Module 4 Choice Q1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Geoffrion-Dyer-Feinberg (GDF) interactive method** in detail. Derive the auxiliary sub-problem formulation and state the step-size rule with the convergence condition.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Background & Assumptions:**
- GDF assumes the DM can articulate **local marginal rates of substitution (MRS)** between objectives at any feasible point.
- It uses **Frank-Wolfe linearization** for inner minimization — well-suited for smooth, convex problems.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Scalarization:**
Given MRS $\lambda_i^{(k)}$, form the weighted sum:

$$\min_{x \in S} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i^{(k)} f_i(x)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Update Rule:**
The new iterate:

$$x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + \alpha^{(k)}(\bar{x}^{(k)} - x^{(k)})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Convergence Condition:**
$$\alpha^{(k)} = \frac{\beta}{k}, \quad 0 < \beta \leq 1$$

This ensures convergence to a Pareto-optimal point as $k \to \infty$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a bi-objective problem:

$$\min_{x \in [0,5]} \{ f_1(x) = (x-1)^2, \quad f_2(x) = (x-4)^2 \}$$

Apply **two iterations** of the GDF method starting from $x^{(0)} = 0$, assuming the DM specifies equal weights $(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0.5)$ and $\beta = 1$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Iteration 0 (3.5 marks):**

Compute gradients at $x^{(0)} = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla f_1(0) &= 2(0 - 1) = -2 \\
\nabla f_2(0) &= 2(0 - 4) = -8
\end{aligned}$$

Scalarized objective:

$$F(x) = 0.5(x-1)^2 + 0.5(x-4)^2$$

Expand:

$$F(x) = x^2 - 5x + 8.5$$

Set $\nabla F = 0$:

$$2x - 5 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2.5$$

So $\bar{x}^{(0)} = 2.5$.

Step-size: $\alpha^{(0)} = 1/1 = 1$.

Update: $x^{(1)} = 0 + 1 \cdot (2.5 - 0) = 2.5$.

**Iteration 1 (3.5 marks):**

At $x^{(1)} = 2.5$:

$$\nabla f_1(2.5) = 2(2.5 - 1) = 3$$

$$\nabla f_2(2.5) = 2(2.5 - 4) = -3$$

Re-form $F$ with same weights, solve analytically:

$$F(x) = x^2 - 5x + 8.5 \Rightarrow x^* = 2.5$$

Since the scalarized optimum has not moved, the algorithm has **converged**.

Final Pareto-optimal design: $x^{(1)} = 2.5$, with $f_1 = 2.25$ and $f_2 = 2.25$.

---

### Question B — Wierzbicki Reference Point Method [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | Module 4 Choice Q2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Write the **achievement scalarizing function** proposed by Wierzbicki. Explain every term, and prove (or argue) the Pareto-compatibility property.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — State the function:**

$$s(x \vert \bar{q}) = \max_{i=1,\ldots,k} \left[ \frac{f_i(x) - \bar{q}_i}{n_i} \right] + \rho \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{f_i(x)}{n_i}$$

Term meanings:
- $f_i(x)$ = value of $i$-th objective at $x$.
- $\bar{q}_i$ = aspiration level (reference point).
- $n_i$ = normalization range.
- $\rho$ = small augmentation constant.
- $\max$ = worst-overachievement term.
- $\sum$ = augmentation term ensuring proper Pareto optimality.

**Step 2 (5.5 marks) — Proof of Pareto Compatibility:**

Suppose $x^*$ minimizes $s(x \vert \bar{q})$ but is **not** Pareto-optimal. Then there exists $\hat{x} \in S$ such that:

$$f_i(\hat{x}) \leq f_i(x^*) \; \forall i, \quad \text{strict for some } i_0$$

Then for each $i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(f_i(\hat{x}) - \bar{q}_i)/n_i &\leq (f_i(x^*) - \bar{q}_i)/n_i \\
\Rightarrow \sigma_i(\hat{x}) &\leq \sigma_i(x^*)
\end{aligned}$$

Hence:

$$\sigma_{\max}(\hat{x}) \leq \sigma_{\max}(x^*)$$

Combined with the strict inequality in $i_0$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i(\hat{x}) < \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i(x^*)$$

Therefore:

$$s(\hat{x}) = \sigma_{\max}(\hat{x}) + \rho \sum \sigma_i(\hat{x}) < s(x^*) = \sigma_{\max}(x^*) + \rho \sum \sigma_i(x^*)$$

This **contradicts** the assumption that $x^*$ minimizes $s$. ∎

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the problem:

$$\min_{x \in [0, 10]} \{ f_1 = x^2, \; f_2 = (x - 8)^2 \}$$

The ideal point is $(0, 0)$, the nadir is $(100, 64)$. The DM specifies reference point $\bar{q} = (10, 10)$. Solve one iteration with $\rho = 0.05$.

**Solution:**

Normalization: $n_1 = 100, n_2 = 64$.

Achievement function:

$$s(x \vert \bar{q}) = \max \left\{ \frac{x^2 - 10}{100}, \frac{(x-8)^2 - 10}{64} \right\} + 0.05 \left( \frac{x^2}{100} + \frac{(x-8)^2}{64} \right)$$

Find the switch point where both arguments equal:

$$\frac{x^2 - 10}{100} = \frac{(x-8)^2 - 10}{64}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$64(x^2 - 10) = 100((x-8)^2 - 10)$$

$$64 x^2 - 640 = 100 x^2 - 1600 x + 6400 - 1000$$

$$0 = 36 x^2 - 1600 x + 6040$$

Solving:

$$x = \frac{1600 \pm \sqrt{1600^2 - 4 \cdot 36 \cdot 6040}}{72} = \frac{1600 \pm \sqrt{2560000 - 869760}}{72}$$

$$x = \frac{1600 \pm \sqrt{1690240}}{72} = \frac{1600 \pm 1300.1}{72}$$

Taking the relevant root: $x \approx 4.17$.

Evaluate at $x = 4.17$: $f_1 \approx 17.4, f_2 \approx 14.7$.

Both near the aspiration $\bar{q} = (10, 10)$ — the algorithm has successfully "aimed" at the reference point.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Module 4: Interactive Methods**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights**: In GDF, $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i = 1$ is **mandatory**. Omitting this costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong step-size formula**: $\alpha^{(k)} = \beta/k$ — students often write $\alpha^{(k)} = k/\beta$ or omit $\beta$. **1 mark** deducted per wrong form.
> 3. **Confusing Wierzbicki's `max` term with `min`**: The achievement function penalizes **over-achievement** (worse than aspiration) — using `min` is a **fundamental conceptual error** (**2–3 marks**).
> 4. **No justification of Pareto optimality**: In Part B derivations, simply stating the optimum without proving Pareto compatibility loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring the augmentation $\rho$ term**: Removing the $\rho \sum f_i$ term makes the solution only **weakly** Pareto-optimal. Examiners deduct **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interactive Methods**
>
> - **Interactive methods** combine DM intuition with algorithmic rigor through **iterative preference feedback**.
> - **Three categories of multi-objective methods**: a priori, a posteriori, interactive.
> - **GDF Method** uses **marginal rate of substitution (MRS)** and **Frank-Wolfe update**: $x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + \alpha^{(k)}(\bar{x}^{(k)} - x^{(k)})$.
> - **Convergence condition** for GDF: $\alpha^{(k)} = \beta/k, \; 0 < \beta \leq 1$.
> - **Wierzbicki's Achievement Function**: $s(x \vert \bar{q}) = \max_i (f_i - \bar{q}_i)/n_i + \rho \sum_i f_i/n_i$.
> - **Two properties of scalarizing functions**: Pareto compatibility and reference compatibility.
> - **Augmentation coefficient $\rho$** is small ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$) and ensures **proper** Pareto optimality.
> - **Light Beam Search** uses a **cone constraint** centered at the ideal point with adjustable width $\delta_i$.
> - **STEM** uses the **$\ell_\infty$ distance** to the ideal point.
> - Interactive methods require **fewer function evaluations** than generating the entire Pareto front.
> - **KTU exam focus**: GDF derivation, Wierzbicki proof, and one numerical iteration of either method.
> - **Remember to normalize** weights and objective ranges to **avoid dimensional inconsistency**.
> - **Real-world applications**: aerospace, automotive, structural, and process design optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpreting the Pareto Front
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interpreting the Pareto Front # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO)**, a design problem involves two or more conflicting objective functions that must be optimized simultaneously. Because no single solution can typically minimize (or maximize) all objectives at once, the concept of optimality is generalized. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pareto Front (PF):** The Pareto Front is the locus of all **Pareto-optimal (non-dominated) solutions** in the objective function space, representing the boundary of the attainable objective region beyond which no improvement in any objective is possible without sacrificing at least one other objective. **Mathematically**, consider a minimization problem with $k$ objectives: $$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \left[f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, f_k(\mathbf{x})\right]$$ The corresponding multi-objective optimization problem is stated as: $$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \left[f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, f_k(\mathbf{x})\right]$$ where $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is the feasible decision space, $\mathbf{x}$ is the decision vector, and $\mathbf{F}: \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ maps decisions to the objective space. A solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is **Pareto optimal** if there exists no feasible $\mathbf{x}$ such that: $$f_i(\mathbf{x}) \le f_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \; \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\} \quad \text{and} \quad f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \; \exists j \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$$ The set of all such Pareto-optimal points in the decision space is the **Pareto Set (PS)**, and its image in the objective space is the **Pareto Front (PF)**. > [!NOTE] > **Dominance Relation:** A solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ *dominates* $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ (denoted $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$) if it is at least as good in all objectives and strictly better in at least one. The Pareto front consists of all **non-dominated solutions**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Budget Laptop vs. Gaming Laptop Trade-Off" Imagine you are buying a laptop with a fixed budget. You want to: - **Minimize** the price (objective $f_1$) - **Maximize** the battery life (objective $f_2$) - **Maximize** the GPU performance (objective $f_3$) A **₹40,000 ultrabook** is cheap and has great battery life, but poor GPU. A **₹1,50,000 gaming laptop** has great GPU and battery is okay, but expensive. A **₹90,000 balanced laptop** is a middle ground. None of these laptops "dominates" the others — each is the best for a different preference. Plotting price (y-axis) versus a performance metric (x-axis) gives a **trade-off curve** — that curve **is the Pareto Front**. The Decision Maker (DM) picks the point on this curve that best matches their priorities. > [!TIP] > Think of the Pareto front as a **negotiation table** between objectives. No objective will "surrender" to improve another without a corresponding sacrifice. ## 1.3 Why Interpretation Matters in KTU Context KTU examiners emphasize that simply *generating* a Pareto front (via NSGA-II, MOEA/D, weighted-sum, etc.) is not enough. The engineer must: 1. **Visualize** the trade-offs clearly (2D, 3D, parallel coordinates, heat maps). 2. **Quantify** the quality of the front using formal metrics. 3. **Select** a final design point using decision-making tools. 4. **Validate** that the obtained front is well-converged, well-distributed, and well-spread. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Dominance Hierarchy — The Logic Behind the Front The construction and interpretation of a Pareto front follow a strict logical sequence: 1. **Sample the Decision Space:** Generate candidate solutions $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}$ either via evolutionary algorithms (NSGA-II, SPEA2) or classical methods ($\varepsilon$-constraint, weighted-sum, goal programming). 2. **Evaluate Objective Vectors:** Map each $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \to \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ in the objective space $\mathbb{R}^k$. 3. **Apply Pairwise Dominance Check:** For every pair of solutions, test whether one dominates the other using the strict inequality rule. 4. **Identify Non-Dominated Set:** All solutions that are not dominated by any other feasible solution form the **Pareto-optimal set**. 5. **Construct the Front:** Plotting or connecting these points (in 2D, they form a curve; in 3D, a surface; in $k$D, a hyper-surface) yields the **Pareto Front**. 6. **Trade-off Analysis:** The slope, curvature, and gaps of the front reveal *sensitivity* — a steep region means a small gain in $f_1$ costs a large sacrifice in $f_2$. 7. **Decision-Maker Selection:** The DM (engineer/manager) chooses a compromise solution using preference information (a priori, a posteriori, or interactive methods). ## 2.2 Classification of Pareto Fronts | Front Type | Description | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Convex** | Bulges toward the origin (minimization); uniform trade-offs | Easy to navigate; classic weighted-sum works | | **Non-convex** | Indented or concave shape | Weighted-sum fails to find middle region; $\varepsilon$-constraint needed | | **Disconnected** | Multiple disjoint trade-off regions | Multiple "best" families of designs exist | | **Degenerate** | Reduces to a single point | All objectives align — single optimum | | **Continuous** | Smooth trade-off curve | Differentiability-based sensitivity analysis possible | | **Discontinuous** | Sharp jumps in trade-off | Indicates phase change / constraint activation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** The **weighted-sum method** $\min \sum w_i f_i(\mathbf{x})$ **cannot** generate non-convex portions of a Pareto front. This is a classic KTU 2-mark question. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Pareto Front Quality Metrics > [!NOTE] > All metrics below assume the true/known Pareto front (or reference set $\mathcal{P}^*$) and the obtained set $\mathcal{P}$. Lower values are better unless stated otherwise. $\nearrow$ indicates "higher is better". | Metric | Formula | Description | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | **Generational Distance (GD)** | $\text{GD} = \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P} \vert} \displaystyle\sum_{v \in \mathcal{P}} \min_{u \in \mathcal{P}^*} \vert\vert v - u \vert\vert_2$ | Average distance from obtained points to nearest reference point | $\downarrow$ | | **Inverted GD (IGD)** | $\text{IGD} = \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert} \displaystyle\sum_{u \in \mathcal{P}^*} \min_{v \in \mathcal{P}} \vert\vert u - v \vert\vert_2$ | Measures convergence **and** spread using reference | $\downarrow$ | | **Spacing (SP)** | $\text{SP} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P} \vert - 1} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathcal{P} \vert} (d_i - \bar{d})^2}$ | Standard deviation of consecutive distances — measures uniformity | $\downarrow$ | | **Maximum Spread (MS)** | $\text{MS} = \sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{k} \max_{i,j} (f_m^{(i)} - f_m^{(j)})^2}$ | Diagonal of hyper-box covering $\mathcal{P}$ | $\uparrow$ | | **Hypervolume (HV)** | $\text{HV} = \text{Vol}\left( \bigcup_{v \in \mathcal{P}} \text{HBox}(v, r) \right)$ | Volume of objective space dominated by $\mathcal{P}$ below reference $r$ | $\uparrow$ | **Notation Caution:** Use $\vert \mathcal{P} \vert$ (with `\vert` or `\mid`) for set cardinality in tables to avoid breaking markdown. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Minimize fuel burn vs. structural weight vs. emission. - **Civil Engineering:** Minimize cost vs. maximize seismic safety vs. minimize construction time. - **Mechanical Design:** Minimize mass vs. maximize stiffness vs. minimize stress concentration. - **Electrical Engineering:** Minimize power consumption vs. maximize signal-to-noise ratio. - **Process Engineering:** Maximize yield vs. minimize energy input vs. minimize waste. The Pareto front is the **scientific basis for engineering compromise design** and is mandatory in modern KTU 2024-scheme project reports. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Connecting Weighted-Sum to a Single Point on the Pareto Front Consider the bi-objective problem (commonly tested in KTU): $$\min_{x \in [0, 2]} \; \left[ f_1(x) = x^2, \;\; f_2(x) = (x-2)^2 \right]$$ We apply the **weighted-sum scalarization** with weights $w_1, w_2 \ge 0$ and $w_1 + w_2 = 1$: $$\min_{x} \; L(x) = w_1 x^2 + w_2 (x-2)^2$$ **Step 1 — Expand the scalarized objective:** $$L(x) = w_1 x^2 + w_2 (x^2 - 4x + 4)$$ **Step 2 — Collect terms:** $$L(x) = (w_1 + w_2) x^2 - 4 w_2 x + 4 w_2$$ Since $w_1 + w_2 = 1$: $$L(x) = x^2 - 4 w_2 x + 4 w_2$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero (first-order optimality):** $$\frac{dL}{dx} = 2x - 4w_2 = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $x^*$:** $$x^* = 2 w_2 = 2(1 - w_1)$$ **Step 5 — Compute the corresponding objective values:** $$f_1(x^*) = (2w_2)^2 = 4 w_2^2$$ $$f_2(x^*) = (2w_2 - 2)^2 = 4(1 - w_2)^2 = 4 w_1^2$$ **Step 6 — Parametric form of the Pareto front:** Let $\lambda = w_1 \in [0, 1]$, then $w_2 = 1 - \lambda$. The Pareto front in objective space is: $$\begin{aligned} f_1(\lambda) &= 4(1 - \lambda)^2 \\ f_2(\lambda) &= 4 \lambda^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Eliminate the parameter to obtain an explicit curve:** Divide the equations: $\sqrt{f_1} + \sqrt{f_2} = 2$ ⇒ squaring yields: $$\sqrt{f_1} + \sqrt{f_2} = 2$$ Squaring once more: $$f_1 + f_2 + 2\sqrt{f_1 f_2} = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2\sqrt{f_1 f_2} = 4 - f_1 - f_2$$ Squaring again: $$4 f_1 f_2 = (4 - f_1 - f_2)^2$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **exact analytical Pareto front** for the test problem — a non-convex curve in the $(f_1, f_2)$ plane. The derivation shows how a single scalarization weights $w_i$ traces **one** point of the front. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Hypervolume for a 2-Objective Front Let the obtained Pareto front consist of $N$ points $\mathbf{v}_i = (f_1^{(i)}, f_2^{(i)})$ sorted by $f_1$ in increasing order (minimization). The reference point is $\mathbf{r} = (r_1, r_2)$. For the **2D case**, the dominated area is the union of axis-aligned rectangles: $$\begin{aligned} \text{HV}_2(\mathcal{P}, \mathbf{r}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left( f_1^{(i+1)} - f_1^{(i)} \right) \cdot \left( r_2 - f_2^{(i)} \right) \\ &\quad + \left( r_1 - f_1^{(N)} \right) \cdot \left( r_2 - f_2^{(N)} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Derivation logic:** The dominated area is decomposed into vertical strips. Each strip has width $\left( f_1^{(i+1)} - f_1^{(i)} \right)$ and height $\left( r_2 - f_2^{(i)} \right)$ — the height being limited by the **closest-to-origin** point (the smallest $f_2$ achieved up to strip $i$). The last strip extends to the reference $r_1$. **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Let $\mathcal{P} = \{(1, 5), (3, 2), (5, 1)\}$ (sorted by $f_1$) with $\mathbf{r} = (6, 6)$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HV} &= (3 - 1)(6 - 5) + (5 - 3)(6 - 2) + (6 - 5)(6 - 1) \\ &= (2)(1) + (2)(4) + (1)(5) \\ &= 2 + 8 + 5 = 15 \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Hypervolume** is the **only** Pareto-compliant unary indicator (knows whether one front dominates another). It is the **gold-standard metric** in KTU viva voce and project evaluations. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Interpreting a 3-Objective Pareto Front For $k = 3$ objectives, the Pareto front is a **2D surface in 3D space**. Standard KTU 2024 interpretation procedure: **Step 1 — Project onto coordinate planes** $(f_1, f_2)$, $(f_1, f_3)$, $(f_2, f_3)$ to get three 2D Pareto front "shadows." **Step 2 — Compute pairwise trade-off ratios:** $$\rho_{12} = \frac{\Delta f_2}{\Delta f_1}, \quad \rho_{13} = \frac{\Delta f_3}{\Delta f_1}, \quad \rho_{23} = \frac{\Delta f_3}{\Delta f_2}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the "knee point"** — the point of maximum curvature where small improvements in one objective require large sacrifices in others. This is often the **preferred compromise** for an undecided DM. **Step 4 — Use parallel coordinates** — plot all $k$ objectives as parallel vertical axes and draw polylines for each Pareto point. Clusters and crossovers reveal objective correlations. **Step 5 — Decision-making methods** (KTU standard list): | Method | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **Pseudo-weights** | A posteriori | $w_i \approx (f_i^{\max} - f_i)/(f_i^{\max} - f_i^{\min})$ from extreme points | | **TOPSIS** | A posteriori | Rank by closeness to ideal point | | **Compromise Programming** | A posteriori | Minimize $L_p = \left( \sum w_i \vert f_i - f_i^{\text{ideal}} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | | **Goal Attainment** | A priori | DM specifies targets $g_i$, weight vector drives $f_i \le g_i$ | | **Interactive (e.g., Light Beam Search)** | Interactive | DM progressively refines preferences | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Pareto Front Generation & Interpretation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Multi-Objective Problem Definition
Decision Variables x and k Objectives f1, f2,...,fk] --> B[Initialize Population
NSGA-II / MOEAD / Epsilon-Constraint] B --> C[Evaluate Objective Vectors
Compute F x for all candidates] C --> D[Fast Non-Dominated Sorting
Rank by Pareto Levels] D --> E[Crowding Distance Assignment
Preserve Diversity on Front] E --> F{Convergence Check
Max Generations or HV Plateau} F -->|Not Converged| G[Selection, Crossover, Mutation] G --> C F -->|Converged| H[Extract Pareto Set PS in Decision Space] H --> I[Map to Objective Space
Pareto Front PF] I --> J[Compute Quality Metrics
GD, IGD, SP, MS, HV] J --> K[Visualize PF
2D Curve, 3D Surface, Parallel Coordinates] K --> L{Decision Maker Preference
Available or Not} L -->|Yes| M[Apply DM Method
TOPSIS, Goal Programming, Knee Point] L -->|No| N[Report PF and Metrics
For External Decision] M --> O[Final Compromise Solution x star] N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Dominance Relation State Machine ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Candidate x1] -->|Compare f1 and f2| B{Strictly Better in at Least One Objective} B -->|Yes| C{Better or Equal in All Other Objectives} C -->|Yes| D[x1 Dominates x2
x1 less than x2] C -->|No| E[Non-Dominated Pair
Both on Pareto Front] B -->|No| F[Worse in All Objectives
Discarded] A2[Candidate x2] -->|Symmetric Check| E ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pareto Front Shape Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pareto Front] --> B[Continuous] A --> C[Disconnected] A --> D[Convex] A --> E[Non-Convex] A --> F[Degenerate Single Point] B --> B1[Smooth Tradeoff Curve] B --> B2[Steep and Flat Regions] C --> C1[Multiple Tradeoff Islands] C --> C2[Weighted Sum Fails] D --> D1[Weighted Sum Works] D --> D2[Even Distribution] E --> E1[Weighted Sum Misses Middle] E --> E2[Use Epsilon Constraint] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Visual Interpretation Tools ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Pareto Front Data] --> V1[Scatter Plot
2D and 3D] P --> V2[Parallel Coordinates
High Dimensional] P --> V3[Radar or Spider Chart
Multi Criteria] P --> V4[Heatmap
Objective Correlation] P --> V5[Decision Map
Colored by Region] V1 --> DM[Decision Maker Analysis] V2 --> DM V3 --> DM V4 --> DM V5 --> DM DM --> F1[Knee Point Detection] DM --> F2[Cluster Analysis] DM --> F3[Tradeoff Slope Mapping] F1 --> SEL[Final Compromise Selection] F2 --> SEL F3 --> SEL ``` > [!TIP] > The knee point is the **geometric point of maximum curvature** on a 2D Pareto front. It is **mathematically** the point that minimizes the distance to the utopia point in a normalized objective space while maximizing the marginal trade-off rate. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the Pareto Front. With a neat sketch, illustrate the Pareto front for a bi-objective minimization problem and identify the non-dominated region.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > The Pareto Front is the set of non-dominated objective vectors in the objective space of a multi-objective optimization problem. For a bi-objective minimization problem with objectives $f_1$ and $f_2$, the Pareto front is a curve passing through all points such that no feasible point lies in the **lower-left quadrant** of any point on the curve. The region **above and to the right** of the front is the **infeasible/improvable region** dominated by front points. **Sketch (ASCII representation):** ``` f2 | * ← Dominated Point | / | * ← Pareto Front (Non-dominated) | / | * |/________ f1 Ideal Point (0,0) — unattainable ``` **Key features to label:** Feasible region, infeasible region, dominated points, non-dominated points, utopia point, and nadir point. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List and briefly explain any three quality metrics used to evaluate a Pareto front.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Generational Distance (GD):** Measures convergence by computing the average Euclidean distance from each obtained Pareto point to the nearest point on the true/reference Pareto front. Smaller GD implies better convergence. 2. **Spacing (SP):** Measures the uniformity of distribution of points along the obtained Pareto front. It is the standard deviation of the distances between consecutive points. A value near zero implies an evenly spaced front. 3. **Hypervolume (HV):** Measures the volume (area in 2D) of the objective space dominated by the obtained Pareto front with respect to a reference point. Larger HV indicates better convergence, spread, and uniformity combined. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Solve the following bi-objective optimization problem using the **weighted-sum method** and derive the analytical Pareto front. Take $x \in [0, 4]$. $$\min_{x} \; \left[ f_1(x) = x^2, \;\; f_2(x) = (x - 4)^2 \right]$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Form the scalarized objective with weights $w_1, w_2 \ge 0$, $w_1 + w_2 = 1$: $$L(x) = w_1 x^2 + w_2 (x - 4)^2$$ Expand: $$L(x) = w_1 x^2 + w_2 (x^2 - 8x + 16) = (w_1 + w_2) x^2 - 8 w_2 x + 16 w_2 = x^2 - 8 w_2 x + 16 w_2$$ Differentiate and set to zero: $$\frac{dL}{dx} = 2x - 8 w_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x^* = 4 w_2 = 4(1 - w_1)$$ Substitute into objectives: $$f_1(x^*) = (4w_2)^2 = 16 w_2^2, \quad f_2(x^*) = (4w_2 - 4)^2 = 16(1 - w_2)^2 = 16 w_1^2$$ Parametric Pareto front (let $\lambda = w_1$): $$f_1 = 16(1 - \lambda)^2, \quad f_2 = 16 \lambda^2$$ Eliminate $\lambda$: $\sqrt{f_1}/4 + \sqrt{f_2}/4 = 1$, giving: $$\sqrt{f_1} + \sqrt{f_2} = 4$$ This is the **non-convex analytical Pareto front** of the problem. **(b)** Compute the **Generational Distance (GD)** and **Spacing (SP)** for the obtained Pareto set $\mathcal{P} = \{(1, 9), (4, 4), (9, 1)\}$ given the reference set $\mathcal{P}^* = \{(0, 16), (4, 4), (16, 0)\}$ on the same analytical front. Assume the Euclidean norm. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Generational Distance (GD):** For each $v \in \mathcal{P}$, find $\min_{u \in \mathcal{P}^*} \vert\vert v - u \vert\vert_2$: - For $v_1 = (1, 9)$: nearest $u = (0, 16)$ ⇒ $d = \sqrt{1 + 49} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071$ - For $v_2 = (4, 4)$: nearest $u = (4, 4)$ ⇒ $d = 0$ - For $v_3 = (9, 1)$: nearest $u = (16, 0)$ ⇒ $d = \sqrt{49 + 1} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071$ $$\text{GD} = \frac{1}{3}(7.071 + 0 + 7.071) = \frac{14.142}{3} = 4.714$$ **Spacing (SP):** Compute consecutive distances $d_i = \vert\vert v_{i+1} - v_i \vert\vert_2$: $$d_1 = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (4-9)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 25} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.831$$ $$d_2 = \sqrt{(9-4)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.831$$ Mean distance: $$\bar{d} = \frac{5.831 + 5.831}{2} = 5.831$$ Spacing: $$\text{SP} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2-1}\left[(5.831 - 5.831)^2 + (5.831 - 5.831)^2\right]} = 0$$ The perfectly even spacing yields $\text{SP} = 0$, indicating **ideal uniformity**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]** **(a)** Define **Pareto optimality**, **dominance**, and **Pareto front**. Explain the difference between **weak** and **strong dominance** with mathematical formulations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Pareto Optimality:** A feasible solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is Pareto optimal if there is no other feasible $\mathbf{x}$ that improves at least one objective without worsening any other. **Dominance:** A vector $\mathbf{u}$ dominates $\mathbf{v}$ ($\mathbf{u} \prec \mathbf{v}$) if $u_i \le v_i$ for all $i$ and $u_j < v_j$ for at least one $j$ (assuming minimization). **Pareto Front:** The image of all Pareto-optimal solutions in the objective space. **Strong vs. Weak Dominance:** - **Strong Dominance ($\mathbf{u} \prec \mathbf{v}$):** $u_i \le v_i \; \forall i$ and $u_i < v_i \; \exists i$. Every objective is at least as good and at least one is strictly better. - **Weak Dominance ($\mathbf{u} \preceq_w \mathbf{v}$):** $u_i \le v_i \; \forall i$. At least as good in all objectives, with strict improvement nowhere required. **Weak Pareto Optimality:** $\mathbf{x}^*$ is weakly Pareto optimal if there is no $\mathbf{x}$ such that $f_i(\mathbf{x}) < f_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \; \forall i$. **Key Difference:** Weak dominance is a **weaker** condition. The set of weakly Pareto-optimal solutions is a **superset** of the strongly Pareto-optimal set. In practice, weakly optimal solutions are often **interior points** of the feasible objective region and are typically not preferred. **(b)** For the following 2D objective vectors, identify the **Pareto-optimal set** and **plot the Pareto front**: $\{(2, 8), (4, 5), (5, 4), (6, 6), (8, 2), (3, 7)\}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Sort and apply dominance check: | Point | $f_1$ | $f_2$ | Dominated by | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 2 | 8 | None | **Pareto Optimal** | | B | 3 | 7 | A (2<3, 8>7 — not both) | Check manually | | C | 4 | 5 | A (2<4, 8>5 — A does not dominate) | **Pareto Optimal** | | D | 5 | 4 | C (4<5, 5>4 — no) | **Pareto Optimal** | | E | 6 | 6 | D (5<6, 4<6 — D dominates E) | Dominated | | F | 8 | 2 | D (5<8, 4>2 — no) | **Pareto Optimal** | Re-check B = (3, 7): Compare to A = (2, 8): $2 < 3$ but $8 > 7$, so A does not dominate. Compare to C = (4, 5): $4 > 3$ so C does not dominate. Compare to D, E, F: same reasoning. **B is Pareto optimal** (only A and B have a similar regime). **Pareto Front Set:** $\mathcal{P} = \{(2, 8), (3, 7), (4, 5), (5, 4), (8, 2)\}$. Dominated points: $\{(6, 6)\}$. **Pareto Front Sketch:** ``` f2 8 * (2,8) ← Pareto 7 * (3,7) ← Pareto 6 *(6,6) ← Dominated 5 *(4,5) ← Pareto 4 *(5,4) ← Pareto 3 2 *(8,2) ← Pareto 1________________________ f1 2 3 4 5 6 8 ``` **Interpretation:** The Pareto front is a monotonically decreasing curve showing that improving $f_1$ requires worsening $f_2$. The dominated point (6, 6) is excluded from the front. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Interpreting the Pareto Front" Questions:** > 1. **Confusing dominated vs. non-dominated**: Students often mark a point as Pareto optimal if it is "good in one objective" — must check **all** objectives. > 2. **Forgetting to set the reference point** for Hypervolume calculation — HV depends on $\mathbf{r}$; always state it explicitly. > 3. **Mixing up minimization and maximization sign conventions** in dominance inequalities. > 4. **Assuming weighted-sum finds the entire front** — it fails on non-convex fronts (a guaranteed 2-mark KTU question). > 5. **Skipping the decision-making step** — KTU wants the *interpretation*, not just the front. Always discuss trade-off slopes, knee point, and DM selection. > 6. **Not labeling the axes/units** in sketches — examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabeled diagrams. > 7. **Confusing Pareto Set (decision space) with Pareto Front (objective space)** — these are distinct but related concepts. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Pareto Front** = non-dominated points in **objective space**; **Pareto Set** = non-dominated points in **decision space**. - [x] A solution is **Pareto optimal** if no feasible solution improves one objective without worsening another. - [x] **Strong dominance** requires strict improvement in at least one objective; **weak dominance** does not. - [x] **Weighted-sum method** can **only** generate convex portions of the Pareto front; use **$\varepsilon$-constraint** for non-convex fronts. - [x] **Hypervolume (HV)** is the **gold standard** metric — captures convergence, spread, and uniformity simultaneously. **Higher is better**. - [x] **GD, IGD, SP, MS** are auxiliary metrics — **lower (or higher for MS, HV)** is better. - [x] **Knee point** = maximum curvature point on the front = preferred compromise solution for an undecided DM. - [x] **Visualization tools**: 2D/3D scatter, parallel coordinates, radar charts, heat maps. - [x] **Decision-making methods**: TOPSIS, Compromise Programming, Goal Attainment, pseudo-weights. - [x] Always check whether the front is **convex, non-convex, connected, or disconnected** — this dictates which algorithm and which metric to use. - [x] KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **interpretation over mere generation** — always discuss trade-offs, knee points, and DM preferences. - [x] In project reports, present the **Pareto front, quality metrics, knee point analysis, and final selected design** together for full credit. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** Memorize the HV formula for 2D and the parametric form of the Pareto front for the standard test problem $f_1 = x^2$, $f_2 = (x-a)^2$. These are the most repeated KTU questions across PEPET743.

topics.title: Multidisciplinary Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Multidisciplinary Optimization # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Multidisciplinary Optimization (MDO)** is a formal methodology in engineering design that uses mathematical optimization techniques to solve coupled engineering problems involving multiple interacting disciplines (such as structures, aerodynamics, propulsion, thermal, controls, manufacturing) simultaneously, while accounting for the interdependencies and couplings between the disciplines to obtain an optimal system-level design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Martins & Lambe, 2013 framework):** > *Multidisciplinary Optimization is a field of research that deals with the design optimization of engineering systems that involve multiple disciplines or subsystems, where the interactions between these disciplines are critical to system performance and cannot be neglected.* Mathematically, a generic MDO problem is expressed as: $$\min_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}} \quad f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$ $$\text{subject to:} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, J$$ $$h_k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, K$$ $$c_{ij}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_{ij}) = 0 \quad \text{(coupling constraints)}$$ Where: - $\mathbf{x}$ = vector of **global design variables** (shared across disciplines) - $\mathbf{y}$ = vector of **state/coupling variables** (outputs of one discipline, inputs to another) - $c_{ij}$ = **coupling equations** linking discipline $i$ to discipline $j$ ## 1.2 The Need for MDO Traditional design follows a **sequential, siloed approach**: 1. Aerodynamicist designs the wing 2. Structural analyst sizes the structure 3. Propulsion engineer fits the engine 4. Control system designer integrates > [!NOTE] > **Why does this fail?** > In this **sequential approach**, the final design is rarely optimal because each discipline optimizes locally without considering system-level trade-offs. Changes in one discipline propagate to others, often invalidating prior assumptions. MDO solves this by treating the **entire coupled system as a single optimization problem**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Team of Specialists" Metaphor Imagine designing a **Formula 1 car** with four specialists: - **Aerodynamicist** (wants thin wings, low drag) - **Structural Engineer** (wants thick wings, high strength) - **Engineer** (wants large air intake) - **Tire Specialist** (wants low weight, high downforce) If they work **in isolation**, the aerodynamicist might design a wing that the structural engineer finds unsafe — the whole project collapses. In the **MDO approach**, the four specialists work as a coordinated team, each contributing their discipline's analysis, while a **chief architect (system-level optimizer)** continuously balances trade-offs so the **entire car wins the race**, not just one component. > [!NOTE] > **The 3 Pillars of MDO:** > 1. **Decomposition** — breaking the system into manageable discipline-level sub-problems > 2. **Coordination** — managing the inter-disciplinary coupling and information flow > 3. **Convergence** — achieving consistency across all disciplines at the optimum ## 1.4 Classification of MDO by Coupling Type | Coupling Type | Description | Example | |---------------|-------------|---------| | **Weak Coupling** | Disciplines nearly independent; small interaction | Civil/Mechanical partition | | **Strong Coupling** | Disciplines strongly interdependent | Aeroelasticity, Thermo-structural | | **One-way Coupling** | Information flows only one direction | Aerodynamics → Structure (fixed CL) | | **Two-way Coupling** | Mutual interaction, must iterate | Flutter analysis | ## 1.5 Key Terminology > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Design Variable ($x$)**: Parameter the designer controls (e.g., wing chord) > - **State Variable ($y$)**: Discipline output (e.g., lift coefficient $C_L$) > - **Coupling Variable**: State variable shared between disciplines > - **Objective Function ($f$)**: Quantity to be minimized/maximized (e.g., weight) > - **Feasibility**: All disciplines must satisfy their constraints simultaneously > - **System-Optimum**: The truly optimal design considering ALL disciplines # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of MDO ### 2.1.1 General Monolithic Formulation (All-At-Once) The most general (and computationally expensive) MDO formulation solves everything as **one large optimization problem**: $$\min_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}} \quad f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i) \leq 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, N\}$$ $$c_{ij}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i, \mathbf{y}_j) = 0, \quad \forall (i, j) \text{ coupled pairs}$$ $$\mathbf{x}_L \leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{x}_U$$ **Disadvantage**: Requires every discipline's analysis to be available in a single optimizer — incompatible with **black-box** commercial codes (NASTRAN, FLUENT, etc.). ### 2.1.2 The Coupling Graph For a system with $N$ disciplines, the **coupling structure** can be represented by a graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - Vertices $V = \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ represent disciplines - Edges $E$ represent coupling relationships For a 3-discipline problem (Aero-Structure-Propulsion), the coupling matrix looks like: $$\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & c_{12} & c_{13} \\ c_{21} & 0 & c_{23} \\ c_{31} & c_{32} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ ## 2.2 MDO Architectures (KTU Critical — High Weightage) The classification of MDO methods is the **most tested area** in KTU exams. Each architecture differs in: - **Where** the optimizer(s) sit(s) - **How** coupling is enforced - **Information flow** between disciplines ### 2.2.1 Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF) — "The Centralized Approach" **Workflow:** 1. Choose global design variables $\mathbf{x}$ 2. Solve the **multidisciplinary analysis (MDA)** for state variables $\mathbf{y}$ so that all coupling equations are satisfied 3. Evaluate objective and constraints using consistent $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ 4. Outer optimizer updates $\mathbf{x}$ using gradient information **Mathematical form:** $$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \quad f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}))$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad g(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x})) \leq 0$$ where $\mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x})$ is obtained by solving MDA: $c(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$ > [!NOTE] > **Advantage**: Produces a **fully feasible** design at every iteration. > **Disadvantage**: Requires gradient computation through MDA — expensive for tight coupling. ### 2.2.2 Individual Discipline Feasible (IDF) — "The Distributed Approach" **Workflow:** Each discipline is solved independently; coupling is enforced as **equality constraints** in the outer optimization. $$\min_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}} \quad f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad c_{ij}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i, \mathbf{y}_j) = 0 \quad \text{(as optimization constraints)}$$ $$g_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i) \leq 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key feature**: Each discipline's analysis runs **independently** with its own guess of coupling variables — perfect for black-box codes. ### 2.2.3 All-At-Once (AAO) No MDA is performed. All variables (design + state) are treated as independent optimization variables. Requires **full derivative information** (sensitivities). ### 2.2.4 Collaborative Optimization (CO) — Two-Level Hierarchy Introduced by **Braun & Kroo (1996)**. Each discipline has its own optimizer: - **System optimizer** (level 1): Optimizes global variables to minimize system objective - **Discipline optimizers** (level 2): Each discipline tries to match targets set by the system optimizer **System-level objective** penalizes deviation from discipline-optimal solutions: $$\min_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}} \quad f_0(\mathbf{x})$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad J_i^* = \min_{\mathbf{x}_i} \left[ (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{z}_i)^T (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{z}_i) \right]$$ $$J_i^* \leq \epsilon$$ where $\mathbf{z}_i$ are **target values** from the system level. > [!NOTE] > **CO Characteristics:** > - Preserves **disciplinary autonomy** (each team uses own tools) > - Mimics **organizational structure** of engineering companies > - **Disadvantage**: Slower convergence; non-feasible intermediate designs ### 2.2.5 Concurrent Subspace Optimization (CSSO) Each discipline optimizes only its **own subspace** of variables while other variables are held at current values. Uses **response surfaces / approximations** to model other disciplines' influence. ### 2.2.6 Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS) Variant of CSSO proposed by **Sobieski** (NASA). Uses **global sensitivity equations (GSE)** to propagate information between subspaces. ### 2.2.7 Analytical Target Cascading (ATC) A **hierarchical decomposition** where the top-level system passes **targets** (acceptable ranges) to subsystems, which then pass sub-targets to their components, cascading down. Each level must satisfy its assigned targets. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | General MDO objective | $\min_{\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}} f(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ | Defines the system goal | | 2 | MDF formulation | $\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}))$ | Centralized with implicit $\mathbf{y}$ | | 3 | IDF formulation | $c_{ij}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i, \mathbf{y}_j) = 0$ as constraints | Distributed, black-box friendly | | 4 | CO system level | $\min \left[ f_0(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i w_i \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{z}_i \rVert^2 \right]$ | Penalize target mismatch | | 5 | MDA residual | $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{y}) = c(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 0$ | Fixed-point iteration | | 6 | Coupling Jacobian | $\frac{\partial c}{\partial \mathbf{y}}$ | Sensitivity of coupling | | 7 | Total derivative (MDF) | $\frac{df}{d\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}}$ | Chain rule through MDA | | 8 | GSE equation | $\frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}} = -\left(\frac{\partial c}{\partial \mathbf{y}}\right)^{-1} \frac{\partial c}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ | Global sensitivity | | 9 | ATC consistency | $\lVert \mathbf{r}_{resp} - \mathbf{t}_{parent} \rVert^2 \leq \epsilon$ | Target satisfaction | | 10 | BLISS sub-problem | $\min_{\mathbf{x}_i} f_i \text{ s.t. } g_i \leq 0, \text{ given } \mathbf{x}_{-i}$ | Discipline-level sub-optimization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units & Conventions:** > - Design variables $\mathbf{x}$: dimensionless or domain-specific (e.g., **meters** for length, **radians** for angles) > - State variables $\mathbf{y}$: discipline outputs (e.g., **Pascals** for stress, **Newtons** for force) > - Objective: typically **weight (kg)** or **cost (currency)** for minimization ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Aircraft Design**: Aero-structural-propulsion trade-off (e.g., wing weight vs. drag vs. fuel burn) 2. **Automotive**: Crashworthiness vs. NVH vs. manufacturing cost 3. **Launch Vehicles**: Structural mass vs. payload capacity vs. trajectory optimization 4. **Civil Structures**: Architectural form vs. structural safety vs. HVAC energy 5. **Turbomachinery**: Aerodynamic performance vs. mechanical integrity vs. thermal limits # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Derivative in MDF (KTU Most Important Derivative) In the **Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)** architecture, the objective $f$ depends on $\mathbf{x}$ only *implicitly* through the state variables $\mathbf{y}$ obtained from the MDA. To perform gradient-based optimization, we need the **total derivative** $\dfrac{df}{d\mathbf{x}}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1: Define the objective** $$f = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}))$$ Here $\mathbf{y}$ is an *implicit* function of $\mathbf{x}$ through the MDA system. **Step 2: Differentiate using the chain rule** $$\frac{df}{d\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}}$$ **Step 3: Identify the implicit relationship from MDA** $$\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$$ This is a vector of $m$ coupling equations. **Step 4: Differentiate the MDA equation with respect to $\mathbf{x}$** $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$$ **Step 5: Solve for the total derivative of $\mathbf{y}$** $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$ $$\frac{d\mathbf{y}}{d\mathbf{x}} = -\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}}\right)^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$ This is the **Global Sensitivity Equation (GSE)**. **Step 6: Substitute back into the total derivative of $f$** $$\frac{df}{d\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} - \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}}\right)^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: The term $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}}\right)^{-1}$ is the **coupling Jacobian inverse**, which captures how state variables change when design variables change. This is the most computationally expensive part — for large systems, it requires solving multiple linear systems. ## 3.2 Derivation: Fixed-Point Iteration for MDA (Jacobi/Gauss-Seidel) When MDA is solved iteratively (the typical case in MDO), we use **fixed-point iteration**: **Step 1: Decompose the coupling into per-discipline equations** $$\mathbf{c}_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i, \mathbf{y}_j) = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N$$ **Step 2: Solve each equation explicitly for the local state** $$\mathbf{y}_i^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{c}_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i^{(k)}, \mathbf{y}_j^{(k)})$$ **Step 3: Jacobi update (parallel)** $$\mathbf{y}_i^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{F}_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_1^{(k)}, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_N^{(k)})$$ **Step 4: Gauss-Seidel update (sequential)** $$\mathbf{y}_i^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{F}_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_1^{(k+1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_{i-1}^{(k+1)}, \mathbf{y}_i^{(k)}, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_N^{(k)})$$ **Step 5: Convergence check** $$\lVert \mathbf{y}^{(k+1)} - \mathbf{y}^{(k)} \rVert_2 \leq \epsilon_{MDA}$$ Typically $\epsilon_{MDA} = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ for high-fidelity MDO. ## 3.3 Derivation: Collaborative Optimization (CO) Penalty Form **Step 1: Define the system-level problem** System optimizer chooses global design variables and **target values** $\mathbf{z}_i$ for each discipline: $$\min_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}} \quad f_0(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \lVert J_i^*(\mathbf{z}_i) \rVert^2$$ **Step 2: Each discipline solves a sub-problem** Discipline $i$ minimizes the deviation from the system-supplied targets: $$J_i^*(\mathbf{z}_i) = \min_{\mathbf{x}_i} \quad (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{z}_i)^T (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{z}_i)$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i) \leq 0$$ $$c_i(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i) = 0$$ **Step 3: System-level constraint** The system is feasible only when each discipline achieves zero deviation from targets: $$J_i^*(\mathbf{z}_i) \leq \epsilon_i, \quad \forall i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does CO work?** > CO mimics real engineering organizations. The system optimizer acts as a **chief engineer**, while discipline optimizers act as **team leads**. Each team owns its decision space, but is "guided" toward system-optimality by the targets. ## 3.4 Analogy Matrix: MDO Architecture Comparison (KTU Frequently Asked) | Architecture | Levels | Disciplinary Autonomy | Intermediate Feasibility | Gradient Requirement | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **AAO** | 1 | None | Infeasible | Full derivatives | Small problems with known gradients | | **MDF** | 1 (with MDA) | None | **Fully feasible** | Through MDA | Tightly coupled, well-modeled systems | | **IDF** | 1 | High | Feasible per discipline | Per discipline | Black-box disciplinary codes | | **CO** | 2 | **Very high** | Not feasible | Optional | Distributed engineering organizations | | **CSSO** | 2 | High | Approximate | Approximations | Subsystem-level tuning | | **BLISS** | 2 | Moderate | Approximate | GSE-based | Hierarchical design | | **ATC** | Many | Very high | Target-satisfying | Each level | Product family design | ## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example: Two-Discipline MDO (Sellar's Problem) **Sellar's Standard MDO Problem (1996)** is the benchmark for testing MDO algorithms. It has two disciplines and is **bidirectionally coupled**. **Problem Statement:** $$\min \quad f = x_1^2 + x_2 + y_1 + e^{-y_2}$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad g_1 = \frac{y_1}{3.16} - 1 \leq 0$$ $$g_2 = \frac{y_2}{24} - 1 \leq 0$$ $$1 \leq x_1 \leq 3, \quad -1 \leq x_2 \leq 1$$ **Discipline 1 (Structural):** $$y_1 = \sqrt{x_1} + z_1, \quad z_1 = y_2$$ **Discipline 2 (Aerodynamic):** $$y_2 = 0.1 x_1 + z_2, \quad z_2 = y_1$$ **Coupling analysis:** **Step 1: Substitute $z_1 = y_2$ into Discipline 1** $$y_1 = \sqrt{x_1} + y_2$$ **Step 2: Substitute $z_2 = y_1$ into Discipline 2** $$y_2 = 0.1 x_1 + y_1$$ **Step 3: Combine to eliminate $y_2$** From Step 1: $y_2 = y_1 - \sqrt{x_1}$ Substitute into Step 2: $y_1 - \sqrt{x_1} = 0.1 x_1 + y_1$ $$\Rightarrow \quad -\sqrt{x_1} = 0.1 x_1$$ This is solvable for $x_1$, demonstrating the **fully coupled nature** of MDO. > [!NOTE] > **For KTU exams**: While Sellar's exact numerical solution is not required, students must be able to: > 1. Identify the **coupling variables** ($z_1, z_2$) > 2. Identify the **global design variables** ($x_1, x_2$) > 3. Recognize that the problem is **two-way coupled** > 4. Sketch the data flow (Discipline 1 → Discipline 2 → Discipline 1) # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 MDO Information Flow — Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Outer Optimizer
Selects global x] --> B[Multidisciplinary Analysis MDA
Solves c x y = 0] B --> C[Discipline 1 Analysis
y1 = f1 x y] B --> D[Discipline 2 Analysis
y2 = f2 x y] B --> E[Discipline 3 Analysis
y3 = f3 x y] C --> F[Compute f x y
Evaluate objective] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Check constraints
g x y le 0] G --> A style A fill:#FFE4B5 style B fill:#87CEEB style F fill:#90EE90 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: In MDF, there is a single outer loop optimizer. Inside each iteration, an **inner MDA loop** resolves the coupling before evaluation. The design is **feasible at every outer iteration**. ## 4.2 MDO Information Flow — Individual Discipline Feasible (IDF) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Outer Optimizer
Selects x and y] --> B[Discipline 1 Analysis
Independent] A --> C[Discipline 2 Analysis
Independent] A --> D[Discipline 3 Analysis
Independent] B --> E[Coupling Constraint Check
c12 = 0, c13 = 0] C --> E D --> E E --> F[Compute objective f] F --> A style A fill:#FFE4B5 style B fill:#FFB6C1 style C fill:#FFB6C1 style D fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#FFA07A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: In IDF, disciplines run **independently in parallel** (great for distributed black-box codes). Coupling is enforced as **constraints in the outer problem**. ## 4.3 MDO Information Flow — Collaborative Optimization (CO) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[System Optimizer Level 1
Minimizes f0 with targets zi] --> B[Set Targets z1, z2, z3] B --> C[Discipline 1 Sub-Optimizer
Match target z1] B --> D[Discipline 2 Sub-Optimizer
Match target z2] B --> E[Discipline 3 Sub-Optimizer
Match target z3] C --> F[Return Ji star to System] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Check Ji star le epsilon] G --> A style A fill:#DDA0DD style C fill:#F0E68C style D fill:#F0E68C style E fill:#F0E68C ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: CO is a **two-level hierarchy**. The system optimizer passes **targets** down, and each discipline optimizer tries to **match its target** while satisfying its own constraints. ## 4.4 Coupling Graph for a 3-Discipline Aircraft MDO ```mermaid graph LR AERO[Aerodynamics
Discipline A] -->|Lift CL| STRUCT[Structural
Discipline B] STRUCT -->|Deflection delta| AERO STRUCT -->|Weight W| PROP[Propulsion
Discipline C] PROP -->|Thrust T| STRUCT AERO -->|Drag D| PROP style AERO fill:#87CEEB style STRUCT fill:#FFB6C1 style PROP fill:#90EE90 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Bidirectional coupling**: Aerodynamics and Structure are **two-way coupled** (aeroelasticity). Propulsion is **one-way coupled** to structure but **two-way coupled** to aerodynamics. This is the canonical **aero-structural-propulsion** MDO problem in aircraft design. ## 4.5 MDO Architecture Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: MDO Problem] --> B{Do you have full
gradient information?} B -->|Yes| C{Is the problem
small to medium size?} B -->|No| D{Do you have
black-box codes?} C -->|Yes| E[Use MDF
Centralized approach] C -->|No| F[Use AAO
All-At-Once] D -->|Yes| G{Need disciplinary
autonomy?} D -->|No| H[Use MDF] G -->|Yes| I{Need fast
convergence?} G -->|No| J[Use IDF] I -->|Yes| K[Use CSSO or BLISS] I -->|No| L[Use CO] style E fill:#90EE90 style F fill:#FFB6C1 style H fill:#90EE90 style J fill:#FFB6C1 style K fill:#FFE4B5 style L fill:#DDA0DD ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU exams**: Memorize the **trigger conditions** for each architecture. This tree is the most likely 14-mark question on MDO. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1: Define Multidisciplinary Optimization (MDO). [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Model Answer:** Multidisciplinary Optimization (MDO) is a formal engineering design methodology that formulates and solves optimization problems involving **multiple interacting engineering disciplines** simultaneously, while explicitly accounting for the **coupling relationships** between them, in order to obtain an **optimal system-level design**. Unlike traditional sequential design, MDO uses mathematical programming to **coordinate** the analyses of all disciplines (e.g., structures, aerodynamics, thermal, controls) within a single optimization framework, capturing the trade-offs between them. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marking Tip (1 mark each):** > - 1 mark: "Multiple disciplines involved" > - 1 mark: "Coupling between disciplines accounted for" > - 1 mark: "System-level optimum" --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between MDF and IDF architectures. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Model Answer:** | Feature | MDF (Multidisciplinary Feasible) | IDF (Individual Discipline Feasible) | |---------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Number of levels** | Single level with inner MDA loop | Single level, no MDA | | **Intermediate feasibility** | Fully feasible | Feasible per discipline, not coupled | | **Coupling enforcement** | Solved in MDA (equality) | Enforced as optimization constraints | | **Discipline analysis** | Sequential/iterative | Independent (parallel possible) | | **Gradient computation** | Requires full implicit differentiation | Per-discipline derivatives only | | **Best for** | Tightly coupled problems | Black-box disciplinary codes | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marking Tip (3 marks):** Present it as a **comparison table** with at least 3 valid points. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **(a)** Explain the various **MDO architectures** in detail with neat block diagrams. Discuss their relative merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For a two-discipline coupled MDO problem with disciplines $D_1$ and $D_2$ sharing coupling variable $y$, derive the **total derivative** of the objective function with respect to the design variable $x$ using the implicit function theorem, explaining the role of the **Global Sensitivity Equation (GSE)**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A: ### Part (a) — MDO Architectures [7 Marks] **1. All-At-Once (AAO) — 1 Mark** In AAO, design variables $x$ and state variables $y$ are treated as **independent optimization variables**. No MDA is performed. The full system is solved as a single large NLP: $$\min_{x, y} f(x, y) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad c(x, y) = 0, \quad g(x, y) \leq 0$$ *Merit*: Simple formulation. *Demerit*: Requires derivatives of all coupling equations; not suitable for black-box codes. **2. Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF) — 1.5 Marks** MDF uses a **nested approach**: the outer optimizer selects $x$, the inner MDA solves $c(x, y) = 0$ for $y$, and the objective is evaluated at the **fully consistent** point. Each iteration produces a **feasible design**. *Merit*: Highest accuracy, fully feasible designs. *Demerit*: Repeated MDA is expensive; requires implicit gradients. **3. Individual Discipline Feasible (IDF) — 1.5 Marks** IDF treats each discipline's analysis as a **black-box** and enforces coupling through **equality constraints** in the outer optimization. Each discipline sees fixed (possibly inconsistent) values of incoming coupling variables. *Merit*: Perfect for legacy/black-box codes; disciplines can be parallelized. *Demerit*: Slower convergence than MDF; intermediate designs not coupled-feasible. **4. Collaborative Optimization (CO) — 1.5 Marks** CO is a **two-level hierarchy**. The system-level optimizer sets **targets** $z_i$ for each discipline, and each discipline-level optimizer minimizes the deviation from its targets subject to its own constraints. The system-level constraint requires that all disciplines' deviations sum to zero. *Merit*: Preserves disciplinary autonomy, mimics organizational structure. *Demerit*: Slow convergence; non-feasible intermediate designs; many extra variables. **5. Concurrent Subspace Optimization (CSSO) and BLISS — 1 Mark** CSSO optimizes each discipline's subspace while approximating the rest using **response surfaces**. BLISS uses **Global Sensitivity Equations (GSE)** to propagate cross-coupling effects. Both are suited for large systems. **6. Analytical Target Cascading (ATC) — 0.5 Mark** ATC generalizes the hierarchical idea: top-level sets targets, sub-systems propagate sub-targets downward, with **deviation penalties** at each level. Used in product family design. > [!NOTE] > **Summary Table [Should be included in the answer]** | Architecture | Levels | Autonomy | Feasibility | Best For | |--------------|--------|----------|-------------|----------| | AAO | 1 | None | Infeasible | Small smooth problems | | MDF | 1+inner | None | Feasible | Tight coupling | | IDF | 1 | Moderate | Per-discipline | Black-box codes | | CO | 2 | High | Non-feasible | Distributed orgs | | CSSO/BLISS | 2 | High | Approx. | Hierarchical | | ATC | Many | Very high | Target-driven | Product families | ### Part (b) — Derivation of Total Derivative [7 Marks] **Given** a 2-discipline MDO problem with: - Discipline 1: $D_1: c_1(x, y_1, y_2) = 0$, output $y_1$ - Discipline 2: $D_2: c_2(x, y_1, y_2) = 0$, output $y_2$ - Coupling: $y_1$ is input to $D_2$, $y_2$ is input to $D_1$ - Objective: $f = f(x, y_1, y_2)$ **Step 1: Identify the implicit relationship** The MDA system is: $$\mathbf{c}(x, y_1, y_2) = \begin{bmatrix} c_1(x, y_1, y_2) \\ c_2(x, y_1, y_2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Apply the Implicit Function Theorem** Since $\mathbf{c}(x, y) = 0$ and $\partial \mathbf{c} / \partial \mathbf{y}$ is invertible, $y = y(x)$ exists. Differentiate: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{dx} = 0$$ **Step 3: Solve for the sensitivity $\dfrac{d\mathbf{y}}{dx}$** $$\frac{d\mathbf{y}}{dx} = -\left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \right)^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial x}$$ This is the **Global Sensitivity Equation (GSE)**. **Step 4: Compute the total derivative of the objective** $$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{y}}{dx}$$ Substituting the GSE: $$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \right)^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial x}$$ **Step 5: Role of the GSE** The GSE captures **how state variables respond to design variable changes** through the chain of discipline couplings. It is the **most expensive computational step** in MDF, as it requires solving $n_x$ linear systems with the same Jacobian $\partial \mathbf{c} / \partial \mathbf{y}$. Approximate methods (finite differences, automatic differentiation, adjoint methods) are used in practice. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For 7 marks, examiners expect:** > - Clear statement of the implicit function theorem > - Step-by-step algebra > - Explicit form of the GSE > - Interpretation of the role of the Jacobian --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a suitable example, explain the concept of **coupled disciplines** in MDO. Define **design variables, state variables, coupling variables**, and **coupling constraints** with an engineering example from aircraft design. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Describe the **Collaborative Optimization (CO)** architecture in detail. Explain how the system-level optimizer and discipline-level optimizers interact. What are the key advantages and limitations of CO compared to MDF? **[7 Marks]** --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B: ### Part (a) — Coupled Disciplines in MDO [7 Marks] **Concept of Coupled Disciplines (3 Marks):** Coupled disciplines are two or more engineering analysis domains whose **inputs and outputs are mutually dependent**. A change in one discipline's output directly affects the other discipline's analysis, requiring **iterative or simultaneous solution**. **Aircraft Design Example — Aero-Structural Coupling (4 Marks):** Consider the design of an aircraft wing: - **Aerodynamics discipline** computes lift $C_L$ and drag $C_D$ as a function of the wing shape, angle of attack $\alpha$, and the structural deflection $\delta$ (since the wing bends under load). - **Structural discipline** computes the deflection $\delta$ as a function of the aerodynamic pressure distribution (which depends on $C_L$). This is a **two-way coupled** problem: $C_L$ depends on $\delta$, and $\delta$ depends on $C_L$. **Definitions:** | Term | Symbol | Definition | Example (Wing Design) | |------|--------|------------|----------------------| | **Design Variable** | $x$ | Parameters the designer chooses | Wing chord $c$, span $b$ | | **State Variable** | $y$ | Discipline outputs | Lift $C_L$, stress $\sigma$ | | **Coupling Variable** | $z$ | State variable shared between disciplines | Deflection $\delta$ (output of structure, input to aero) | | **Coupling Constraint** | $c(x, y_i, y_j) = 0$ | Equations linking disciplines | $z_1 = y_2$ and $z_2 = y_1$ | **Mathematical Statement for Aero-Structural Wing:** $$\min \quad W(x, y) \quad \text{(minimize wing weight)}$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad C_L \geq C_{L,\text{req}}$$ $$c_1: y_1 = f_{\text{aero}}(x, z_2) \quad \text{where} \quad z_2 = y_2$$ $$c_2: y_2 = f_{\text{struct}}(x, z_1) \quad \text{where} \quad z_1 = y_1$$ Here $y_1 = C_L$, $y_2 = \delta$ (deflection), $z_1, z_2$ are the coupling variables. ### Part (b) — Collaborative Optimization (CO) [7 Marks] **Origin and Concept (2 Marks):** Collaborative Optimization was introduced by **Braun and Kroo (1996)** at Stanford. It is a **two-level, distributed** MDO architecture designed to **preserve disciplinary autonomy**, mirroring the **organizational structure** of large engineering companies. **Two-Level Hierarchy (3 Marks):** 1. **System-Level Optimizer (Outer Loop):** - Variables: global design variables $x$ and discipline targets $z_i$ - Objective: minimize system objective $f_0(x)$ plus weighted penalty for discipline-target mismatches: $$\min_{x, z} \quad f_0(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \lVert J_i^*(z_i) \rVert^2$$ - Constraint: $J_i^*(z_i) \leq \epsilon_i$ for each discipline 2. **Discipline-Level Optimizers (Inner Loop):** - Each discipline $i$ solves independently: $$J_i^*(z_i) = \min_{x_i} \quad (x_i - z_i)^T (x_i - z_i)$$ $$\text{s.t.} \quad g_i(x_i, y_i) \leq 0$$ $$c_i(x_i, y_i) = 0$$ - The objective $J_i^*$ is the **squared distance from the target** $z_i$. **Interaction Flow (1 Mark):** - System optimizer proposes $z_i$ for each discipline. - Each discipline optimizer computes its $J_i^*$ (best achievable deviation from target). - System optimizer updates $z_i$ to reduce the total penalty. - Process repeats until all $J_i^* \leq \epsilon_i$. **Comparison with MDF (1 Mark):** | Aspect | CO | MDF | |--------|-----|-----| | Disciplinary autonomy | Very high | None | | Intermediate feasibility | Not coupled-feasible | Fully feasible | | Convergence | Slow (many inner loops) | Faster (single inner loop) | | Implementation | Complex | Simpler | | Best for | Distributed orgs | Tightly coupled single-team problems | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing CO with CSSO**: CO uses **target matching** as objective; CSSO uses **subspace optimization** with response surfaces. Don't mix them. > 2. **Forgetting the system-level penalty term**: Many students write only the discipline sub-problem. The system-level objective with $w_i \lVert J_i^* \rVert^2$ is the **key feature** of CO. > 3. **Mis-stating coupling**: In MDO, the **coupling equation** is $c(x, y) = 0$, not $c(x) = 0$. The state $y$ must appear explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting units**: If $x$ is in meters and $f$ is in kg, the penalty term has units of m². The weight $w_i$ must convert this consistently. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU MDO Essentials** ### 🎯 Core Definitions - ☐ **MDO** = Optimization of coupled multi-disciplinary systems - ☐ **Coupling** = Mutual dependency between discipline outputs/inputs - ☐ **MDA** = Multidisciplinary Analysis — solves the coupled system for a given $x$ - ☐ **GSE** = Global Sensitivity Equation — relates $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ to coupling Jacobians ### 🏗️ The 7 MDO Architectures (Most Important) - ☐ **AAO** — All variables free; no MDA; full derivatives needed - ☐ **MDF** — Centralized, fully feasible, expensive gradients - ☐ **IDF** — Distributed, black-box friendly, coupling as constraints - ☐ **CO** — Two-level, target-matching, high autonomy - ☐ **CSSO** — Subspace optimization with response surfaces - ☐ **BLISS** — Subspace + Global Sensitivity Equations - ☐ **ATC** — Hierarchical target cascading for product families ### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas - ☐ MDF objective: $\min_x f(x, y(x))$ - ☐ GSE: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial c}{\partial y}\right)^{-1} \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x}$ - ☐ Total derivative: $\dfrac{df}{dx} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \left(\dfrac{\partial c}{\partial y}\right)^{-1} \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x}$ - ☐ CO system objective: $f_0 + \sum w_i \lVert J_i^* \rVert^2$ ### ⚠️ Common KTU Pitfalls - ☐ Don't write CO without the **system-level penalty term** - ☐ Don't confuse **state variables** $y$ with **design variables** $x$ - ☐ Always specify the **coupling direction** (one-way vs. two-way) - ☐ In Sellar's problem, identify that the coupling is **bidirectional** - ☐ Remember: MDF gives **feasible** designs at every iteration; CO does **not** ### 🎓 Exam Strategy - ☐ For 3-mark questions: write **3 crisp points**, not paragraphs - ☐ For 7-mark sub-questions: include a **labeled diagram** (MDF/IDF/CO flow) - ☐ For derivations: show **every algebraic step** — partial credit is given - ☐ Memorize the **architecture comparison table** — it's a frequent 14-mark question - ☐ End with a **summary table** for architecture questions > [!NOTE] > **Time Allocation Tip (for 14-mark MDO question):** > - 3 minutes: Sketch the architecture diagram > - 4 minutes: Write the mathematical formulation > - 4 minutes: Explain advantages/disadvantages with examples > - 3 minutes: Final summary table and verification --- *End of Notes — Multidisciplinary Optimization (Module 4)*

topics.title: Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF) — Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Multidisciplinary Design Optimization (MDO), **Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)** is a classical, monolithic MDO architecture in which the **Multidisciplinary Analysis (MDA)** is driven to **full convergence** at every single function evaluation performed by the optimizer.

In mathematical terms, the MDF formulation treats the **state (coupling) variables as dependent**, eliminating them analytically through the converged MDA. The optimizer therefore operates exclusively on the **shared/design variables**, while every intermediate design candidate it generates is guaranteed to satisfy all interdisciplinary coupling equations simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** MDF is one of the three classical MDO architectures — the others being **All-at-Once (AAO / ADF / SAND)** and **Individual Discipline Feasible (IDF)**. A student must be able to write the MDF formulation, draw its information-flow schematic, and contrast it against IDF and ADF for full marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Working Prototype" Method

Imagine you are designing a **four-wheel-drive car** as a team of mechanical, electrical, and software engineers:

| Architecture | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **MDF** | At every design change, the team **fully assembles and tests the prototype** on a test track before deciding the next change. Only working prototypes are evaluated. |
| **IDF** | Each engineer **designs and tests their own subsystem** independently (engine, battery, ECU) on benches — no full prototype is ever assembled during optimization. |
| **ADF (SAND)** | The team sets up one giant equation that includes every component dimension, every coupling equation, every constraint — and solves it as a single huge problem. |
