In **MDF**, the design is always *physically realizable and multidisciplinary-feasible* during the search — but each evaluation is expensive because the prototype (MDA) must be fully built and tested every time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in engineering:** In aerospace (e.g., aircraft wing design coupling aerodynamics, structures, and controls), the MDA is the **"truth model"** — a high-fidelity, expensive simulation. MDF treats this expensive simulation as a black box the optimizer calls repeatedly.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notation (KTU Convention)

The following notation is universally adopted in MDO literature and the KTU 2024 scheme textbooks:

- $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ — **shared (global) design variables**
- $y \in \mathbb{R}^{n_y}$ — **state (coupling) variables**, output of MDA
- $f(x, y)$ — **objective function**
- $g(x, y) \le 0$ — **inequality constraints**
- $h(x, y) = 0$ — **equality constraints**
- $A(x, y) = 0$ — **Multidisciplinary Analysis (MDA)** residual vector
- $N$ — total number of disciplines, indexed $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$

> [!TIP]
> A **discipline** is a self-contained engineering analysis (e.g., aerodynamics, structures, propulsion). The MDA is the simultaneous numerical solution of all $N$ disciplines together with their coupling variables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of MDF

The MDF optimization problem is written in the standard Nonlinear Programming (NLP) form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}} \quad & f\big(x, y(x)\big) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j\big(x, y(x)\big) \le 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, m \\
& h_k\big(x, y(x)\big) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p \\
& A\big(x, y(x)\big) = 0 \quad \text{(MDA convergence)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key idea:** The state variables $y$ are **not** decision variables of the optimizer. They are functions of $x$, obtained implicitly by converging the MDA at every evaluation.

---

## 2.2 The Multidisciplinary Analysis (MDA) — What the Black Box Does

For a system of $N$ coupled disciplines, each discipline $i$ has its own analysis equation:

$$
y_i = A_i(x, y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N)
$$

The MDA residual is the stacked vector:

$$
A(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} A_1(x, y) \\ A_2(x, y) \\ \vdots \\ A_N(x, y) \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

This system is typically solved by a **fixed-point iteration** (Gauss-Seidel, Jacobi, or Newton-Raphson on the coupled Jacobian):

$$
y^{(k+1)} = A\big(x, y^{(k)}\big)
$$

The iteration converges when $\Vert y^{(k+1)} - y^{(k)} \Vert < \varepsilon_{\text{MDA}}$.

---

## 2.3 Gradient Computation in MDF — The Total Derivative

Because MDF eliminates $y$, the gradient of any response $F(x, y(x))$ w.r.t. $x$ requires the **chain rule through the converged MDA**:

$$
\frac{dF}{dx} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

The sensitivity $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is obtained by **implicit differentiation** of $A(x, y) = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0
$$

Solving for the Jacobian:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} \frac{\partial A}{\partial x}
$$

This matrix is called the **MDA Jacobian** and is the heart of the MDF sensitivity analysis. Its computation requires either:

1. **Direct differentiation** — analytically differentiating each discipline code and propagating through MDA.
2. **Adjoint method** — solves a linear system backward for efficiency when there are few objectives/constraints and many design variables.
3. **Finite differences** — perturb each $x_i$ and re-converge the MDA (brute force, $n_x$ extra MDAs per gradient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The matrix $\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]$ is the **coupling Jacobian** of the multidisciplinary system. Its inverse must exist and be well-conditioned for MDF gradients to be valid.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete high-yield formula set** for MDF-type questions. Memorize these before the ESE.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MDF NLP form | $\min f(x, y(x))$ s.t. $g, h, A(x,y)=0$ | $y$ eliminated via MDA |
| 2 | MDA residual | $A(x,y)=0$ | Black-box call inside optimizer |
| 3 | MDA fixed-point | $y^{(k+1)} = A(x, y^{(k)})$ | Converged when $\Vert y^{(k+1)}-y^{(k)}\Vert < \varepsilon$ |
| 4 | Total derivative | $\dfrac{dF}{dx} = \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Chain rule through MDA |
| 5 | MDA Jacobian | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1}\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial x}$ | Implicit diff. of $A=0$ |
| 6 | Multidisciplinary Feasibility | $A(x^*, y(x^*)) = 0$ | Strictly enforced at every $x^*$ candidate |
| 7 | Cost per iteration | $1$ full MDA + sensitivity cost | Expensive |
| 8 | Variable count | $n_x$ decision variables | Only shared vars |
| 9 | Gradient via adjoint | $\lambda^T \dfrac{\partial A}{\partial x}$, $\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^T \lambda = \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}$ | Used for large $n_x$ |
| 10 | Comparison marker | MDF $\Rightarrow$ Monolithic w.r.t. disciplines | vs. IDF, AAO |

---

## 2.5 Why MDF is Used in Real Engineering

| Engineering Field | Typical MDA | Why MDF fits |
|---|---|---|
| **Aero-structural** | CFD + FEM | High-fidelity solvers reused as black-box |
| **Automotive crash** | Explicit FEM codes | Single solver wrapped as MDA |
| **Propulsion** | Thermodynamic cycle + structural | Each discipline has legacy code |
| **Turbo-machinery** | CFD + rotor dynamics | Coupling stable, convergence fast |

In all these fields, MDF is preferred because the **MDA already exists** as a validated engineering tool — engineers only need to add an outer optimization loop (e.g., via gradient-free methods like SQP or genetic algorithms).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MDF Total Gradient

We begin with the assumption that the MDA residual function $A(x, y)$ is $C^1$ (continuously differentiable) and that the local Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}$ is nonsingular at the point of interest.

### Step 1 — Define the response of interest

Let the scalar response be:

$$
F(x, y) = f(x, y)
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize that $y$ depends on $x$ through MDA

Because the optimizer picks $x$ only, the converged MDA enforces $A(x, y) = 0$, which defines an implicit function $y = y(x)$ (by the Implicit Function Theorem).

### Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for total derivative

Differentiating $F$ with respect to $x$ along the implicit manifold $A=0$:

$$
\frac{dF}{dx} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

### Step 4 — Implicit differentiation of $A(x, y(x)) = 0$

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\big[A(x, y(x))\big] = \frac{\partial A}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$

$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{\partial A}{\partial x}
$$

Multiplying both sides by the inverse of the coupling Jacobian (which is nonsingular):

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} \frac{\partial A}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute back into the total derivative

$$
\frac{dF}{dx} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \cdot \left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} \frac{\partial A}{\partial x}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The first term $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ is the *direct* effect of changing $x$. The second term captures the *indirect* effect propagating through the converged disciplines. For an MDF architecture with weakly-coupled disciplines, the indirect term is often small, and gradients can be approximated by the direct term alone.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Two-Coupled-Discipline MDF

Consider a system with **two disciplines**:

- Discipline 1: $y_1 = A_1(x, y_2) = x_1 + 0.5\, y_2$
- Discipline 2: $y_2 = A_2(x, y_1) = x_2 + 0.3\, y_1$

Here $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$, $y = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \end{bmatrix}$, and the MDA residual is:

$$
A(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - x_1 - 0.5\, y_2 \\ y_2 - x_2 - 0.3\, y_1 \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

### Step 1 — Coupling Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}$

$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ -0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Determinant and Inverse

$$
\det\!\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right) = (1)(1) - (-0.5)(-0.3) = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85
$$

$$
\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.85}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Direct partial $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial x}$

$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = -I
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} \frac{\partial A}{\partial x} = -\left(\frac{1}{0.85}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\right)(-I)
$$

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{0.85}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Numerically, $\dfrac{1}{0.85} \approx 1.1765$:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1765 & 0.5882 \\ 0.3529 & 1.1765 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Converged $y(x)$ via fixed-point MDA

Solving the linear MDA system $A(x,y)=0$ exactly (i.e., converging MDA to $\varepsilon = 0$):

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.85}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For example, if $x_1 = 1.0$ and $x_2 = 2.0$:

$$
y_1 = \frac{1}{0.85}(1.0 + 1.0) = 2.3529
$$

$$
y_2 = \frac{1}{0.85}(0.3 + 2.0) = 2.7059
$$

### Step 6 — Verification of gradient by direct numerical check

Perturb $x_1$ by $\Delta x_1 = 0.01$, keep $x_2 = 2.0$:

- New $x_1' = 1.01$
- New $y_1' = \frac{1}{0.85}(1.01 + 1.0) = 2.3647$
- New $y_2' = \frac{1}{0.85}(0.303 + 2.0) = 2.7094$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dy_1}{dx_1} \approx \frac{2.3647 - 2.3529}{0.01} = 1.1800 \;\;\text{(analytical: 1.1765)} \;\; \checkmark
$$

$$
\frac{dy_2}{dx_1} \approx \frac{2.7094 - 2.7059}{0.01} = 0.3500 \;\;\text{(analytical: 0.3529)} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The analytical gradient matches the numerical perturbation, confirming the MDF total-derivative derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics — MDF Information Flow

## 4.1 MDF Architecture Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **monolithic information flow** in an MDF architecture — the optimizer sees only $x$, the MDA black-box produces $y$, and all responses $f, g, h$ are evaluated using the *fully converged* multidisciplinary state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    optA[Optimizer Loop]:::opt --> pickX[Select Design Vector x]
    pickX --> callMDA[Call MDA Black Box A of x y]
    callMDA --> initGuess[Initialize y with initial guess y0]
    initGuess --> discLoop{Discipline Solver Iterations}
    discLoop -->|not converged| updateY[Update Coupling Variables y]
    updateY --> discLoop
    discLoop -->|converged| convergedY[Converged State y of x]
    convergedY --> evalF[Evaluate Objective f of x y]
    evalF --> evalG[Evaluate Constraints g of x y]
    evalG --> evalH[Evaluate Equality Constraints h of x y]
    evalH --> buildResp[Build Response Vector R]
    buildResp --> optA
    classDef opt fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer loop is the **optimizer**, the inner loop is the **MDA**. The arrow returning to the optimizer carries only the scalar/vector responses $(f, g, h)$ — never $y$. The optimizer never "sees" $y$ as an independent variable.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — MDF vs. IDF vs. AAO

This block diagram compares the three classical MDO architectures at the function-evaluation level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MDF[MDF Architecture]
        direction TB
        optM[Optimizer] --> xVecM[Shared Design Vector x]
        xVecM --> mdaBlock[Converged MDA A of x y equals 0]
        mdaBlock --> respM[Responses f g h]
        respM --> optM
    end
    subgraph IDF[IDF Architecture]
        direction TB
        optI[Optimizer] --> xAndY[Augmented Vector x plus y]
        xAndY --> disc1[Discipline 1 Solver]
        xAndY --> disc2[Discipline 2 Solver]
        xAndY --> discN[Discipline N Solver]
        disc1 --> respI[Responses f g h]
        disc2 --> respI
        discN --> respI
        respI --> optI
    end
    subgraph AAO[AAO or SAND Architecture]
        direction TB
        optA2[Single Large Optimizer] --> bigX[Vector of x plus y plus lambda]
        bigX --> coupledEq[All Disciplines plus Coupling as Equality Constraints]
        coupledEq --> respA[Responses f g h]
        respA --> optA2
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic of MDA Convergence (Fixed-Point Iteration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Design x fixed]) --> y0[Initialize y superscript 0]
    y0 --> compA[Compute A of x y superscript k]
    compA --> checkNorm{Norm of y superscript k plus 1 minus y superscript k less than epsilon}
    checkNorm -->|no| incK[k equals k plus 1] --> compA
    checkNorm -->|yes| outY[Output Converged y equals y of x]
    outY --> nextX([Optimizer updates x])
```

---

## 4.4 Summary Schematic — Where Gradients are Computed in MDF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startG[Required Total Derivative dF of dx] --> split[Split into Direct plus Indirect Parts]
    split --> direct[Direct Term partial F over partial x]
    split --> indirect[Indirect Term via d y over d x]
    indirect --> implicDiff[Implicit Differentiation of A equals 0]
    implicDiff --> invJ[Inverse of Coupling Jacobian partial A over partial y]
    invJ --> combine[Combine partial F over partial y times dy over dx]
    combine --> finalGrad[Final Total Gradient]
    direct --> finalGrad
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q:** Define the **Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)** formulation. State two of its distinguishing features compared to IDF.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Multidisciplinary Feasible (MDF)** formulation is an MDO architecture in which the **multidisciplinary analysis (MDA) is fully converged at every function evaluation** performed by the optimizer. The optimizer works only with the shared design variables $x$, while the state (coupling) variables $y$ are eliminated by solving the MDA residual $A(x, y) = 0$.

**Distinguishing features (any two):**
1. **Strict multidisciplinary feasibility** is enforced at every candidate $x$ — only points satisfying all coupling equations are evaluated.
2. The optimizer's decision space has dimension **$n_x$ only** — state variables $y$ are not independent decision variables.
3. Each optimization iteration requires **one full MDA convergence**, making it computationally expensive but conceptually simple.
4. Gradient computation requires the **MDA Jacobian** via implicit differentiation.

*(Mark split: Definition 1.5 marks, features 0.75 each, total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Write the mathematical expression for the total derivative $\dfrac{dF}{dx}$ in the MDF formulation and identify each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{dF}{dx} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} \frac{\partial A}{\partial x}
$$

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ | Direct effect of design variable change on the response |
| $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}$ | Direct effect of state variable change on the response |
| $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1}\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial x}$ | Sensitivity of MDA state to design variable change (via implicit diff.) |

*(Mark split: Equation 1.5 marks, identification of terms 1.5 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For MDF questions, students commonly lose marks by: **(a)** confusing MDF with IDF and writing IDF formulation, **(b)** forgetting the role of the MDA Jacobian in gradient computation, **(c)** not specifying that MDA is converged *inside* every optimizer iteration, and **(d)** omitting units or failing to identify which variables are decision variables vs. eliminated states.

---

### Question A — `(14 Marks)`  **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

A coupled two-discipline system is defined by:

- **Discipline 1 (Aerodynamics):** $y_1 = A_1(x, y_2) = 2\,x_1 + 0.4\,y_2$
- **Discipline 2 (Structures):** $y_2 = A_2(x, y_1) = 1.5\,x_2 + 0.25\,y_1$

Design variables: $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$, coupling variables: $y = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Formulate the MDF optimization problem and write down the converged expressions for $y_1(x)$ and $y_2(x)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the MDA residual**

The MDF formulation treats $x$ as the only decision variable. The state $y$ is eliminated by converging:

$$
A(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - 2x_1 - 0.4 y_2 \\ y_2 - 1.5 x_2 - 0.25 y_1 \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear MDA system**

From the second equation: $y_2 = 1.5 x_2 + 0.25 y_1$. Substituting into the first:

$$
y_1 = 2x_1 + 0.4 (1.5 x_2 + 0.25 y_1) = 2x_1 + 0.6 x_2 + 0.1 y_1
$$

$$
0.9\, y_1 = 2 x_1 + 0.6 x_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; y_1 = \frac{2 x_1 + 0.6 x_2}{0.9} = \frac{20 x_1 + 6 x_2}{9}
$$

$$
y_2 = 1.5 x_2 + 0.25 \cdot \frac{20 x_1 + 6 x_2}{9} = 1.5 x_2 + \frac{5 x_1 + 1.5 x_2}{9}
$$

$$
y_2 = \frac{13.5 x_2 + 5 x_1 + 1.5 x_2}{9} = \frac{5 x_1 + 15 x_2}{9}
$$

**Step 3 — General MDF optimization formulation**

$$
\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}} \; f\big(x, y(x)\big) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g(x, y(x)) \le 0, \quad h(x, y(x)) = 0
$$

with $y(x)$ given above and $A(x, y(x)) = 0$ implicitly enforced. *(3 marks for formulation, 4 marks for derivation.)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Compute the MDF total gradient $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ analytically. Then verify one entry by numerical perturbation using $x = (1, 1)^T$ with $\Delta x_1 = 0.01$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Coupling Jacobian**

$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.4 \\ -0.25 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \frac{\partial A}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Inverse of the coupling Jacobian**

$$
\det = 1 - (-0.4)(-0.25) = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9
$$

$$
\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.9}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.4 \\ 0.25 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Total gradient**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\frac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1}\frac{\partial A}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{0.9}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.4 \\ 0.25 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{0.9}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.222 & 0.667 \\ 0.556 & 1.667 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical verification at $x = (1, 1)^T$**

Converged $y$ values: $y_1 = \dfrac{20 + 6}{9} = 2.889$, $y_2 = \dfrac{5 + 15}{9} = 2.222$.

Perturbed $x' = (1.01, 1)^T$:

- $y_1' = \dfrac{20(1.01) + 6}{9} = \dfrac{26.2}{9} = 2.911$
- $y_2' = \dfrac{5(1.01) + 15}{9} = \dfrac{20.05}{9} = 2.228$

Numerical sensitivities:

$$
\frac{dy_1}{dx_1} \approx \frac{2.911 - 2.889}{0.01} = 2.200 \;\;\text{vs. analytical } 2.222 \;\; \checkmark
$$

$$
\frac{dy_2}{dx_1} \approx \frac{2.228 - 2.222}{0.01} = 0.600 \;\;\text{vs. analytical } 0.556 \;\; \checkmark
$$

*(Mark split: Jacobian 1.5, inverse 1.5, gradient derivation 2, numerical check 2.)*

---

### Question B — `(14 Marks)`  **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

Compare and contrast the three classical MDO architectures — **MDF**, **IDF**, and **AAO/SAND** — based on:
- variable treatment,
- feasibility guarantees,
- per-iteration cost,
- and gradient computation strategy.

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Present a comparative table and write the explicit optimization formulations.

**Solution:**

**Comparative table:**

| Criterion | **MDF** | **IDF** | **AAO / SAND** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision variables | Only $x$ | $x$ and $y$ (augmented) | $x$, $y$, $\lambda$ (all variables) |
| Multidisciplinary feasibility | Guaranteed at every iteration | NOT guaranteed during search | Enforced only at final optimum |
| MDA inside optimizer | Yes — fully converged | No — each discipline run independently | No — coupling as equality constraints |
| Per-iteration cost | High (1 full MDA) | Lower ($N$ decoupled analyses) | Same as IDF — but larger problem |
| Gradient strategy | Total derivative via MDA Jacobian | Partial derivatives directly available | Single KKT system |
| Best when | Disciplines tightly coupled, MDA stable | Disciplines loosely coupled, expensive MDA | Disciplines have no feasible MDA path |
| Drawback | MDA may fail to converge | Multidisciplinary infeasibility during search | Very large NLP, hard convergence |

**Explicit formulations:**

- **MDF:** $\min_x f(x, y(x))$ s.t. $g, h$, with $A(x, y(x)) = 0$.
- **IDF:** $\min_{x, y} f(x, y)$ s.t. $g(x, y) \le 0$, $h(x, y) = 0$, with $y = A(x, y)$ relaxed (each discipline solved independently given $y$ as input).
- **AAO:** $\min_{x, y} f(x, y)$ s.t. $g, h$, $A(x, y) = 0$ (coupling now an explicit equality constraint in the NLP).

*(Mark split: Table 4 marks, formulations 3 marks.)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the practical engineering situations in which **MDF is preferred** over the other two architectures. Justify with reference to the MDA Jacobian and gradient computation.

**Solution:**

**MDF is preferred when:**

1. **A high-fidelity MDA already exists** as legacy software (e.g., NASTRAN for aeroelasticity). Wrapping it as a black-box and running an outer optimizer is the cheapest engineering option. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Disciplines are tightly coupled** — the MDA converges robustly, and the inverse of the coupling Jacobian $\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1}$ is well-conditioned. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Multidisciplinary feasibility is critical at every intermediate design**, e.g., aircraft load-bearing structures where an infeasible intermediate wing shape has no physical meaning. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Gradient-based optimizers (SQP, IP)** are used, and total derivatives computed via the MDA Jacobian (or adjoint) provide fast, accurate sensitivities. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Number of design variables $n_x$ is moderate** — so finite-difference or direct-differentiation gradients are affordable. *(1 mark)*

**Why the MDA Jacobian matters:**

- The matrix $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}$ must be **invertible** for MDF sensitivities to exist.
- For **loosely coupled** disciplines, $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}$ is near-singular — gradients become noisy and the optimizer may diverge. In that case, IDF is preferred.
- For **non-converging MDA** (e.g., strong fluid–structure interaction), MDF fails entirely — AAO/SAND is the fallback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical rule of thumb for KTU:** *"If your MDA converges, use MDF. If MDA is unstable but each discipline runs cleanly, use IDF. If MDA is meaningless (e.g., conceptual design), use AAO."*

*(Mark split: 5 situations × 1.4 marks ≈ 7 marks.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MDF (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** MDF converges the MDA fully inside every optimizer iteration; only the design variables $x$ are independent.
- **Key equation:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\left[\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}\right]^{-1}\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial x}$ — derived via implicit differentiation of $A(x,y)=0$.
- **Total derivative of any response:** $\dfrac{dF}{dx} = \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
- **Feasibility:** Multidisciplinary feasibility is *strictly guaranteed* at every candidate $x$.
- **Variable count:** $n_x$ decision variables — the smallest among MDF, IDF, AAO.
- **Per-iteration cost:** Highest (1 full MDA per evaluation), but conceptually simplest.
- **Gradient methods:** Direct differentiation, adjoint method, or finite differences — all require the coupling Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial y}$.
- **MDA convergence criterion:** $\Vert y^{(k+1)} - y^{(k)} \Vert < \varepsilon_{\text{MDA}}$ (Gauss-Seidel / Newton).
- **Comparison anchors:**
  - MDF = monolithic w.r.t. disciplines
  - IDF = decoupled feasibility during search
  - AAO/SAND = single large NLP with coupling as constraint
- **Engineering preference:** Use MDF when MDA is stable, high-fidelity, and convergence-robust.
- **Examiner's trap:** Students forget that $y$ is **not** a decision variable in MDF — it is eliminated via MDA, not optimized over.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET743 - DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS)** is a formal, hierarchical decomposition strategy for solving **Multidisciplinary Design Optimization (MDO)** problems. Proposed by **Sobieszczanski-Sobieski (1988)**, it partitions a coupled, large-scale engineering optimization problem into a single **System-Level (Top-Level)** optimization that coordinates several independently executed **Subsystem-Level (Bottom-Level)** optimizations. The coordination is achieved via the **Chain Rule of Sensitivity Analysis**, wherein the system optimizer propagates design changes downward and gathers gradient feedback upward.

Mathematically, BLISS reformulates a single monolithic optimization:

$$\min_{X,Y} \; F(X, Y) \quad \text{subject to system-level constraints}$$

into a coordinated set of $1 + N$ sub-problems (1 system + $N$ disciplinary subsystems) that exchange sensitivity information $\partial Y / \partial X$ across an iterative loop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Corporate CEO & Department Heads" Model

Imagine a multinational engineering company designing a new supersonic aircraft.

- **System-Level (CEO)**: The CEO does not personally design wings or engines. Instead, the CEO sets **high-level corporate targets** (total weight, total cost, range) and allocates a global budget (the *system design variables* $X$). Each quarter, the CEO looks at performance reports and re-allocates resources.
- **Subsystem-Level (Department Heads)**: Each department head (Aerodynamics, Structures, Propulsion) optimizes their own department — minimizing drag, minimizing structural mass, maximizing thrust — using the resources handed down by the CEO.
- **Coupling Variables ($Y$)**: Departments do not work in isolation. Aerodynamics needs the wing shape (set by Structures), and Structures needs the aerodynamic pressure distribution. These *shared, interdependent design variables* are the **coupling variables**.

> [!NOTE]
> The CEO cannot see the *internal* decisions of each department (e.g., how exactly the aerodynamics team picks airfoils). The CEO only sees the **net effect** of departmental decisions via **sensitivity reports**. This is exactly the BLISS philosophy — *separation of concerns* combined with *gradient-based coordination*.

## 1.3 Why BLISS Was Created — The MDO Bottleneck

In a fully-coupled (All-At-Once, AAO) MDO problem, every discipline contributes hundreds of design variables and thousands of state variables. A monolithic optimizer faces:

| Bottleneck | Consequence | BLISS Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensionality Explosion | Intractable Hessian matrices | Decomposed Hessian (block-diagonal) |
| Organizational Reality | Design teams cannot be merged | Parallel subsystem execution |
| Disciplinary IP | Aerodynamics codes ≠ Structures codes | Black-box subsystem wrappers |
| Disciplinary Optima | Local minima dominate global | Two-level coordination escapes these |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BLISS is **the most widely taught and historically foundational MDO architecture**. It is explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 PEPET743 Module 4 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BLISS Problem Statement — Formal Mathematical Formulation

The complete (undecomposed) MDO problem is:

$$\min_{X, Y} \; F(X, Y)$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad g_j(X, Y) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, J$$

where:
- $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n_X}$ = **System-Level (Global) Design Variables** (shared across all disciplines, set at the top level).
- $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n_Y}$ = **Coupling (State/Interface) Variables** that link the disciplines.
- $F$ = **System-Level Objective** (global metric like total mass, total cost).

The BLISS decomposition partitions $Y$ into $N$ disciplinary vectors $Y_i$ (one per subsystem) and introduces internal subsystem variables $y_i$ that are *not* visible to the system optimizer.

## 2.2 The Three Mathematical Tiers of BLISS

### Tier 1 — System-Level Optimization Problem

$$\min_{X} \; F\bigl(X, Y(X)\bigr)$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad g_j(X, Y(X)) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, J$$

The system optimizer manipulates **only** $X$. It does *not* directly optimize $Y$. Instead, $Y$ is implicitly determined as a function of $X$ through the subsystem-level solves.

### Tier 2 — Subsystem-Level Optimization Problem (one per discipline)

For each discipline $i = 1, \ldots, N$:

$$\min_{y_i} \; f_i\bigl(y_i;\, X, Y_j, \, j \neq i\bigr)$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad h_{i,k}\bigl(y_i;\, X, Y_j\bigr) \leq 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, K_i$$

where:
- $f_i$ = **Subsystem-Level Objective** (e.g., aerodynamic drag, structural compliance).
- $y_i$ = **Local (Private) Design Variables** of subsystem $i$.
- $X$ is *frozen* during subsystem optimization (treated as a parameter).
- $Y_j$ for $j \neq i$ are the **received coupling variables** from other disciplines.

### Tier 3 — Coupling (Compatibility / Interdisciplinary) Equations

These enforce that what one discipline *outputs* is consistent with what another *receives*:

$$C_i\bigl(y_i;\, X, Y_j\bigr) = Y_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N$$

This is typically solved via a *fixed-point iteration* over $Y$ (Jacobi or Gauss-Seidel sweeps).

## 2.3 The Chain-Rule Sensitivity Equation — The Heart of BLISS

The total derivative of $F$ w.r.t. $X$ is:

$$\frac{dF}{dX} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial X} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial Y} \cdot \frac{dY}{dX}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\partial F / \partial X$ and $\partial F / \partial Y$ are easy — they are local partial derivatives of the system objective.

The hard term is $\dfrac{dY}{dX}$. In BLISS, this is obtained by differentiating the **compatibility equations** $C_i = Y_i$:

$$\frac{d Y_i}{d X} = \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial X} + \sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial Y_j} \cdot \frac{d Y_j}{d X}$$

This is a **linear system in the unknown sensitivities** $\dfrac{dY_j}{dX}$:

$$\left[ I - \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y} \right] \cdot \frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial X}$$

Solving this system gives the system optimizer the gradient it needs to update $X$ using any gradient-based method (e.g., SQP, MMA).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $F(X,Y)$ | System-level objective | Global metric (e.g., total aircraft mass) |
| $f_i(y_i; X, Y_j)$ | Subsystem $i$ objective | Discipline-level metric (e.g., drag, stress) |
| $X$ | System design variables | Vector of length $n_X$, controlled at top level |
| $Y$ | Coupling (state) variables | Vector of length $n_Y$, solved at subsystem level |
| $y_i$ | Local (private) variables | Internal to subsystem $i$ |
| $g_j(X,Y) \leq 0$ | System constraints | Inequalities, $j=1,\ldots,J$ |
| $h_{i,k} \leq 0$ | Subsystem constraints | Discipline constraints, $k=1,\ldots,K_i$ |
| $C_i = Y_i$ | Compatibility equation | Interdisciplinary consistency |
| $\dfrac{dF}{dX}$ | Total gradient | $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial X} + \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial Y} \cdot \dfrac{dY}{dX}$ |
| $\dfrac{dY}{dX}$ | Sensitivity matrix | Solved from linear system $\bigl[I - \partial C / \partial Y\bigr] \cdot \dfrac{dY}{dX} = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial X}$ |
| $L_i$ | Subsystem Lagrangian | $f_i + \lambda_i^{T} h_i + \mu_i^{T}(C_i - Y_i)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Summary**: System optimizer updates $X$ → freeze $X$ → subsystems iterate on $Y$ to solve $C_i = Y_i$ → subsystem optimizers update local $y_i$ → assemble $\partial C / \partial X$ and $\partial C / \partial Y$ → solve for $dY/dX$ → update $X$. The loop repeats until $X$ converges.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

BLISS-class architectures are deployed in:

- **Aerospace** — NASA’s *High-Speed Civil Transport* (HSCT) studies, Boeing 787 wing/empennage MDO.
- **Automotive** — Full-vehicle crashworthiness + NVH + manufacturability co-design (e.g., Ford, BMW).
- **Civil Engineering** — Bridge–wind–traffic coupled optimization of long-span suspension bridges.
- **Naval Architecture** — Hull-form + structural + propulsion integrated design.
- **Energy Systems** — Wind-farm layout + turbine aerostructural + grid-output co-optimization.

The defining practical advantage: **each discipline can keep its own legacy solver (FEA, CFD, BEM) untouched** — BLISS only requires that each solver expose its gradients via the compatibility equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BLISS Sensitivity Equation

### Step 1 — Start from the Compatibility Equations

By definition, for each discipline $i$, the *outputs* of the discipline analysis must equal the *inputs* the other disciplines expect:

$$
Y_i = C_i(y_i,\, X,\, Y_j),\qquad i=1,\ldots,N
$$

We collect all $N$ such equations into a single stacked vector equation:

$$
Y = C(X, Y)
$$

### Step 2 — Implicit Function Theorem

The above is an *implicit* definition of $Y$ as a function of $X$. By the Implicit Function Theorem, the derivative exists provided the Jacobian $\partial Y / \partial Y - \partial C / \partial Y = I - \partial C / \partial Y$ is non-singular.

### Step 3 — Total Differential of $Y = C(X, Y)$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $X$:

$$
\frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial X} + \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y}\cdot \frac{dY}{dX}
$$

### Step 4 — Isolate $dY/dX$

Move the unknown term to the left:

$$
\frac{dY}{dX} - \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y}\cdot \frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial X}
$$

$$
\left[I \;-\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y}\right]\cdot \frac{dY}{dX} \;=\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial X}
$$

### Step 5 — Invert to Get the Sensitivity Matrix

$$
\frac{dY}{dX} \;=\; \left[I \;-\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y}\right]^{-1} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial X}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight**: The matrix $\bigl[I - \partial C / \partial Y\bigr]$ is the **Global Sensitivity Equation Matrix (GSEM)** of BLISS. Its diagonal dominance is a precondition for stable convergence. If disciplines are weakly coupled, $\partial C / \partial Y \approx 0$ and the matrix reduces to $I$, recovering *Collaborative Optimization*-like behavior.

### Step 6 — Substitute Back into $dF/dX$

Now apply the chain rule to the system objective:

$$
\frac{dF}{dX} \;=\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial X} \;+\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial Y}\cdot \frac{dY}{dX}
$$

Substituting the expression from Step 5:

$$
\frac{dF}{dX} \;=\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial X} \;+\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial Y}\cdot \left[I \;-\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial Y}\right]^{-1}\cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial X}
$$

This is the gradient handed to the system-level optimizer.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Subsystem KKT Conditions

Each subsystem $i$ minimizes $f_i(y_i)$ subject to local constraints $h_{i,k}\leq 0$ and the coupling constraint $C_i = Y_i$. The Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_i \;=\; f_i(y_i) \;+\; \sum_k \lambda_{i,k}\, h_{i,k}(y_i) \;+\; \mu_i^{T}\bigl(C_i(y_i, X, Y_j) - Y_i\bigr)
$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}_i / \partial y_i = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_i} \;+\; \sum_k \lambda_{i,k}\frac{\partial h_{i,k}}{\partial y_i} \;+\; \left(\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial y_i}\right)^{T}\mu_i \;=\; 0
$$

Complementary slackness: $\lambda_{i,k}\, h_{i,k} = 0,\;\; \lambda_{i,k}\geq 0,\;\; h_{i,k}\leq 0$.

The multipliers $\mu_i$ are precisely the *prices* exchanged across disciplines — they encode how a perturbation in the coupling variable $Y_i$ affects each subsystem's optimum.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BLISS vs. Other MDO Architectures

| Criterion | BLISS | MDF (Monolithic) | CO (Collaborative Opt.) | IDF (Individual Discipline Feasible) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coupling iterations | Yes (fixed-point on $Y$) | None | Yes (across disciplinary targets) | None |
| System-level control | Full $X$ | None | Targets only | Full $X$ |
| Subsystem autonomy | High (own $y_i$) | None | High | High |
| Disciplines always feasible? | No | Yes | No | **Yes** |
| Gradient computation | Chain rule (exact) | Exact | Approximate | Exact |
| Implementation complexity | Medium | High (monolithic) | Low | Medium |
| Convergence speed | Fast (Newton-level) | Fast | Slow (often) | Fast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, remember: **BLISS is a "bi-level" architecture by name, but in practice it has $N+1$ levels (one system + $N$ subsystems)**. The "bi" refers to the *two types* of levels — coordinating and coordinated — not the count.

|---|---|---|
| 1 | System → Subsystems | Proposed $X$ |
| 2 | Subsystem $i$ → Coupling Bus | Output $Y_i$ |
| 3 | Coupling Bus → Subsystems | Inputs $Y_j$, $j \neq i$ |
| 4 | Subsystem → Sensitivity Bus | Jacobian blocks $\partial C_i / \partial X$ and $\partial C_i / \partial Y$ |
| 5 | System → Subsystems | Updated $X$ for next iteration |
